Castleman, 5 Rand. 195; s. c., 16 Am. Dec. 741; Coltrane v. Worrell, 30 Gratt. 484; Geiss v. Beall, 3 Wis. 367; Smith v. McGeehee, 14 Ala. 404; Bent v. Priest, 10 Mo. App. 562; Clement’s Appeal, 49 Conn. 519. A trustee receiving gifts from the tenants of his estate should be punished more severely than simply by being compelled to give up the sum he has received, though possibly the deprivation of all his commission might be too severe. Jacobus v. Munn, 38 N. J. Eq. 622.

An agent who had charge of his principal's real estate during his absence, let a portion of it and received rents, but neglected to pay taxes, in consequence of which the property was sold at a tax sale, the agent being the purchaser. It was held that the court would enjoin the agent from executing a writ of possession under a judgment obtained by default against his principal for the land so purchased. Morris v. Joseph, 1 W. Va. 256; s. c., 91 Am. Dec. 386.

A trustee who borrows money for his own use, assigns an order or decree in favor of the trust estate as security, the assignment is invalid. Brewster v. Galloway, 4 Lea (Tenn.), 558. A trustee will not be permitted to settle a debt owing to him in his fiduciary capacity by merely canceling one due from him individually to the trust debtor. If, however, the cestui que trust adopts the settlement and sues the trustee’s sureties to make good the amount, the cestui que trust will not afterwards be permitted to recover it from the original debtor. Sweet v. Jeffries, 67 Mo. 420. A party who receives an estate from another under an agreement to deliver it to a third person cannot refuse to do so because the person who delivers it to him is indebted to himself or others. Sledge v. Clopton, 6 Ala. 589. A trustee is not permitted to use the trust property for his own benefit, and his being the father of the cestuis que trust, and maintaining them in the meantime, do not affect the question. Myers v. Myers, 2 McCord Eq. (S. Car.) 213; s. c., 16 Am. Dec. 648. One who, by agreement with the owner of land which is about to be sold on execution, becomes the purchaser, under a promise to reconvey, stands, until the obligation to reconvey is discharged, in the relation of trustee, within the rule which restricts

<br>

### --- Page 0348 ---

1172

DUTIES AND OBLIGATIONS OF TRUSTEES. [§ 516.]

has been fully sustained by the courts of the United States.¹

§ 516.

The Mixing of Trust and Private Funds.—It is the duty of a trustee to keep the trust property in his possession quite distinct from his own funds. The rule is well established that where a trustee mixes and so confuses a trustee from dealing with the trust property for his own benefit. Vestal v. Sloan, 76 N. Car. 127.

The rule prohibiting an executor or administrator from making a profit by buying up claims against the estate at less than their face value, and obtaining allowance in full out of the estate, applies to proceeds arising from the sale of decedent’s real estate, though the administrator is not vested with the title thereto, since he is authorized by Act 1834 (P. L. 76) to sell it to pay debts of the deceased when the personal estate is insufficient. In re Cake’s Estate, 157 Pa. St. 457; s.c., 27 Atl. Rep. 773. As to the duty of county officials and other public trustees, Chief Justice Howard of the Indiana Supreme Court, in Winchester Electric Light Co. v. Veal, 44 N.E. Rep. 353, 354, says: “An officer may not use the public funds by way of investment in any kind of property or business. Neither may he loan such funds, either with or without interest; but such official is required to keep the public moneys in a safe place, ready to be paid over to the proper person whenever demanded. He may, therefore, provided he is not forbidden by law, deposit such moneys in a bank, subject to his check. A bank is usually a safe place to keep funds. It is true that some risk is taken in making such deposits. The bank may fail, and the money be lost. The public official who deposits money in a bank must take that hazard. If the money is lost he shall make it good. But, so long as there is no law against making such deposits, he may exercise his judgment as to making them; and the making of such deposits will not subject him to the charge of embezzling the public funds in his charge. The deposits, however, must be subject to check. Loans to a bank, whether as time deposits or on certificates, or secured by bank paper or otherwise, are as obnoxious to the statute as like loans made to an individual or other corporation.” An officer, on the ground of public policy, is liable for public moneys intrusted to him and lost through the failure of the bank in which they were deposited by him though he was not negligent. Tillinghast v. Merrill (1896) (N. Y.), 45 N. E. Rep. 375.

¹ Siter v. McClanahan, 2 Gratt. 280; Maynard v. Cleveland, 76 Ga. 52; Smith v. McGeehee, 14 Ala. 404; Brown v. Cowell, 116 Mass. 461; Graff v. Castleman, 5 Rand. 195; Sherman v. Lanier, 39 N. J. Eq. 249; Cleveland v. Pollard, 37 Ala. 556; Vason v. Beall, 58 Ga. 500. Transaction is forbidden when indirectly entered into. Robbins v. Butler, 24 Ill. 387; St. James’ Church v. Church of the Redeemer, 45 Barb. 356; Denham v. Millions, 70 Ala. 596; Carter v. Burr, 46 N. J. Eq. 134. It is not, however, a fraud per se for a trustee to lend a part

<br>

### --- Page 0349 ---

§ 516.] DUTIES AND OBLIGATIONS OF TRUSTEES.

the property which he holds in trust with his private funds that they cannot be accurately separated, he is liable for the entire sum of the united funds. Moreover, the burden is upon the trustee to show, beyond a reasonable doubt, just what part belongs to him and what belongs to the trust estate.

If the property is of such a character that it can be readily distinguished, the trustee will be entitled to the of the trust fund to his son, if the loan is well secured. Caldwell v. Boyd, 109 Ind. 447. Rev. St. ch. 3, §§ 51, 52, empower cities and towns to receive money in trust, for benevolent, religious or educational purposes, and provide that “interest shall be allowed if the fund shall be used by the city or town; otherwise it shall be placed at interest or income, the city or town being hereby made responsible for its security.” Under a will containing no directions as to how the principal should be invested, a city held in trust $100,000 whose income had been appropriated for the support of a public library in accordance with the terms of the gift. Held, that the principal could be used by the city to build a city hall, interest to be paid by the city at a proper rate for the purposes prescribed by the will. Ayer v. City of Bangor, 85 Me. 611; S. C., 27 Atl. Rep. 523. See also prohibiting use of trust funds for private purposes by trustees: Willett v. Blanford, 1 Hare, 253; Vyse v. Foster, L. R. 8 Ch. App. 309; Vaughan v. Noble, 30 Beav. 34, 39; Scott v. Tyler, 2 Dick. 712; Hill v. Simpson, 7 Ves. 152; Jones v. Stohwasser, L. R. 16 Ch. D. 577. Compare Taylor v. Hawkins, 8 Ves. 209; Nugent v. Gifford, 1 Atk. 463; Mead v. Orrery, 3 Atk. 235. ‘As long ago as 1681, it was said in argument, and approved by the then lord chancellor of England, in Dashwood v. Elwall, 2 Ch. Cas. 56, that “if an executor hath orphan’s or other man’s money in his hands, and hath power to lend it, if he do so, and take security in his own name, which faileth, he shall answer the debt in his own money, unless that he indorse the bond, or do some other thing at the time of lending the money or taking the security, which may doubtless declare the truth,” and this because “heed was to be taken that we make not such examples under which dishonest men may shelter themselves.” If this were not the rule, it was also said: “It will be in the power of one who deals for several persons and for himself also, taking security by bond in his own name, if any of the debts fail, to gratify whom he pleaseth with good securities, yea, himself, and play the securities, good or bad, into his own hands, or what he pleaseth.” Thus were set forth in the language of the time a rule, and the reason of it, by which courts of equity have universally required trustees to account; and it can never be departed from, without danger that wrong will be done.” Mitchell v. Moore, 95 U.S. 590. See also Massy v. Banner, 4 Madd. 413; Wren v. Kirton, 11 Ves. 377; McAllister v. The Commonwealth, 30 Pa. St. 536; Stanley’s Appeal, 8 Pa. St. 431.

<br>

### --- Page 0350 ---

1174 DUTIES AND OBLIGATIONS OF TRUSTEES. [§ 516.

proportion that belongs to him. If the goods are of the same kind and value, but not capable of being identified, as in the case of a mixture of grain, of coffee, of tea, or of wine, a division may be made, and each party may legally claim his rightful proportion. But if the property is of such a character as to be incapable of separation and division, the trustee, as the party responsible for the wrongful mixture of the funds, must bear the entire loss. In a case where a solicitor deposited some of his own deeds as a security for a sum of money due to two clients in a box belonging to them, he retained the box, but after his death it was found that he had abstracted the deeds from it, and they could not be distinguished from the solicitor’s other deeds, it was held that the clients had a lien on all the solicitor’s deeds for their debt. In a recent case the doctrine appears to have been modified in England, to the extent of simply making the amount of the trust fund mixed with the private funds of the trustee a charge on the property wherever the sum can be definitely ascertained. In Knatchbull v. Hallett, Sir George Jessel, M. R., in his opinion, stated the rule as follows: “Where a trustee has mixed the money with his own there is this distinction, that the cestui que trust, or beneficial owner, can no longer elect to—

1 White v. Lupton, 15 Ves. 482.  
An agent or bailiff, confounding his principal’s property with his own, charged with the whole, except what he can prove to be his own, and in this instance, the case of a breach of the terms, upon which the court dissolved an injunction, the inquiry was directed with costs. Ibid. “In order to follow trust money it must be shown that the trust fund is in existence, and forms part of the fund claimed. Where it was shown that the actual bank notes of which the trust fund was composed had not been paid by the trustee to his account at his bankers (the fund claimed), and that that account was proved not to have been considered by the bankers a trust account, it was held that the cestui que trust had no right to that fund.” Ex parte Hardcastle, 29 W. R. 615. “It is a well established doctrine in this court, that if a trustee or agent mixes and confuses the property which he holds in a fiduciary character with his own property, so that they cannot be separated with perfect accuracy, he is held liable for the whole.” Cook v. Addison, L. R. 7 Eq. 466, 470.

2 Mason v. Morley, 34 Beav. 471, 475. See also Hart v. Ten Eyck, 2 Johns. Ch. 108; Chedworth v. Edwards, 8 Ves. 46; Ex parte Hardcastle, 29 W. R. 615.

<br>

### --- Page 0351 ---

§ 517.] DUTIES AND OBLIGATIONS OF TRUSTEES. 1175

take the property, because it is no longer bought with the trust money simply and purely, but with a mixed fund. He is, however, still entitled to a charge on the property purchased for the amount of the trust money laid out in the purchase, and that charge is quite independent of the fact of the amount laid out by the trustee. The moment you get a substantial portion of it furnished by the trustee, using the word ‘trustee’ in the sense I have mentioned, as including all persons in a fiduciary relation, the right to the charge follows. That is the modern doctrine of equity. This rule has been accepted by the courts of the United States. In New York it has been held that if a person having charge of the property of another so confounds it with his own that it cannot be distinguished, he must bear all the inconvenience of the confusion, and he must distinguish his own property or lose it, and if damages are given against him, it will be to the utmost value of the article.

§ 517. Duty to Dispose of Property Liable to Waste.—Where a trust estate is held for the benefit of several persons in succession, as for a tenant for life with remainders over, and the trust property is of such a character that it is going to waste, or is liable to decay and loss, the trustee must convert it into property of a permanent and productive character.

1 Knatchbull v. Hallett, L. R. 13 Ch. D. 696, 709. See also Fellowes v. Mitchell, 1 P. Wms. 83; Gray v. Haig, 20 Beav. 219; Leeds v. Amherst, 20 Beav. 239.

2 Hart v. Ten Eyck, 2 Johns. Ch. 62, 108. See also Grave's Appeal, 50 Pa. St. 189; Cogbill v. Boyd, 77 Va. 450; Morrison v. Kinstra, 55 Miss. 71.

3 Howe v. Lord Dartmouth, 2 Lead Cas. in Eq. 262; s.c., 7 Ves. 137. Before incurring expenses impairing the principal of the trust fund the trustee should obtain the approval of the court. But in a case where the court can clearly see that the expenses incurred were necessary, they will be allowed just as if leave had been obtained before incurring them. See Hatton v. Weems, 12 Gill & J. 88; Hester v. Wilkinson, 6 Humph. 215; s.c., 44 Am. Dec. 303; Haigood v. Wells, 1 Hill Eq. 59. A trustee, though restricted in the expenditure of the income of the trust money, may, in an emergency, be justified in expending more than the profits of the current year, as where there is a drought and consequent failure of crops or unusual

<br>

### --- Page 0352 ---

1176

by the limitations of the instrument.

DUTIES AND OBLIGATIONS OF TRUSTEES.

[§ 517.

If the conversion of the property is forbidden by the instrument the trustee will be governed by its directions, and if the instrument confers upon the trustee a discretionary power in regard to it, he

sickness, making it necessary to incur heavy medical bills, yet in such case he must aver and prove evidence of the emergency and render a full account. Down v. Bullock, 7 Ired. Eq. 102. See also Woodman v. Good, 6 W. & P. 169; Arthur v. Master in Equity, 1 Harp. Eq. 47. A trustee is justified in using the interest or even a portion of the principal of the fund placed in his hands for accumulation, in trust for a minor until he arrives at full age, for the maintenance and education of the child, where there is no other property adequate for these purposes, and the minor is an orphan of tender years and there is no devise over and no third person interested in the fund. In re Potts, 1 Ashm. 340. Where land limited over and charged with the support of A for life includes old and dilapidated buildings, and has never produced enough to furnish a plain support for A, and to pay the taxes and assessments thereon, and the life tenants are, through poverty, unable to pay such taxes and assessments, the fee in the land may be sold by order of court under the statute. Herring’s Case, 35 N. J. Eq. 359. The trustee has such powers and duties as the author of the trust may think proper to confer, and his general duty is to protect the trust fund or estate from waste or misappropriation; if the trustee be in possession of the trust fund, for the purpose of performing specific duties in regard to it, he is held to a rigid and strict account;

but where he is merely invested with the legal title, and the beneficiaries are entitled to the possession of the fund, in whatever form it may be, the better to enable them to have the use and benefit of it, especially where the corpus of the fund may be trenched upon, the trustee should be bound only to good faith and reasonable diligence, and be liable only for gross negligence. Carter v. Rolland, 11 Humph. 331. See also Connell v. Ferguson, 5 Coldw. 406; Bradshaw v. Cruise, 4 Heisk. 263. ‘All that Howe v. Lord Dartmouth decided, and that was not the first decision to the same effect, is that, where the residue or bulk of the property is left en masse, and it is given to several persons in succession as tenants for life and remainder-men, it is the duty of the court to carry into effect the apparent intention of the testator’s. How is the apparent intention to be ascertained, if the testator has given no particular direction? If, although he has given no direction at all, yet he has carved out parts of the property to be enjoyed in strict settlement by certain persons, it is evident that the property must be put in such a state as will allow of its being so enjoyed. That cannot be, unless it is taken out of a temporary fund and put into a permanent fund. But it is merely an inference from the mode in which the property is to be enjoyed, if no direction is given as to how the property is to be managed. It is equally clear that, if a person gives certain prop-

<br>

### --- Page 0353 ---

§ 517.] DUTIES AND OBLIGATIONS OF TRUSTEES. 1177  
may, if he acts in good faith, postpone the conversion. If the property in question is specifically settled by the will, or deed, that will determine the duty of the trustee. Primarily, the trustee must be governed in this duty, as in others, by the directions of the instrument, whether express or implied. So far as that can be ascertained from the instrument, he must execute the trust in accordance with the intention of the donor, and, secondly, he must maintain the strictest impartiality between the different parties in interest. So far as the matter is left to the discretion of the trustee, he must not favor the tenant for life at the expense of the remainder-man, nor the remainder-man at the expense of the tenant for life! In the main, the decisions turn upon the intention of the grantor, but, as has been said, “they nevertheless, upon the whole, show an inclination on the part of successive judges to allow small indications of intention to prevent the application of the general rule.” In the leading case of Hinves v. Hinves, Vice Chancellor Wigram said: ‘The court, in applying the rule, has leant against conversion as strongly as is consistent with the supposition that the rule itself is well founded.’ In a case where a testator gave several leasehold houses, held upon short terms, to trustees to pay the income to his wife for life, and then to his grandchildren, and he gave his trustees, one of whom was his wife, power to retain any portions of his property in the same state in which it existed at the death of the testator, the will expressed an intention that the property as it existed at the death of the testator shall be enjoyed in specie, although the property be not, in a technical sense, specifically bequeathed, to such a case the rule does not apply. The rule is settled with sufficient clearness; the difficulty arises only in its application to particular cases where the intention of the testator is expressed with more or less distinctness.’ Hinves v. Hinves, 3 Hare, 609, 611.

If the property is specifically given to one person for life, with remainder over afterwards, then, although there is a danger that one object of his bounty will be defeated by the tenancy for life lasting as long as the property endures, yet there is a manifestation of intention which the court cannot overlook. Pickering v. Pickering, 4 Myl. & Cr. 298.

14 McDonald v. Irvine, L. R. 8 Ch. D. 101.  
2 McDonald v. Irvine, L. R. 8 Ch. D. 101, 112.  
8 Hinves v. Hinves, 3 Hare, 609.

<br>

1178 [§ 518.

DUTIES AND OBLIGATIONS OF TRUSTEES,

which it should be at his decease, or to sell and convert the same as they should in their absolute discretion think fit, it was held that the special power to retain existing investments took the case out of the general rule as to conversion of perishable property, and that the trustees were at liberty to retain the short leaseholds and any other investments held by the testator for such period as they should think fit.);

§ 518. Concerning the Purchase of Trust Property from Beneficiaries.—It is an inflexible rule of equity that a trustee must not deal with trust property for his own benefit. As an individual he must not purchase, or lease, or accept a mortgage of trust property, either from himself as a trustee, or from his co-trustees. He must not do this, even where the transaction is absolutely fair and just to the beneficiaries, unless it is done under an express power in the instrument, or by permission of the court.?

1 Gray v. Siggers, L. R. 15 Ch. D. 74. See also Gisborn v. Gisborn, 2 App. Cas. 300; Tabor v. Brooks, L. R. 10 Ch. D. 273.

2 Cook v. Sherman, 20 Fed. Rep. 167; Buford v. Guthrie, 14 Bush, 677; Kennedy v. Kennedy, 2 Ala. 571; Bryan v. Duncan, 11 Ga. 67; Brackenridge v. Holland, 2 Blackf. 377; s.c., 20 Am. Dec. 123; Buell v. Buckingham, 16 Iowa, 284; s. c., 85 Am. Dec. 516; Smith v. Townshend, 27 Md. 368; s.c., 92 Am. Dec. 637; Farnam v. Brooks, 9 Pick. 212; Brown v. Cowell, 116 Mass. 461; Schwarz v. Wendell, Walk. (Mich.) 267; Staats v. Bergen, 17 N.J. Eq. 554; Graves v. Waterman, 63 N. Y. 657; Bruch v. Lantz, 2 Rawle, 392; s.c., 21 Am. Dec. 458; McCants v. Bee, 1 McCord Eq. 383; s. c., 16 Am. Dec. 610; Hickman v. Stewart, 69 Tex. 255; Coffee v. Ruffin, 4 Coldw. 487; Puzey v. Seiner, 9 Wis. 370; Coles v. Trecothick, 9 Ves. 247; Morse v. Royal, 12 Ves. 355; Webb v. Shaftesbury, 7 Ves. 488; Gibson v. Jeyes, 6 Ves. 277; Tennant v. Trenchard, L. R. 4 Ch. App. 547; Randall v. Errington, 10 Ves. 427; Ex parte James, 8 Ves. 337. A trustee may not receive personal gain by buying, at a discount, a claim against the cestui que trust, and then nominally assigning it to another, that the full amount of the claim may be realized by garnishment of the trust fund. Petrie v. Badenoch, 102 Mich. 45; s. c., 60 N. W. Rep. 449. Defendants, as plaintiff’s agents and trustees, and assuming to act as such, bought in plaintiff’s land at sheriff's sale, taking title to themselves to secure payment of their bid, but later refused to let plaintiff redeem, and sold to another. Held, that plaintiff could recover the full value of the land, less the sum paid by defendants with interest to the time when he offered to redeem, and

<br>

### --- Page 0355 ---

518.] DUTIES AND OBLIGATIONS OF TRUSTEES.

not merely the difference between the price paid and that received by the defendants. Loomis v. Satterthwaite, (1894) (Tex. Civ. App.), 25 S. W. Rep. 68. Infant beneficiaries, electing to avoid a purchase of the trust subject by the trustee at a sale made by the sheriff under a decree of court, may do so by original bill in equity. English v. Moneypenny, 26 Wkly. Law Bul. 250. An agreement by a guardian that his note to the bank might be paid out of ward’s money deposited by him in such bank, and an allowance by the probate court to the guardian, made after such allowance, will not justify an application of the money, for that purpose, especially where the guardian is indebted to the ward. Johnson v. Payne & Williams Bank, 56 Mo. App. 25. A trustee under a mortgage for the benefit of infants securing payments at times stated, cannot purchase the land and release the mortgage to the detriment of the cestuis que trust, and where he executes a discharge before the trusts expressed could have been performed, it is of no effect as against the infants, and such mortgage being recorded is notice to subsequent mortgagees of the terms of the trust set out in it. Kirsch v. Tozier, 63 Hun, 607; s. c., 18 N. Y. Supl. 334. The rule which disables a trustee from purchasing for his own benefit at a sale made by him in the discharge of a fiduciary duty is not applicable to the guardian of minor heirs who purchases land belonging to the ancestor’s estate at a sale conducted by the administrator under the order of the Probate Court, since the guardian has no power or control over such sale. Barber v. Bowen, 47 Minn. 118; s. c., 49 N. W. Rep. 684. See Hoyt v. Latham, 143 U.S. 553; s. c., 125 O. Rep. 568. “The burden is on the trustee to establish that there was such a bona fide contract as will support the purchase in a court of equity on a careful and zealous examination of all the circumstances, and a rigid inquiry into the perfect fairness and propriety of the transaction.” Graves v. Waterman, 63 N. Y. 657. Dealings between a trustee and a beneficiary in relation to the trust are not prohibited, but the court watches them with great jealousy, and the trustee is required to show affirmatively that the dealings were fair and for a reasonable consideration, so as to remove all suspicion that any advantage was taken of the confidence which the fiduciary relation inspires. Allen v. Bryant, 1 Ired. Eq. (N. Car.) 195; Marshall v. Stephens, 7 Humph. (Tenn.) 159. A cestui que trust, more than 20 years after the death of her trustee, and the sale of the trust estate as a part of the trustee’s absolute property, secured the appointment of another trustee who then brought action to recover the trust estate. Held that, in such action, the court could properly instruct the jury that if the purchaser knew when he bought the land that it belonged to the cestui que trust, or was subject to a trust in her favor, then he would become trustee, would hold the land for her, and could not hold it adversely to her, and that neither prescription nor the statute of limitations was available for a defense. Sullivan v. Latimer, 38 S. Car. 158; s. c., 148 E. Rep. 933. An administrator cannot purchase for his own benefit a distributee’s share of the estate. In re Randall’s Estate, 29 N. Y. Supl. 1019. A contract made between the executor

<br>

### --- Page 0356 ---

1180 [§ 518.

DUTIES AND OBLIGATIONS OF TRUSTEES,

A trustee is not absolutely prohibited from purchasing trust property direct from a beneficiary. But purchases of this character are not regarded with favor in equity, and if the transaction is impeached it will not be upheld unless the trustee is able to prove by positive and unequivocal testimony that he and the beneficiary were at arm’s length in the transaction, that no confidence was reposed in him by the beneficiary, that the bargain was profitable to the beneficiary, and that he was fully informed in regard to the value of the property and of the nature of his interest in it. And a trustee cannot acquire the right to purchase of the beneficiary by resigning his trusteeship for that purpose. In a recent case in Florida it was held that "the law regards with a jealous eye all such transactions between persons occupying these confidential relations. As a general rule, a court of equity will avoid the contract altogether, without proof of fraud, and will never sustain it, except where the party holding the position of trust and confidence clearly proves the entire fairness of the transaction, and that it was advantageous to the cestui que trust. The transaction will not be sustained if the trustee has

McCagg, 32 Ill. 429; Miles v. Wheeler, 43 Ill. 123; Munn v. Burges, 70 Ill. 604; Ward v. Armstrong, 84 Ill. 151; Fast v. Mc- and the life beneficiary to whom the income of a certain fund was to be paid by the terms of the will, after eighteen years had passed without any accounting by the executor, whereby he agreed to support the beneficiary during her life in lieu of paying her income of the fund, will not bind the beneficiary, though she was of sound mind and fully understood the nature and effect of the contract, unless evidence is adduced, which affirmatively shows that she had knowledge of all facts bearing on the contract, and that she acted therein advisedly and without undue influence. In re Hodges, 63 Vt. 661. See also Whiteside v. Taylor, 105 Ill. 496; Johnson v. Johnson, 5 Ala. 90; Wickliff v. Robinson, 18 Ill. 145; Dennis v. Pherson, 98 Ill. 496; Allen v. Jackson, 122 Ill. 607; Colton v. Fields, 28 Ill. App. 354; Reed v. Peterson, 91 Ill. 288; Rio Grande R. Co. v. Armendaiz, 5 Tex. Civ. App. 449, 23 S. W. Rep. 568; Lyon v. Taylor, 49 Ill. App. 639; Redhead v. Parkway Driving Club, 7 Misc. Rep. 275; De Chambrun v. Cox, 60 Fed. Rep. 471; s. c., 9 C. C. A. 86.

Gibson v. Jeyes, 6 Ves. 277; Morse v. Royal, 12 Ves. 374; Ex parte Lacey, 6 Ves. 625.

Randall v. Errington, 10 Ves. 427; Coles v. Trecothick, 9 Ves. 247; Ex parte James, 8 Ves. 337; Spring v. Pride, 4 De G., J. & S. 395.

§ 519.] DUTIES AND OBLIGATIONS OF TRUSTEES. 1181  
taken advantage of any information received by him in such capacity, nor if the cestui que trust entered into the transaction in ignorance of her legal rights.’ The purchase by a trustee from the cestui que trust, of the property which is subject to the trust is not interdicted by the statute of Oregon, and will be upheld by the court where no advantage was taken of the fiduciary relation, no fraud was practiced, and the consideration was adequate.”

§ 519. Concerning the Giving of Information.—It was formerly maintained that trustees are bound to give certain information when approached by a stranger in regard to the trust estate. In Brown v. Savage, Vice Chancellor Kindersley said that trustees “[m]ust, for their own security, give correct information, when inquiry is made of them, whether they have had notice of any prior assignments affecting their trust property.” But in the recent case of Low v. Bouverie it was held that if the trustee takes upon himself to answer the inquiries of a stranger about to deal with the cestui que trust, he is not under any legal obligation to

---

1 Saunders v. Richards (1895), 35 Fla. 28; s. c., 16 So. Rep. 679, 685; Mills v. Mills, 63 Fed. Rep. 511. See also Spencer’s Appeal, 80 Pa. St. 317; Tatum v. McLellan, 50 Miss. 1; Puzey v. Senior, 9 Wis. 341; Brown v. Crowell, 116 Mass. 461. The fact that the attorney of a cestui que trust was present and advised his client at the latter's sale of trust property to the trustee may be considered in rebuttal of the presumption of the trustee's fraud, but does not relieve the trustee of the burden to prove his good faith. Cole v. Stokes, 113 N. Car. 270; s. c., 18 S. E. Rep. 321.  

2 Mills v. Mills, 63 Fed. Rep. 511.  

3 Lewin on Trusts, 704. See also Burrowes v. Lock, 10 Ves. 470; s.c. (1891), 3 Ch. 94 n.3; Slim v. Croucher, 1 De G., F. & J. 518. “The principle of this rule has been well stated by the Lord Justice Turner to be not for the security of the person taking the assignment against the danger of the trustees parting with the property in ignorance of the assignment, but to protect persons proposing to advance their money upon the assignments of property of this description against prior assignments not disclosed to them. Such persons have no means of ascertaining whether any prior assignments, or charges have been created but by applying for information to the trustees, who must, for their own security, give correct information, when inquiry is made of them, whether they have had notice of any prior assignments affecting their trust property.” Brown v. Savage, 4 Drew. 635, 639; s. c., 5 Jur. (N. S.) 1020.

<br>

### --- Page 0358 ---

1182 DUTIES AND OBLIGATIONS OF TRUSTEES. [§ 520.

do more than to give honest answer to the best of his actual knowledge and belief; he is not bound to make inquiries himself. Provided he answers honestly, he incurs no liability to the inquirer, unless he binds himself by a statement amounting to a warranty, or so expresses himself as to be estopped from afterwards denying the truth of what he has said. A statement to operate as an estoppel must be clear and unambiguous. In his opinion in this case Lord Justice Lindley said: “There is no trust or other relation between a trustee and a stranger about to deal with a cestui que trust, and, although probably such a person in making inquiries may be regarded as authorized by the cestui que trust to make them, this view of the stranger’s position will not give him a right to information which the cestui que trust himself is not entitled to demand. The trustee, therefore, is, in my opinion, under no obligation to answer such an inquiry. He can refer the person making it to the cestui que trust himself.”

§ 520. Purchasing at His Own Sale.—The rule of equity by which this subject is governed is, that a trustee must not deal with trust property for his own profit or benefit. As the representative of the cestui que trust, he is bound in all his dealings with the trust property to act

trust fund has been dealt with and where it is. But it is no part of the

1 Low v. Bouverie (1891), 3 Ch. 82. Mr. Lewin in his well known work refers to that case (Brown v. Savage, supra) as an authority for the proposition that trustees are bound to answer such inquiries. But when this opinion is examined it can scarcely be supported, and if such a doctrine were logically carried out would impose very serious duties upon trustees. The duty of a trustee is properly to preserve the trust fund, to pay the income and the corpus to those who are entitled to them respectively, and to give all his cestuis que trust, on demand, information with respect to the mode in which the

duty of a trustee to tell his cestui que trust what incumbrances the latter has created, nor which of his incumbrancers have given notice of their respective charges. It is no part of the duty of a trustee to assist his cestui que trust in selling or mortgaging his beneficial interest and in squandering or anticipating his fortune; and it is clear that a person who proposes to buy or lend money on it has no greater rights than the cestui que trust himself.” Ibid. 99.

2 Low v. Bouverie (1891), 3 Ch. 82, 100.

<br>

### --- Page 0359 ---

§ 520.] DUTIES AND OBLIGATIONS OF TRUSTEES. 1183

with sole reference to his interests. And there is a strong presumption that where he buys at his own sale, as an individual, he is selling as trustee for his own profit. But while the rule of equity forbids him to purchase, and the act on its face will be regarded by the court with disfavor, it is voidable only, not essentially void. But in all cases the burden is on the trustee to prove, not only that the transaction is fair and just, but also that it is for the benefit of the cestui que trust. But since the transaction is voidable only, and not absolutely void, it may be confirmed by the parties by the failure of the cestui que trust to elect that it shall be treated as void, or by his express acceptance of the sale. Moreover, by long acquiescence on the part of the cestui que trust, the act may be treated as valid. In a recent case before the Supreme Court of the United States, Chief Justice Fuller, in his opinion, said: “Undoubtedly the doctrine is established that a trustee cannot purchase or deal in the trust property for his own benefit or on his own behalf, directly or indirectly. But such a purchase is not absolutely void. It is only voidable, and as it may be confirmed by the parties interested directly, so it may be by long acquiescence or the absence of an election to avoid the conveyance within a reasonable time after the facts come to the knowledge of the cestui que trust.”

1 Hammond v. Hopkins, 143 U.S. 224, 251. See also McGuinn v. Schaeffer, 7 Watts, 412; Chorpening’s Appeal, 32 Pa. St. 315; s. c., 72 Am. Dec. 789; Hallman’s Estate, 13 Phila. 562; Hammond v. Stanton, 4 R. I. 65; Wilson v. Central Bridge Co., 9 R. I. 590; McCants v. Bee, 1 McCord Eq. 383; s. c., 16 Am. Dec. 610; Matthews v. Dragaud, 3 Desaus. Eq. 25; Tisdale v. DUTIES AND OBLIGATIONS OF TRUSTEES.

[§ 520.

In the case of Davoue v. Fanning, Chancellor Kent expounded the doctrine in the following lucid and well considered sentences, viz.:

“However innocent the purchase may be in the given case, it is pernicious in its consequences.”

Cas. 607; s.c., 18 Eng. L. & Eq. 34; Fox v. Mackreth, 2 Bro. Ch. 400; Gibson v. Jeyes, 6 Ves. 226. “That a trustee with power to sell cannot sell to himself so as to divest the title of the cestui que trust, is an acknowledged principle in equity, and need

<br>

### --- Page 0361 ---

§ 520.] DUTIES AND OBLIGATIONS OF TRUSTEES.

cestui que trust is not bound to prove, nor is the court bound to judge, that the trustee has made a bargain advantageous to himself. The fact may be so, and yet the party not have it in his power distinctly and clearly to show it.

It is not to be discussed. If he does purchase for himself he becomes a constructive trustee, made such by his own fraud, and equity will treat him and all purchasers from him with notice as holding the property in trust for the original beneficiary. If there are any circumstances connected with the sale which validate it, these are matters of proof on the part of the trustee or purchaser. If not proved, and nothing appears but the fact of the sale to himself by the trustee, and of purchase by a third party from him, with notice, the sale must be held void; and if the purchaser asserts the title against the cestui que trust, the latter may recover the property from him. De Everett v. Henry, 67 Tex. 402, 404.

A trustee, as such, became an accommodation indorser on his beneficiary's note, suffered a judgment to be rendered therefor against the beneficiary, and at the sale on the execution purchased the equitable interest at an inadequate price. It was held that this purchase was voidable, and that the trustee could be compelled to reconvey to the beneficiary and answer for all rents and profits. Rickett’s Appeal (Pa.) (1888), 12 Atl. Rep. 60.

In absence of any proof of collusion, the fact that property was sold by a trustee for less than half its actual value, and was shortly afterward resold to him by the purchaser for the same amount, is not sufficient to charge him with speculation in violation of his duty. Boehlert v. McBride, 48 Mo. 505.

A sale by a trustee to himself, for a grossly inadequate consideration, made through the medium of a third person, should be set aside. McNeil v. Gates, 41 Ark. 264.

A sale of a trust estate and an immediate re-sale, as within two days, to the trustee, is conclusively presumed to be simply a means of getting the property into the hands of the trustee, and is as fraudulent as a direct sale by the trustee himself. Abbot v. American Hard Rubber Co., 33 Barb. (N. Y.) 578.

Plaintiffs averred that the defendant trustee purchased at his own sale through a relative. The answer denied collusion between the defendant and the purchaser, but admitted that the immediate purchaser soon after conveyed to the defendant. It did not aver that the first purchaser paid for the property or received consideration from the defendant for the conveyance to him. It was held that this answer was an admission that the defendant was in fact a purchaser at his own sale. Higgins v. Curtiss, 82 Ill. 28.

A sale of the trust estate by the trustee to one who acts merely as the agent of the wife of the trustee will be set aside, as a matter of course, on the application of a cestui que trust, although the sale may have been a fair one and for the best price obtainable. Bassett v. Shoemaker, 46 N. J. Eq. 538; s.c., 20 Atl. Rep. 52.

An executor who purchases the share of a distributee in the estate of his testator, either for himself or as agent for another, must show...

<br>

### --- Page 0362 ---

1186 DUTIES AND OBLIGATIONS OF TRUSTEES.

[§ 520.

There may be fraud, as Lord Hardwicke observed, and the party not be able to prove it. It is to guard against this uncertainty and hazard of abuse, and to remove the trustee from temptation, that the rule does, and will, permit the cestui que trust to come, at his own option, and without showing actual injury, and insist upon having the experiment of another sale. This is a remedy which goes deep and touches the very root of the evil. It is also well established that what a trustee cannot do in propriam personam he cannot do through the agency of another, and what he cannot do in his own interests he cannot do for the benefit of a third party. In Jones v. Jones, the court said: “Under the principle, therefore, that the reason of the law is its life, and that the same causes must produce the same effects, it follows that the rule must be identical, whether the trustee buys the estate or deals with his beneficiary, for his own advantage or the advantage of another.”

That the purchase was fairly made, and for full value, in order to support it. Biggs, J., dissenting. State v. Jones, 53 Mo. App. 207. An investment agent, who has assumed the position of trustee to his client, cannot, with money raised on pledge of trust profits, buy in at foreclosure trust property for his own benefit, without notice to his client, however fair the sale may be. Darling v. Potts, 118 Mo. 506; Ss. C., 24 S. W. Rep. 461. Where a guardian who purchased land on which he held a subsequent mortgage for his wards, with his own money, at sale under judgment, received more from the rents than he paid for the land, it is unnecessary, in a suit by the wards to have the purchase declared a trust in their favor, to allege tender of the amount paid by him. Taylor v. Calvert (1894), 138 Ind. 67; s. c., 37 N. E. Rep. 531. In a contest as to the value of certain property which one party had held in trust for the other, and for which he was bound to account, what the property brought at a tax sale at which the trustee bought is not admissible in his favor, either to show independent title to himself, or the value of the property at the time he received it. Pettyjohn v. Liebscher (1893), 92 Ga. 149; s. c., 78 S. E. Rep. 1007. That the purchaser at partition sale, who was one of the heirs to the property sold, was also a trustee of one of the heirs, will not invalidate the sale. Hopper v. Hopper (1894), 79 Md. 400; s. c., 29 Atl. Rep. 611.

1 Davoue v. Fanning, 2 Johns. Ch. 251.

2 Jones v. Jones, 53 Mo. App. 207, 219. Where the purchaser of real estate at a foreclosure sale was, at the time, the confidential adviser of the foreclosure defendant upon matters in connection therewith, he was held to be disqualified, on account of that trust relationship, from becoming a purchaser

<br>

### --- Page 0363 ---

§ 521.] DUTIES AND OBLIGATIONS OF TRUSTEES. 11387

§ 521. Duty to Deposit Money in a Bank.—Where money belonging to the trust estate comes into the hands of a trustee, it is his duty to deposit it in a bank of good standing, to remain until it is used or invested. And where money is so deposited, the trustee will not be held liable for the failure of the bank unless he leaves it on deposit for a longer period than is necessary and proper. The rule is, that he must do with trust money what, as a prudent business man, he would do with his own funds. But it is the usage of judicious business men to commit their money to the care of a bank of good standing for safekeeping. But the presumption is, that the deposit of a particular sum, when there is no daily deposit, is for a limited period; that it is a temporary arrangement. For this reason, where a trustee leaves money on deposit in a bank for an unnecessarily long period, or after it should have been paid out or invested, and the bank fails, he will be held responsible for the loss. The length of time that will be held unnecessary, and that in consequence will render the trustee liable, has not been definitely fixed by the authorities, but will be determined by the circumstances of the case. In Johnson v. Newton, Vice Chancellor Sir Wm. Page Wood said: "No case has been cited in the argument, nor do I know of any case, in which executors, who have merely left moneys belonging to the estate in the hands of the bankers of the testator, for a period of no more than nine months after his decease, have been held liable to make good the fund lost by the failure of the bankers. The executors are, no doubt, bound to exercise their judgment on the safety of the place of deposit, whether it be that which the testator had in his life chosen, or whether it be selected by themselves; and when a loss unfortunately happens, the question must always be, how far the executors must be held to be answerable under the circumstances of the case." In a guardian who deposits in his own name money of his ward, in a bank for himself and required to account as trustee. Wakeman v. Dodd, 27 N. J. Eq. 564. Cooke, L. R. 4 Ch. D. 123.

Johnson v. Newton, 11 Hare, 168; Phillips v. Lamar, 27 Ga. 227; Norris v.

<br>

### --- Page 0364 ---

1188

DUTIES AND OBLIGATIONS OF TRUSTEES.

§ 521.

In a very recent case before the chancery division of the High Court of Justice, Mr. Justice Kay said:

“It is extremely difficult in these cases to know where to draw the line. Here there is an estate producing £700 a year,

which subsequently fails, must bear the loss occasioned thereby, although the bank was at the time of the deposit sound, and the guardian then made on the certificate of deposit an indorsement that the money was the property of the ward. Jenkins v. Walter, 8 Gill & J. 218; s. c., 29 Am. Dec. 539.

The purchase by a trustee, with the trust funds, in good faith, of a certificate of deposit, payable at a future day, issued by a national bank, the stock of which was selling at par, and occasionally at a small premium, and which was issuing large numbers of such certificates to individuals, savings banks, and trust companies, is not such an imprudent investment of the trust funds as will make the trustee liable for a loss arising from the failure of the bank before the day stipulated for the payment of the certificate. Hunt’s Appeal, 141 Mass. 515.

A trustee who deposits money in a bank and causes to be credited to his own private account money of the trust fund, without giving any notice that it is not his private property, or making any special agreement in regard to it, thereby converts it to his own use, so that the bank, in the absence of any notice that it is not his private property, may apply it as such. School District v. First National Bank, 102 Mass. 174.

Where one without authority sold the plaintiff’s horse to the defendant, receiving in payment a bank check, which he indorsed and gave the plaintiff in payment of a debt he owed him, and the latter received it in ignorance of the sale, collected the amount thereof, and applied the proceeds to the payment of the debt, it was held, in an action to recover the horse, that plaintiff’s receipt and collection of the check was not a ratification of the sale, and that he had a right to appropriate the check to the extinguishment of the debt in payment of which it was given him. Thacher v. Pray, 113 Mass. 291. See also Lime Rock Bank v. Plimpton, 17 Pick. 159; Gilbert v. Welch, 75 Ind. 557.

Trustees made personally liable for a loss arising from placing trust moneys with bankers on a deposit account, which was not authorized by the will, and that, notwithstanding a trustee indemnity clause against losses by a banker of moneys deposited for safe custody. Rehden v. Wesley, 29 Beav. 213.

An executrix allowed £190 (part of the estate) to remain at a banker’s, in her name as executrix, a loss occurred by their bankruptcy a year after the testator’s death. Held, that she was not personally liable for the loss. Swinfen v. Swinfen, 29 Beav. 211.

“Executors, trustees or guardians will not be liable if, in the ordinary discharge of their duty, they deposit the assets temporarily in a bank, although the bank may fail. But the trustee must be careful to make the deposits in the name of the trust estate, and not to his personal credit and not to mix the trust funds with his own; otherwise he will be liable.” 

<br>

### --- Page 0365 ---

§ 521.] DUTIES AND OBLIGATIONS OF TRUSTEES. 1189

gage of £500 is paid off, and the trustees pay that money into a bank for the purpose of getting another mortgage. The question is, whether it was within their power, as trustees, to leave that sum in the bank for fourteen months. If, after six months, they could not get a mortgage, they ought to have invested the money in consols. Without attempting to draw a hard and fast line,—for I consider that each of these cases must be judged on its merits,—I say that leaving that money in the bank for fourteen months was leaving it there too long. The moment they began to leave the money there too long they became responsible for all the consequences of their fault, and they are, therefore, liable for the sum which has been lost. Where trustees deposit trust funds in a bank, it is their duty to make the deposit as trustees and eo nomine. If they deposit trust funds in their own names, or as a personal account, they will be held liable for any loss that may be incurred in consequence of the deposit. In a case in New York relating to this subject, the court said: "These deposits were made by the administrator and the executors, not in their representative characters, but in their individual names, and in such case the rule is well settled the trustee must bear the loss resulting from the failure of the depositary. Conceding that

Banks of deposit are a recognized necessity in the commercial world, a trustee who would continuously keep for any considerable length of time a large sum of money about his person or in his house, rather than deposit it for safekeeping in a solvent and reputable bank or trust company, where all the precautions may be exercised for its safety, might be justly regarded as derelict in duty. No one would be accredited with the exercise of common prudence who would keep his money in this way; and a trustee, as we have said, is held generally for such care and diligence as an ordinarily prudent man would exercise in the conduct and management of his own business." Laws’ Estate, 144 Pa. St. 499, 506. Where a bank has no knowledge of trust character of fund, it may apply deposit on individual debt. School District v. First National Bank, 102 Mass. 174; Lime Rock Bank v. Plimpton, 17 Pick. 159; s. c., 28 Am. Dec. 286. Even when directed to deposit in certain bank his act in taking certificate of deposit in his own name will make trustee liable on failure of bank. Cory v. Cory, 119 Ind. 593. See also Naltner v. Dolan, 108 Ind. 500.

Cann v. Cann, 51 L. T. (N. S.) 770.

<br>

### --- Page 0366 ---

1190 DUTIES AND OBLIGATIONS OF TRUSTEES. [§ 522.

R was a banker, and his bank a proper place to deposit trust money in, yet a deposit made under an agreement to pay in six months, or after the lapse of any other number of months, rendered the transaction a loan upon time, and such a loan imposed the risk of loss upon the special administrator.1

§ 522.

To be Prepared to Render an Account of His Trusteeship.—It is the duty of a trustee to keep a full and accurate account of all his transactions relating to trust property, or to trust funds. His account must show just what he has received in trust and what expenditures he has made. It must be kept altogether distinct from his personal accounts, and there should be no mingling of his private with the trust funds. Where a trustee is unable, on account of illiteracy, to keep accounts in a proper manner, it is his duty to employ a competent accountant and to make the expense a charge upon the trust estate. On this point a court of equity will be exacting. In Wroe v. Seed the court said: “Now if a testator appoints a person to discharge the duties of an executor, inasmuch as he is sworn to discharge his duties as executor, his first duty plainly is, if he cannot keep accounts, to provide some one who can, because in this court the first and primary duty of every executor or trustee, having money in his hands to be received and to be paid, is that an account of his receipts and payments should be kept to be produced to those interested in the account when it is properly demanded.” Where

1 Baskin v. Baskin, 4 Lans. 90, 94.

2 Wroe v. Seed, 4 Giff. 425, 429.

Where trustees for creditors neglected on the application of a creditor to furnish an account of the trust estate, and the creditor filed his bill to which the trustees put in an answer, setting forth an account substantially correct, the court made the acting trustees pay the plaintiff's costs to the hearing, but gave both trustees their subsequent costs out of the estate.

Springett v. Dashwood, 2 Giff. 521.

Cestuis que trust have an absolute right to investigate the accounts of the trustees. Trustees cannot settle an account due to one of themselves so as to preclude such an investigation. In re Fish (1893), 2 Ch. 413, 426. Where a trustee defendant to an administration summons (1892) alleged he had expended in educating and maintaining A, the residuary legatee, during his minority which expired in 1880, the whole of

<br>

### --- Page 0367 ---

§ 522.] DUTIES AND OBLIGATIONS OF TRUSTEES. 1191

trustees have failed to render an account, or where their account is defective and insufficient, the court, on petition, will order an application for an account to stand over in order to afford an opportunity to render a proper account, with vouchers, out of court, the costs being reserved.) In a recent case the rule is stated by Mr. Justice Chitty, as follows: ‘A trustee is bound to give his cestui que trust proper information as to the investment of the trust estate, and where the trust estate is invested on mortgages it is not sufficient for the trustee merely to say, “I have invested the trust money on a mortgage,” but he must produce the mortgage deeds, so that the cestui que trust may thereby ascertain that the trustee’s statement is correct, and that the trust estate is so invested. The general rule, then, is, what I have stated, that the trustee must give information

to his cestui que trust as to

the residue, and A alleged no fraud:
Held, that A’s right to an account
was barred by § 8 of the Trustee
Act, 1888. In re Page (1893), 1 Ch.
304. A legatee has a clear right to
have a satisfactory explanation of
the state of the testator’s assets,
and an inspection of the accounts,
but he is not entitled to a copy
thereof, at the expense of the estate. Ottey v. Gilby, 8 Beav. 602.
In Kemp v. Burn, 4 Giff. 348, where
the trustees under a will refused to
furnish the solicitor of the residuary legatee with an account, though they offered to permit the plaintiff herself or a professional accountant to inspect the accounts, the court ordered them to pay the costs of a suit to administer the testator’s estate. The court observed: ‘In a
case like the present, where an account is demanded of trustees and executors of a will by a residuary legatee, there seems to me no doubt what the duty of the executors is. Their duty is to keep proper accounts and to have them always

the investment of the trust

ready when called upon to render
them.’ Where trustees had refused information and an account of the property to the plaintiffs who had an interest in the estate, and other proceedings had subsequently been taken whereby the costs of the suit were greatly increased, the trustees were directed to pay the costs of the suit up to the hearing, as if it had been an ordinary administration suit, and as to the rest of the costs each party to bear his own. Talbot v. Marshfield, L.R. 3 Ch. App. 622. In In re Bosworth, 58 L. J. Ch. 432, it is held that trustees are entitled, on being required by a person claiming to be a cestui que trust, to furnish accounts and information in respect of the trust estate, to demand that they should be guaranteed against the expense of complying with the requisition; and this rule is not affected by the circumstance that one of the trustees happens to be a solicitor.
¹ In re Hayter, 32 W. R. 26.

<br>

### --- Page 0368 ---

1192 DUTIES AND OBLIGATIONS OF TRUSTEES. [§ 522.

estate. Where a portion of the trust estate is invested in consols it is not sufficient for the trustee merely to say that it is so invested, but his cestui que trust is entitled to an authority from the trustee to enable him to make proper application to the bank, as has been done in this case, in order that he may verify the trustee’s own statement; there may be stock standing in the name of a person who admits he is a trustee of it, which, at the same time, is incumbered, some other person having a paramount title may have obtained a charging order on the stock, or placed a distringas upon it. Where the beneficiary is entitled, personally, to inspect the trust accounts and papers, he is entitled to do it through his solicitor, but if he requires a copy of an account, or of any document, he must meet the necessary expense. It would be manifestly unjust to make such an expense a charge upon the trust estate, or to compel the trustee to do it at his personal expense. And the rule is not modified by the circumstance that a trustee is a solicitor. In Martin v. Lamb, Mr. Justice Kekewich said: "It so happened in this particular case that one of the trustees was a solicitor; that is to say, the gentlemen who would have to be consulted, and whose costs would have to be paid. Does that make any difference? To my mind, none at all. It would be extremely hard to make the independent gentleman pay costs, and I think it is extremely hard to make a solicitor give up a certain amount of time, and a certain amount of the time of his clerk and office staff, to produce information for the benefit of somebody else who is not willing to pay for it. To him his time is money, and his means of earning his income, and I think he ought to be paid for the information which he has to give."

1 Lee v. Wilson (1892), L. R. 1 Fla. 593; Veazie v. Forsaith, 76 Me. 562; Hall v. Cushing, 9 Pick. 395; Ex parte Bailey, 13 R. 1453; Amory v. Lowell, 104 Mass. 265; Stone v. Littlefield, 151 Mass. 485; Booth v. Ammerman, 4 Bradf. 129; Tup

2 Martin v. Lamb, L. J. 58 Ch. 172; Weston v. Jenkins, 128 Mass. 432, 433. See also Cowin v. Grayell, 34 W. R. 735. In keeping accounts distinction between capital and income should be observed. As examples illustrating this necessity, see Merritt v. Jenkins, 17 Ch. 86, 89.

<br>

### --- Page 0369 ---

§ 523.] DUTIES AND OBLIGATIONS OF TRUSTEES.

§ 523.

1193

To Distribute Trust Property.—As it is the duty of the trustee to exercise diligence and care in his management and control of the trust estate, so also it is his duty to act with prudence and caution in his distribution of it. In this direction he is held to a rigid accountability. If he delivers the trust property or the trust funds to the wrong person, either by mistake or in consequence of some deception practiced upon him, however sincere and honest he may have been, he will be required to make good the loss to the estate.¹

¹ Per v. Fuller, 7 Rich. Eq. 170; Greene v. Smith, 17 R. I. 28; Heard v. Eldredge, 109 Mass. 258; s.c., 12 Am. Rep. 687.

In re Hulkes, 35 W. R. 194; Cutler v. Boyd, 60 L. T. 859; Hemming v. Neil, 62 L. T. 649. In case of doubt as to the proper disbursement of trust funds application should be made to the court for instructions. Talbot v. Radnor, 3 Myl. & K. 252; Sporle v. Burnaby, 10 Jur. (N. S.) 1142. "The court ought to be lenient in the construction of these questions, when it considers the difficulties imposed on trustees, and wherever a trustee comes and asks the court for a declaration of its opinion, upon a difficulty created by the document which constitutes him a trustee, it is the duty of the court to hold, that if there be a reasonable question or doubt, the trustee is entitled to come to the court to have that question determined." Merlin v. Blagrave, 25 Beav. 125, 138. The employment of an agent will not relieve. This is simply the case of a person employing his servant to do an act, and the servant deceiving him; and any loss so occasioned must fall on the employer, and not on the cestui que trust. Of the two innocent persons, therefore, one of whom must suffer

In Ashby v. Blackwell it was held that by the wrongful act of the solicitor, the loss must fall on the trustee who employed him, and did not take all the precaution he might have taken against being deceived. The fund must be replaced with interest at 4 per cent.² Bostock v. Floyer, L. R. 1 Eq. 26, 28. A trustee is liable for the loss of a trust fund caused by his solicitor having committed a fraud on the occasion of the investment of the fund on mortgage. Upon the occasion of the investment of a trust fund on mortgage, the trustee employed the same solicitor as the mortgagor. Subsequently he had reason to suspect the sufficiency of the security but took no steps to inquire into the matter. It afterwards turned out that the solicitor had practiced a fraud on the trustee, and that the security was insufficient. Held, that the trustee was liable for the loss occasioned to the trust estate. Sutton v. Wilders, L. R. 12 Eq. 373. The decision in Saltmarsh v. Barrett, 31 Beav. 349, that executors who, acting bona fide, have distributed the assets upon what turn out to be an erroneous construction of the will, are not liable to be charged with interest upon the principal sums wrongly paid, which must be refunded to the estate, dissented.

<br>

### --- Page 0370 ---

1194 [§ 523.

DUTIES AND OBLIGATIONS OF TRUSTEES.

A trustee, whether a private person or body corporate, must see to the reality of the authority empowering him to dispose of the trust money; for, if forged, it is in consideration of law and equity a nullity, and the right remains as before. Where there is any ground for doubt in regard to the person to whom a payment should be made, or to whom trust property should be delivered, it is the privilege and the duty of the trustee to ask the instruction and direction of the court, and for this reason he is without apology in making payment to the wrong party. Certain rules that were formerly accepted have been abrogated or modified by statutory enactments. In England, by § 23 of the Trustee Act of 1893, — re-enacting 22 & 23 Victoria, ch. XXXV., § 26,— it was enacted that "a trustee, acting or paying money in good faith under or in pursuance of any power of attorney, shall not be liable for any such act or payment, by reason of the fact that at the time of the payment or act the person who gave the power of attorney."

This departs from the principle established in Attorney-General v. Kohler (9 H. L. Cas. 654), and Attorney-General v. Alford (4 DeG., M. & G. 843). But although as a general rule executors are liable to be charged with interest at 4 per cent. on sums improperly paid or improperly retained by them, they are not liable for interest to the legatee (or his representatives) to whom, with full knowledge on his part and in common mistake, the payments which he must refund have thus been erroneously made. Powell v. Hulkes, L. R. 33 Ch. Div.

Ashby v. Blackwell, 2 Eden, 299, 302. A testator who died and whose will was proved in England, bequeathed a legacy to a married woman, whose domicile, as well as that of her husband, was in Scotland. The husband died a few months after the testator, without having received the legacy. After his decease the executors of the testator, with knowledge of the before mentioned circumstances of domicile, paid the legacy to the widow. It was proved according to the Scotch law the payment should have been made to the husband’s personal representatives. It was held that in absence of proof that the executors knew the Scotch law on the subject, the payment to the widow was a good payment. Leslie v. Baillie, 2 Y. & C. Ch. 91.

Talbot v. Radnor, 3 Myl. & K. 252; Sporl v. Burnaby, 10 Jur. (N.S.) 1142; Ashby v. Blackwell, 2 Eden, 302; Eaves v. Hickson, 30 Beav. 136; Merlin v. Blagrave, 25 Beav. 137; Bostock v. Floyer, L.R. 1 Ch. App. 26; Sutton v. Wilder, L. R. 12 Eq. 873. As to mistake in construction of settlement: Barratt v. Wilder, 30 Beav. 442; Davies v. Hodgson, 25 Beav. 177; Griffiths v. Porter, 25 Beav. 236.

<br>

### --- Page 0371 ---

§ 524.] DUTIES AND OBLIGATIONS OF TRUSTEES. 1195  
was dead or had done some act to avoid the power, if this fact was not known to the trustee at the time of his so acting or paying. Provided, that nothing in this section shall affect the right of any person entitled to the money against the person to whom the payment is made, and that the person so entitled shall have the same remedy against the persons to whom the payment is made as he would have had against the trustee.”

§ 524. The Subject Continued.—It is not enough for a trustee to act in good faith and with the best intention. In Powell v. Hulkes it was held that if executors, although acting bona fide, distribute assets upon an erroneous construction of the will, they are liable to be charged with interest upon the principal sums wrongly paid, which must be refunded to the estate. But where the payments have been wrongly made to a legatee who shared the mistake of the executors and acquiesced, they are not liable to pay interest to that legatee, who must himself refund what he has wrongly received. In Hallows v. Lloyd it was held that new trustees are not fixed with notice through retiring trustees of incumbrances affecting the trust estate, of which no notice appears amongst the trust documents, and the existence of which, though known to the retiring trustees, is not disclosed to them.” In his opinion in this case, Mr.

1 Powell v. Hulkes, 35 W. R. 174.

2 Hallows v. Lloyd, L. R. 39 Ch. D. 686. “I have great doubt whether I can affect the trustee with notice, upon such evidence as this. If the question arose in one of those cases, in which questions of notice usually occur, that is, of purchasers for good consideration, this evidence would not do. If the notice had been of a deed actually executed, it certainly would do; but this is not notice of a deed but only of an intention to execute a deed; and there is no case or reasoning which goes so far as to say, that a purchaser shall be affected by notice of a deed in contemplation. This brings it to a question, whether a trustee shall be more easily affected than the owner of an estate. Now, as to trustees, I take it, the court expects from a trustee, that reasonable circumspection which a man, in conscience, ought to use on the occasion before him, and, to be sure, after knowing of the draught of this deed of appointment, he might reasonably have objected to paying the money without further inquiry. If he had refused, somebody must have filed a bill against him, and then the court would have seen in some

<br>

### --- Page 0372 ---

1196 DUTIES AND OBLIGATIONS OF TRUSTEES. [§ 524.

Justice Kekewich said: ‘The exact position of these trustees is difficult to define, but they were appointed under the Conveyancing Act, 1881, which provides (section 31, subsection 5): “Every new trustee so appointed, as well before as after all the trust property becomes by law or by assurance, or otherwise, vested in him, shall have the same powers, authorities and discretion, and may in all respects act, as if he had been originally appointed a trustee by the instrument, if any, creating the trust.” I think, therefore, that whether the trust property or the legal estate was vested in these gentlemen or not, they were from the date of their appointment trustees for all the purposes of the testator’s will, and I see no reason why they should not have the same protection as if they had been the original trustees.

The act only amplifies and develops the common forms previously in use. The matter is to be considered in the same manner, whether any such deed had been executed in pursuance of that draught. I am now going on ground untrod in principle and reasoning as well as in specie, for I know of no case in which lata culpa has been imputed to a trustee on a ground quite so slender as this.” Cothay v. Sydenham, 2 Bro. Ch. 391, 393.

Hallows v. Lloyd, L. R. 39 Ch. D. 686, 692. Trustee is allowed a reasonable time in which to settle up estate and keep funds in bank for a reasonable time. Swinfen v. Swinfen, 2 Beav. 211; Wilks v. Groom, 3 Drew. 584. “No case has been cited in the argument, nor do I know of any case in which executors, who have merely left moneys belonging to the estate in the hands of bankers of the testator, for a period of no more than nine months after his decease, have been held liable to make good the fund lost by the failure of the bankers. The executors are no doubt bound to exercise their judgment on the safety of the place of deposit, whether it be that which the testator had in his lifetime chosen, or whether it was selected by themselves; and when a loss unfortunately happens, the question must always be, how far the executors must be held to be answerable under the circumstances of the case.” Johnson v. Newton, 11 Hare, 167. Where a mortgage to trustees shows that they are required to collect the sum secured, and distribute it to the parties entitled thereto; that, to accomplish such purpose, they have authority to execute and deliver a proper satisfaction piece; and that they have special authority to satisfy the mortgage for certain specified purposes — a satisfaction piece executed by them is sufficient to extinguish the lien of the mortgage, without any consent thereto by the beneficiaries of the trust. Bendheim v. Morrow, 73 Hun, 90; S. C., 25 N.Y. Suppl. 900.

<br>

### --- Page 0373 ---

CHAPTER XXIV.

THE INVESTMENT OF TRUST FUNDS.

§ 525. Introductory.  
526. The General Rule.  
527. The General Rule Continued.  
528. Permissible Securities.  
529. Investment under a Discretionary Power.  
530. The Rule in England.  
531. The Rule in the United States.  

§ 582. The Subject Continued.  
533. Statutory Regulations.  
534. Investment of Funds for Charitable Purposes.  
535. The Change of Investments.  
536. Investments for Accumulations.  

§ 525. Introductory.—One of the most important and, at the same time, one of the most difficult of the duties devolving upon trustees is that of the investment of the trust funds. No other more largely calls into exercise their fidelity and discretion. In this work they are under obligation to act with supreme, if not sole, reference to the interests of the beneficiaries. They are bound to act with deliberation and in the exercise of the best judgment of which they are capable, and to avail themselves of the best counsel and assistance that they are able to procure. So far as it is apparent that they are acting in this manner, and they have made no legal or business mistake, their acts will be upheld in equity, and they will not be held liable for any loss to the trust estate. But a failure to act with prudence, and with the best judgment of which they are capable, or to exercise unquestionable fidelity, will make them personally responsible for their acts. It has been well and pertinently said by an eminent jurist that "trustees are justly and uniformly considered favorably, and it is of great importance to bereaved families and orphans that they should not be held to make good losses in the de-

<br>

### --- Page 0374 ---

1198 INVESTMENT OF TRUST FUNDS. [§ 526.

preciation of stocks, or the failure of the capital itself, which they held in trust, provided they conduct themselves honestly and discreetly and carefully, according to the existing circumstances in the discharge of their trusts. If this were held otherwise no prudent man would run the hazard of losses which might happen without any neglect or breach of good faith.'

§ 526. The General Rule.—No sharply defined or hard and fast rules for the guidance of trustees in the investment of trust funds have been laid down by American courts, but they have established such general principles for their direction as admit of universal application. In the leading case of Harvard College v. Amory, before the Supreme Judicial Court of Massachusetts, the court, in a thoroughly considered opinion by Mr. Justice Putnam, after calling attention to the fact that the English rule, which required trustees to invest in public funds only, had never been recognized in that commonwealth, laid down the following general rule, viz.: 'All that can be required of a trustee to invest is that he shall conduct himself faithfully and exercise a sound discretion. He is to observe how men of prudence, discretion and intelligence manage their own affairs, not in regard to speculation, but in regard to the permanent disposition of their funds, considering the probable income, as well as the probable safety of the capital to be invested.' In a recent case before the same court,

1 Harvard College v. Amory, 9 Pick. 446, 465.

2 Harvard College v. Amory, 9 Pick. 446, 461. See also Campbell v. Miller, 88 Ga. 304; Hun v. Cary, 82 N. Y. 65; King v. King, 37 Ga. 205; Lovett v. Thomas, 81 Va. 245; Finlay v. Merriman, 39 Tex. 56; Morrow v. Saline County, 21 Kan. 484; Lent v. Howard, 89 N.Y. 169; Barney v. Saunders, 16 How. 535; Cromie v. Bull, 81 Ky. 646; Moore v. Hamilton, 4 Fla. 112; Dufford v. Smith (N.J.), 18 Atl. Rep. 1052; Gray v. Fox, 1 N. J. Eq. 259; Harding v. Larned, 4 Allen, 426; Clarke v. Garfield, 1 Allen, 427; Ormiston v. Olcott, 84 N. Y. 339; Nance v. Nance, 18 Car. 209. The law does not hold a trustee responsible for errors in judgment when he has been careful to enlighten that judgment, but we think the law does require of a trustee, even under a will like this, more than good faith and honest judgment. We think it must be assumed that the testatrix made this part of her will

<br>

### --- Page 0375 ---

§ 526.] INVESTMENT OF TRUST FUNDS. 1199  
Chief Justice Gray, referring to this rule, said: “The rule thus judicially declared nearly half a century ago has been since constantly adhered to in this commonwealth, and has been applied to cases in which the terms of the trust contained no special provisions upon the subject. If a more strict and precise rule should be deemed expedient, it must be enacted by the legislature. It cannot be introduced by judicial decision without working great hardship and injustice.”

With reference to the well-known legal and equitable rule governing trustees, and that she intended the trustee of her appointment to be mindful of them. True, she left the investment of the trust estate to his judgment, but it was to his judgment as trustee, enlightened and guided by the approved rules applicable to the investment of trust funds, not to his uninformed, personal judgment, exercised without reference to legal rules and principles. We do not think the language of the will was intended, or has the effect, to relieve the trustee from the observance of the well-known principles applicable to the investment of trust funds. He must always bear in mind that he is dealing with trust funds, which were not given him to be used in developing or furthering business enterprises, but to be guarded carefully and invested cautiously, so that principal, as well as interest, may be forthcoming at the appointed time.

While he must be as diligent and painstaking in the management of the trust estate as the average prudent man is in managing his own estate, he may not always place the trust funds where he, or the average prudent man, would place his own funds. In measuring the duty of the trustee with the usual conduct of the man of average prudence in the care of his own estate, reference is to be had to the conduct of such a man in making permanent investments of his own savings outside of ordinary business risks, rather than to his conduct in taking business chances.

There are often occurring good business chances in which a man may invest some of his own money without danger of being called imprudent, whatever the result. But it will be generally conceded that a mere business chance or prospect, however promising, is not a proper place for trust funds. While, of course, all investments, however carefully made, are more or less liable to depreciate and become worthless, experience has shown that certain classes of investments are peculiarly liable to such depreciation and loss. These, of course, would be avoided by every prudent man who was investing his own money with a view to permanency and security, rather than chance of profit.

A trustee should therefore avoid them, even though he sincerely believes a particular investment of that class to be safe, as well as profitable. Shares in the stock of new mining companies are conspicuous examples of this class.  
Mattocks v. Moulton, 84 Me. 545.  
Brown v. French, 125 Mass.

<br>

### --- Page 0376 ---

1200 INVESTMENT OF TRUST FUNDS. {§ 527.

§ 527. The General Rule Continued.—In many of the States the courts have given decisions bearing more or less directly on this subject, and it is of frequent occurrence that the instrument gives directions in regard to the property in which the trust funds shall be invested, and indicates the time and manner of investment. Wherever the State courts have formulated rules for the guidance of trustees in the discharge of this duty, such rules are a good and sufficient authority on the subject, and where the instrument gives directions in regard to investments, of course they are to be followed. In the absence of any legal or equitable rule of which such directions are in contravention, the intention of the donor, in this direction, as in others, is to be conscientiously regarded. But in the absence of specific directions in the instrument, of equitable rules or statutory enactments, trustees must act in good faith and in the exercise of a sound discretion. Where there are no specific rules for their guidance trustees must be governed by the general principles which courts of equity have enunciated for their direction.³

See also Kinmouth v. Brigham, 5 Allen, 270; Clark v. Garfield, 8 Allen, 427; Lovell v. Minot, 20 Pick. 116; Harvard College v. Amory, 9 Pick. 461. A trustee having full discretion invested trust funds in the note of a manufacturing corporation, indorsed by its president and secretary. The corporation was in good financial standing, and the indorsers were reputed to be men of ample means. It was held that such an investment of trust funds was unauthorized. Simmons v. Oliver, 74 Wis. 633; s. C., 43 N.W. Rep. 561. The trustee of a sum amounting to $16,260.05, in May, 1881, invested $3,573.75 of it in the stock of a railroad company, which had been constructed at great expense through a new and comparatively unsettled country, and was heavily in debt, and whose continued prosperity depended upon many circumstances which could not be predicted. In the August following he invested $2,475 more in the same stock. Both investments were made by him in entire good faith, and after careful inquiry of many persons as to the value of the stock and the propriety of the investment. It was held that he did not exercise a sound discretion in making the second investment, and should be charged with the amount thereof. Dickinson’s Appeal, 152 Mass. 184.

¹ In re Weston, 91 N.Y. 513; Lovell v. Minot, 20 Pick. 119; Calhoun’s Estate, 6 Watts, 185; In re Hunt, 141 Mass. 515; Neff’s Appeal, 57 Pa. St. 96; King v. Talbot, 40 N.Y. 76; Davis v. Harman, 21
