§ 528.  
OF TRUST FUNDS.

Permissible Securities.—It is well settled that trust funds must not be invested in any personal securities, however reliable they may be supposed to be, or in any stocks or bonds of private corporations, unless they have Gratt. 200; Myers v. Zetelle, 21 Gratt. 759. In New York and Pennsylvania the courts do not look with favor on investments made on mortgages on lands outside of the State. See Ormiston v. Olcott, 84 N.Y. 339; Rush’s Estate, 12 Pa. St. 375, 378. But see Denton v. Sanford, 103 N.Y. 607. Where trustees purchased mortgages in good faith and in the exercise of reasonable caution and business judgment, they will not be held liable for a depreciation in the value of the security. Rawling’s Estate, 13 Phila. 337. See also In re Shield's Estate, 14 Phila. 307. The investment of trust property should be made with the view of permanence and not in a spirit of speculation. Emery v. Batchelder, 78 Me. 233. A trustee authorized to invest in landed securities has no authority to buy land with the funds, and the cestui que trust will not be estopped by his knowledge or acquiescence to question his power to do so. Zimmerman v. Frailey, 70 Md. 561. See also Jenkins v. Walter, 8 Gill & J. 218. A clause in an instrument creating a trust and exempting the trustee from liability except for willful and intentional breaches of trust, does not exempt from liability for losses arising from investments made without instituting proper inquiries and exercising reasonable judgment in respect to the value of the securities received; and the fact that he neither made nor intended to make any personal gain does not exonerate him. Tuttle v. Gilmore, 36 N.J. Eq. 618. "A trustee is required to give to the business of the trust his disinterested zeal, skill and attention, and is bound to do no act that will bring his individual interests in conflict with those of his cestui que trust. He must not allow his individual interests to directly or indirectly influence him adversely, and all that he does in the execution of the trust must be for the promotion of the interests of the beneficiary. Investments of trust funds must be made with a view to the good of the beneficiary, and for no other purpose. They must not be made to favor friends, nor to promote individual interests; but they must be made so that the full benefit shall go where the creator of the trust directed. Safe investments must be sought, and the general rule is that a mortgage on real estate incumbered by prior liens is not a safe investment." Justice Elliott in Shuey v. Latta, 90 Ind. 136, 1389. See also Slauter v. Favorite, 107 Ind. 291, 296; State v. Greensdale, 106 Ind. 364; Norwood v. Harness, 98 Ind. 13; Marquess v. La Baw, 82 Ind. 55; Sanders v. State, 49 Ind. 228. See generally, McCullough v. McCullough, 44 N.J. Eq. 313; Fowler v. Colt, 25 N.J. Eq. 202; Salisbury v. Colt, 27 N.J. Eq. 492; McEachin v. Stewart, 106 N. Car. 33; Peckham v. Newton, 15 R.I. 321; Williams v. Williams, 16 Lea, 164; Mills v. Swearingen, 67 Tex. 269; New England Trust Co. v. Eaton,

<br>

### --- Page 0378 ---

1202 INVESTMENT OF TRUST FUNDS. [§ 528.

acquired such a reputation for permanence and stability as to command universal confidence, and even then it is a question how far it is permissible for a trustee to invest in them. In the recent case of Dickinson’s Appeal, Mr. Justice Field, in delivering the opinion of the court, stated the rule in Massachusetts in the following language, viz.: “A prudent man, possessed of considerable wealth, in investing a small part of his property, may wisely enough take risks which a trustee would not be justified in taking. A trustee whose duty it is to keep the trust fund safely invested in productive property ought not to hazard the safety of the property under any temptation to make extraordinary profits. Our cases, however, show that trustees in this commonwealth are permitted to invest portions of trust funds in dividend-paying stock and interest-bearing bonds of private business corporations when the corporations have acquired, by reason of the amount of their property and the prudent management of their affairs, such a reputation that cautious and intelligent persons commonly invest their own money in the stocks and bonds as permanent investments.” To the rule as above stated there is one

140 Mass. 532; Lee v. Lee, 55 Ala. 598; In re Nyce’s Estate, 5 Watts & S. 256; Pim v. Downing, 11 Serg. & R. 66; Kimball v. Redding, 31 N. H. 352; King v. King, 3 Johns. Ch. 552.

Dickinson’s Appeal, 152 Mass. 184. Second mortgages are not desirable. Gilmore v. Tuttle, 32 N. J. Eq. 611; Shuey v. Latta, 90 Ind. 136; Norris v. Wright, 14 Beav. 29; Drosier v. Brereton, 15 Beav. 221. It is not sound discretion in trustees to invest in personal securities. Harding v. Larned, 4 Allen, 426; Clark v. Garfield, 8 Allen, 427; Willis’ Appeal, 22 Pa. St. 330. But see Knowlton v. Bradley, 17 N. H. 458. Investment of trust funds by a trustee of an express trust in stock in a private 

corporation, though in good financial standing, without an order of court to that effect, constitutes a violation of his duty and though he acts with his best judgment and discretion, is no defense, though he tenders the stock in court for the benefit of cestui que trust, to an action on his bond. Tucker v. State, 72 Ind. 242. Bank stock is not a proper investment. Gilbert v. Welsch, 75 Ind. 557; Ackerman v. Emmott, 4 Barb. 526; Tucker v. Tucker, 33 N. J. Eq. 235. Or, even where the bank is the Bank of the United States: In re Nyce’s Estate, 5 Watts & S. 254; Hemphill’s Appeal, 18 Pa. St. 303. But see In re Hunt, 141 Mass. 515. A loan to a private manufacturing corporation not sanctioned. Sim-

exception. Where the instrument contains express power or permission to loan on personal securities, the direction may be followed, and any loss that may be incurred will be at the expense of the estate.1

mons v. Oliver, 74 Wis. 633; s.c., 43 N. W. Rep. 561. Municipal bonds not always favored. Tucker v. Tucker, 33 N. J. Eq. 235; Trustees v. Clay, 28 Mon. 385. Patent right and business of manufacturing patented articles are improper investments of trust funds. Trull v. Trull, 13 Allen, 407. Trustees who are directed to hold, manage, and invest the trust funds, and pay the income to certain beneficiaries, cannot invest such funds in a hazardous mercantile undertaking unless clearly authorized to do so. Adams v. Nelson, 31 Wkly. L. Bull. 46. A trustee is guilty of laches who loans money belonging to the estate on an unsecured note. Nobles v. Hogg (1892), 36 S. Car. 322; s.c., 15 S. E. Rep. 359. Where trustees are authorized to change the investment of the trust fund, they may use part of the capital of the fund in building on land owned by the trust. Stevens v. Stevens, 80 Hun, 514; s.c., 30 N. Y. Supl. 625. Trustees, when considering whether trust funds should be invested on mortgages, must, notwithstanding the opinions of valuers, solicitors, or other trusted agents, exercise their own judgment as to the security. They must regard the transaction, not only from the view of securing the capital, but must also consider whether the income of the property is sufficient to provide the interest on the amount advanced. Although the position of the borrower may reasonably be considered by them, they should not advance a sum

But loans of this character will

largely in excess of the proper amount, relying on the pecuniary position of the borrower. Somerset v. Poulett, 62 L. J. Ch. 720; s.c., 68 L. T. 613. A trustee who has made a loan partly of trust money and partly of his own, and taken a note for the whole payable to himself individually, is liable for any loss of interest on the trust money, without regard to his motives in making the loan. In re Hodge’s Estate (1894), 66 Vt. 70; s.c., 28 Atl. Rep. 663. There is in Rhode Island no statute law or rule of court which points out any particular classes of securities for the investment of trust funds. The fact that securities were bought and held by the testator commends them for retention so long as there is no doubt of their safety. Peckham v. Newton, 15 R.I. 321; s.c., 4 Atl. Rep. 758. The trustee of a minor child cannot convey land of the trust estate to a land company formed for speculative purposes, and receive as the price stock of the company. Randolph v. East Birmingham Land Co. (1894), 104 Ala. 355; s.c., 16 So. Rep. 126.

not be justified by indefinite and general statements in the instrument. The directions must be express, and they must be strictly followed.

§ 529. Investment Under a Discretionary Power.—In England it has been held that where the instrument empowers the trustees to loan the trust funds “at their discretion,” they are simply authorized to choose between the different forms of security that are authorized by law. Such a power does not justify the investment of trust funds in any securities not thus authorized. In an English

104; Moore v. Hamilton, 4 Fla. 112; Sullivan v. Howard, 20 Md. 191; Spear v. Spear, 9 Rich. 184; Swoyer’s Appeal, 5 Pa. St. 377; Wilson’s Appeal, 115 Pa. St. 95.

Pocock v. Redington, 5 Ves. 799; Wilkes v. Stewart, G. Coop. 6; Mills v. Osborne, 7 Sim. 30; Wynne v. Warren, 2 Heisk. 118; Forbes v. Ross, 2 Bro. Ch. 430; s. C., 2 Cox, 113; Stickney v. Sewell, 1 Myl. & Cr. 814; Francis v. Francis, 5 De G., M. & G. 108; De Jarnette v. De Jarnette, 41 Ala. 708; Langston v. Olivant, G. Coop. 33; Cock v. Goodfellow, 10 Mod. 489; Fitzgerald v. Pringle, 2 Moll. 534.

Bethell v. Abraham, L. R. 17 Eq. 24; s. C., 29 Ch. D. 889. A testator directed that his trustees should invest the moneys coming to their hands, in respect of his estate, in their names or under their control, in such modes of investment as they in their uncontrolled discretion should think fit. Before the commencement of an action to administer the testator’s estate, the trustees (who were also executors) invested moneys forming part of it in the purchase of bonds of a foreign government, bonds of a colonial railway company, and shares of a bank on which there was a further liability. The chief clerk, in taking the accounts of the testator’s estates, disallowed the trustees the sums which they had expended in the purchase of these bonds and shares. The shares in the bank had been previously sold at a profit. It was held that, though the investments in question ought not to be retained by the trustees, yet, as they had acted bona fide, and no loss had resulted to the trust estate, they ought not be disallowed the sums which they had laid out in making the investments. Brown v. Brown, L. R. 29 Ch. D. 889. A testator directed his trustees to lay out and invest £15,000 upon government real or personal security, or in such stocks, funds or shares as they might in their absolute discretion think fit, and pay the interest to his wife for life; the capital to be for his children. The trustees, with the sanction of the widow, invested a portion of the money upon railway stock, bearing a high rate of interest. Upon her death the securities were greatly reduced in value. Held, that the trustees were bound to invest upon securities of a permanent nature; that in the absence of evidence to the contrary, it must be assumed from the rate of interest that these investments were not permanent; and that the

<br>

### --- Page 0381 ---

§ 529. INVESTMENT OF TRUST FUNDS, 1205

Case, where trustees having power to invest certain moneys belonging to a testator’s estate at their discretion, and having also power to continue or change securities from time to time, as to the majority should seem meet, applied to the court, in a suit for the administration of the trust estate, for liberty to invest the moneys in and to convert securities into American funds or railway stocks. Infants were interested in the trust estate. It was held that if the trustees had the discretion they claimed (which was doubtful), the court ought not, in a case where infants were interested, to permit them to exercise that discretion in the way they proposed.

£15,000 must now be invested for the benefit of the children of the testator. Stewart v. Sanderson, L.R. 6 Eq. 26. Where executors are by the will made trustees of a fund, and directed to place it with one of several companies for investment, they will not be liable on default of the company selected if they exercise due care in selecting it. Pinney v. Newton, 66 Conn. 141; S.C., 33 Atl. Rep. 591. A mere fact that a trustee of a fund consisting of certain bonds fails to sell them at the request of the husband of the beneficiary, although they were paying no interest, is not sufficient to charge him with liability for their depreciation in value. Johns v. Herbert, 2 App. D.C. 485. Where one of two trustees has funds of the trust estate in his possession, and, with the consent of his co-trustee, invests them in mortgages taken in his own name, such mortgages as between the trustee making the investment and the trust estate are property of the latter. Roosevelt v. Land & River Imp. Co., 3 Hun, App. Div. 563. Where the trustee fails to invest within a reasonable time, he is chargeable with interest and also liable for losses attributable to his neglect. Ryder v. Beckerton, 3 Swanst. 80; Bates v. Scales, 12 Ves. 402; Lyse v. Kingdom, 1 Call (Va.), 184; Lomax v. Pendleton, 3 Call (Va.), 538; Chase v. Lockerman, 11 G. & J. (Md.) 185; Schieffelin v. Stewart, 1 Johns. Ch. 620; Garniss v. Gardner, 1 Edw. Ch. 128; Trafford v. Boehm, 3 Atk. 440; Handly v. Snodgrass, 9 Leigh (Va.), 484; Shipp v. Hettrick, 63 N. Car. 329; Owen v. Newton, 11 Hare, 160; Phillipson v. Gatty, 7 Hare, 516; Challen v. Shippam, 4 Hare, 555; Watts v. Girdlestone, 6 Beav. 188; Mathews v. Brice, 6 Beav. 239; Moyle v. Moyle, 2 Russ. & Myl. 241; Lowry v. 1206 INVESTMENT OF TRUST FUNDS. [§ 530.

vest,” as related to securities, points to a loan, and not to any employment of the funds in speculative investments, or in trade, or other forms of business. The rule applies also to directions to place out at interest or on security.

§ 530. The Rule in England.—The equitable rules established by the English courts in regard to the powers of trustees as to the investment of trust funds have been materially modified by recent statutory enactments. The Trust Investment Act of 1889 was amended, revised and re-enacted in the Trustee Act of 1893. For sections 1 to 6, which contain that portion of the act which relates to the powers and duties of trustees as to the investment of trust funds, see note below.

¹ Bethell v. Abraham, L. R. 17 Eq. 24; Cock v. Goodfellow, 10 Madd. 489; Dickinson v. Player, C. P. Coop. (1837-8) p. 178; Harris v. Harris, 29 Beay. 107; Murphy v. Doyle, L. R. 29 Ir. 333; In re Kavanaugh, L. R. 27 Ir. 495.

² § 1. A trustee may, unless expressly forbidden by the instrument (if any) creating the trust, invest any trust funds in his hands, whether at the time in a state of investment or not, in manner following, that is to say: (a) In any of the parliamentary stocks or public funds or government securities of the United Kingdom: (b) On real or heritable securities in Great Britain or Ireland: (c) In the stock of the Bank of England or the Bank of Ireland: (d) In India three and a half per cent. stock and India three per cent. stock, or in any other capital stock which may at any time hereafter be issued by the secretary of state in council of India under the authority of act of Parliament, and charged on the revenues of India: (e) In any securities, the interest of which is for the time being guaranteed by Parliament: (f) In consolidated stock created by the Metropolitan Board of Works, or by the London County Council, or in debenture stock created by the receiver for the Metropolitan Police District: (g) In the debenture or rentcharge, or guaranteed or preference stock of any railway company in Great Britain or Ireland incorporated by special act of Parliament, and having during each of the ten years last past before the date of investment paid a dividend at the rate of not less than three per centum per annum on its ordinary stock: (h) In the stock of any railway or canal company in Great Britain or Ireland whose undertaking is leased in perpetuity or for a term of not less than two hundred years at a fixed rental to any such railway company as is mentioned in subsection (g), either alone or jointly with any other railway company: (i) In the debenture stock of any railway company in India, the interest on which is paid or guaranteed by the secretary of state in council of India: (j) In the “B” annuities of the Eastern Bengal,

<br>

§ 530. 

INVESTMENT OF TRUST FUNDS.

securities relates to ordinary trusts, but where the trust fund consists of capital money arising under the Settled Land Acts, 1882 to 1890, or is money which is liable to be laid out, under the trusts of a settlement, in the purchase of the East Indian, and the Scinde Punjaub and Delhi Railways, and any like annuities which may at any time hereafter be created on the purchase of any other railway by the secretary of state in council of India, and charged on the revenues of India, and which may be authorized by act of Parliament to be accepted by trustees in lieu of any stock held by them in the purchased railway; also in deferred annuities comprised in the register of holders of annuity Class D, and annuities comprised in the register of annuitants Class C, of the East Indian Railway Company: (x) In the stock of any railway company in India upon which a fixed or minimum dividend in sterling is paid or guaranteed by the secretary of state in council of India, or upon the capital of which the interest is so guaranteed: (j) In the debenture or guaranteed or preference stock of any company in Great Britain, or Ireland, established for the supply of water for profit, and incorporated by special act of Parliament, or by Royal Charter, and having during each of the ten years last past before the date of investment paid a dividend of not less than five pounds per centum on its ordinary stock: (m) In nominal or inscribed stock issued, or to be issued, by the corporation of any municipal borough having, according to the returns of the last census prior to the date of investment, a population exceeding fifty thousand, or by any county council, under the authority of any act of Parliament or provisional order: (n) In nominal or inscribed stock issued or to be issued by any commissioners incorporated by act of Parliament for the purpose of supplying water, and having a compulsory power of levying rates over an area having, according to the returns of the last census prior to the date of investment, a population exceeding fifty thousand, provided that during each of the ten years last past before the date of investment, the rates levied by such commissioners shall not have exceeded eighty per centum of the amount authorized by law to be levied: (o) In any of the stocks, funds, or securities for the time being authorized for the investment of cash under the control or subject to the order of the High Court, and may also from time to time vary any such investment.

§ 2. (1) A trustee may, under the powers of this act, invest in any of the securities mentioned or referred to in section one of this act, notwithstanding that the same may be redeemable, and that the price exceeds the redemption value. (2) Provided that a trustee may not under the powers of this act purchase at a price exceeding its redemption value any stock mentioned or referred to in sub-sections (g), (i), (k), (1), and (m), of section one, which is liable to be redeemed within fifteen years of the date of purchase at par or at some other fixed rate, or purchase any such stock as is mentioned or referred to in the sub-sections aforesaid, which.

<br>

1208

INVESTMENT OF TRUST FUNDS.

[§ 530.]

of land, the trustee must invest it according to the direction of the tenant for life, in some of the modes specified in section 21 of the Settled Land Act of 1882, or, at the option of the tenant for life, on the securities in which money pro-

is liable to be redeemed at par or at some other fixed rate, at a price exceeding fifteen per centum above par or such other fixed rate. (3) A trustee may retain until redemption any redeemable stock, fund, or security which may have been purchased in accordance with the powers of this act. § 3. Every power conferred by the preceding sections shall be exercised according to the discretion of the trustee, but subject to any consent required by the instrument, if any, creating the trust with respect to the investment of the trust funds. § 4. The preceding sections shall apply as well to trusts created before as to trusts created after the passing of this act, and the powers thereby conferred shall be in addition to the powers conferred by the instrument, if any, creating the trust. § 5. (1) A trustee having power to invest in real securities, unless expressly forbidden by the instrument creating the trust, may invest and shall be deemed to have always had power to invest (a) on mortgage of property held for an unexpired term of not less than two hundred years, and not subject to a reservation of rent greater than a shilling a year, or to any right of redemption or to any condition for re-entry, except for non-payment of rent; and (b) on any charge, or upon mortgage of any charge, made under the Improvement of Land Act, 1864. (2) A trustee having power to invest in the mortgages or bonds of any railway company or of any other description of company may, unless the contrary is expressed in the instrument authorizing the investment, invest in the debenture stock of a railway company or such other company as aforesaid. (3) A trustee having power to invest money in the debentures or debenture stock of any railway or other company may, unless the contrary is expressed in the instrument authorizing the investment, invest in any nominal debentures or nominal debenture stock issued under the Local Loans Act, 1875. (4) A trustee having power to invest money in securities in the Isle of Man, or in securities of the government of a colony, may, unless the contrary is expressed in the instrument authorizing the investment, invest in any securities of the Government of the Isle of Man, under the Isle of Man Loans Act, 1880. (5) A trustee having a general power to invest trust moneys in or upon the security of shares, stock, mortgages, bonds, or debentures of companies incorporated by or acting under the authority of an act of Parliament, may invest in, or upon the security of, mortgage debentures duly issued under and in accordance with the provisions of the Mortgage Debenture Act, 1865. § 6. A trustee having power to invest in the purchase of land or on mortgage of land may invest in the purchase, or on mortgage of any land, notwithstanding the same is charged with a rent under the powers of the Public Money Drainage Acts, 1846 to 1856, or the

<br>

### --- Page 0385 ---

§ 531. The Rule in the United States.—The rule for the government of trustees in the investment of trust funds established by the English courts has been adopted to some extent by the courts of this country. It has given direction to the decisions of the courts of New York, New Jersey and Pennsylvania, and, perhaps, in a degree, to those of most of the other States. In a leading case in Pennsylvania, Chief Justice Black, in delivering the opinion of the court, said: "In England it has been held for more than a century past to be settled law that a trustee can only protect himself from risk when he invests the trust fund in real or government securities, or makes the investment in pursuance of an order by the court. The same rule has been adopted in its whole length and breadth by the courts of New York and New Jersey. In Pennsylvania this doctrine does not appear ever to have been either affirmed or denied. So far as our own authorities go the question is an open one. But the time has come when the interests and rights of trustees, as well as orphans, married women and insane persons, demand the settling of it, and we think the rule here ought to be as it is elsewhere, not because we feel bound by the precedents of a foreign State, but because we cannot resist the considerations of justice and policy by which they are supported."

Landed Property Improvement (Ireland) Act, 1847, or by an absolute order made under the Improvement of Land Act, 1864, unless the terms of the trust expressly provide that the land to be purchased or taken in mortgage shall not be subject to any such prior charge. See also the following late decisions construing the Trustee Investment Acts: In re Owthwaite (1891), 3 Ch. 494; In re Tucker (1894), 1 Ch. 724; 8. c. (1894), 3 Ch. 429; Elve v. Boyton (1891), 1 Ch. 501; Pearson-Gee v. Pearson (1895), W. N. 90. The Trustee Investment Act, 1889, § 3, gives trustees power to vary investments, not only of moneys invested in any description of fund mentioned in the section. The fact that the deed or will creating the trust gives no express power to vary the investments is not material. In re Dick, C. A. (1891), 1 Ch. 423.

Settled Land Act, 1882, § 33. See also In re Mackenzie’s Trusts, L. R. 23 Ch. D. 750; In re National Permanent Building Society, L. R. 43 Ch. D. 431.

INVESTMENT OF TRUST FUNDS.

[§ 531.

In a leading case in New Jersey it was held that it is a rule well settled in the English Chancery, and adopted by this court, that if trustees loan money without due security, they are

1 Worrell’s Appeal, 23 Pa. St. 44, 48, 49; In re Nyce’s Estate, 5 W. & S. 254; Morris v. Wallace, 3 Pa. St. 319; Twaddle’s Appeal, 5 Pa. St. 15; Rush’s Estate, 1 Pars. (Pa.) 24; Stanley’s Appeal, 8 Pa. St. 431; Burton’s Estate, 1 Pars. (Pa.) 24; Ihmsen’s Appeal, 43 Pa. St. 431; Pleasant’s Appeal, 77 Pa. St. 356; Angue’s Estate, 2 Phila. 137; Seidler’s Estate, 5 Phila. (Pa.) 85; Gaw’s Estate, 34 Leg. Int. 66; 8. C., 24 Pitts. L. J. (Pa.) 128; Shield’s Estate, 14 Phila. (Pa.) 307; Jack’s Appeal, 94 Pa. St. 367; Pleasanton’s Appeal, 99 Pa. St. 362; Eyster’s Appeal, 16 Pa. St. 372; Fahnestock’s Appeal, 104 Pa. St. 46. An executor is not at fault nor responsible for the loss of an investment in bank stock, when it is lost by the failure of the bank, if the investment was made by the testator himself, Parker v. Glover, 42 N. J. Eq. 559, in exposition of a statute. Pope v. Matthews, 18 S. Car. 444. An executor commits no breach of trust by allowing trust funds to remain where they were invested by the testator himself in bonds secured by mortgage on real estate, both, etc., of a turnpike company. Miller v. Proctor, 20 Ohio St. 442. “From our examination of the authorities and the cases referred to, we have come to the conclusion that, as a general rule, it is the duty of trustees to invest funds held by them in government or State securities, or in bonds and mortgages, on unincumbered estate; that while this rule is not arbitrary and inflexible so as to admit of no possible exceptions, it is

the basis upon which trustees should usually act; that, in any event, the trustee is bound to employ such diligence, care and prudence, in the management of the trust, as diligent, careful, prudent men of discretion and intelligence generally employ in their own like affairs; and that for a neglect to make use of such diligence, care and prudence, the trustee becomes liable.” Mills v. Hoffman, 26 Hun (N. Y.), 594. See Ormiston v. Olcott, 22 Hun (N. Y.), 270; s. c., 84 N. Y. 339; Judd v. Warner, 2 Dem. (N. Y.) 104; Bates v. Underhill, 3 Redf. (N. Y.) 365; Baker v. Disprow, 3 Redf. (N. Y.) 348; In re Foster, 15 Hun (N. Y.), 287; Adair v. Brimmer, 74 N. Y. 539; Clark v. St. Louis, Alton, etc. Rd., 58 How. Pr. 21; Goodwin v. Howe, 62 How. Pr. 134. It is a breach of trust in the absence of express authority for trustees to employ trust funds in business or trade. Kyle v. Barnett, 17 Ala. 306; Martin v. Raborn, 42 Ala. 648; Fiagg v. Ely, 1 Edw. (N.Y.) 206; King v. Talbot, 40 N. Y. 76; Stedman v. Frieder, 20 N. Y. 437; Lucht v. Behrens, 28 Ohio St. 231; Pitkin v. Pitkin, 1 Conn. 307; Alsop v. Mather, 8 Conn. 548; Muntz v. Brown, 11 La. Ann. 472. An executor has no right without authority from a competent court to invest the funds of the estate in municipal bonds or bank stock. Tucker v. Tucker, 33 N. J. Eq. 235. The chancellor has jurisdiction to enforce trusts and prevent a perversion or abuse thereof, and trustees authorized to invest stock in a permanent and

<br>

### --- Page 0387 ---

§ 531.) INVESTMENT OF TRUST FUNDS. 1211

liable in case of insolvency. As to what is due security, the principle to be extracted from the English authorities is that the loaning of trust moneys, and especially where infants are concerned, on private or personal security, is not a compliance with the rule that requires due security to be taken, and, of course, that such loans are made at the risk of the trustee. In England a trustee loaning money must require adequate real security, or resort to the public funds. In this country there are few opportunities for investing in the public stock; the stock of private companies is not considered safe, and investments in that species of stock would scarcely be encouraged by a court of equity; there is no other bona fide landed security that would come within the rule, and the court would advise it to be taken in all cases where public stock cannot be had.1 The following discussion of the principle on which this rule is based is from the well considered opinion of the court in a leading case before the Supreme Court of New York: "But it is contended that the rule has never been established in this State, and that it would be oppressive to enforce it against persons acting in good faith until it had been fully settled and become generally known. It is probably a sufficient answer to say that it has long been the uniform rule in the country from which we derive, and in many cases by constitutional provisions, the principal rule for our conduct. We have no other foundation for a vast proportion of the principles which govern our actions, both in our individual and representative capacities. Possibly, in extreme cases, and particularly where some rule is based upon principles inconsistent with the nature of our institutions, such rule should be declared inapplicable and not in force. But this is neither the one nor the other. The rule is highly beneficial both for the trustee and cestui que trust. The trustee knows that he is safe so long as he acts honestly and con-

profitable stock have no right to pay their debts therewith or withdraw it from a safe investment, where the interest was regularly paid, and invest them in stock of City of Lexington. Transylvania Univ. v. Clay, 2 B. Mon. 386, 385.

1 Gray v. Fox, 1 N. J. Eq. 259,

<br>

### --- Page 0388 ---

1212 INVESTMENT OF TRUST FUNDS. [§ 532.

fines himself within the prescribed limits. The interests of the cestui que trust, who is ordinarily incapable of guarding his own rights, are securely protected. His property cannot be jeoparded and wasted by hazardous speculations.

§ 532. The Subject Continued.—In the States above mentioned the courts favor investments in government or State securities, and after these in bonds and mortgages on unincumbered real estate. In Pennsylvania the investment of trust funds in the stocks or bonds of private corporations is prohibited by the fundamental law. In New York it has recently been held that the investment of trust funds in railroad or bank stocks is at the risk of the trustee, and the tendency of recent decisions in New Jersey and Pennsylvania is in the same direction. Investments in the stocks

1 Ackerman v. Emott, 4 Barb. 626, 646. In a case where real estate security was allowed, it was held that a company owning coal lands and a canal and which gave the trustee a preferred claim on their income, substantially gave real security, as the realty held by the company exceeded in value the sum of the debts. Twaddle’s Appeal, 5 Pa. St. 15. See also Halsted v. Meeker, 3 Green Ch. 136; Lathrop v. Simulley, 23 N. J. Eq. 192; Thomsen’s Appeal, 43 Pa. St. 431; Lamar v. Micou, 112 U.S. 452.

2 Constitution Pennsylvania, art. 8, § 22. “It may now be considered as settled law that in Pennsylvania an investment by a guardian or other trustee, unless authorized by the deed of trust, in the stock of an incorporated company, whether a bank, railroad, canal, manufacturing or mining corporation, cannot be made at the risk of a ward or other cestui que trust.” Worrell’s Appeal, 23 Pa. St. 44. “It is not necessary in this case to decide whether, where a discretion is left, the trustee should always adhere to the securities pointed out by the act of assembly; but we must say that we think it the safest and wisest course. In our own books we have no such gross violation of the rules governing investments by trustees as the present; the nearest being that of the Estate of Esther Barton, 1 Pars. Eq. Cas. 24. decided by Judge King in 1842. There, at the time of the investment, the stocks and loans were valuable paying securities; and even this case is much shaken by the later decisions of Hemphill’s Appeal, 18 Pa. St. 303, and Worrell’s Appeal, 23 Pa. St. 44, if not substantially overruled.” Pray’s Appeal, 34 Pa. St. 100. “The measure of diligence and care required of a trustee is precisely that which a man of ordinary prudence would practice in the care of his own estate. This rule has been so often laid down in our books that it seems unnecessary to refer to authorities on that point; a reasonable degree of vigilance and the exercise of good faith is the standard of the trustee’s duty.” Fahnestock’s Appeal, 104 Pa. St. 46.

<br>

### --- Page 0389 ---

§ 533. ] INVESTMENT OF TRUST FUNDS. 1213  
of business corporations, or in any other securities than those of the public funds, or mortgages on real estate, are not regarded by the courts with favor. Loans on second mortgages are held objectionable.

§ 533. Statutory Regulations.—In a number of the States there are statutes by which trustees are governed in their investment of trust funds. In some of them no specific directions are given, but the courts are authorized by statute to direct investments and are left to the exercise of their discretion in regard to the character of the securities that they may order.

Gray v. Fox, 1 N. J. Eq. 259, 268; Lamar v. Micon, 112 U.S. 452; Thmsen’s Appeal, 43 Pa. St. 431; Hemphill’s Appeal, 18 Pa. St. 303; Worrell’s Appeal, 9 Pa. St. 508, and 23 Pa. St. 44; Lathrop v. Smalley, 23 N. J. Eq. 192; Halsted v. Meeker, 18 N. J. Eq. 136.

Robinson v. Robinson, 11 Beay. 371; Lockhart v. Reilly, 1 De G. & J. 476; Shuey v. Latta, 90 Ind. 136. A trustee, being directed to invest money, loaned the same on a third mortgage, one of the prior mortgages being due to himself. Held that the trustee could not terminate the trust, as against himself, by turning over the trust bond and mortgage to his successor in the trust, and buy in the property for himself, without notice to the cestuis que trust and order of court. MeEachen v. Stewart, 114 N. Car. 370; s. c., 198 E. Rep. 702.

In New Jersey by statute the courts have authority to order investments. They most always use this discretion by confining themselves to real and government authorities. Lathrop v. Smalley, 23 N. J. Eq. 192; Tucker v. Tucker, 33 N. J. Eq. 235; Gray v. Fox, 1 N. J. Eq. 259; Woodruff v. Ward, 35 N. J. Eq. 467. In Maryland good bank stock, as well as government securities and mortgages on real estate, have always been considered a proper investment. Hammond v. Hammond, 2 Bland, 306; Gray v. Lynch, 8 Gill, 603; Murray v. Feinour, 2 Md. Ch. 418. So in Mississippi investment in bank stock is allowed. Smith v. Burns, 25 Miss. 422. In South Carolina, before the war, no more definite rule appears to have been laid down than that guardians and trustees must manage the funds as in their own affairs. Boggs v. Ryder, 4 Rich. Eq. 408, 411; Spear v. Spear, 9 Rich. Eq. 184, 201; Snelling v. McCreary, 14 Rich. Eq. 291, 300. In Georgia the English rule was never adopted, a statute of 1845, which authorized executors, administrators, guardians and trustees, holding any trust funds, to invest them in securities of the State, was not considered compulsory; and before January 1, 1863 (when that statute was amended by adding a provision that any other investment of trust funds must be made under a judicial order, or else be at the risk of the trustee), those who lent the fund at interest, on what was at

<br>

### --- Page 0390 ---

1214

INVESTMENT OF TRUST FUNDS.

[§ 533.]

cases the courts enunciate a general rule, and in others they will order in a particular case. But in whatever form the order of the court may be given, it must be obeyed. To the trustee it is the law, and he cannot disregard it without making himself liable for any loss that may come.

The time considered by prudent men to be good security were not held liable for a loss without their fault. Cobbs’ Digest, 333; Code of 1861, § 2308; Brown v. Wright, 39 Ga. 96; Moses v. Moses, 50 Ga. 9, 33. In Alabama, the Supreme Court, in Bryant v. Craig, 12 Ala. 354, 359, having intimated that a guardian could not safely invest upon either real or personal security without an order of court, the legislature, from 1852, authorized guardians and trustees to invest on bond and mortgage, or on good personal security, with no other limit than fidelity and prudence might require. Code of 1852, § 2024; Code of 1867, § 2426; Foscue v. Lyon, 55 Ala. 440, 452; Lamar v. Micou, 112 U.S. 452, 469. 

“In Massachusetts, by a usage of more than half a century, approved by a uniform course of judicial decisions, it has come to be regarded as too firmly settled to be changed, except by the legislature, that all that can be required of a trustee to invest is that he shall conduct himself faithfully and exercise a sound discretion, such as men of prudence and intelligence exercise in the permanent disposition of their own funds, having regard not only to the probable income, but also to the probable safety of the capital; and that a guardian or trustee is not precluded from investing in the stock of banking, insurance, manufacturing or railroad corporations, within or without the State. Harvard College v. Amory, 9 Pick. 446, 461; Lovell v. Minot, 20 Pick. 116, 119; Kinmouth v. Brigham, 5 Allen, 270, 277; Clark v. Garfield, 8 Allen, 427; Brown v. French, 125 Mass. 410; Bowker v. Pierce, 130 Mass. 262. 

In New Hampshire and in Vermont, investments, honestly and prudently made, in securities of any kind that produce income, appear to be allowed. Knowlton v. Bradley, 17 N. H. 468; Kimball v. Reding, 31 N. H. 352, 374; French v. Currier, 47 N. H. 88, 99; Barney v. Parsons, 54 Vt. 623.” Lamar v. Micou, 112 U. S. 452, 468. 

Where property is devised to a trustee to pay the income to a person for life, with remainder to the children of the life tenant, the court cannot require the trustee to invest part of the corpus of the estate in household furniture and stock for the use of the life tenant, the remainder-men not being before the court. Stouffer v. Claggett (1895) (Md.), 32 Atl. Rep. 284. 

There is in Rhode Island no statute law or rule of court which points out any particular classes of securities, for the investment of trust funds. The fact that securities were bought and held by the testator commends them for retention so long as there is no doubt of their safety. Peckham v. Newton, 15 R.I. 321; s.c., 4 Atl. Rep. 758. Where the vendors of land knew that the money paid therefor belonged to the wards of the vendee, they were put on inquiry as to the authority of the guardian to so invest the money; and if, in an action by a subsequent...

<br>

§ 533. ] INVESTMENT OF TRUST FUNDS. 1215

from his investments. Where a fund has been left to repair losses and to compensate for shrinkages in order to prevent any decrease of the value of the trust estate, it may be used as a bonus for the encouragement of improvements adapted to secure that purpose. In a recent case in Missouri a testator set apart a reserve fund to be retained by trustees to guard against losses by shrinkage in the value of his real estate, or other contingencies, and to be invested and reinvested from time to time, until the termination of the trust. After his death a corporation, having determined to construct somewhere in St. Louis a large hotel, selected a site opposite to testator’s property, which guardian, it appears that no order directing the payment of the ward’s money therefor was made, and that the investment was not confirmed by the court as required by statute, such vendors are liable for the funds so received. Smoot v. Richards (Tex. Civ. App.), 27 S.W. Rep. 967. See generally, the following authorities for court rules in various Western and Southern States regulating investments: Tucker v. Car. 209; Allen v. Gaillard, 1 S. Car. 279; Mayer v. Mordecai, 1 S. Car. 383; Womack v. Austin, 1 S. Car. 421; Sanders v. Rogers, 1 S. Car. 452; Matthews v. Heyward, 2 S. Car. 239; Creighton v. Pringle, 3 S. Car. 773; Singleton v. Lowndes, 9 S. Car. 465; Davidson v. Moore, 14 S. Car. 251; Gamble v. Gibson, 59 Mo. 585.

1 Wheeler v. Perry, 18 N. H. 307; Tucker v. State, 72 Ind. 242; Sharpe v. Rockwood, 78 Va. 24; Ferguson v. Epes, 77 Va. 499; Allen v. Gaillard, 1 S. Car. 279. When a trustee appointed by the court fails to invest trust funds as directed by it, he may be proceeded against by rule, with his sureties as co-defendants to the rule; and under this proceeding he may be ordered to return the funds to the court where they will remain in judicial custody till the order be obeyed relative to the investment. Dickinson v. Trout, 8 Bush, 441. See also Horn v. Lockhart, 17 Wall. 570; Smith v. Wilmington Coal Co., 83 Ill. 498; Richardson v. Knight, 69 Me. 285.

### --- Page 0392 ---

1216 INVESTMENT OF TRUST FUNDS, [§ 534.

was at the time rapidly depreciating in value, but were unwilling to erect the hotel there unless property owners on that street would subscribe a sufficient amount as bonus. The trustees, doubting their power to donate from the reserve fund to this enterprise, applied to the court for instructions. It was held that such donation, helping to prevent further decrease in values, would be an “investment” within the meaning of the will.²

§ 534. Investment of Funds for Charitable Purposes. — In New York, where express trusts are prohibited by statute, corporations for that purpose take the place of ordinary trustees for charitable purposes. Such corporations exercise the functions of trustees, and to all intents and purposes are trustees of all charitable trusts. And, except as their powers may be modified by statute, or by the decisions of the courts, they are subject to the same equitable and statutory rules as other trustees for charitable purposes. In England this rule has been established by a recent decision of the Chancery Division of the Supreme Court of Judicature. In a case where a corporation, incorporated by a special act, held funds for charitable purposes, it was held that the corporation were trustees within the meaning of the Trust Investment Act, 1889, and were entitled to invest any trust moneys in their hands on any of the securities mentioned or referred to in section 3 of that act, unless they were expressly forbidden to do so by the instrument creating the trust. But it was further held that if a trustee has not, under the instrument creating his trust, power to vary the investments of the trust funds, section 3 does not authorize him to sell existing investments for the purpose of reinvesting the proceeds on securities mentioned therein.³

² Drake v. Crane (1895), 127 Mo. 85; s.C., 295. W. Rep. 990.

³ In re Manchester Royal Infirmary, L. R. 43 Ch. D. 420. Funds of a benefit building society invested in the names of trustees for the society under the direction of the board are not trust funds subject to the powers conferred by the Trust Investment Act, 1889. In re Funds National Permanent, etc. Building Society, L. R. 43 Ch. D. 431. See “Investments of Funds for Endowments or Charities.” 12 Sol. J. & 

<br>

### --- Page 0393 ---

INVESTMENT OF TRUST FUNDS. 1217

§ 535.

§ 585. The Change of Investments.—It is an established rule that after trust funds have been once invested trustees have no power to change the securities. Great care and deliberation should be exercised in the investment of trust funds, and the investment should be made with a view to permanency; and, having been properly made, it should not be disturbed. Like other rules, however, this is subject to exceptions. The instrument may invest a trustee with power to exercise his judgment in regard to a change of investment, and circumstances may render it very important that a change should be made. In a case in New Jersey it was held that "a power given to executors to change investments of personal estate, as may be thought most advantageous for the estate, will authorize the executors to dispose of an unproductive and constantly depreciating stock at less than par, though the testator expressed a wish that that stock should not be sold for less than par, unless thought necessary." Where a change is demanded by the interests of the beneficiaries, or by a regard for the safety of the fund, and no change is authorized by the instrument, the court will empower the trustee to make such a change. If the funds are loaned they may be called in and reloaned on other securities, or the trustee may sell the trust property and invest the proceeds in other property.

Rep. 8. A municipal trustee cannot apply the trust fund except as charged in the instrument. See Trustees v. Bradbury, 11 Me. 1185; Poultney v. Wells, 1 Aik. (Vt.) 180; Chambers v. St. 44; Montpelier v. E. Montpelier, 27 Vt. 704; S. C., 29 Vt. 12; Perin v. Carey, 24 How. 465; McDonough Will Case, 15 How. 367.

that is deemed safer or more productive. § 536. Investments for the Accumulation of the Income.—Where the instrument creating the trust directs that the estate, or any part thereof, shall be invested with a view to the accumulation of the income, and the trustee refuses or neglects to make such investment, or for any reason, for which he is responsible, fails to accumulate as directed, he will be required

1 Allen v. Graves, 3 Bush, 491.

Even where power to vary securities has been given expressly to trustees, it must not be exercised capriciously or without good reason. Brice v. Stokes, 11 Ves. 324; De Manneville v. Crompton, 1 Ves. & B. 359; Fowler v. Reynall, 3 Macn. & G. 500. Where trustees under a will exceeded their power by buying real estate with trust funds, and continued to buy and sell at first with a profit, but ultimately with a loss of a considerable portion of the fund, no want of good faith being shown, they were held liable for the amount of the trust fund before the first purchase of real estate only, with interest from the time the beneficiaries should have received the income. Baker v. Disbrow, 3 Redf. 348. See also Forrest v. Elwes, 4 Ves. 497; Cormvise v. Bourgum, 2 Ga. Dec. 15; Washington v. Emery, 4 Jones (N. Car.), 32. Where an investment in a particular stock is directed by the testator, it cannot be varied except with the consent of all the parties interested, and the court will not order a change to pay compound interest on where some of these parties are not sui juris. Cantrell, 10 Yerg. 263; Burrill v. Sheil, 2 Barb. 457. Where consent to a variation is required in the will, a waiver of the provisions of the will will not be a sufficient consent. Plympton v. Plympton, 6 Allen, 178. The Trust Investment Act, 1889, § 3, gives trustees power to vary investments, not only of moneys invested by them under the section, but also of moneys invested in any description of fund mentioned in the section. The fact that the deed or will creating the trust gives no express power to vary the investments is not material. In re Dich, C. A. (1891), 1 Ch. 423. One holding a fund as trustee for remainder-men, may change transient security into cash to be permanently invested for their benefit. Mason v. Commerce Bank, 90 Mo. 452.

If the instrument provides for the semi-annual addition of the interest to the principal, semi-annual rests will be made in the settlement; if the additions were to be made annually, the rests will be annual. The object of that direction is to charge compound interest, and the decree, though perhaps going farther than usual, was held under the circumstances properly executed by a computation of interest upon such receipt from the day it was received; the balance of receipt, with the interest so calculated, and payments, being struck at the end of the half year; and that balance, so composed of principal and interest, being carried forward as an item in the account producing interest. Raphael v. Boehm, 11 Ves. 92. Brown v. Sansome, 1 McClel. & Y. Exch. 427. Saunders, as follows: "On the subject of compounding interest on trustees there is not, and indeed could not well be, any uniform rule which could justly apply to all cases. Where a trust to invest has been grossly and willfully neglected; where the funds have been used by the trustees in their own business, or profits made, of which they give no account, interest is compounded as a punishment, or as a measure of damages for undisclosed profits, and in place of them. For mere neglect to invest simple interest only is imposed. Six months’ rests have been made only where the amounts received were large, and such as could be easily and at all times invested." In Kentucky it has been held that the court may order the proceeds of a sale in the hands of a trustee to be invested by him so as to be made productive pending the litigation, and if the trustee fails or refuses to make the investment accordingly, he may be ordered to bring in the whole amount, with compound interest from the date of the order directing the investment.

CHAPTER XXV.

THE LIABILITIES OF TRUSTEES.

§ 537. Introductory.  
538. The General Rule.  
539. Limitations of the Rule.  
540. Liability in Breach of Trust.  
541. Liability as Co-trustee.  
542. The Right of Contribution.  
543. Liability for Co-trustees.  
544. The Subject Continued.  
545. Liability of Co-trustees as Determined by their Bond.  
546. The Subject Continued.  
547. Liability of Trustees on Covenants.  
548. Liability of Trustee in Delegation of Powers and Duties.

550. For Loss from Insufficient Security.  
551. Liability of a Father as Trustee for Son.  
552. For Delay in Investment of Trust Funds.  
553. The Measure of Liability.

§ 537.  
Introductory.—The liabilities of trustees are so essentially and intimately related to their duties, as such, that they cannot be wholly separated. The treatment of the one involves, in a general sense, a discussion of the other. Where there is no failure in the discharge of duty, it is the rule that no liability for losses, or for injury to the trust estate, is incurred; but where it appears that a trustee is derelict in his duty to the beneficiary, he becomes personally liable for the evil consequences of his acts. In the discharge of his official duties the trustee is amenable to equitable rules, and such rules are not necessarily in full accord with those of a court of law. He may deal with the trust estate, in many respects, in a manner that is not strictly legal and still be upheld in equity. But in equity,

1 Upson v. Badeau, 3 Bradf. Sur. 13; Poole v. Munday, 103 Mass. 174; Jones v. Lewis, 2 Ves. 241; Cross v. Smith, 7 East, 246. See also cases where trustees have been held liable because of neglect: Perry v. Wooton, 5 Humph. 524; Morris v. Morris, 9 Heisk. 815; Cooper v. Day, 1 Rich. Eq. 26; Cogbell v. Boyd, 77 Va. 450; But—

<br>

### --- Page 0398 ---

1222

LIABILITIES OF TRUSTEES.

[§ 537.

no less than at law, he will be held responsible for a faithful discharge of his fiduciary obligations.

And where he comes short in his duty in this direction, he will be held to a strict personal accountability for any injuries or losses which may result from his acts.

On the various points in which the relation of liability to the discharge of duty has been made plain in the preceding chapters, the rule will not be repeated in the following sections.

ler v. Boston, etc. R. Co., 24 Hun, 99; Whitehead v. Whitehead, 85 Va. 870; Wood v. Myrick, 17 Minn. 408; Harry v. Glover, 2 Hill Eq. 515; Worling v. Allie, 13 N. Y. Sup]. 791; Westmoreland v. Holland, 19 W. R. 302; Simpson v. Gowdy, 19 Ind. 292; Cross v. Petree, 10 B. Mon. 413; Neff’s Appeal, 57 Pa. St. 91; Hester v. Wilkinson, 6 Humph. 215; s. c., dé Am. Dec. 303.

A trustee who sells trust property and fails to take security for the payment of the purchase money, is liable for any loss resulting. Hurtt v. Fisher, 1 Harr. & G. 88.

Where executors employ a person not authorized to practice to foreclose a mortgage due to the estate of the testator, and he forecloses the same in the name of another person, as solicitor, but from the ignorance of the person so employed by the executors the mortgage is irregularly foreclosed, so that a part of the debt is lost, such executors are answerable to the legatees for the amount of such loss.

It seems that if a person not legally authorized to practice law, is employed to conduct judicial proceedings, he is not legally responsible to his employer for any loss the latter may sustain in consequence of the ignorance of the person so employed in respect to legal proceedings. Wakeman v. Skinner, 3 Barb. Ch. 148.

But in a case where he is acting under competent, legal advisors, he makes an innocent mistake in a doubtful point of law, the trustee will not be held accountable. Vez v. Emery, 5 Ves. 142; Kimball v. Reding, 31 N.H. 352; s. c., 64 Am. Dec. 333; Miller v. Proctor, 20 Ohio St. 442.

Where an executor has settled what appears to be a final account, it must be a very strong case of fraud proved in such settlement, or of clear accident or mistake, to make it just to reopen and revive the account after the lapse of twenty years and the death of the parties concerned.

The executor is not responsible for more money than he received with interest, unless in case of very supine negligence or willful default. A claim for damages would also be subject to the operation of the statute of limitations. Taylor v. Benham, 5 How. 233.

A trustee who wrongfully or by mistake pays money to a third person in the belief that he is thereby relieving the trust property from an incumbrance, will not thereby escape responsibility, in the matter of his trust, to the beneficiaries under the deed; and the cestuis que trust in the absence of fraud are entitled to no relief against the party wrongfully receiving the money. Wasson v. Garrett, 2 Baxt. (Tenn.) 477.

<br>

### --- Page 0399 ---

§ 538. ] LIABILITIES OF TRUSTEES, 1223

§ 538. The General Rule.—The responsibility of the trustee for his management of the trust estate is primarily to the beneficiary or beneficiaries. If they are sui juris, they may, at any time, lay claim to the trust property, and their claim will be recognized in whatever form it may be at the time, and if it has passed out of his hands they may follow it into the possession of third persons and recover it, unless it has been transferred to a bona fide purchaser for a valuable consideration and without notice. In addition to their claim upon the trust property, wherever it may be found, the beneficiaries have a personal claim on the trustee, whenever the property has passed out of his hands, or is held by him in such manner as to render his act a breach of trust. Where the property has not passed beyond their reach, the beneficiaries may, at their election, recover the property or establish their claim against the trustee. The personal liability of the trustee to make good any loss occasioned by his breach of trust is simply an equitable contract obligation. The liability will be commensurate with the loss. He will be held to a full compensation for all the loss and all the injury sustained by the estate in consequence of his wrongful act. The claim of the beneficiaries may be enforced by a suit in equity against the trustee, or against his estate after his death, and the statute of limitations will not be a defense, unless there are statutory regulations by which the action of the court is controlled. In an English case, where a trustee had ex-

Bray, 33 Beav. 96; Underwood v. Stevens, 1 Meriv. 712, 717; Cresswell v. Dewell, 4 Giff. 460; Brewer v. Swirles, 2 Sm. & Gif. 219; Fletcher v. Green, 33 Beav. 426; Cocker v. Quayle, 1 Russ. & My. 535; Kellaway v. Johnson, 5 Beav. 319. As to infants as trustees: Whitmore v. Weld, 1 Vern. 326, 328; Hindmarsh v. Southgate, 3 Russ. 324; Corey v. Gertchen, 2 Madd. 40; Wright v. Snowe, 2 De G. & S. 321.

“The principle, as I have always understood it, is this: A married woman is liable—or rather her separate estate is liable (for there is no personal liability, as far as she is concerned)—to make good all contracts which are made by her with express reference to the separate estate, or which from the nature of the contract itself must...

[§ 538,

ecuted a deed containing merely an appointment of him as trustee and a declaration by him that he accepted the office, it was held that no covenant by him would be implied, and that a claim for trust money received and misapplied by

be intended to be so referred; but she is not liable even for general contracts which from their nature cannot be so referred; a fortiori, she is not liable for general torts, but her husband is liable. Her separate estate may be liable for a fraud relating to the separate estate, that is, dealing with the separate estate by way of fraudulent representation.” Wainford v. Heyl, L. R. 20 Eq. 321, 324. The directors of a corporation are liable to the stockholders and creditors of the corporation for a fraudulent breach of trust. And in a suit instituted against them, on account of such fraud, it is not necessary to make all the directors parties. A proceeding against the trustees for a fraudulent breach of trust, is an exception to the rule that, in a suit against trustees, all of the trustees must be made parties. In a suit brought by a creditor or a stockholder of a corporation against the directors thereof for a fraudulent breach of trust, the corporation itself, if in existence, is a necessary party. In such suit all the directors or stockholders of the corporation should also be made parties; or the bill should be filed by the complainant, as well for himself as for all others standing in the same situation. Cunningham v. Pell, 5 Paige Ch. 607. The failure of a testamentary trustee, who accepted from the executor unsecured notes in lieu of money in payment of the legacy, to collect the notes for ten years, the payee in the meantime becoming insolvent, renders him personally liable. Hunt v. Gontrum (1894), 80 Md. 64; s. c., 30 Atl. Rep. 620. Where a daughter conveys property in trust to her father, to be used by him in a mercantile business, the father, daughter and her brothers each to receive a one-ninth interest, and after the father’s death one of the brothers is appointed trustee by the court, the trustee is personally liable for goods purchased for use in the business for which the trust was formed, though the seller charged the goods to the trading name under which the trust business was conducted, and knew that the trustee was purchasing in his representative capacity. Connally v. Lyons, 82 Tex. 664; s.c., 18 S. W. Rep. 799. The fact that equitable grounds exist for charging the trust estate for the value of the services rendered by defendant is no reason for charging plaintiff personally. U. S. Trust Co. v. Stanton, 139 N. Y. 531; s.c., 34 N.E. Rep. 1098. In an action by a substituted testamentary trustee to foreclose a mortgage executed to his predecessor, plaintiff is not liable to a personal judgment on a counterclaim for the excess of fees over the amount of the mortgage due defendant as attorney under retainer by his predecessor, as the contract bound the latter individually, and plaintiff did not assume any responsibility. U. S. Trust Co. v. Stanton, 139 N. Y. 531; s.c., 34 N. E. Rep. 1098. Where a trustee in good faith has refused to convey land to the beneficiary, he is

In his opinion in this case, Lord Justice Gifford said: “It has been suggested that there may be such a thing as a specialty in equity which is not a specialty at law, but I do not hesitate to say, in the plainest possible language, that if there be not a specialty at law, and not such an instrument as that an action at law can be maintained upon it, this is not so; all equitable debts rank pari passu, and there is no distinction between one description of equitable debt and another.”

Not liable for costs in an action to compel such a conveyance. Fitzgerald v. Wynne, 1 App. D.C. Rep. 107. In an action by the administrator of the obligee on a bond, in which the obligor bound himself to pay her, her executors or administrators, the same named “for her own interest and as trustee” for certain others, defendant cannot show that some such others have arranged to settle with her concerning their respective shares separately. Young v. Patterson, 165 Pa. St. 423; s. c., 30 Atl. Rep. 1011; 36 W. N. C. 100.

1 Holland v. Holland, L. R. 4 Ch. App. 449.

2 Holland v. Holland, L. R. 4 Ch. App. 449. “There can be but few cases of conveyances in trust, other than by deed. If the doctrine contended for by the plaintiff were correct, a debt by specialty would arise in every instance of a breach of trust, in one taking under a deed. Nothing is clearer, upon authority, than that this position cannot be sustained. A breach of trust, generally speaking, creates a debt by simple contract only.” Benbury v. Benbury, 2 Dev. & Bat. Eq. 235, 239. “I am of the same opinion. I am not at all inclined to depart from the cases which have been decided on the 42d section of the statute, and those cases dispose of the question as to the rents. With respect to the general personal estate, I am of the opinion that it would be most dangerous to hold that a demand against the assets of a deceased trustee, or personal representative, in respect of a breach of trust or misappropriation, committed by him, is barred at the expiration of six years from his death. Courts of equity in dealing with equitable debts are not bound by the statute of limitations, 21 Jac. 1, ch. 16, and although they have in many instances adopted a rule grounded on an analogy to that statute, they do not extend that analogy to demands arising out of breaches of trust. The appeal must be dismissed with costs.” Obee v. Bishop, 1 DeG., F. & J. 137, 142. A deed appointing new trustees recited, that they had agreed to become trustees, and then assigned the trust premises to them to hold on the trusts of the original deed. There was no expressed agreement or declaration that they would execute the trusts. It was held, that a debt created by a breach of trust was not a specialty debt. Wynch v. Grant, 2 Drew. 312.

### --- Page 0402 ---

§ 539. Limitation of the Rule.—The liability of the trustee may be in a degree modified by the terms of the instrument creating the trust. Where the instrument fixes the liability of the trustee, the court will make that provision the measure of his liability. But limitations of this character are not favored in equity and the language of the instrument will be rigidly construed. Such limitations will be upheld only as the language is explicit and as they are in harmony with the general object and purpose of the trust. In Tuttle v. Gilmore, Mr. Justice Magie, in his opinion, said: “In my judgment it is clear, both from principle and authority, that the liability imposed on and accepted by a trustee may be limited by the terms of the instrument creating the trust. If there is such a clause of limitation the rule for measuring the trustee’s liability is to be sought in that clause properly construed. In construing such a clause, the meaning to be attributed to it should be consistent with the purpose and object of the trust, and a strict rule of construction should be applied as against the claim of restriction.

But if, when so construed, a limitation on the liability of the trustees was clearly intended, the trustee is entitled to the benefit of it.”

Tuttle v. Gilmore, 36 N. J. Eq. 617, 622. “It would seem on principle unreasonable to contend that parties creating a trust could not, by their agreement, limit the liability which is thus imposed by one and accepted by the other of them. Nor do I think that such a proposition has ever been distinctly enunciated and decided. On the contrary, in Bartlett v. Hodgson, 1 T. R. 42, the usual indemnity clause in a trust deed was held to take away the responsibility to which the trustees but for that clause would be subject. In Wilkins v. Hogg, 3 Giff. 116 (a case arising on trusts created by will), counsel urged that a clause restricting the liability of trustees was of no force, but Vice Chancellor Stuart said on that point: ‘The argument has proceeded on the assumption that the usual indemnity clause amounts to nothing; that it never receives a literal interpretation, but that the court will look generally at the conduct of the trustees, and for any carelessness or any act that a prudent man ought not to have committed, will visit the trustee who has been guilty of such acts, whatever may be the language of the will. That is not the law of this court.’ In the same case on appeal (8 Jur. [Ch. N. S.] 25), Lord Westbury said, ‘he should have been glad to find a case warranting the conclusion that, a duty having been under—

<br>

### --- Page 0403 ---

§ 540. Liability in Breach of Trust.—The term “breach of trust,”—using the words in their ordinary sense,—suggests a fraudulent and, at least, quasi-criminal act. But the technical meaning, as here employed, is much broader and more comprehensive. Not only all fraudulent and wrongful acts, but also every violation of duty, and every neglect of duty, as it is imposed upon the trustee by equity, is a breach of trust. Even in a case where his failure to discharge his duty is the result of forgetfulness or oversight, and there is no suggestion of fraud, or of any criminal intention, his course is designated by this term. Having accepted the trust, he must execute it; he must discharge the duties of the trust with diligence and care, and in every act there must be undoubted good faith and honesty of intention. And any failure to meet these obligations, whether of a positive or negative character, will be treated in equity as a breach of trust. A trustee will not be held free from liability for any loss to the trust estate that is attributable to any lack of diligence and care, or of good faith, and of a sound discretion, on his part. To this extent he will be held responsible in equity, and he will not be released from this obligation even where the instrument exempts him from liability except for deliberate breaches of trust. In a recent case in New Jersey it was held that where a clause in an instrument creating a trust exempts the trustee from liability, except for willful and intentional breaches of trust, the trustee is not exempt from liability for losses arising from his having made sales or investments without instituting proper inquiries and exercising a reasonable judgment in respect to the value of the consideration or securities re-

taken, any words qualifying that holding that limitations on the duty should be nugatory, but such liability of assignees in assignments for benefit of creditors are effective: Litchfield v. White, 7 N. Y. 438; Olmstead v. Herrick, 1 E. D. Smith, 310; Hutchinson v. Lord, 1 Wis. 286; McIntire v. Benson, 20 Ill. 500; Robinson v. Nye, 21 Ill. 592." Ibid. 621. 

It was perfectly competent for a testator to define what should be the incidents to the duty of a trustee, so long as he kept within the bounds of the law. In accord with this view are the American cases cited.

<br>

### --- Page 0404 ---

1228

LIABILITIES OF TRUSTEES.

[§ 540.

ceived, nor for losses arising from investments of trust funds in second mortgages, where no circumstances are shown to justify a resort to such hazardous securities. The fact that the trustee neither made, nor intended to make, any personal gain from his acts, does not exonerate him from liability under that clause.?

The rule, as above stated, is not limited in its operation to trustees under express trusts.

Ch. 135; s. c., 8 Am. Dec. 561; Cromie v. Bull, 81 Ky. 646; Mattocks v. Moulton, 84 Me. 545; Chase v. Lockerman, 11 Gill & J. 185; s. c., 35 Am. Dec. 277; Stewart v. Kissam, 11 Barb. 271; Crabb v. Young, 92 N. Y.56; Denton v. Sanford, 103 N. Y. 607; Hart v. Ten Eyck, 2 Johns. Ch. 76; Perrine v. Vreeland, 53 N. J. Eq. 102, 596. Where a trustee obtained from the beneficiary a release of his contingent remainder in the trust fund, for an inadequate consideration, and concealed from him a material fact, as, for example, that part of the funds had been used in the purchase of land which had been conveyed to the trustee’s wife, such release would be invalid for the suppression of this fact, though the beneficiary is of full age, and employed counsel to examine the records, to determine the value of his interest. Waldrop v. Leaman, 30 S. Car. 428. See also Deegan v.

It includes all persons who have become trustees.

Capner, 44 N. J. Eq. 339; McCook v. Harp, 81 Ga. 229. Where a trustee has bought for the trust estate a mortgage owned by himself on property at the time worth much less than the amount of the mortgage, his liability for the amount of such investment is not affected by the fact that on a sale of the property under the mortgage it is bid in by order of the beneficiaries, and a conveyance of it made to one of them, when their suit is based altogether upon the false representations of the trustee as to the value of the property, and, on learning the real value of the property, they promptly demand that he shall take back the same and account for the amount invested in the mortgage. Nichols’ Appeal, 157 Mass. 20. Good faith will not shield trustee from liability for imprudence and inexcusable neglect. Bogart v. Van Velsor, 4 Edw. Ch. 718. A majority of the trustees in one religious corporation were also trustees of another. Acting as such trustees, they conveyed certain real estate from one corporation to the other without the payment of any price, but for the sole purpose of affording pecuniary aid gratuitously. It was held that these facts alone established the fraudulent character of the transaction. St. James’ Church v. Church of the Redeemer, 45 Barb. 356.

### --- Page 0405 ---

§ 541.] LIABILITIES OF TRUSTEES. 1229

by operation of law, or who by their own acts have placed themselves in a fiduciary relation.

§ 541. Liability as Co-trustee.—Where two or more trustees have concurred in a breach of trust they incur a joint and common liability. And where the act is deliberate, where there is intentional misappropriation of trust funds, or gross and criminal neglect in the management of the trust estate, the beneficiary may, at his election, bring a suit against one or more of the number. As, in such a case, there is no right of contribution among the trustees, he may sue one or more without including the others. Each trustee is liable for the entire loss sustained, or for the whole sum that may be due, and a decree against two or more jointly is enforceable against any one of the number. In the leading case of The Attorney-General v. Wilson, Lord Chancellor Cottenham expounded the rule by which this point is governed in the following vigorous and impressive language: "It was then urged that, if that be so, all the governing body, at least all who took any part in these transactions, ought to be co-defendants. Upon this point, also, Lord Hardwicke’s authority in the Charitable Corporation Case is of the highest value. It was urged that, as the injury had arisen from the misconduct of many, each ought to be answerable for so much only as his particularly misconduct had occasioned; but Lord Hardwicke said: ‘If this doctrine should prevail, it is, indeed, laying the ax to the root of the tree. But if, upon inquiry, there should appear to be a supine negligence in all of them, by which a gross complicated loss happens, I will never determine that they are not all guilty; nor will I ever determine that a court of equity cannot lay hold of every breach of trust, let the person guilty of it be either in a private or public capacity.’ In cases of this kind, where the liability arises from the wrongful act of the parties, each is liable for all the consequences, and there is no contribution be-

¹ Rackham v. Siddall, 1 Macn. & G. 248; Hennessey v. Bray, 33 Beav. 607; Pearce v. Pearce, 22 Beav. 96.

<br>

### --- Page 0406 ---

1230

LIABILITIES OF TRUSTEES.

[§ 541.]

tween them, and each case is distinct, depending upon the evidence against each party.

It is, therefore, not necessary to make all parties who may, more or less, have joined in the act complained of; nor would anyone derive any advantage from their being all made defendants, because, as the decree would be general against all found to be guilty of the charge, it might be executed against any of them. It is evident that Lord Hardwicke, in the case of the Charitable Corporation, considered that each defendant would be liable for each transaction in which he had been a party.*

* Attorney-General v. Wilson, 1 Cr. & P. 1, 29. See Monell v. Monell, 5 Johns. Ch. 283; s. c., 9 Am. Dec. 298; Royall v. Mackenzie, 25 Ala. 363; Graham v. Davidson, 2 Dey. & B. Eq. 165; Evans’ Estate, 2 Ashm. 470; McMurray v. Montgomery, 2 Swan, 374; Fox v. Tay, 89 Cal. 339; s. c., 23 Am. St. Rep. 474; Porter v. Moores, 4 Heisk. 25; Hughlett v. Haghlett, 5 Humph. 474; Bruen v. Gillet, 115 N.Y. 10; s.c., 12 Am. St. Rep. 764; Bone v. Cooke, McClel. 168; Langford v. Gascoyne, 11 Ves. 333; Williams v. Nixon, 2 Beav. 472; Joy v. Campbell, 1 Sch. & Lef. 341; Rodbard v. Cooke, 36 L. T. (N.S.) 504; s. c., 25 W. R. 555; Johnson v. Johnson, 2 Hill Eq. (S. Car.) 277; McKim v. Aulbach, 130 Mass. 484; s. c., 39 Am. Rep. 473; Deaderick v. Cantrell, 10 Yerg. 263; s. c., 31 Am. Dec. 578. The rule that where trustees unite in the execution of a power of sale and one receives the money all will be liable, does not govern where the one receiving was allowed to hold the money for twenty years with the consent of the cestui que trust who was sui juris and the other trustee received no benefit. Laurel County Court v. Trustees, etc. (1892), 93 Ky. 379; s. c., 20 S. W. Rep. 258.

Treating this, therefore, as an action at law, it follows also that the objection taken that other trustees should have been joined as defendants cannot prevail. In actions ex delicto, the plaintiff may sue one, some or all of the wrong-doers.** Hun v. Cary, 82 N. Y. 65, 79; s. c., 37 Am. Rep. 546. Where one pays money to a trustee, whom he knows to be on the verge of insolvency, with the expectation that he will appropriate it for his own use, he cannot, on being appointed trustee in the other's place, recover the lost funds from his co-trustee, but is liable therefor himself. Darnaby v. Watts (1894) (Ky.), 28 S. W. Rep. 338.

Persons who take assignments of mortgages from one described therein, and in the assignment, as trustee of a certain estate, without the concurrence of the co-trustee, are liable to the estate for the full value of the mortgages. Cumming v. Landed Banking & Loan Co., 22 Can. S. C. Rep. 246. The trustees of an estate, though they continue the business, involving additional liabilities, do not sustain the co-partnership relation; and a retiring trustee is not liable for purchases in the course of the business, made after his retirement by the other trustees, though the purchases be from one with whom the firm had

<br>

### --- Page 0407 ---

§ 542. The Right of Contribution.—Where trustees stand convicted of a breach of trust the right of contribution is determined by the character of the offense. Where the breach of trust consists simply of a failure to discharge some duty incumbent upon a trustee, or of some positive act in which there is no evidence of fraud, or of any lack of good faith, the right of contribution will be upheld by the court. If one of the trustees has paid the sum for which they are all equally liable, he will have a valid claim upon all the others for contribution. In a case of this nature the suit of the beneficiary for the recovery of the loss sustained should be brought against all the trustees who have incurred the liability, though judgment for contribution will not be rendered on the suit of a beneficiary.

The leading case is that of Lingard v. Bromley.

In that case it was held that a contribution could be enforced previously dealt, and who had no notice of the discharge of the trustee. Noyes v. Turnbull, 7 N.Y. Supl. 114; affirmed 136 N.Y. 639.

A double responsibility rests upon a trustee in dealing with one whom he knows to be also a trustee, as any dealings between them in wrong of the latter’s trust affects both with an equitable responsibility. Heath v. Webster, 40 Mich. 457. One trustee is not exempted from liability by the fact that the duties of the trustees have been exclusively performed by his co-trustee with his concurrence and consent. He is accountable for the management of the co-trustee, to whom he has thought proper to delegate his power, to the same extent as if they had been executed by himself. Macubbin v. Cromwell, 7 Gill & J. 157. The trustee who receives any of the proceeds of a transaction must personally see to their application. He may not turn them over to his co-trustee for investment or distribution and in that way escape responsibility. Fox v. Tay, 89 Cal. 339; 8 S.C., 23 Am. St. Rep. 474. Where trustees divide the amount of the trust fund between them they are both responsible for the whole amount, and not each for the amount received by him only. Thomas v. Scruggs, 10 Yerg. 400. “Where there are several trustees who unite in a breach of trust, they are equally liable to the cestuis que trust, and the latter in seeking relief against the breach of trust may proceed against all or either of the trustees.” Gilchrist v. Stevenson, 9 Barb. 9, 16. See also Bailey v. Inglee, 2 Paige, 279; s.c., 4 Paige, 23; Wilson v. Moore, 1 Myl. & K. 126. One of several trustees cannot remain passive, if he knows of irregular conduct on the part of his associates without rendering himself liable for such irregularities. In re Niles, 113 N.Y. 547. See also Rehden v. Wesley, 29 Beay. 213; Ashurst v. Mason, L.R. 20 Eq. 225.
