PAYMENT OF DEBTS AND LEGACIES.

[§ 589.

efit the trust property, the courts will consider his conduct not excusable, but in many instances laudable.

Sanders’ Uses and Trusts, 431. ‘Administrators had authority to compromise a claim or compound a debt before the statute was enacted. The common law recognized the power, but attended it with the peril to the administrator of being charged with the amount released to the debtor, if through his bad faith or negligence he permitted the estate to be prejudiced; and the burden was upon him to show that he had acted judiciously. But in the absence of collusion between the administrator and the debtor, or of fraud on the part of the latter—such as would vitiate the contract, the compromise or compounding was binding upon each of the parties to it if executed upon a sufficient consideration, just as it would be if neither party was administrator. The statute, says Parker, C. J., in Wyman’s Appeal, 13 N.H. 18, in speaking of a provision similar to ours, has provided a mode in which the administrator, by obtaining a previous authority from the judge’ (the court under the Arkansas statute), ‘may compromise with the debtor with perfect safety, and without being subject to expense in sustaining his acts. But the right to compromise which existed prior to the statute is not taken away. It may still be exercised as before, subject to the same limitations and risk.’ And this is the effect given to such legislation in other jurisdictions.” Wickes v. Slaughter, 49 Ark. 235, 237. See also Chase v. Bradley, 26 Me. 531; Boyd’s Sureties v. Oglesby, 23 Gratt. 674, 686, 687; Bean v. Farnam, 6 Pick. (Mass.) 268; Chouteau v. Suydam, 21 N.Y. 179; Wood v. Tunnicliffe, 74 N.Y. 38; Childs v. Updyke, 9 Ohio St. 333; Moore v. O’Brannon, 14 Ohio St. 177; Chadbourn v. Chadbourn, 9 Allen, 173; Woolforke v. Sullivan, 23 Ala. 548.

‘In Merritt v. Merritt, 62 Mo. 150, it was said: “The prevailing rule now established in this court is, that executors and administrators stand in the position of trustees to those interested in the estates upon which they administer, and are liable only for want of due care and skill, and that the measure of care and skill required of them is that which prudent men exercise in the direction and management of their own affairs.” Applying this rule to the compromise made by the executors in this case, and on principle, it cannot be seen why it should not be applied, it abundantly appears from evidence, that the executors, in making this compromise, acted diligently, in good faith, with all the prudence, care and skill that a prudent man, with the light then obtainable in regard to the value of the security, the situation of the debtor’s affairs, and the relation they sustained to the obligation which the estate held against them, could have exercised, in a manner which then seemed to be for the best interest of the estate. And even if it could have been shown, which, however, was not done, that the executors were mistaken and that they might have made the whole of the debt compromised, yet under such circumstances they ought not to be held liable for more than the amount which they actually real-

<br>

### --- Page 0521 ---

§ 589.

PAYMENT OF DEBTS AND LEGACIES.

of the States the powers of trustees in this direction are modified or determined by statute. Jacobs v. Jacobs, 99 Mo. 427, 435. How. St., § 5852, makes it the duty of a special administrator to collect the goods and chattels of the decedent, and preserve the same for the administrator who may afterwards be appointed. Held, that where there is no property belonging to an estate except a claim for damages in litigation at the decedent’s death, and no money to meet the expenses of the suit, the administrator is not obliged to carry on the suit, but may compromise it. Grece v. Helin (1892), 91 Mich. 450; s.c., 51 N. W. Rep. 1106.

Maynard v. Cleveland, 76 Ga. 52; Clarke v. Cordis, 86 Mass. 466. R. S. 1881 (Indiana), § 2299; R. S. 1894, § 2454, is as follows: “Every executor and administrator shall proceed with diligence to collect the debts and demands due the estate of the deceased. Where the interest of the estate may be promoted thereby, the court, or judge thereof in vacation, may order the executor or administrator to compound debts, and to buy in property, either personal or real, at sales under execution in favor of the estate.” By L. 1888, ch. 571, amending L. 1847, ch. 80, § 1, executors and administrators may be authorized by the surrogate of the county where they are appointed, for good cause shown and on such terms as the surrogate approves, to compromise any claim or to sell any uncollectible, stale, or doubtful debts or claims at public sale on such notice as the surrogate approves. § 2 of the Act of 1874 declares: “Nothing in this act contained shall prevent any party, interested in the final settlement of said estate, from showing, on the final settlement of the accounts of said executor or administrator, that such debt or claim was fraudulently or negligently compromised or compounded.” This act is not restrictive of common law power, but is intended to afford executors and administrators additional protection in its exercise. Chouteau v. Suydam, 21 N.Y. 179. See also In re Scott, 5 N.Y. Leg. Obs. 378. The surrogate has power, on cause shown, to authorize an executor or administrator to compound claims due to the estate, whether the debtor be solvent or insolvent. Berrien’s Estate, 16 Abb. Pr. (N. S.) 23. But see also Howell v. Blodgett, 1 Redf. 323. An executor has power, and it is his duty when it is for the interest of the estate, to compound a debt due to it, and he can enforce a note taken by him as the result of such compromise in an action upon it, the prior indebtedness being merged therein. Leland v. Manning, 4 Hun, 7. An application for leave to compromise a claim must fully inform the surrogate as to all the facts and circumstances warranting the acceptance of a sum less than the face of the claim. If insolvency exists it should be stated. So if the reason is a doubt as to success in prosecuting the claim. All the evidence the applicant possesses should be stated, and the consent of the parties in interest obtained so far as possible. Whether the attorney has any interest, and what, should appear. An ex parte application for leave to compromise a claim due to the estate will

<br>

### --- Page 0522 ---

§ 590. Claims of Creditors for Interest.—The creation of a trust by deed for the payment of simple contract debts, or of any debts which do not draw interest, will not of itself so change their character as to entitle the holder of the claim to interest in the future. It was formerly held that "if a man in his life creates a trust for payment of debts, annexes a schedule of some debts, and creates a trust term for the payment, as that is in the nature of a specialty, that will make them, though simple contract debts, carry interest." But it is now well settled in England that a trust by deed for the payment of debts, though they are scheduled, will not make them bear interest which they would not otherwise bear. And where there are any specialty debts to which such a direction can properly be held to apply, it is immaterial that the direction of the instrument is that the debts be paid with interest. Of course this rule will not apply where there is an express direction not be granted where no facts are stated beyond the existence and status of the debt and its nature, and that one of the executors and the attorney in the suit believe the compromise to be advantageous. The same evidence should be produced as would be required to justify the compromise if attacked on an accounting. In re Richardson, 9 N. Y. Supl. 638.

¹ Stewart v. Noble, Vern. & Serv. 528; Tait v. Northwick, 4 Ves. 618; Carr v. Burlington, 1 P. Wins. 229; Bottomley v. Fairfax, 1 P. Wms. 334; Maxwell v. Wettenhall, 2 P. Wins. 26. Where the question of interest is not reserved by the decree, it cannot be given on petition; the object of a petition being only to carry on what is directed by the decree. Interest not given from the confirmation of the report on demands liquidated by it, but not bearing interest in their nature, as legacies and arrears of annuities, though both legacies and annuities were charged upon land, and the annuities were not paid out of the rents and profits, as possession was taken by mortgagees, and though one of the annuities was the provision of a widow. Interest is computed by the master’s report, upon such debts only as carry interest according to the rate they carry, and upon farther directions subsequent interest is directed only on those upon which the report has already computed interest; but no interest is computed on simple contract debts by the report or order afterwards. Creuze v. Hunter, 2 Ves. Jr., 157.

² Shirley v. Ferrers, 1 Bro. Ch. 41; Hamilton v. Houghton, 2 Bligh, 169.

³ Hamilton v. Houghton, 2 Bligh, 169, 187; Tait v. Northwick, 4 Ves. 618.

<br>

### --- Page 0523 ---

§ 591. PAYMENT OF DEBTS AND LEGACIES. 1347  
in the instrument to pay interest, or where the contract provides for the payment of principal and interest. But it has been held that, in the case of bond debts, the creditors will not be entitled to receive more for both principal and interest than is provided for in the bond.2 But where a fund has come into the possession of trustees, under the provisions of a trust for the payment of debts, and, instead of making immediate payment of the debts, the trustees have invested the fund in securities bearing interest, and there has been an accumulation of interest, the creditors, though their claims may be under simple contracts, will be entitled to interest on them.*

§ 591. The Mode of Raising Money.—The creation of a trust for the payment of debts should indicate the method of raising the money for the purposes of the trust. Where the property from which money is to be raised for the payment of debts consists, either wholly or in part, of realty, the instrument should direct either the sale or the mort-

1 Hamilton v. Houghton, 2 Bligh, 169, 184; Bath v. Bradford, 2 Ves. 588; Stewart v. Noble, Vern. & Scriv. 536.

2 Burke v. Jones, 2 Ves. & B. 284; Hughes v. Wynne, 1 Myl. & K. 20; Anon., 1 Salk. 154.

3 Pearce v. Slocombe, 3 Y. & C. 84. By the term of a deed of trust executed by two partners for the benefit of certain persons, some of whom were the joint creditors of the firm, and others the separate creditors of one of the partners, it was declared that the joint creditors should be paid within a year after the execution of the deed, and that the surplus of the joint estate, after payment of the joint creditors, should be applied for the benefit of the separate creditors. Joint estate, sufficient to pay the joint creditors, was got within the year, but in consequence of difficulties in relation to the separate creditors, the trustees made no distribution of any of the funds, but caused a suit to be instituted for carrying into execution the deed of trust, and in the meantime invested at interest both the joint and separate estate. Upon the court, some years afterwards, decreeing payment to the various creditors: Held, that the joint creditors (although their debts did not in their nature carry interest) were entitled to be paid, out of the joint estate, interest at £4 per cent. upon their respective debts, from the time appointed by the deed for payment of the principal moneys due to them; and that, until the joint creditors had received satisfaction both of their principal and interest, the separate creditors were not entitled to receive any payment whatever out of the joint estate. Pearce v. Slocombe, 3 Y. & C. 84.

<br>

### --- Page 0524 ---

1348 PAYMENT OF DEBTS AND LEGACIES. [§ 591.]

gaging of the estate by the trustees for that purpose. But it is well established that, in the absence of such direction, the power to sell, or to mortgage, is implied in the conveyance of an estate for the purpose of the payment of debts. The primary object of the creation of a trust of this character is the raising of money for this purpose. The debts cannot be paid without money, and the money cannot be raised without the sale or the mortgaging of the trust property. The creation of the trust, then, without any express direction of this character, implies the power to sell, or to mortgage, the trust estate for this purpose.

Gould v. Lamb, 11 Met. 84; Walker v. Brungard, 13 Sm. & M. 723. “Was the trustee, P MS, authorized by law to sell the property conveyed by the deed of trust for the purpose of paying the debts due from RP, there being in the deed no express power to do so? We think he was; the property was conveyed for the payment of the debts; this was the primary object of the conveyance. This object could only be attained by a sale of the property. A right to sell, then, is implied, as the trust would fail without it—a right to sell necessarily implies a right to convey, for without it no sale could be made, and the right to sell is co-extensive with the property conveyed, if the sale of the whole of it be necessary for the payment of the debts intended to be secured.” Williams v. Otey, 8 Humph. 563, 568. Where land was conveyed to a trustee, to sell and pay certain debts, and pay the balance to one of the creditors, and the trustee sold such title as was vested in him without warranty, it was held that the trustee was not liable to the purchaser for any defect in the title to any part of the premises, and that the proceeds in his hands

The power ought not to be refunded to the purchaser. Sutton v. Sutton, 7 Gratt. 234. Where a testator, by his will, left all his real estate to trustees with power to sell and to pay certain specific debts, and in the same will devised all his estate to his children, it was held that, after the debts had been satisfied, the trustees had no power to sell, and any conveyance by them thereafter was void. Murdock v. Johnson, 7 Coldw. 605. “This is not to be regarded as a mere naked power. On the contrary, the estate is devised to G P, in fee, but upon certain trusts specified in the will, and the question is whether it is within the scope of those trusts to alienate any of the real estate. This is not a mere trust to hold for the benefit of the cestui que trust, or to permit her to occupy or dispose of otherwise; but it is an active trust, to hold the estate and apply the income and principal in certain modes stated. Among other things, all that part of his estate formerly given to his daughter E, the plaintiff, he now gives to his son in trust, to invest and reinvest in stocks or safe securities, and the income thereof, with $200 of the principal, to pay annually.

<br>

### --- Page 0525 ---

§ 591. PAYMENT OF DEBTS AND LEGACIES. 1349  
to sell, of course, implies the power to convey. The deed of the trustee will be a good title. In a case where a father conveyed land to his son L, his heirs and assigns, upon condition that the land be charged with one-half of the debts which the grantor might owe at his decease, and upon trust to apply the rents and profits to the use and benefit of the grantor’s other son B for life, and upon B’s decease to convey the land to B’s surviving children, or, if he should leave no children, to his heirs-at-law, it was held that a conveyance, made by L after a sale by auction, without license or order of court, but purporting to be made for the purpose of paying said debts, passed a good title, although the price obtained was affected by the doubts entertained of L’s right to sell, and that the purchaser was not bound to see to the application of the purchase money.¹ In his opinion in this case, Chief Justice Shaw said: “In order to execute his trusts and save the estate from forfeiture, for the benefit of his cestui que trust, he must raise the money for the payment of such debts, and this can only be done by a sale; therefore, he must sell. It is part of his duty as trustee, and, of course, within the scope of his fiduciary authority. Take the familiar case of a conveyance of real and personal property to trustees for the payment of the settlor’s debts; of necessity, the estate must be sold to raise money for such payment, the estate is vested in the trustees by the deed of an owner, it is within the scope of their fiduciary authority to sell; therefore, they have no  

Now, this carries an implication strictly necessary, that the real estate may be sold, because in no other mode can it be invested in stocks. Besides, suppose there is not personal estate enough to pay over $200 annually in money, still it is to be paid in money; but the trustee can be enabled to pay money only by the means of a sale. This shows, then, that he has a power to sell, and having such power there is nothing to limit it, but that sound judgment and discretion and that good faith, which are the foundations upon which such trusts are created.” Purdie v. Whitney, 20 Pick. 25, 27. As to power to mortgage, see McBrayer v. Cariker, 64 Ala. 50; Dibrell v. Carlisle, 51 Miss. 785; Rogers v. Rogers, 111 N. Y. 228; Weeks v. Cornwell, 104 N. Y. 325; Taylor v. Franklin Sav. Bank, 50 Fed. Rep. 289; Pike v. Baldwin, 68 Iowa, 263; Miller v. Redwine, 75 Ga. 130; Gilbert v. Gilbert, 39 Iowa, 657.  

¹ Goodrich v. Proctor, 1 Gray, 567.

<br>

### --- Page 0526 ---

1350 PAYMENT OF DEBTS AND LEGACIES. [§ 591.

occasion to obtain license to sell, their own deed transfers a good title to the purchaser. In Missouri it has been held that an unsealed instrument conveying land in trust to secure the payment of a debt is not sufficient per se to authorize a sale and conveyance of the land by the trustee, and that an unsealed instrument conveying land in trust to secure the payment of a debt creates an equitable lien upon the land which can be enforced by a court of equity. In England it has been held that where property is conveyed to trustees for the payment of debts in general, they are able to make a good title to a purchaser or mortgagee. They are not bound to make any investigation in regard to the legality or propriety of the sale, or to inquire whether there are unpaid debts that are a charge upon the property, and they are not required to see to the application of the purchase money. If there is any misappropriation of the trust funds the claim of the creditors is against the trustees.

1 Goodrich v. Proctor, 1 Gray, 567, 570. Land is conveyed in trust first, that the debts of the grantor shall be paid out of the rents and profits; second, for the support of the grantor, his wife and children, and third, at his death to be divided among his children. Held, the trustees have no authority to make sales of the land conveyed, for the payments of the debts of the grantor, or for any other purpose, however urgent the necessity for such sales. Mundey v. Vawter, 3 Gratt. 518.

2 Sappington v. Boly, 12 Mo. 567. An unsealed instrument of writing conveying land in trust to secure the payment of a debt is not sufficient, per se, to authorize a sale and conveyance of the land by the trustee. An unsealed instrument of writing conveying land in trust to secure the payment of a debt creates an equitable lien upon the land, which can be enforced by a court of equity. Ibid. But where a trustee, for the payment of debts, sells without authority, but in his capacity of trustee, and in the presence and with the acquiescence of the cestui que trust, the purchaser will take a good title in equity. Spencer v. Hawkins, 4 Ired. Eq. 288.

Spear, 1 Yeates, 553; Cadbury v. Duval, 10 Pa. St. 267; Houser v. Shore, 5 Ired. Eq. 357; Gardner v. Gardner, 3 Mason, 393; Goodrich v. Proctor, 1 Gray, 567; Sims v. Lively, 14 B. Mon. 433; Lining v.

### --- Page 0527 ---

§ 592.] PAYMENT OF DEBTS AND LEGACIES, 1351

§ 592. Payment of Legacies.—The rules governing a trust for the payment of debts are not applicable to the payment of legacies. There is the obvious distinction in the case that debts constitute an obligation that attaches to the estate, while legacies are a voluntary gift. Legacies, whether general or specific, are payable from the personal estate of the decedent, but the debts must first be paid, and where the personal estate is exhausted in the payment of debts the legacies will fail, unless they are made a charge on the realty. Where the grantor makes the legacies a charge on the real estate, the person to whom the estate passes under the will holds it as a trustee for the payment of the legacies. In New York the rule governing the case has been stated, as follows: The real estate is not charged with the payment of legacies, unless the intention of the testator to that effect is expressly declared, or clearly to be inferred from the language and dispositions of the will. The usual clause, devising all the rest of his real and personal estate, not before devised, is not sufficient to show an intention to charge the real estate; nor is the mere direction that all debts and legacies are to be paid. But if the real estate be devised, "after payment of debts and legacies," it is charged with the payment of them. Though the real estate be charged, yet the personal estate is the proper payment of the legacies; but it is said that the debts having been paid and paid out of the personal estate, and nothing remaining but the legacies, the case falls within the general rule applicable to cases where legacies alone are charged upon the real estate. I find no authority for such a proposition. The rule applies to the state of things at the death of the testator, and if the debts are afterwards paid, and the legacies alone are left as a charge, that circumstance does not vary the general rule. Johnson v. Kennett, 3 Myl. & K. 624, 631.

Peyton, 2 Desaus. 378; Coombs v. Jordan, 3 Bland, 284; Wormley v. Wormley, 8 Wheat. 422. The general rule, as to which there is no dispute, is this: Where legacies alone are charged, the purchasers of the real estate are bound to see to the application of the purchase money. Where debts are charged generally, or where debts and legacies are charged generally, the purchasers of the real estate are not bound to see to the application of the purchase money. The real estate being, in this case, charged generally with the payment of debts and legacies, would not, therefore, prima facie, in the hands of a purchaser, be liable to the payment of the legacies; but it is said that the debts having been paid and paid out of the personal estate, and nothing remaining but the legacies, the case falls within the general rule applicable to cases where legacies alone are charged upon the real estate. I find no authority for such a proposition. The rule applies to the state of things at the death of the testator, and if the debts are afterwards paid, and the legacies alone are left as a charge, that circumstance does not vary the general rule. Johnson v. Kennett, 3 Myl. & K. 624, 631.

<br>

[§ 592.

fund for the payment of debts and legacies, and is to be first applied before charging the real estate. If an executor pays one legatee, and there is afterwards a deficiency of assets to pay the others, the legatee so paid must refund a proportionable part. But if the deficiency of assets has been occasioned by the waste of the executor, the legatee who is paid may retain the advantage he has gained by his legal diligence, as against his co-legatees, but not against a creditor. A legatee may compel an executor to bring into court money in his hands, or to give security, where the legacy is payable at a future day.)

1 Lupton v. Lupton, 2 Johns. Ch. 614. See also Trent v. Trent, 1 Dow. 102; Minor v. Wicksteed, 3 Bro. Ch. 627; Miles v. Leigh, 1 Atk. 574; Webb v. Webb, Barn. 86; Dowman v. Rust, 6 Rand. 587; Gridly v. Andrews, 8 Conn. 1; Hoes v. Van Hoesen, 1 Comst. 122; Montgomery v. McElroy, 3 Watts & S. 370; Ripple v. Ripple, 1 Rawle, 386; Wright’s Appeal, 12 Pa. St. 256; Brandt’s Appeal, 8 Watts, 198; Logan v. Deshay, 1 Clarke Ch. 209; Harris v. Fly, 7 Paige, 421; Paxson v. Potts, 2 Green Ch. 191; Simmons v. Drury, 2 Gill & J. 32. Personal property is the primary fund for the payment of legacies, but the will may make them payable out of the proceeds arising from the sale of real estate, or make them a charge upon such real estate. If the personal property is not sufficient to pay the debts and legacy, the latter will fail unless the words of the will indicate a different intention. Duncan v. Wallace, 114 Ind. 169. In equity it is well established that the personal estate is the natural and primary fund for the payment of debts and legacies, even where they are expressly charged upon the real estate, descending or devised; and that the real estate is only to be resorted to as an ancillary fund, after the personalty has been exhausted. The real estate is never charged with the payment of legacies, unless the intention of the testator so to charge it is either expressly declared or fairly and satisfactorily to be inferred from the language of the will. A bequest of $500 to each of the testator’s two grandsons, to be paid by his executor, with a devise of the remainder of his estate, real and personal, to his executor, is not evidence of an intention to charge the real estate in the hands of the devisee with the payment of the legacies. Stevens v. Gregg, 10 Gill & J. 143. If a specific devise of land be made and a general legacy be bequeathed, without charging the legacy upon such land, the legatee may, to charge the land, show that there was no personal property out of which to pay the legacy. The rule is that the land is not to be burdened with the legacy unless it is shown that there was no personal property to satisfy it; for if there be some personal property, the land is not liable. Davidson v. Coon, 125 Ind. 497.

<br>

### --- Page 0529 ---

§ 592. ] PAYMENT OF DEBTS AND LEGACIES. 1353

been held that if a testator devises land in trust, for the benefit of his son W and family, during W’s life, with remainder to W’s wife and sons, adding the following provision: “This devise, charged with this condition, viz.: that the wife and sons of said W shall pay to all the daughters of said W $200 each, as they shall respectively arrive at the age of eighteen years,” the legacies are thereby charged upon the land devised, and, if not paid, the legatees may maintain a bill in equity to compel the sale of so much of the land as may be necessary to raise money for them, and for expenses and costs. Where there are debts and legacies to be paid, and the testator makes of his real and personal estate a single fund for the payment of both debts and legacies, the legacies will become a charge on the realty at the point where the personal estate fails. In such case the devise of the residue is held to refer to what remains after the payment of both debts and legacies. In Tracy v. Tracy it was held that the general rule is that real estate is not to be charged with the payment of legacies, unless the intention of the testator is expressly declared in the will, or clearly to be inferred from the language and disposition of the will. But where the real and personal estate are blended in one devise to the same person, the real estate

Sherman v. Sherman, 4 Allen, 392. A testator devised real estate upon trust for payment of his debts, funeral and testamentary expenses, and subject thereto upon various trusts; and bequeathed legacies and annuities. Held, that a case for marshalling at the instance of a legatee of an annuity where the personal estate had been exhausted in payment of debts. Paterson v. Scott, 1 De G., M. & G. 531. “There being no charge upon the real estate for the payment of debts, and there being an ample fund of personal estate for the payment both of specialty and simple contract debts, I am asked to throw the specialty debts at least upon the real estate, that enough of the personal estate may be left for the payment of legacies, which are not charged upon the real estate; and for the payment of which I am clearly of opinion in this case there is no fund but the surplus of the personal estate, if there shall be any, after payment of all the debts of the testator. I cannot marshal the assets for the payment of the legacies.” Keeling v. Brown, 5 Ves. 359, 362.

becomes chargeable, together with the personal, for both debts and legacies.4

§ 598. The Payment of Legacies Continued.—The rule in regard to the charge of legacies on real estate, under

1 Tracy v. Tracy, 15 Barb. 503.  
Where the personal estate would have been sufficient to pay a certain legacy but for expenses growing out of a contest of the probate and subsequent proceedings on accounting, the legacy was held not charged on the land; and this although the legatee was of kin to the testator and the residuary devisees strangers in blood. Brill v. Wright, 112 N. Y. 129.  
Where, after making specific bequests and giving other legacies of $1,000 each to three persons, two of whom were nephews, the testator gave the residue, both real and personal, to his daughter, and at the time of making the will he had over $30,000 in personal property, some of which he subsequently invested in real estate, but at the time of his death he had not sufficient personal property to pay his debts and funeral expenses, the legacies were held not to be a charge on the real estate. Schnorr v. Schroeder, 45 Hun, 148.  
“It has been contended, that under the first clause of the will: ‘First, I will and direct, that all my legal debts, legacies, and funeral expenses, shall be fully paid, etc.,’ where the testator has charged his real estate by will duly attested, both debts and legacies shall take place of every other disposition, and that the legacies shall stand in the same place as debts, even to the disappointment of the devisees; and that there is no reason why they should not have the same preference.  
The principle, however, is perfectly different; the one being purely voluntary, the other obligatory. Whenever a man makes a will, he is supposed to do that which conscience obliges him to do; and if he shows an intention that his debts shall take place of every other disposition, and that he meant they should be paid, the court will strictly enforce that intention.  
The same principle will not apply to legacies which have been attempted to be put upon the same footing; but it does not follow that where a testator says, ‘In the first place I will, that all my debts and legacies shall be paid;’ and then gives certain legacies; and at the latter end of it repeats these words, that such legacies as well as his debts, shall be a charge upon the realty, and that because the latter must have a preference, the former shall also have it. The estate so contended to be charged is specifically devised; and I cannot see any reason why pecuniary legacies should have any preference to such specific devises. If I was to direct these legacies to be so raised and paid, it would be giving them that undue preference. It has been argued that there are words in this will strong enough to show the testator’s intention, that the legacies in question should be so raised; but I am of opinion no such construction can be put upon them. They will not bear such an inference. * * * No matter whether first or last: I must advert to the general tenor of the instrument to see what was the intention. No

<br>

### --- Page 0531 ---

§ 593. ] PAYMENT OF DEBTS AND LEGACIES. 1355  
all ordinary circumstances, will be determined by the language of the will. Where the provisions of the will are not in violation of any legal principle it will be executed in accordance with the intention of the testator. On this point the rule in Maryland has been stated, as follows: ‘The natural presumption as to every testator, unless the contrary appears on the face of the will, is that all his devises, bequests and directions shall be gratified and conformed to. He assumes himself to be in a condition to make them; none have any preference, except by his intent. He is presumed to know the established principles of construction. In a case of deficiency of assets all specific have a priority in payment over general legacies. All specific legacies, in this respect, stand on the same footing, unless testator otherwise intended. All debts and legacies are payable out of personal estate, and are only chargeable upon real estate by the will, either by its expressed terms, or necessary implication from its language.

In Connecticut it has been doubted this court will aid the intention, where a testator affects to charge an estate, which by law he could not do; and where he means to do it for a valuable or meritorious consideration, it will interpose and even supply a defect, but then there must be clear, express and unequivocal evidence of the intention, that the testator meant to comprehend that estate, and to create that charge upon it, to which the court is called upon to give its effect; as, among other instances, in that of supplying a surrender.” Kightley v. Kightley, 2 Ves. Jr. 328, 330, 332.

Wms. 91; Francis v. Clemow, 1 Kay, 435; Gyett v. Williams, 2 Johns. & H. 429.

Where the testator devised his farm to his son, subject to the life estate of his wife in a part of the...

1356

PAYMENT OF DEBTS AND LEGACIES.

[§ 593.

held that it is a general rule that a will speaks from the death of the testator, and not from its date, unless its language, by a fair construction, indicates the contrary intention.

Where the testatrix gave sundry legacies to her same, and then gave to each of his daughters a legacy of $1,000, to be paid by his son, in six annual payments, from the death of his mother, and made the son his residuary devisee and legatee, it was held that the legacies to the daughters were an equitable charge upon the farm devised; and that a purchaser from the devisee with constructive notice took the land subject thereto. Harris v. Fly, 7 Paige, 421. Where the will contains no specific devise of real estate, but after giving pecuniary legacies, devises and bequeaths the “rest, residue and remainder” of the testator’s real and personal estate, the legacies are charged upon the land. Schulters v. Johnson, 38 Barb. 80. See also Church of Holy Cross v. Wachter, 42 Barb. 43. “A testator devised and bequeathed his residuary estate to his nine children equally. By a codicil he authorized his executors to sell his real estate not specifically devised, and directed that the pecuniary legacies should not be paid over to the life tenant, but upon their decease, respectively, to their issue, they receiving only the income; no provision was made in the will for the payment of the testator’s debts, his personal property was much more than sufficient to pay them, but the residue, with the $113,000 and the $15,000 added, was insufficient to pay the pecuniary legacies. Held, that the intent of the testator was to charge the pecuniary legacies upon the residuary real estate, and to authorize the sale thereof, if necessary, to make the sum required for their payment. Kalbfleisch v. Kalbfleisch, 67 N. Y. 354, 355.

M died leaving a will executed the day previous to his death, by which, after a bequest to his wife of $1,000, and to his son M of $400, he gave the residue of his estate to his four children, to be divided equally between them. The personal estate left by the testator was insufficient to pay his funeral expenses. In an action to have the widow's legacy declared to be a charge upon the real estate: Held, that the intent of the testator was that both legacies should be so chargeable; and that the widow, in case the land was insufficient to pay both legacies, was not entitled to a preference in payment, but simply to share pro rata with the other legatee; and this, although he had not claimed his right, and denied that the legacies were chargeable on the real estate. McCorn v. McCorn, 100 N. Y. 511.

The will of T, after directing the payment of his debts and funeral expenses and after giving a series of legacies, gave the residue of his estate, real and personal, to his wife. Then followed this clause: “And I authorize my executor, after paying my just debts and funeral expenses, to pay over to my wife $5,000 in cash out of the bequeath to her and before any of the other bequeaths are paid off.” The executors were authorized and directed to sell and dispose of all of the real and personal estate,

<br>

### --- Page 0533 ---

§ 594.) PAYMENT OF DEBTS AND LEGACIES. 1357

grandchildren, and devised the residuum of her real estate to her children; and it also appeared that at the making of her will she owned no personal estate, though she became the owner of such estate before her death, it was held that such legacies were payable out of the personal estate. Parol evidence is not admissible to vary the construction of a will where there is no ambiguity created by the application of extraneous circumstances. Where there are a number of legacies, and a part of them are made a charge on the real estate in express terms, and subsequently the testator adds to the will other legacies which are not made a specific charge, it is held that the legacies of the latter class are not to be treated as a charge on the realty. It is held, also, that a testator may direct in his will that a definite portion of his estate shall be exempt from the payment of legacies, where legacies are made a general charge upon the real estate.

§ 594. The Rule Relating to the Surplus.— Where there is a residue of the trust estate, after all the debts and legacies have been satisfied, under a trust for the payment of debts and legacies, the surplus will continue to be vested in the trustee, and for the benefit of the grantor, or of his heirs. Where a surplus remains after the payment of debts and charges under the trust, such surplus will be liable to the claims of creditors who have not assented to the trust.

with power to reserve certain parcels of real estate until prices specified could be obtained therefor. In an action to obtain a construction of the will, held, that the intent of the testator was to charge the payment of the legacies upon the real estate; also that the gift to the wife was in lien of dower. Le Fevre v. Toole, 84 N. Y. 95, 96.

5 Dubose v. Dubose, 7 Ala. 235; Hastings v. Baldwin, 17 Mass. 558; Vernon v. Morton, 8 Dana, 247; Wright v. Henderson, 7 How. (Miss.) 539. The assent of creditors cannot be presumed to an assignment which stipulates for a credit of six months for the bal-

<br>

### --- Page 0534 ---

§ 595. Duty of Bankrupt Trustee Touching His Own Indebtedness.—Where a trustee is personally indebted to the trust estate, and has become a bankrupt, it is his duty to make proof of the debt, and where he neglects this duty, he is liable for the loss, notwithstanding he may have obtained his certificate as a bankrupt. The rule governing a case of this character is stated by Sir John Romilly, M. R., in the following language: "Suppose a person owing money to a trust estate becomes bankrupt, and the trustee is a distinct and separate person; knowing of the bankruptcy, he is bound to prove the debt; if he does not, he commits a breach of trust, and would be held liable for all that he might have received under the commission, if he had proved the debt as he ought to have done. Is the case altered because the trustee is himself the debtor? I think not. The original debt, no doubt, is barred, but the amount of dividends which the trustee might have received under the commission is a liability subsequently attaching to the trustee in that character, and is not affected by the bankruptcy or the certificate."

The trust property under a general assignment may be attached by a dissenting creditor, if not wanted to satisfy the claims of those creditors who had become parties to the assignment prior to such attachment; the trustee process, though the usual, not being the only remedy in such cases. Todd v. Bucknam, 2 Fairfield, 41. Any execution creditor, independent of the statute right of payment, may compel creditors, secured by mortgage or deed of trust, to close the trust by sale and distribute the surplus, if any will be the probable result of a sale; and without such probability, upon indemnifying the other party against the costs of suit and sale. Dubose v. Dubose, 7 Ala. 235.

Orrett v. Corser, 21 Beay. 52, 57. See also Miles v. Bacon, 4 J. J. Marsh. 468; Rathbun v. Platner, 18 Barb. 272. Where one accepts a trust, by which a debtor devotes all his property to the payment of his creditors, the trustee thereby waives any specific lien he may have on the property by virtue of execution, and must take according to the stipulations of the deed of trust. Harrison v. Mock, 10 Ala. 185.

CHAPTER XXIX.

TRUSTS UNDER ASSIGNMENTS FOR CREDITORS.

§ 596. Introductory.—An assignment for the benefit of creditors is a conveyance by a debtor of his property to trustees in trust for the payment of debts. Where the debtor is solvent he may assign the whole or a part of his property, and he may provide for the sale of the property, and for the use of the proceeds in the discharge of his obligations; or the trustee may be only empowered to appropriate the income of the estate to that purpose. The trust may provide for the payment of all or of a part of his debts, and it may make provision for the full payment of all his creditors, or for a proportion or percentage of the whole sum due. Assignments for the benefit of creditors came into use under the common law, but with the advancement of legal science a system of rules for their administration

<br>

### --- Page 0536 ---

1360 ASSIGNMENTS FOR CREDITORS. [§ 596.

has grown up under decisions in equity, and both in England and in the United States the creation and execution of trusts of this class are to a great extent regulated by statute. But statutory rules relate to the manner in which the power or right to create such a trust shall be exercised. They recognize the right of a debtor to create a trust, or to make an assignment of his property for the benefit of his creditors. In prescribing the mode of procedure, the design is to provide against the abuse of the right to create such a trust. Such assignments are purely voluntary on the part of the debtor. They are of the nature of a contract, and, like other contracts, they are binding only as they receive the assent of the parties concerned. An assignment for the benefit of creditors implies the actual transfer of the property to an assignee, or trustee, so that the assignor is devested of all control over it. The appointment of an agent, with power of attorney, or the employment of an attorney to make collections and to apply the proceeds to the payment of debts, is not an assignment for the benefit of creditors. Where a general assignment is made, and it includes a stipulation for the release of the debtor by all the creditors, it must include all the property of the debtor not exempt from execution, and, as a rule, this is the only kind of an assignment that can be made by an insolvent debtor. Where an assignment purports to be general it must be so in fact in order to be sustained. An

Crookshank, 1 Phila. 193; Bishop v. Hart, 28 Vt. 71; Mussey v. Noyes, 26 Vt. 462; U.S. v. Mott, 1 Paine, 188, 195. An assignment for the benefit of creditors is void if it does not include the assignor’s real estate. An assignment was made of all one’s ‘goods, chattels, stocks, promissory notes, debts, choses in action, evidences of debt, claims, property and effects of every description, * * * wherever the same may be situated, to-wit: all the goods, chattels and property now in the store lately

§ 597.

ASSIGNMENTS FOR CREDITORS.

An assignment that purports to be a conveyance of a part only of the property of a debtor is a partial assignment. So an assignment of an interest in a partnership by one who has separate property is a partial assignment, and, as such, fraudulent and void.

§ 597. Formalities Required.—The general rule that in the creation of an express trust no particular form of

occupied by the assignor; also certain other specified chattels and debts. Held, that this did not include real estate. Price v. Haynes, 37 Mich. 487.

Mussey v. Noyes, 26 Vt. 462. An assignment by partners of partnership effects for the benefit of creditors, contained a condition that the creditors should execute by a day certain, a release of their claims, to the assignors individually and as copartners. Both the partners had separate property. Held, that the assignment was fraudulent and void. Thomas v. Jenks, 5 Rawle, 221. One partner, after the dissolution of a copartnership, has no power to make new contracts, or to create new liabilities to bind the firm, without some special authority to do so. Such authority may be inferred from all the circumstances of the case. A valid assignment of all the partnership estate, for the benefit of creditors of the firm, would, ipso facto, be a dissolution. But an assignment, void for illegality, does not work such dissolution. An assignment for the benefit of creditors, wherein the substantial requirements of the statute are not complied with, is void. Simmons v. Curtis, 41 Me. 373.

"By the terms of the statute, the assignment must embrace all the debtor’s property. In other words, the assignment must be a general one, and the question arises whether the unintentional omission of a trifling portion of the debtor’s property from the assignment vitiates it? We think not, but are of opinion that such an assignment embraces all of the debtor’s property, within the meaning of the law. To hold that the omission of any portion of the debtor’s property, however trifling, from the assignment, vitiates it, is placing such a construction upon the statute as tends largely to defeat its purposes. A substantial compliance with the statute is all that is required, and if a general assignment is intended to be made, and it embraces substantially all of the debtor’s property, it cannot be deemed fraudulent and void because some trifling portion of it has been omitted by inadvertence." Krug v. McGilliard, 76 Ind. 28, 30.

Where it is apparent from the whole scope and tenor of a deed of assignment, that the debtor, in good faith, intended to resort to the assignment law, and has made an assignment of all his property for the benefit of all his creditors, but has inserted in the deed stipulations which are constructively invalid, such assignment, if not actually fraudulent, will stand, while the invalid provisions will be controlled by the statute governing assignments. Henderson v. Pierce, 108 Ind. 462, 470.

<br>

1362 ASSIGNMENTS FOR CREDITORS.

[§ 597.]

Words is necessary is applicable to the creation of a trust by assignment for the benefit of creditors. But whatever may be the form of words employed, the transaction must amount to an absolute conveyance of the property of the debtor to trustees in trust for the benefit of the creditors. In Pennsylvania it has been held that no particular form of words is necessary to constitute an assignment for the benefit of creditors under the statute, but the transaction must be in substance an absolute transfer of property of the assignor in trust for the benefit of his creditors. A mortgage executed by the assignor to a trustee for creditors, in consideration of an extension of time for the payment of the assignor’s debts, being a mere security, cannot be treated as an assignment for the benefit of said creditors under the statute. But, in general, it may be said that—

Lorenz, 45 N. Y. 51; S. C., 3 Daly, 23; Kercheis v. Schloss, 49 How. Pr. 284. See also as to verbal assignment of personalty: Lockwood v. Canfield, 20 Ill. 126; Newell v. Hill, 2 Mete. 180; Boyden v. Moore, 11 Pick. 362; Loftin v. Lyon, 22 Ala. 540; Higginbottom v. Peyton, 3 Rich. Eq. 398; Brown v. Chamberlain, 9 Fla. 464; Gordon v. Green, 10 Ga. 534; Foster v. Lowell, 4 Mass. 308. Bank, 6 Gratt. 174; Darling v. Rogers, 22 Wend. 483; Hennessey v. Western Bank, 6 Watts & S. 300; Helfrich v. Obermeyer, 15 Pa. St. 113. An assignment to the assignee, “his heirs, executors, administrators and assigns,” instead of to “his successors and assigns,” is proper where the assignment includes real estate. Bates v. Simmons, 62 Wis. 69. An assignment made to the assignee, “his heirs, executors, administrators and assigns,” is in proper form. It describes the quality of the estate conveyed, not a class of persons to whom it is conveyed. Flagler v. Schoeffel, 40 Hun, 178. In a State where preferences are forbidden, a judgment given to a particular creditor, the effect of which is to prefer said creditor, is not an assignment. Blakey’s Appeal, 7 Pa. St. 449. A debtor in failing circumstances may prefer certain of his creditors to the exclusion of his other creditors; but such preferences cannot be made as a part of the general assignment, under the statute for the benefit of all his creditors. John Shillito Co. v. McConnell, 130 Ind. 41. An additional withholding and secreting by the assignor of assets of a

<br>

### --- Page 0539 ---

§ 597. ASSIGNMENTS FOR CREDITORS. 1363

General assignments should be in writing, and it is fit, if not necessary, that the ordinary forms of conveyance should be observed. The general rule that, while an express trust need not be created in writing, it must be proved in writing, will apply to trusts of this class. Assignments of real estate for the benefit of creditors are included in the provisions of the statute of frauds, and must be made in writing. In some of the States this is required by statute. In a case in Connecticut, A, having a claim against B for having willfully and maliciously mutilated the model of a propeller belonging to A, and, being insolvent, made an assignment for the benefit of his creditors under the statute. In making this assignment it was intended and agreed by A that such claim against B should be included therein and conveyed to the assignee, as well as the property specified, but through the mistake of the draftsman it was omitted. On a bill in chancery, brought by C, a purchaser of such claim from the assignee, praying for the reformation of the assignment according to the intention of A, and other relief, it was held that such claim, not having been included in the assignment, there was no assignment thereof in writing, pursuant to the statute, and the Court of Probate had no jurisdiction over it; and, consequently, C was not entitled to the relief sought. If, however, such claim had been specified in the assignment, it would have passed to the assignee, being in its nature assignable. The proper form of the instrument is that of a deed conveying the property and including a declaration of trust, in which the objects of the assignment are clearly set forth, but writings of a very informal character have been held to create a trust. The assignment may include more than one in-

substantial value from the possession of the assignee is a fraud upon the rights of creditors of the assignor, and renders a general assignment for the benefit of creditors void. Every party must be deemed to have intended the natural and inevitable consequences of his own acts, and so, when they are voluntary and necessarily operate to defraud others, he will be deemed to have intended the fraud.  
Coursey v. Morton, 132 N. Y. 556.

A mere mistake of computation ought not to vitiate such an assignment, and we are

### --- Page 0541 ---

§ 598.] ASSIGNMENTS FOR CREDITORS. 1365

§ 598. Reservations by the Assignor.—In general assignments it is the rule that all the property of the debtor not exempt from execution must be assigned. Where the instrument of assignment provides for a reservation of any part of the property for the benefit of the debtor, or of that of his family, the conveyance will be treated as void. The rule on this point is clearly stated by the court in Cheatham v. Hawkins, as follows: "An assignment cannot cover up and preserve the property for the debtor’s use, or protect it from the remedies and demands of the creditors. Here is not only a retention of possession by the assignor, which is presumptive evidence of fraud, but there is the further power to dispose of it for the debtor’s benefit, and, still more, the exercise of that power annihilates the thing itself. We have, then, one of the strongest cases of presumptive fraud. It is clear that if there is no proof to rebut, the presumption of fraud raised by the law becomes conclusive." This view has been upheld by the Su-

not prepared to say from an inspection of the record that there is anything more than that apparent in this case. The court below found that there was not, and we must presume that the evidence justified that finding unless the contrary appears." Barroilhet v. Fisch, 63 Cal. 462, 463.

Cheatham v. Hawkins, 76 N. Car. 335, 338. "It is quite true that the mere fact that an assignor, at the time of making his assignment, retained a portion of the assets in his possession is not, as a matter of law, conclusive upon the question of fraud. It may be explained, and if the explanation is such as to satisfy the tribunal which passes upon the facts that there was no fraudulent intent on the part of the assignor the assignment will not be set aside on account of it. Such was the case of Fay v. Grant (53 Hun, 44), where the plaintiff proved that the assignors retained certain inconsiderable sums of money, out of the firm property; but the assignors gave evidence to show that it was done under a mistaken notion of their rights, and without any intent to defraud, and as soon as their attention was called to their mistake they paid back the money, and thereupon the case was submitted to the jury, who found that there was no fraudulent intent in doing the act. But while such retaining of assets is not necessarily fraudulent, if it is unexplained or is connected with suspicious circumstances, it is sufficient to warrant a finding that the assignment, in connection with which it was made, was made with intent to defraud, and to set it aside." Constable v. Hardenbergh, 4 Hun, App. 143, 145. "It has been decided that a failing debtor may

<br>

### --- Page 0542 ---

1366

Supreme Court of the United States.

ASSIGNMENTS FOR CREDITORS.

[§ 598.]

In Means v. Dowd,

Mr. Justice Miller, in delivering the opinion of the court, said:

“The prevailing doctrine, however, is unquestionably that which we have stated, and its fundamental essence is that a debtor may not prefer one creditor, or set of creditors, by an assignment of his property; but if, in that assignment, a provision is made for the debtor or his family, the whole assignment is void. It is in evidence that both the parties knew of this debt and spoke of it, and of their determination that the creditors should not have the farm; and as no other consideration is pretended but the support of the family, it seems to me the assignment is fraudulent and void as to creditors.” Jackson v. Parker, 9 Cow. 73, 86.

A debtor, in making an assignment of his property for the benefit of creditors, may exact releases from creditors as a condition of preference under the deed, where he dedicates all of his property not exempt by law to the payment of his debts. A general deed of assignment of all of the assignor’s property is not rendered void by an exception of “such real estate and personal property as is exempt under the law from forced sale.” King v. Hargadine, 60 Ark. 1.

A deed conveying a plantation, etc., to trustees, for the benefit of certain creditors of the grantor, is not void merely because it provides that the trustees may, if they think proper, permit the grantor to reside upon the plantation and have the management thereof, under the supervision of the trustees, until the growing crop is sold. Planters’ & Merchants’ Bank v. Clark, 7 Ala. 765.

If the deed of trust contains a proviso that, in case any of the creditors named should not, within the time limited in the deed, which contained a release of the debtor from his debts, become parties to it, the shares or proportions of such creditors, so neglecting or refusing to execute the deed, should be paid by the trustees to the assignor himself, the deed is fraudulent and void, under the statute of frauds. Austin v. Bell, 20 Johns. 442.

Though an assignor for the benefit of creditors expressly reserves his exempt property, and retains the proceeds of sales made on the day of assignment to an amount legally exempt, the deed of assignment will be declared void at the instance of creditors, unless the sum so retained was selected by the assignor himself as exempt property. Mahorner v. Forcheimer (1895) (Miss.), 1850 Rep. 570.

The reservation, in a deed to secure creditors, of the right to manage property therein conveyed for three years before sale, the rents and profits in the meantime to belong to the creditors secured, is not such an unreasonable restraint upon the power of sale as to constitute a fraud in itself. Noyes v. Carter (1895) (Va.), 23 S. E. Rep. 1.

An instrument duly acknowledged, reciting that the signer desires to make a fair distribution of his assets among such creditors as will accept their proportionate shares of his estate, and thereby conveying to another, in trust for them, all of his property not exempt by law, is an assignment, within Sayle’s Civ. St., art. 65, providing that an assignment may be made for the benefit of

<br>

### --- Page 0543 ---

§ 598.] ASSIGNMENTS FOR CREDITORS. 1367

is that an insolvent debtor making an assignment, even for the benefit of his creditors, cannot reserve to himself any beneficial interest in the property assigned, or interpose any delay, or make provisions which would hinder and delay creditors from their lawful modes of prosecuting their claims. A provision in an instrument of assignment that the debtor shall retain possession of the property, or any part thereof, for a definite period, or until the occurring of a designated event, is not regarded with favor by the courts, and under ordinary circumstances will not be sustained. In Alabama it has been held that a deed of trust providing for the security of creditors, designated in the deed, but providing also that the debtor shall retain the use of the property until a day subsequent to that when the debts are due, is invalid as a conveyance, without the assent of all the beneficiaries, the contrary not being expressed in the deed. But where the instrument by which the trust is created provides that the business of the debtor shall be temporarily continued under the direction of the assignee, or that any work in process, and unfinished at the creation of the trust, shall be completed, and it appears

such creditors only who will consent to accept their proportionate shares of the assigned estate in full settlement of their respective claims, provided that one-third of such indebtedness is paid. Creditors who have consented to receive under an assignment are estopped from denying its validity. Whitehill v. Shaw (1896) (Tex. Civ. App.), 338 W. Rep. 886.

Messenger, 49 Pa. St. 465; Knight v. Waterman, 36 Pa. St. 258. Courts will not uphold an assignment containing stipulations for control by the assignor over the assigned property or the instrument. Riggs v. Murray, 2 Johns. 565; Green v. Trieber, 3 Md. 11; Price v. Fritzell, 44 Md. 521; Breckenbach v. Winkhaus, 67 How. Pr. 512; Averill v. Loucks, 6 Barb. 470; Brown v. that the interests of the creditors will be promoted by such continuance, the stipulation will be sustained. But if it appears that such plan is designed to promote the interests of the assignor, or that he is to receive the profits of the continued business, or any part thereof, the assignment will be treated as fraudulent and void. But it has been held that the circumstance that a debtor is permitted to remain in possession of personal property conveyed by the instrument for three or four months is not, ipso facto, evidence of a fraudulent intent.*

§ 599. The Subject Continued.—Where an assignment provides that any surplus that may remain after the payment of all the creditors shall revert to the assignor, such reservation will not invalidate the trust. But where the assignment is partial or for the benefit of preferred creditors, such a reservation will not be upheld.

however, will not apply to a partial assignment by a solvent debtor. Where such debtor assigns a part of his property for the payment of a class, any surplus after such payment will revert to the assignor under the general rule. In New York and in some of the other States, it is held as a rule that a stipulation in an instrument creating a trust for the benefit of creditors, under an assignment, that is made a condition precedent to the receiving of the benefits of the trust by the creditors, invalidates the assignment.
