### --- Page 0600 ---

CHAPTER XXX.

TRUSTEE FOR TENANT FOR LIFE AND REMAINDER-MAN.

§ 621. Powers and Duties of Trustees.

622. Tenant for Life as Constructive Trustee.

623. Where the Tenant for Life is Entitled to Possession.

624. Tenant for Life as New Trustee.

§ 621.

Powers and Duties of Trustees.—Trustees for tenant for life and remainder-man are equally trustees for each of the parties. They hold the property for the benefit of each, and they are not permitted to favor one at the expense of the other. In every act relating to the estate, the interests of each must be consulted and held equally sacred. Where the legal title to the real estate is in the trustee he is entitled to the possession of the title deeds, and where he delivers them to the tenant for life, or by his negligence suffers him to obtain possession of them, and any loss occurs in consequence, or any improper use is made of the deeds, the trustees will be held responsible.

Denton v. Denton, 8 Jur. 388; S. C., 7 Beay. 388; Wright v. Miller, 4 Seld. 9; Kaye v. Powel, 1 Ves. Jr. 408; Pugh v. Vaughn, 12 Beay. 517. The grantor of land seized to his own use for life can do nothing, while in possession, to impair an estate created by him in remainder, and not having reserved the power, can neither revoke the estate in remainder nor declare new uses. Aubuchon v. Bender, 44 Mo. 560.

It is held also that certain property was devised to a trustee "out of the proceeds, interest, rents, income or profits" of two-thirds of which the trustee was to pay to the testatrix’s brother such sums as, in the trustee’s discretion, were necessary for his support; and on the brother’s death without issue, the remainder to go to residuary legatees. The trustee in the course of six years paid the brother a...

<br>

### --- Page 0601 ---

§ 621.

TENANT FOR LIFE AND REMAINDER-MAN,

where there is no express direction in regard to it in the instrument, and the nature of the duties imposed upon the trustee by the grantor require that he should exercise a control of the estate, he will be entitled to the possession and management thereof.

Where property is of such a character that the tenant for life can have the use of it without conversion he is entitled to possession, though the sum exceeding the gross proceeds of the estate, and more than four times two-thirds of the net proceeds. Held, that the trustee abused his discretion, exceeded his powers, and had no authority to thus impair the capital of the trust fund; and held, further, that the beneficiary and the residuary legatees had such an interest as enabled them to institute proceedings for the appointment of a new trustee, and for an accounting. Haydel v. Hurck, 5 Mo. App. 267.

Real estate was devised to the wife of the testator for life, remainder in fee to his children. The wife and children, after his death, united in a joint sale and conveyance of the land to a purchaser, notes and mortgage for the purchase money being taken in the name of the wife, and finally invested in other lands, title to which was taken in her name. Held, that upon her death these latter lands went, in equity, to the remainder-men under the will of her husband, and not to her heirs-at-law, precisely as the estate devised would have gone if it had not been conveyed. Clifford v. Farmer, 79 Ind. 529.

To sustain a construction of a will, whereby the capital of a trust fund may be impaired by using it in payment of taxes and interest on mortgages and in maintaining the realty used by the life tenant, it must contain words of the most unmistakable import, pointing unequivocally in that direction. In re Albertson, 113 N. Y. 434.

Where a fund is held in trust for the benefit of one person for life, and to go to another in remainder, and a loss of a part of the fund occurs, arising out of insufficient security of a particular investment, such loss is to be apportioned between the tenant for life and remainder-man in the proportion which the principal sum involved in the insufficient security bears to the interest due upon it at the time when the security is realized upon and the amount of the loss determined. Hagan v. Platt, 48 N.J. Eq. 206.

If a trustee under a will, who holds a fund in trust to pay the income to a person during his life, with remainder over, makes an investment in bonds which are payable at a day certain, and are bought at a premium, he is not obliged to pay the entire net income to the tenant for life, but is entitled to deduct such an amount from the actual interest received on each bond as will, by successive deductions, make good to the capital the amount of premium paid upon the original purchase of the bond, without regard to the market value of the bond at the time of making such deductions. N. E. Trust Co. v. Eaton, 140 Mass. 532.

Tidd v. Lister, 5 Madd. 429; Jenkins v. Milford, 1 J. & W. 609.

<br>

### --- Page 0602 ---

1426 TENANT FOR LIFE AND REMAINDERMAN, [§ 622.]

right of control is in the trustees, but the trustees are entitled to the possession of all money and stocks.¹

§ 622. Tenant for Life as Constructive Trustee.

Where a tenant for life disposes of any interest in the trust estate, whatever it may be, he will hold the proceeds as a constructive trustee. The money will be a part of the trust estate. His interest in it will be, not that of an owner or proprietor, but simply the interest which he has in the remainder of the trust estate, viz.: that of a tenant for life. He is not permitted to avail himself of his possession of the property to secure any personal benefit in addition to his life interest. Where it was the intention of the settlor to give him a life interest with remainder over, he will not be sustained in any act, the object of which is to defeat that intention. In a case where a tenant for life received 5,000 from a railway company, in consideration of his not opposing a bill for a railway to pass through his estate; and the act having passed, portions of the estate were taken, and the purchase money paid into court and invested, and the income paid to tenant for life during his life, it was held that the £3,000 so received came within the 73d section of the Land Clause Act; and the tenant for life was declared trustee of the same, for himself and the persons entitled in remainder to the estate; and was ordered to pay the same accordingly.²

¹ Young v. Miles, 10 B. Mon. 290.

² Pole v. Pole, 2 Dr. & Sm. 420.

which an action at law would lie, yet, says he, it has been held on very high authority, that this is a trust, necessarily implied from the nature of the transaction, and which Chancery will enforce in favor of the beneficiary, although no express agreement has been entered into to that effect.³ Joyce v. Gunnels, 2 Rich. Eq. 259, 268. In 1867 P executed a deed of which the operative words were, “I do hereby give and grant to L P one lot of land * * to contain two acres of land, reserving to myself possession during life.” Held, in

³ In several cases it has been held, and may now be considered as settled, that the tenant for life stands in the nature of a trustee to the remainder-man. It is only necessary to inquire whether this is a direct declared trust, or only a trust by implication of law. The simplest form of implied trust is put by Mr. Justice Story, as where money or property is delivered by A to B for the use of C, or to be delivered to C. Although this might be regarded rather as a bailment for which an action at law would lie, yet, says he, it has been held on very high authority, that this is a trust, necessarily implied from the nature of the transaction, and which chancery will enforce in favor of the beneficiary, although no express agreement has been entered into to that effect.

<br>

### --- Page 0603 ---

§ 623.] TENANT FOR LIFE AND REMAINDER-MAN,

§ 623.

1427

Where the Tenant for Life is Entitled to Possession.—In general it is the rule that the trustee is entitled to the possession of the title deeds and to the control of the real estate, where the legal title is vested in him, and he may maintain an action at law for the recovery of the deeds, if the tenant for life has secured possession of them.1

The absence of evidence that words of inheritance were omitted by mistake to contain only a life estate, and there being nothing apparent in the contents of the instrument inconsistent with an intention to convey a life estate, a court of equity would not decree a correction for the purpose of conveying the fee. The fact that the executor of the life tenant purchased the land from the representative of the vendor could not constitute him a trustee for the heirs or devisees of the vendee. Ray v. Commissioners, 110 N. Car. 169. The tenant for life of slaves as a trustee for the remainder-man is bound to account, and the burden of proof to show the increase or diminution is thrown upon him. Horry v. Glovers, 2 Hill Eq. 515. Where a feme covert has a separate estate for life in some, and undivided interest as tenant in common, in others, of the slaves in question, she is a trustee for those claiming in reversion and as cotenants. Clarke v. Saxon, 1 Hill Eq. 69. See also Brown v. Curry, 19 Ala. 805; Wilson v. Ely, 6 N. J. Eq. 181; Wilson v. Edmonds, 24 N. H. 545. But see Fox v. Buckley, L. R. 3 Ch. D. 511; Egbert v. Butler, 21 Beav. 560.

1Strode v. Blackburne, 3 Ves. 225; Harrington v. Price, 3 B. & Ad. 170; Ivie v. Ivie, 1 Atk. 431; Ford v. Peering, 1 Ves. Jr. 76; Webb v. Lymington, 8 Ves. 322 n; Doe v. Passingham, 6 B. & C. 305; Barclay v. Collett, 4 Bing. N. Cas. 650;

But

Denton v. Denton, 8 Jur. 388; s. c., 7 Beav. 388; Powell v. Knox, 16 Ala. 368. Bill to charge a trustee, as having by delivering the title deeds to a tenant for life enabled him to make a mortgage of a settled estate as tenant in fee, dismissed; the fraudulent purpose of enabling him to mortgage, resting upon the evidence of a single witness, and being positively denied by the answer, as far as the allegations of the bill gave an opportunity of answering, but without costs on the ground of negligence. Evyans v. Bicknell, 6 Ves. 174. “On this question I am of opinion, that any person entitled to a vested remainder in an estate may maintain a bill against the tenant for life, for the sole purpose of production and inspection of the title, deeds and documents relating to the estate, in the possession of the tenant for life, in order to enable the remainder-man to deal with his property as he may consider most for his advantage. I think it unnecessary to go through the various authorities that were cited and commented upon before me on this subject, they all tend in the same direction; and although, in some cases, as in that of Lord Lempster v. Lord Pomfret, 1 Ambl. 154; s. c., 1 Dick. 238, they intimate, that the ordering of this production is not a matter of right, but rests on the discretion of the court, and that it will not be directed, unless for a purpose which the court shall deem

<br>

### --- Page 0604 ---

1128 TENANT FOR LIFE AND REMAINDER-MAN. [§ 623,

where the trustees in a case of this character had acquiesced for a period of four years in the possession of the estates and of the title deeds, by the tenant for life, they were not permitted to proceed with an action for their recovery, on the bringing of the deeds into court by the tenant for life. But where it is plain from the words of the will that the testator did not intend that the estate should remain under the personal control and management of the trustee, or where there is nothing in the instrument to indicate the intention of the testator, and it is plain that the interest of the tenant for life requires that he should have possession of the estate, his possession will be sustained in equity. In the leading English case of Tidd v. Lister it was held that it is not a matter of course that the tenant for life is to be put into possession, but that this must be determined by the intention of the testator. In the opinion in this case, the rule is stated by the Vice Chancellor, Sir John Leach, as follows: "There may be cases in which it may be plain from the expressions in the will, that the testator did not intend that the property should remain under the personal management of the

to be proper, still I think the principle is, that the person entitled in remainder, or his mortgagee, who stands in his place, is entitled to and may compel such production; and if it be suggested that the purpose for which the documents are required is an improper one, that the burden of the proof of this lies on the person resisting the production." Davis v. Earl of Dysart, 20 Beav. 405, 414. "The legal tenant for life is prima facie entitled to the custody of the title deeds. The rule is subject to some qualification; it does not apply to a case in which the legal estate is vested in trustees on particular trusts, one of which is to receive the rents and to pay them over to the tenant for life; in such a case I have held that the trustee was entitled to the custody of the deeds. This is a case of a tenant for life of a legal estate, and I am of opinion he is entitled to the deeds." Garner v. Hannyngton, 22 Beav. 627, 630. As a suit affecting the estate was being prosecuted, the custody of the deeds did not depend on the question who had the legal right to them, but on the question what custody was most convenient for the purpose of the suit, and that the court of appeal would not interfere with the discretion of the vice chancellor on the subject. Stanford v. Roberts, 6 Ch. App. 306.

¹ Denton v. Denton, 8 Jur. 388; s.c., 7 Beav. 388; Blake v. Bunbury, 1 Ves. Jr. 194.

<br>

### --- Page 0605 ---

§ 623.] TENANT FOR LIFE AND REMAINDER-MAN. 1429  
trustees. There may be cases in which it may be plain from the nature of the property, that the testator could not mean to exclude the cestui que trust for life from the personal possession of the property, as in the case of a family residence. There may be very special cases in which this court would deliver the possession of the property to the cestui que trust for life, although the testator’s intention appeared to be that it should remain with the trustees, as where the personal occupation of the trust property was beneficial to the cestui que trust, there the court taking means to secure the due protection of the property for the benefit of those in remainder, would, in substance, be performing the trust according to the intention of the testator. It is held  

Tiddy v. Lister, 5 Madd. 428, 431. A testatrix by her will made in 1878, devised and bequeathed all her real and personal estate upon trust and directed her trustees to invest the residue of her personal estate as therein mentioned, and stand possessed of the income and the net rents of her real estate upon trust to pay the same to her niece, a married woman, during her life, and she directed her trustees, out of the rents of said real estate, to keep in repair all buildings on the estate and the chancel of the church. The will contained no express devise of the real estate to the trustees and no power of leasing. On the death of the testatrix, which took place in 1884, the niece as tenant for life under the Settled Land Act, 1882, claimed the right to let and manage the property and also receive the rents, she undertaking to keep the buildings and chancel of the church in repair, and she gave the trustees a general notice of her intention to do so. The trustees disputed her right to receive the rents or otherwise to interfere with the management of the estate, and brought an action against her to restrain her from receiving the rents or giving notice to the tenants to pay the rents to her. Held, that so long as the tenant for life kept the buildings and chancel of the church in repair, the court would not interfere by injunction, but that she must do nothing till the trustees for the purposes of the Settled Land Act, 1882, had been appointed and served with the requisite notice. Wade v. Wilson, 33 W. R. 610. A testator, after devising two-fifths of certain real estate to which he was entitled to trustees upon trust to receive the net rents and pay them to M for life for her separate use, devised his residuary real and personal estate to the same trustees upon trust to receive the annual and other rents, interest and income of his said real and personal estate, and after defraying thereout all ground and landlord’s rent (if any), and all taxes, charges and expenses of insurance, repairs, collection and other necessary outlay to pay the net annual rents, interest and income, then left to M for life for her separate use. The will con-

<br>

### --- Page 0606 ---

1430 TENANT FOR LIFE AND REMAINDER-MAN. [§ 624.

also that where the legal title is in the tenant for life, subject to a term vested in trustees for raising a charge, he will be put into possession, on condition of securing the payment of the charge, provided it appears that the current income is sufficient to satisfy the incumbrance imposed by the charge. As a rule where the tenant for life takes the legal title and the equitable interest he has the right of possession, and his possession will be commensurate with the title. But in such case the tenant for life is a constructive trustee, or a trustee by implication of law for the remainder-men, and is liable to be called upon for an account.

§ 624. Tenant for Life as New Trustee.—In the case of a tenant for life, with remainder over, the object of the original appointment of a trustee is to secure the rights of both the tenant for life and the remainder-men. It is for this reason that the control of the property is not left by the testator to the tenant for life, or that he is subjected to some restraint in the control and management of the property, and this, in the abstract, would seem to be a good reason for refusing to appoint the tenant for life. In the case of a remainder-man the same rule is applicable. But it has been held that there are circumstances under which the tenant for life may be appointed as a new trustee. In the case of Forster v. Abraham the appointment of the tenant for life as a new trustee was sustained, and, in his opinion, Sir George Jessel, M. R., stated the grounds of the decision, as follows: "Looking, then, both to the reason of the thing, and to the practice and usage of conveyances, I see no reasonable ground for saying that the appointment of the tenant for life, per se, is improper. I find also that, although, as a rule, the Court of Chancery

tained no power of leasing. Held, that M was not “entitled to possession or receipt of the rents and profits” of the testator’s residuary real estate within the meaning of § 32 of the Leases and Sales of Settled Estates Act, and consequently that M was not entitled to grant leases thereof. Taylor v. Taylor, L. R. 20 Eq. 297.

1 Blake v. Bunbury, 1 Ves. 194; Tidd v. Lister, 5 Madd. 432; Denton v. Denton, 8 Jur. 388; s. c., 7 Beay. 388.

<br>

### --- Page 0607 ---

§ 625.] TENANT FOR LIFE AND REMAINDER-MAN, 1431  
a discretion, I may say, as regards which I respectfully wish to express my cordial assent to what fell from Lord Justice Turner in the case of In re Tempest, yet it is not an arbitrary discretion, but to be applied according to settled rules. Then, exercising that discretion, as I said before, the general rule is that the Court of Chancery will not appoint the tenant for life, although it may do so, and sometimes has done so. Therefore, there is no insuperable objection to the tenant for life per se, that is, nothing to make the appointment invalid.

§ 625. Payment of Charges by Tenant for Life.—Where a tenant for life pays a specific charge or incumbrance from his own funds, he is held in equity, in the absence of any evidence showing an intention to extinguish the incumbrance, in the position of a transferee of the charge, notwithstanding that he takes an ordinary reconveyance; and, on his death, the remainder-man takes the legal estate, subject to the equitable lien or charge. In a case before the High Court of Chancery in Ireland it was held that where a term for years was, by settlement, vested in trustees to raise by sale, or mortgage, money to discharge the debts of the tenant for life, who soon after, with his own money, pays the debts without taking assignments of affidavits. You may take the order. It will be drawn up in court, and not in chambers. Ex parte Clutton, 17 Jur. 988. A testator appointed A B (the tenant for life), and C D trustees. The will contained no power to appoint new trustees. C D having disclaimed, A B (under the powers of the 23 & 24 Vict. ch. 145, § 27) appointed a single trustee in his place. Held, that the other cestui que trust were entitled to have a third trustee appointed, and that the statute did not take away the jurisdiction of the court to increase the original number of trustees. Viscountess D’Adhemar v. Bertrand, 35 Beav. 19.

1 In re Tempest, Law Rep. 1 Ch. 485.

2 Forster v. Abraham, L. R. 17 Eq. 351, 355. E F S presented a petition for the appointment of E B C, the tenant for life under the will, to be a new trustee, along with the continuing trustee. The petition was granted, the court, through Sir W. P. Wood, saying: “I have a great objection, of course, to appoint one of the cestuis que trust to be a manager of this property. But in this case the trusts are so onerous, that it would perhaps be very difficult to find any stranger who would undertake them, and, indeed, that appears from the affidavits.”

<br>

1432 TENANT FOR LIFE AND REMAINDER-MAN. [§ 625.

the securities, a mortgage of it by the trustees several years after, by direction of the tenant for life, is a due execution of the trust. Tenant for life has, during his whole life, a right to call for an execution of the trust, and to stand in the place of creditors. After an express declaration of the tenant for life to charge, it cannot, from the debts being paid by him, from no assignment of the securities being taken, or from length of time, be presumed he intended to exonerate the estate. From tenant for life paying off a charge is not to be presumed he meant to exonerate the estate. Not necessary for tenant for life to take assignment from creditors of a charge on the estate he paid in order to keep it alive. The presumption that a tenant for life did not mean by paying off debts to exonerate the estate may be rebutted and disproved. Tenant in tail not obliged to keep down interest on a charge affecting the estate; if he do, his personal representative will not be allowed it out of the estate.?

1 Redington v. Redington, 1 B. & B. 131. See also Drinkwater v. Combe, 2 S. & St. 340; Astley v. Miller, 1 Sim. 298. Tenant for life of devised estates paid off certain bond debts of the testator and cancelled the bonds; after a considerable lapse of years he filed his bill to have the amount of the bond debts raised out of the settled estate for his own benefit. Held, that the principle of a court of equity that a tenant for life, paying off an incumbrance upon the settled estates, intends prima facie to keep alive the charge against the inheritance for his own benefit does not apply, or in a very remote degree, to the case of a tenant for life paying off the bond debts of the settlor; and supposing the intention to be established by the tenant for life taking an assignment of the bond debts to a trustee for himself; yet the claim cannot be made available against the inheritance, if the tenant for life has allowed such a time to elapse as that the bond creditor, if suing himself, would have had no right against the estate. Morley v. Morley, 25 L. J. Ch.1. In St. Paul v. Dudley, 15 Ves. 172, Lord Eldon says: ‘Tenant for life takes a surrender of a copyhold to him and his heirs. The legal effect of that is, that the property becomes parcel of the manor. It is said that an equity arises out of the transaction, and the fact that the tenant for life paid the money for the inheritance, that those, who are entitled to the manor in remainder, shall be considered as trustees of that copyhold estate for him. The fact of payment of the money constitutes a case very clear. It is no more than the lord buying the copyhold. ‘That has been long familiarly known, and I never be-

<br>

### --- Page 0609 ---

§ 626.

TENANT FOR LIFE AND REMAINDER-MAN,

Repairs and Improvements.—Subject to certain modifications, it is the rule that where real estate is conveyed to trustees in trust for a tenant for life, with remainder over, the trustees have no power to raise money out of the corpus of the estate for the purpose of making substantial and permanent repairs, either on the building fore heard this equity stated. The actual intention may probably be defeated. In the case that has been cited by Sir Samuel Romilly of the devise of a manor, the devisor afterwards purchasing a copyhold within it, he probably did not intend it to pass by the will, yet the court of Kings’ Bench held that it did pass. It has been said that this is analogous to the case of tenant for life paying off an incumbrance. It is so in this respect. If the tenant for life at the time he pays off the debt in that transaction, merges the security by taking an assignment, connecting it with the legal estate of inheritance, upon that transaction, prima facie there is no charge.

A tenant for life and sole trustee of settled estates, by will, purchased reversions in fee of leases for lives (his own life being one), and for years, parts of the settled estates, and the estates were conveyed, as to the reversion of the leases for lives, to him upon the trusts of the will; as to one of the reversions of the leases for years to him absolutely, and as to the other reversions of the leases for years to him upon the trusts of the will. He died while the leases for years would have been running out, and without having declared any intention that the purchase moneys were to enure for the benefit of those entitled under the will. The first tenant in tail died in the tenant for life’s lifetime intestate, leaving a widow and a son heir in tail. Held, that as to all the reversion purchased, the estate for the tenant for life was entitled to be repaid the purchase money; that as regarded the legal personal representative of the first tenant in tail, both the estates, of which there had been leases for years, must be treated as being subject to those leases for the benefit of his first tenant in tail’s estate; that as to the estate conveyed to the tenant for life absolutely, it belonged to the legal personal representative of the first tenant in tail; and that as to the other estate conveyed to him upon the trusts of the will, she was entitled to a leasehold interest of the same extent as the term would have conferred. Isaac v. Wall, L. R. 6 Ch. Div. 706.

Where a charge on an estate becomes vested absolutely in the owner of the inheritance of the estate, the three tests usually applied for ascertaining whether the charge has merged are: First, whether there has been an actual expression of intention to that effect; secondly, whether the acts done by the owner of the estate are only consistent with the charge being kept on foot; and thirdly, whether it is for the interest of the owner that the charge should not merge in the inheritance. Tyrwhitt v. Tyrwhitt, 32 Beav. 244.

<br>

[§ 626.]

Such expenses, if incurred, will be a charge on the interest of the tenant for life.

In Nairn v. Majoribanks, Lord Eldon declared that even if the Master should report that it would be for the benefit of all parties interested that improvements should be made on the mansion house, he would not confirm the report.²

It is held that the legislature has no power to authorize such expenditures.³

In England it has been held that, in the case of legal estates, the court has no jurisdiction to make any order for charging the cost of repairs, or any part thereof.

‘Bostock v. Blakeney, 2 Bro. Ch. 653; Hibbert v. Cooke, 1 S. & St. 552; Caldecott v. Brown, 2 Hare, 144; Nairn v. Majoribanks, 3 Russ. 582; Thurston v. Dickinson, 2 Rich. Eq. 317; Cogswell v. Cogswell, 2 Edw. Ch. 231; Jones v. Dawson, 19 Ala. 672; Thurston v. Thurston, 6 R. I. 296; Corbet v. Laurens, 5 Rich. Eq. 301; Gambril v. Gambril, 3 Md. Ch. 259; Cairns v. Chaubert, 3 Edw. If the widow were a life tenant, the executor having no means with which to pay taxes and make repairs, and the life tenant being unable to do so, if another person advanced money for this purpose, at the instance and with the approval of the executor, it would be a charge against the estate which a court of equity would enforce, it being a trust estate and the title being in the executor. Griffin v. Fleming, 72 Ga. 697.

If the tenant of a dove house, warren, or park, take so many that such sufficient store be not left as he found when he came in, this is waste; and to suffer the pale to decay, whereby the deer are dispersed, is waste. It is waste to suffer a wall of the sea to be in decay, so as by the flowing and reflowing of the sea, the meadow or marsh becomes unprofitable. Substantial damage to the remainder, done contrary to the ordinary course of good husbandry, by impoverishing fields by tillage or suffering pastures to become overgrown with brush is waste.

A tenant for life is bound to keep down ordinary charges for taxes and repairs, out of the rents and profits. If the estate be charged with an incumbrance, he is generally bound to keep down the interest out of the rents and profits. Pierce v. Burroughs, 58 N. H. 302, 304. See also Wilson v. Edmonds, 24 N. H. 517; Wood v. Griffin, 46 N. H. 230, 238; Clemence v. Steere, 1 R. I. 272.

on the corpus of the estate.’ It is otherwise, however, where the trustee holds the legal title in fee and has a power or trust for sale. In such case the court may authorize the raising of money for making such repairs as are necessary for the preservation of the property.?

§ 627. Liability for Current Charges.—Where a capital sum is secured on real estate held by trustees for tenant for life, with remainder over, it is payable out of the corpus, but the interest on the capital sum is payable out of the income of the estate. In England this rule obtains where a charge is payable as an annuity. In such a case the annuity must be valued, and the tenant for life will be required to pay an amount equal to the interest on the valuation at 4 per cent. per annum.?

1 De Thiesser v. De Thiesser, L. R. (1893) 1 Ch. 153; s.c., 1 Ch. App. 153.

2 Freke v. Calmady, L. R. 32 Ch. D. 408; Coles v. Courtier, L. R. 34 Ch. D. 136; Conway v. Fenton, L. R. 40 Ch. D. 518; Jackson v. Talhot, L. R. 21 Ch. D. 789. But see Hibbert v. Cooke, 15 S. & St. 552; Dent v. Dent, 30 Beav. 363.

3 Marshall v. Crowther, L. R. 2 Ch. D. 197; Whitbread v. Smith, 3 De G., M. & G. 741; Allhusen v. Whittell, L. R. 4 Eq. 295; Bulwer v. Astley, 1 Ph. 422; Playfair v. Cooper, 17 Beav. 187; Grissell v. Leather, 68 L. T. 522; Jones v. Mason, L. R. 39 Ch. D. 534. A testator devised his real estate (in the events which happened) to the use of the plaintiff for life; with remainder to the use of the plaintiff’s first, and other sons successively in tail male; with remainder over to the use of another person in fee. The will provided that, immediately after the death of a prior tenant for life (who died before the testator), the trustees of the will should receive the rents, and, after all charges that mature, or are paying thereout the interest on the mortgages on the estates, should accumulate the residue, until the amount of the accumulations should be sufficient to discharge the principal of the mortgages. And the testator directed that, as soon as the accumulations should be sufficient to pay off the mortgages the trustees should forthwith pay them off. And he declared that the plaintiff, or other the person for the time being entitled for life or in tail to the estates under the limitations of the will, should not be entitled to receive any part of the rents until the mortgages had been paid off. After the testator’s death the mortgagees sold those parts of the estates which were comprised in their mortgages. The proceeds of sale were not enough to pay the whole of the mortgage debts, and the balance was paid out of the accumulations of rents. A surplus of accumulations remained. Held, that the tenant for life was entitled to be let into possession of the estates which remained unsold, and to have

<br>

### --- Page 0612 ---

1436 TENANT FOR LIFE AND REMAINDERMAN. [§ 628,  
payable annually, excepting such as relate to capital sums, are payable from the income. In this class of charges are included all rates, taxes, rents payable on leasehold hereditaments, etc., etc. Formerly it was doubtful in England whether trustees could expend trust money in insuring trust property against loss by fire, but by the Trustee Act of 1893 trustees were authorized to take insurance to any amount, not exceeding three-fourths of the value of the building, or other property insured, and to pay the premiums out of the income, but the statute does not apply to simple trusts.

§ 628. Rights in Perishable Property.—It is the rule that where a trustee holds in trust perishable property it is his duty to convert it and to invest the proceeds in property of a permanent value. This rule is based upon the presumption that it was the intention of the testator that the property left to the tenant for life should pass without diminution to those entitled in remainder. This rule is stated by Sir John Leach, M. R., in Alcock v. Sloper, as

the surplus of the accumulations  
paid to him. Norton v. Johnstone, L. R. 30 Ch. D. 649. A testator devised all his real estate (except two specified estates) to trustees in fee, in trust for his son for life, with remainder in trust for the son’s first and other sons successively in tail male, with remainders over. And he devised the two specified estates upon similar trusts for the benefit of his two daughters respectively for life, with remainders to their respective issue. The testator had, upon the marriage of his son, entered into a covenant, for himself, his heirs, executors and administrators, with the son, to pay, during the joint lives of the son and his wife, an annuity of £700 to the son or his wife. After payment of the testator’s debts and testamentary expenses his personal estate was insufficient to provide for the annuity. Held, that the annuity must be treated as a debt of the testator; that it must be apportioned between the three devised estates according to their respective values; and that each tenant for life, on paying his proportion of the annuity would be entitled, in respect of each payment of the annuity, to a charge upon the corpus of the estate, but must keep down the interest upon the amount so charged. Townson v. Harrison, L. R. 43 Ch. D. 55.

Fountain v. Pellet, 1 Ves. Jr. 337, 342; Pine v. Cooper, 17 Beav. 187, 193; Miller v. Huddleston, 3 Macn. & G. 513; Shore v. Shore, 4 Drew. 510; In re Waugh, 25 W.R. 555; In re Leinster, L. R. 21 Ir. 152.

§ 628.] TENANT FOR LIFE AND REMAINDER-MAN. 1437

follows: “Where a testator limits his residuary property to one for life, with remainder over, it is prima facie to be inferred that the testator means that the same property which is given to the tenant for life should go to those entitled in remainder; and, if any part of the residue be of a wasting nature, as long annuities, or leasehold estate, in order to effect this general purpose of the testator, such wasting property must be sold and converted into permanent property. Although this intention of the testator is prima facie to be inferred, it may plainly appear upon the whole context of the will that the testator had not that meaning, but that his intention was that the tenant for life should derive the same income from the residuary estate as he had himself derived from his property up to the period of his death.”

It has been the constant practice of the court of equity to require security for the forthcoming of property on the termination of a life estate, or on any other event when the rights of the remainder-men or reversioners spring up, whenever those rights appear to be in danger. Cordes v. Adrian, 1 Hill Ch. 154. CHAPTER XXXI.

TRUSTS UNDER POWER OF SALE MORTGAGES AND DEEDS OF TRUST.

Introductory.—Mortgages containing a power of sale and deeds of trusts given as security for a debt, both at law and in equity, are substantially the same thing. A mortgage of realty is a conveyance by a debtor to his creditors, as security for the payment of the debt, and containing the provision, either express or implied, that on the payment of the debt the instrument shall be void. This definition applies to the deed of trust given as security for the payment of a debt as really as to the mortgage in its...

<br>

### --- Page 0616 ---

1440 TRUSTS UNDER POWER OF SALE, ETC. [§ 629,

proper or ordinary form. There are various forms of trust deeds, designed to answer a variety of purposes, but the discussion of trust deeds in this connection is to be limited to that form or class of instruments of conveyance which are of the nature of a mortgage, or are given as security for the payment of a debt. A general discussion of deeds of trust or of mortgages does not fall within the design and scope of a work on the law of trusts and trustees. The treatment, as it belongs to this work, relates exclusively to trusts created under power of sale mortgages, or deeds of trust given as security for the satisfaction of a loan, or other pecuniary obligation. This view of deeds of trust as mortgages is sustained by the decisions of the courts. In Ohio it has been held that a deed conveying land to a trustee, merely as collateral security for the payment of a debt, with the condition that it shall be void on the payment of the debt when due, with power to the trustee to sell the land and pay the debt, in case of failure to pay on the part of the debtor, is a mortgage within the meaning of the registry act, and only takes effect from the time of delivery for record.1 In Hoffman v. Mackall, the same court said: "A deed of trust in the nature of a mortgage is a conveyance in trust by way of security, subject to a condition of defeasance, or redeemable at any time before the sale of the property. A deed conveying land to a trustee as mere collateral security for the payment of a debt, with the condition that it shall become void on the payment of the debt when due, and with power to the trustee to sell the land and pay the debt

1 Woodruff v. Robb, 19 Ohio, 212. See also Myers v. Estell, 48 Miss. 404; Turner v. Watkins, 31 Ark. 429; Austin v. Sprague Mfg. Co., 14 R.I. 464; Union Co. v. Sprague, 14 R.I. 452; McLane v. Paschal, 47 Tex. 365; Wright v. Henderson, 12 Tex. 43; Thompson v. Marshall, 21 Oreg. 171; Hurley v. Estes, 6 Neb. 386; Webb v. Hoselton, 4 Neb. 308; Kyger v. Ryley, 2 Neb. 20; Newman v. Samuels, 17 Iowa, 528; Lenox v. Reed, 12 Kan. 223; Fitch v. Wetherbee, 110 Ill. 475; Union Mut. L. Ins. Co. v. White, 106 Ill. 67; Sargent v. Howe, 21 Ill. 148; Flint, etc. Co. v. Auditor-Gen., 41 Mich. 635; Hand v. Winn, 52 Miss. 788; Clark v. Wilson, 53 Miss. 129; Bartlett v. Teab, 1 McCrary (U.S.), 176, s.c., 1 Fed. Rep. 768; Southern R.R. Co. v. Doyle, 11 Fed. Rep. 253.

<br>

### --- Page 0617 ---

§ 629.] TRUSTS UNDER POWER OF SALE, ETC. 1441

In case of default on the part of the debtor, is a deed of trust in the nature of a mortgage. By an absolute deed of trust the grantor parts absolutely with the title, which vests in the grantee unconditionally, for the purpose of the trust. The latter is a conveyance to a trustee for the purpose of raising a fund to pay debts; while the former is a conveyance in trust for the purpose of securing a debt subject to a condition of defeasance. In Martin v. Alter the owner in fee of real estate conveyed the same to a trustee to secure a debt to a third person. After the granting clause to the trustee in fee there was a condition that if the debt was paid at maturity the conveyance was to be void, otherwise the trustee was authorized to sell the land at public sale to pay the same. It was held that this conveyance was a deed of trust in the nature of a mortgage and not an absolute conveyance in trust to secure the debt. The legal title remains in the grantor or mortgagor in possession after default, subject to the right of the trustee or creditor to enforce the condition of the mortgage. The fact that the conveyance is to a trustee, with power of sale in case of default, does not change its character in this respect. A judgment against the grantor who remains in possession of the premises, with the acquiescence of the mortgagee after default, is a lien on said premises, subject to said mortgage. 

1 Hoffman v. Mackall, 5 Ohio St. 124, 130.

2 Martin v. Alter, 42 Ohio St. 94. Powers of sale are not the creatures of statute, but of the convention of the parties. Statutes merely regulate the manner of their execution. A party may grant a valid power of this kind in the absence of any statute either authorizing its creation or regulating its exercise. The Act of 1877, although repealing most of the statutory provisions on the subject, neither prohibited the creation of such powers nor affected the common-law power of parties to make such contracts on such terms as they saw fit. The power in this mortgage is one to sell at public auction. It is true it adds "agreeably to the statute in such case made and provided," that is, the sale shall be conducted in conformity to the provisions of statute, if any, regulating the subject. If it was necessary in order to sustain the power, it would be proper to assume that the parties referred to the unrepealed sections of Title 1, although we think it more reasonable to construe it as referring to the statute in force at the time the power should be executed. But if there was in fact no statute at all regulating the matter, the

<br>

### --- Page 0618 ---

1442

§ 630.

TRUSTS UNDER POWER OF SALE, ETC.

[§ 6380.

Origin and Character of Powers of Sale.—

Mortgages with power of sale or deeds of trust with power to sell and convey without an equity of redemption are of comparatively recent origin. Lord Eldon affirms that in his early practice such powers were unknown in England.

It appears, however, that at a much earlier period these powers came under discussion by the English courts.)

Power is nevertheless complete in itself, and capable of being executed without the aid of any statute. A sale at public auction implies a sale in a public place, after reasonable notice of the time and place of sale, so as to secure the presence of bidders and the best possible price for the property. A power in a mortgage to the mortgagee to sell is in the nature of a trust, and imposes upon him, by implication, the duty to execute it in good faith, and to exercise all reasonable diligence to obtain the best price; and doubtless the usual practice in making sales of real property on judicial process, or under power of sale, would, in the absence of any statute or provision in the mortgage regulating the subject, furnish the test and standard of the mortgagee’s duty in the matter of making sale. These powers are always subject to the control of the courts, if attempted to be exercised in a manner oppressive to the mortgagor. Inasmuch as the Act of 1877 repealed section 13 of title 1, giving the mortgagor a year’s redemption after sale under a power, there might have been a question whether the sale under this power was not absolute, and not subject to any right of redemption. But that question is not here involved, and can never now arise in the present case, as the right of redemption, if any ever existed, has long since expired. We are therefore of opinion that the power contained in this mortgage was, at the time of its creation, a valid common law power, capable of being executed even in the absence of any statute regulating the manner of its exercise. But it is unquestionably true that while the power itself rests upon the convention of the parties, yet the manner of its exercise, being analogous to the remedy in judicial proceedings, is, even as to existing mortgages, subject to legislative change and modification in any way not inconsistent with the express terms of the contract of the parties, and which leaves to the mortgagees an available and effective mode of executing the power.” Webb v. Lewis, 45 Minn. 285, 288.

1 Croft v. Powell (1729), 1 Comyns, 603; Corder v. Morgan, 18 Ves. 344; Curling v. Shuttleworth, 6 Bing. 121; Forster v. Hoggart, 15 Q. B. 155; Clay v. Willis, 1 B. & C. 304; Longwith v. Butler, 8 Ill. 32; Slee v. Manhattan Co., 1 Paige, 57; Lawrence v. Farmers’ Loan & T. Co., 3 Kern. 200; Hyde v. Warren, 46 Miss. 13; Bradley v. Phila. R. R. Co., 36 Pa. St. 141; Fogarty v. Sawyer, 17 Cal. 589; Bronson v. Kinsie, 1 How. 321; Johnson v. Clark, 5 Ark. 321; Cotterell v. Long, 20 Ohio, 464; Shepard v. Richardson, 145 Mass. 32, 86; Martin v. Alter, 42 Ohio St. 94; Rogan v. Walker, 1 Wis. 527; Flagg v. Mann, 2 Sumn. 533; Crocker v. Robert—

<br>

### --- Page 0619 ---

§ 630.] TRUSTS UNDER POWER OF SALE, ETC. 1443

1813 Chancellor Kent affirmed that these powers were not in use in Great Britain, but a few years later he had learned of their existence. After their validity was established their growth was very rapid. From the outset courts of equity have regarded these powers with special disfavor, and where their authority has not been limited by statutory enactments they have uniformly held them invalid. Lord Eldon and other eminent English jurists very earnestly opposed the recognition of such powers, as tending to nullify the equity of redemption, and thus putting the debtor into the power of the creditor. Undoubtedly these powers enable the creditor to take advantage of the necessities of his debtor, and where such power is demanded by the loaner of money, there is great danger, to say the least, that his power will be used in the oppression of the borrower. The Lord Chancellors of England did well, manifested their lofty sense of right and justice, in their opposition to the granting of such powers, and their opinion ought to be decisive, not only with courts of chancery, but as well with modern legislators. But notwithstanding their protests these powers have come into frequent use in England, and in most of the States in this country.

son, 8 Iowa, 404; Fanning v. Kerr, 7 Iowa, 450; Sargent v. Howe, 21 Ill. 149; Woodruff v. Robb, 19 Ohio, 217; Bloom v. Rensselaer, 15 Ill. 505. "The first question presented by this record, as to the mortgagee’s right to sell under a power of sale, without the aid of a court of chancery, is one of considerable importance and about which much diversity of opinion prevails amongst the profession of our State. For this reason, it becomes necessary to examine it with care, and to give it due deliberation. At common law, a mortgage vested the legal title in the mortgagee, liable to be defeated upon performance of the condition. After default, the legal estate became absolute. There is no question that, by the consent of both the mortgagor and mortgagee, the harshness of this rule might be mitigated. The parties were at liberty to prevent the absolute forfeiture, by stipulating that the mortgagee, after default, might sell, so as to evolve the real value of the land and have the debt satisfied, and no more. Such a power was a common law power, an appointment, and, considering the legal estate all the time in the mortgagor, it may be called a power appendant, or annexed to the estate. It seems clear, then, that the power in question would have been a valid one at common law." Longwith v. Butler, 8 Ill.

<br>

### --- Page 0620 ---

1444

§ 631.

TRUSTS UNDER POWER OF SALE, ETC.

[§ 631.

The Validity of Powers of Sale.—The validity of a power of sale in a mortgage or deed of trust is now generally recognized in this country, and in some of the States provision for such powers is made by statute. But in the following, and, perhaps, in some other States, a power of sale in a trust deed or mortgage is treated as void: Illinois, Indiana, Iowa, Kansas, Kentucky, Louisiana, Nebraska, Nevada and Oregon.1 In some of the States it is provided

32,36. As to validity of powers of sale see farther, Hurt v. Kelly, 43 Mo. 238; Elliott v. Wood, 45 N. Y. 71; Dobson v. Rocey, 8 N. Y. 216; Carson v. Blakey, 6 Mo. 273; Destrehan v. Scudder, 11 Mo. 484; McNees v. Swaney, 50 Mo. 388; Clark v. Condit, 18 N. J. Eq. 358; Longwith v. Butler, 8 Ill. 32; Bloom v. Rensselear, 15 Ill. 503; Leffler v. Armstrong, 4 Iowa, 482; Boyd v. Ellis, 11 Iowa, 97; Sims v. Hundley, 2 How. (Miss.) 896; Hyde v. Warren, 46 Miss. 13; Fogarty v. Sawyer, 17 Cal. 589; Bradley v. Chester Valley R. R. Co., 36 Pa. St. 141; Mitchell v. Bogan, 11 Rich. 686; Selby v. Cooling, 23 Beay. 418. In the States following the validity of deeds of trust and power of sale mortgages is sustained: Alabama, Arizona, Arkansas, California, Colorado, District of Columbia, Georgia, Maine, Maryland, Massachusetts, Michigan, Minnesota, Mississippi, Missouri, Montana, Nevada, New Jersey, New Mexico, New York, North Carolina, North Dakota, Pennsylvania, Rhode Island, South Carolina, South Dakota, Tennessee, Texas, Utah, Vermont, West Virginia, Wisconsin, Wyoming. In the following States the contrary principle prevails: Connecticut, Delaware, Florida, Idaho, Illinois, Indiana, Indian Territory, Iowa, Kansas, Kentucky, Louisiana, Nebraska, New Hampshire, Ohio, Oklahoma, Oregon, Washington. “When we use the term, deed of trust, we do not mean a case where the grantor parts wholly with his title, giving it to a trustee absolutely for the purpose of raising a fund to pay debts, though this is, properly speaking, a deed of trust. But we mean cases where the conveyance is to secure a debt in case of default, thus assimilating the transaction to a mortgage, and where the intention of the grantor, instead of parting with his estate, is to retain it in case he performs his legal obligations according to its terms. Instruments of this latter class are also, but less accurately, called deeds of trust, and are those which it is our design to consider. In substance they are mortgages with specific provisions for foreclosing or barring the equity of redemption.” Judge Dillon in 2 Am. Law Reg. 642.

1 Eaton v. Hunt, 20 Ind. 457; Farley v. Eller, 29 Ind. 322; Todd v. Johnson, 51 Iowa, 192; Thompson v. Marshall, 21 Ore. 171; Robey v. Smith, 131 Ind. 342; s. c., 30 N. E. Rep. 1093; Ventres v. Cobb, 105 Ill. 33; Bowman v. Ash, 36 Ill. App. 115; Sanford v. Kane, 24 Ill. App. 504; s. c., 127 Ill. 591; Hamilton v. Lubukee, 51 Ill. 415; Tay-

<br>

§ 631.] TRUSTS UNDER POWER OF SALE, ETC. 1445

by statute that the legal title shall remain in the mortgagor instead of passing to the mortgagee, and that the mortgagee has a mere lien as security for his debt, but the validity of the power of sale is not affected by this rule. In England this power is not only recognized, but is conferred upon the mortgagee by statute, where it is not given in express terms by the mortgage, but it is a condition precedent to the exercise of this power that a notice in writing shall be given to the mortgagor for six months. It was formerly held that a power to mortgage, conferred upon a trustee, did not involve the authority to grant a power of sale. But

lor v. Reid, 103 Ill. 349; Willard v. Boggs, 56 Ill. 163; Harper v. Ely, 56 Ill. 179; Samuel v. Holladay, 1 Woolw. C. C. 400; Webb v. Hoselton, 4 Neb. 308; Hurley v. Estes, 6 Neb. 386; Wheeler v. Sexton, 34 Fed. Rep. 154; Comstock v. Michael, 17 Neb. 288. “The validity of such a power, and of the sale made under it, at common law, may be conceded; and it is also true that in the Nebraska statutes can be found no express prohibition upon such a power; yet it seems to me that the Supreme Court of Nebraska, by two or three decisions at an interval of many years, has ruled against the validity of a sale made under such a power and limited the mortgagee’s remedies to proceedings in court.” Brewer, J., in Wheeler v. Sexton, 34 Fed. Rep. 154.

1 Calloway v. People’s Bank, 54 Ga. 441. “It has always seemed to us a somewhat anomalous doctrine that a mortgagor of real estate may include in the mortgage a power of the creditor himself to sell the mortgaged premises without any order of foreclosure in a regular proceeding; such power being entirely ex parte, and carrying, as claimed, not only the right to ascertain the amount due on the mortgage debt, but to judge of the necessity for a sale, its time, place, terms, etc., and to execute title to the premises so sold. This anomaly is more striking in those States as in South Carolina where it is expressly provided by statute that a mortgage of real estate is a mere security, and, even after condition broken, the legal title remains in the mortgagor or his heirs. We incline to think that experience in the administration of the law has shown that this effort by a summary proceeding, to avoid litigation and expense, has really increased both and demonstrated the wisdom of Lord Eldon when he said: ‘How can it be right that such a clause shall be inserted in a deed under which a party is trustee for himself; * * * while, however, the court will not now set aside a power authorizing the creditor, who is the interested party, to sell lands mortgaged, for the reason that it is the contract of the parties themselves, yet all the authorities agree that as such power may be so easily used for purposes of oppression, the courts should scrutinize sales made under them very closely.’” Johnson v. Johnson, 27 S. Car. 309.

<br>

### --- Page 0622 ---

1446 TRUSTS UNDER POWER OF SALE, ETC.  [§ 682.]

the present tendency appears to be in the direction of holding that the power of sale is an incident of a mortgage, and that a power to mortgage includes the right to give a power of sale.

§ 632. Limitations of Power of Sale in Mortgages and Trust Deeds.—In that class of States in which the equity of redemption has been abolished by statute, as a rule there is some limitation of the power of the mortgagee as conferred by the instrument. In a recent case in Michigan, the court said: “Our statutes may, perhaps, authorize a power of sale, although in some respect variant, to be carried into effect by such notices and proceedings, and subject to such conditions, as are required by law. This introduction of powers of sale into mortgages was, as is well known, a device to escape redemption; but in this country, from the beginning, the legislatures have stepped in, and so regulated the sales as to give them proper publicity, and usually made them subject to some reasonable redemption. As in all other cases of remedy by act of the party, it has been held that every essential provision of law shall be complied with and so appear. The case of a trust may add to it, but it cannot take away from these.

1 Clarke v. The Royal Panopticon, 4 Drew. 26; s.c., 27 L. J. Ch. 207; Sanders v. Richards, 2 Coll. 568; Bridges v. Longman, 24 Beav. 27; Cook v. Dawson, 29 Beav. 123; Cruikshank v. Duffin, L. R. 13 Eq. 555; Vane v. Rigden, L. R. 5 Ch. 663; Bennett v. Union Bank, 5 Humph. 612; Orr v. Rode, 101 Mo. 387. “I am of opinion that a power of sale is a necessary incident to a mortgage, and that when a testator says that a sum of money is to be raised by mortgage, he means it to be raised in the way in which money is ordinarily raised by mortgage, and therefore that the mortgage may contain what mortgages in general do contain, namely, a power of sale. I entirely agree with what the master of the rolls said in Cooke v. Dawson, that a power to mortgage includes a power to give to a mortgagee all such remedies as are proper to be given to him, so as to mortgage the estate on the best terms, and one of these remedies is a power of sale. I will therefore express my opinion that these trustees, who are directed to raise a sum of money by mortgage of their estates in such manner as they shall think fit, are entitled to make a mortgage with a power of sale, but the power must only be exercisable after six months’ notice.” In re Chawner’s Will, L. R. 8 Eq. 568, 569.

<br>

### --- Page 0623 ---

§ 632.

conditions.

TRUSTS UNDER POWER OF SALE, ETC.

1447

Where the trust appears on the face of a deed, it is notice to everyone of the trustee’s character and duties; and it is declared by statute that every deed in violation of such a trust shall be held absolutely void.294

There is some difference in the statutory regulations and decisions in equity in the different States of this class.

In Minnesota, where a mortgage executed in April, 1877, con-

1 Pierce v. Grimby, 77 Mich. 273, 280. The executor of a mortgagee may exercise the power of sale contained in a mortgage, when the deed in terms confers such power upon the mortgagee and his executor. The Act of 1887 was intended to confer the power of sale upon executors and administrators when such power is not given in the deed. Young v. Morrison, 109 N. Car. 520. A mortgage authorized a foreclosure for failure to pay interest or taxes. Default was made both in payment of the interest and of the taxes. Held, that a tender of the principal and interest before the principal was due would not prevent a foreclosure for breach of the condition to pay taxes. Da Silva v. Turner (1896), 166 Mass. 407; S.C., 44 N.E. Rep. 532. A provision in a deed of trust, that if default occurs in the payment of any of the secured notes, then all are to become due is valid, and authorizes the trustee to sell for the payment of all the unpaid notes on default in the payment of any one of them. Meier v. Meier, 105 Mo. 411. We have enjoined foreclosure sales by advertisement upon tender of the amount due, not including attorney's fee; but we have never held such a foreclosure void because of including the attorney fee in the sum for which the land was sold. Emmons v. Van Zee, 78 Mich. 171. A trust deed of land provided for a sale on default, “as in cases of foreclosing mortgages, * * * by bill in chancery, * * * by some suitable person, to be appointed in writing by any person interested in such trust fund.” Held, that a conveyance to a purchaser at a sale conducted as a sale under judicial process, by a person appointed in writing by one interested in the debt, conveyed to him the legal title. Lang v. Stansel (1895), 106 Ala. 389; s.c., 17 So. Rep. 519. A petition in summary proceedings, instituted by the purchaser at a sale under a mortgage, foreclosed by advertisement pursuant to the statute, must allege the existence of a power of sale contained in the mortgage foreclosed, and that the mortgage has been recorded in the county where the property was situated. Cowdrey v. Turner, 85 Hun, 451. The fact that the debt secured by a trust deed is barred by limitations does not preclude the exercise of the power of sale in the deed. Mott v. Maris (1894) (Tex. Civ. App.), 295 S.W. Rep. $25. 23 and 24 Vt. ch. 145, provides that a power of sale is conferred upon mortgagees in all cases, even where none is given by the terms of the mortgage, but it is made a condition precedent to the exercise of such power that six months’ written notice shall have first been given to the mortgagor or his assigns, or posted on the premises.

<br>

### --- Page 0624 ---

1448 TRUSTS UNDER POWER OF SALE, ETC.  [§ 682.

tained a power of sale authorizing the mortgagee, in case of default in the conditions of the mortgage to sell the mortgaged premises at public auction, and convey the same to the purchaser agreeably, it was held that this was a complete and valid common law power, capable of being executed, even in the absence of any statute regulating the manner of its exercise. Also that there is nothing in the retrospective application of the statute, to the mode of executing this power, which conflicts with the terms of the mortgage, or impairs its obligation as a contract. Hence, that a sale under the power made in 1879, in accordance with this statute, was valid.¹ In Maryland the rule has been stated by the court, as follows: “It has repeatedly been held by this court that a power of sale in a mortgage, conferred on the mortgagee, is a power coupled with an interest, and, so being, is appurtenant to the estate, and passes to the executors, administrators and assigns, and is not lost by the death or insanity of the mortgagor; but where the power is conferred upon a third person, who has no interest in the estate, it is a collateral power. The selection of the donee presumably being made, in that case, with reference to the special skill and ability of the individual selected, or by reason of the personal confidence reposed in him by the parties, it is not an incident of the estate conveyed, does not pass on the decease of the donee to his representatives, and can be executed only by the person named, ‘for a matter of personal confidence is not to be extended beyond the express words and clear intention of the settlor.’”²

¹ Webb v. Lewis (1891), 45 Minn. 285; S. C., 47 N. W. Rep. 803.

² Barrick v. Horner (1893), 78 Md. 253; S. C., 27 Atl. Rep. 1111, 1112. Under the constitution and statute of Texas, which provide that no lien can be created upon a homestead except for the purchase money, “or for work and materials used in constructing improvements thereon,” a trust deed upon a homestead to secure money borrowed for the purpose of erecting a building thereon creates no lien. A trustee’s sale under such deed of trust passes no title under the Texas statute which provides that “every sale must be upon judgment rendered by some court of competent jurisdiction, foreclosing such lien and ordering sale.” Ellerman v. Wurz (1890) (Tex.), 14 S. W. Rep. 333.

<br>

### --- Page 0625 ---

§ 633. ] TRUSTS UNDER POWER OF SALE, ETC. 1449

§ 633. Who May Exercise the Power of Sale.—The power of sale, in a deed of trust or mortgage, confers the right to sell, in the event of a failure on the part of the grantor to meet the obligation, upon the grantee. This is an element of the security given by the grantor, and with the instrument may be conveyed to a third person. Where this is done the person who becomes entitled to the money secured by the deed or mortgage may execute the power of sale. It is not material that such conveyance is not in technical language. In a recent case it was held that a mortgagor cannot impeach the validity of an assignment of the mortgage by his mortgagee, and, in an action of ejectment by the purchaser at the mortgage sale, it is proper to exclude evidence that there was no consideration for such an assignment. The Code provides that “where power to sell lands is given to the grantee in any mortgage, * * * the power is part of the security, and may be executed by any person * * * who, by assignment or otherwise, becomes entitled to the money thus secured.” It was held that an assignment of “all our rights, and powers, and title, in and to the within mortgage and note,” was sufficient to authorize the assignee to execute a power of sale contained in the mortgage.1

1 Johnson v. Beard, 93 Ala. 96; s. c., 9 So. Rep. 535. See Bradford v. King, 18 R. I. 743. In Texas a mortgagee may act as trustee to sell under a mortgage to himself, and therefore an employee of a mortgagee may also be authorized in the deed to act as trustee, and a sale by him will not be void merely because he acts by direction of his employer. Randolph v. Allen, 73 Fed. Rep. 23. The executor of a mortgagee may exercise the power of sale contained in a mortgage, when the deed in terms confers such power upon the mortgagee and his executors. The Act of 1887, ch. 147, was intended to confer the power of sale upon executors and administrators when such power is not given in the deed. Yount v. Morrison, 109 N. Car. 520. Under Code of Alabama, which provides that, where a power to sell lands is given to the grantee in any mortgage, the power is part of the security, and may be executed by any person who, by assignment or otherwise, becomes entitled to the money thereby secured, apt words of conveyance are not necessary to entitle the assignee of a mortgage to exercise a power of sale contained therein. Martinez v. Lindsay (1891), 91 Ala. 334; s. c., 8 So. Rep. 787. A sale under a power in a deed of trust is not void because the trustee was

### --- Page 0627 ---

§ 633.] TRUSTS UNDER POWER OF SALE, ETC. 1451  
power. In a recent case in Illinois it was held that a power of sale in a mortgage to a non-resident of the State in which the mortgaged premises are situated may be exercised by the administrator appointed in the State of his residence, as the legal title to the mortgage vests in such administrator, and the power is not one dependent upon the laws of either State relating to administration. In a recent case in Rhode Island trustees under a will held a mortgage belonging to the estate in their individual names. Upon their death their executors assigned the mortgage, and the assignee sold the property, under a power in the mortgage, to one of the succeeding trustees, who sold it to H. H gave a mortgage thereon to the succeeding trustees in the amount of the original mortgage. All of said conveyances were without consideration, and the succeeding trustees sought to foreclose the original mortgage. It was held that the trustees, being the equitable holders of the original mortgage under said conveyances, and not being the "assigns" of the original trustees, within the meaning of that word as used in said mortgage, the foreclosure should be in accordance with the practice of equity. But

terest in the estate nor in the debt. Held, that he had merely a naked power, which on his death did not pass to his legal representatives. The fact that H subsequently became assignee of the mortgage did not enlarge his rights with regard to selling the property, since the power not having been conferred on the mortgagee, H, by merely succeeding to the mortgagee's rights, could not obtain a power which the latter never had. Barrick v. Horner (1893), 78 Md. 243; Ss. C., 27 Atl. Rep. 1111. See also Martinez v. Lindsey, 91 Ala. 334; Young v. Roberts, 15 Beav. 558; Berry v. Skinner, 3 Md. 567; Harnickell v. Orndorff, 35 Md. 341; Cohoes Co. v. Goss, 13 Barb. 137; McGuire v. Van Pelt, 55 Ala. 344; Buell v. Underwood, 65 Ala. 285;

Russum v. Wanser, 53 Md. 92; Levy v. Clary, 38 Mich. 223; Pickett v. Jones, 63 Mo. 195; Bush v. Sherman, 80 Ill. 160; Wilson v. Troup, 2 Cow. 195; Dameron v. Eskridge, 104 N. Car. 621; Williams v. Teachey, 85 N. Car. 402; Holmes v. Turner, 150 Mass. 535; Bourland v. Kipp, 55 Ill. 376; Niles v. Ransford, 1 Mich. 338; Bausman v. Kelly, 38 Minn. 197; Dolbear v. Norduft, 84 Mo. 619; Woonsocket Inst. v. American Worsted Co., 13 R. I. 255; Bradford v. Belfield, 2 Sim. 264; Townsend v. Wilson, 1 Barn. & Ald. 608.

Young v. Roberts, 15 Beav. 558.

Stevens v. Shannahan, 160 Ill. 330.

Bradford v. King (1894), 18 R. I. 743; s.c., 31 Atl. Rep. 166.

<br>

### --- Page 0628 ---

1452 TRUSTS UNDER POWER OF SALE, ETC. [§ 634,

it has recently been held that where a deed of trust authorizes the trustee to sell in case of default, and provides that the beneficiary may substitute another trustee in case the trustee named in the deed fails or refuses to execute it, a sale made by a substituted trustee, where the original trustee has never been asked to make the sale, is invalid, since the trustee could not be said to "fail" to act until he had been requested to act and had omitted to do so.¹

§ 634. The Subject Continued.—In some of the States the right to foreclose a mortgage by advertisement and sale, or to sell under a deed of trust, is regulated by statute. In North Dakota it has been held that to enable a person claiming as assignee of the mortgagee to foreclose a mortgage upon realty by advertisement and sale, the record must show that the legal title to the mortgage is in the assignee.² In the leading case of Wilson v. Troup it was held that in New York it is not necessary to the validity of a mortgage, or of a purchase under a power of sale therein, even as against subsequent purchasers, that the power to execute it should be registered according to the statute, and that it does not lie with the mortgagor to object that a sale and conveyance have been made under the power, it having been recorded.³ In Minnesota it has been held that to authorize the foreclosure by advertisement of a mortgage by an assignee thereof, the assignment must have been duly acknowledged and recorded; and if the same is not properly acknowledged, so as to entitle it to be recorded, the foreclosure is a nullity, but a person who procures an instrument to be executed in the name of another without authority is estopped from disputing the validity thereof, as against anyone acting upon the faith of its genuineness.⁴

¹ Stallings v. Thomas (1892), 55 Ark. 326; s. c., 18 S. W. Rep. 184.
² Lowry v. Mayo, 41 Minn. 388.
³ Morris v. McKnight (1890), 1 N. Dak. 266; s. c., 47 N. W. Rep. 375.
⁴ Wilson v. Troup, 2 Cow. 195; Jackson v. Golden, 4 Cow. 266; Wells v. Wells, 47 Barb. 416. A valid title will pass by a sale of mortgaged premises by an assignee of the mortgage to a bona fide purchaser without notice, under and in pursuance of the provisions of a power of sale contained in the deed.

<br>

### --- Page 0629 ---

§ 635. Delegation of Power of Sale.—Touching the right of a mortgagee, or trustee, to delegate his power of sale, there is some conflict of recent authorities. It has been maintained that a mortgagee, in his capacity as a trustee, may act through another in the sale of property under a power of sale mortgage, but that a trustee under a deed of trust must at least be personally present, and that the sale must be conducted under his direction. In support of this view numerous authorities may be cited. It is held that a mortgagee may employ an auctioneer to conduct the sale, and that his presence at the sale is not essential to its validity. In a very recent case in Georgia it was held that where, by the terms of a mortgage, a power is conferred upon the mortgagee to sell the mortgaged property in satisfaction of the debt secured thereby, such stipulation does not impose upon the mortgagee such a special personal trust as that the sale thereunder could only be conducted by himself in person. The conduct of such a sale is a purely ministerial act, does not in any manner affect the real execution of the power, and may be performed by the mortgagee through the instrumentality of an auctioneer or any similar agent. Some recent decisions sustain the rule as

mortgage, although payment of the mortgaged debt has been tendered to such assignee, if not immediately followed by a suit to redeem, founded thereon; or if, in pursuance of the provisions of the power of sale, the assignee is himself the purchaser, and the title is made to him accordingly, he, by his deed, may thereafter convey a good title to a bona fide purchaser from him without notice. The omission to record the assignment of a mortgage which contains a power of sale will not prevent the assignee from selling the mortgaged premises, under a power of sale contained in the mortgage, if nobody is misled thereby. Montague v. Dawes, 94 Mass. 397.

Landrum v. Union Bank, 6 Mo. 48; Vail v. Jacobs, 62 Mo. 130; Graham v. King, 50 Mo. 22; Bales v. Perry, 51 Mo. 449; Singer Mfg. Co. v. Chalmers, 2 Utah, 542; Wicks v. Westcott, 59 Md. 270; Hoit v. Russell, 56 N. H. 559; Cranston v. Crane, 97 Mass. 459; Youart v. Hopkins, 24 Ill. 326; Fogarty v. Sawyer, 23 Cal. 570; Watson v. Sherman, 84 Ill. 263; Hubbard v. Jarrell, 23 Md. 66.

Palmer v. Young, 96 Ga. 246. See also Parker v. Banks, 79 N. Car. 480; Hubbard v. Jarrell, 23 Md. 66; Palmer v. Hendrie, 28 Beav. 341; Munn v. Burges, 70 Ill. 604; McHany v. Schenk, 88 Ill. 357; Welsh v. Coley, 82 Ala. 363; s. c., 2 So. Rep. 733. But see Dunlap v.

<br>

### --- Page 0630 ---

1454

above given in the case of a sale under a deed of trust.

TRUSTS UNDER POWER OF SALE, ETC.

[§ 635.]  
In a case in West Virginia it was held that a trustee in a deed of trust, executed to him upon a tract of land to secure the payment of money, when required to sell under said trust deed, must be personally present at the time and place of sale and supervise the same, and cannot delegate his powers to a stranger, authorizing such stranger to make the sale in his absence, unless authorized so to do by the deed of trust.  

Wright, 11 Tex. 597; Harris v. Catlin, 53 Tex. 8; Crafts v. Dougherty (1888), 69 Tex. 477; s.c., 6 S. W. Rep. 850; Bitter v. Calhoun (1888) (Tex.), 8 S. W. Rep. 523. A deed of trust given to secure a note provided, among other things: (1) That in case of the absence, death, etc., of the trustee, the sheriff of the county should execute the power of sale conferred upon the trustee. (2) That any statement by "the said trustee" in the deed to be executed by him in pursuance of a sale, as to the non-payment of the note, the advertisement, sale, etc., should be prima facie evidence of the fact. Held (1), that the death of the grantor did not revoke the power of the sheriff to sell. (2) That when the contingency arose in which the sheriff was authorized to act, he became pro hac vice the trustee, and proper recitals in a deed executed by him were to be received as prima facie evidence. White v. Stephens, 77 Mo. 452. Even if a trustee can delegate his power of sale under a deed of trust, one claiming through a sale under the deed of trust, made by another than the trustee, on whom he alleges the trustee had conferred his power, has the burden of proving such delegation. Littell v. Jones (1892), 56 Ark. 139; s.c., 19 S. W. Rep. 497.

Smith v. Lowther (1891), 35 W. Va. 300; s.c., 13 S. E. Rep. 999. "The course marked out for the trustee to pursue must be strictly followed by him, for the method of enforcing collection through such deeds is a harsh one. The grantor of the power is entitled to have his directions obeyed, to have the proper notice of sale given, to have it take place at the time and place and by the person appointed by him. He gives these directions because he thinks that a sale made by the person selected, and under the circumstances stated, will be to his interest and make his property produce the largest amount of money. Of the prescribed conditions, none is more important than that which requires that the trustee shall in person make the sale. He is chosen because of the confidence the grantor has in his integrity and discretion. The trustee, in making the sale, and during the time the property is under the hammer, is expected to protect the interest of the grantor, to see that no fraud is practiced detrimental to his interests, and that no improper bid is accepted, and that the property is not knocked off without giving fair opportunity for it to bring its reasonable value. Perhaps the agent selected by the trustee to attend to this important matter is not one to whom the grantor himself would have intrusted it. He has

<br>

### --- Page 0631 ---

§ 636. | TRUSTS UNDER POWER OF SALE, ETC. 1455  
This rule is sustained in Virginia. In a very recent case the court said: "It is a principle, also, of such transactions that the trustee is charged with a personal confidence, and must, therefore, act in person, and not by agent." But in a recent case in Mississippi, the opposite view is sustained. It was held that the personal attendance by the trustee, at a sale of land under a trust deed, is not essential. He may act through others in advertising and making the sale. All that is necessary is that the terms of the trust deed shall be complied with as to this, and that the trustee shall approve the sale and execute a deed.

§ 636. The Subject Continued.—In most of the States it is held that with respect to all duties which are not either mechanical or ministerial, unless they are specifically prescribed by the instrument, a personal confidence is reposed in the trustee, and this confidence cannot be delegated to another. In Missouri it has been held that a special authority must be strictly pursued, and the office and duties of a trustee, being matters of confidence, cannot be delegated by him to another, unless an express authority to do so be conferred on him by the instrument creating the trust. He is incapacitated from delegating any duty, unless the power is expressly given, which involves the exercise of any discretion or judgment. Mere mechanical or ministerial duties, as, for example, causing advertisements of sale to be put up, proclaiming the sale at auction, and receiving bids, may be done by others. The particular medium of advertisement, the manner of conducting the sale, the best method of offering the property, and the reposed confidence in the party selected by him and that confidence cannot be transferred without his consent. The trustee can no more absent himself while the sale is going on than he can make it at a time or place or for a character of consideration different from that authorized in the deed. Fuller v. O'Neal, 69 Tex. 349.

Morriss v. Virginia State Ins. Co., 17 Gratt. 289, 290; s.c., 18 S.E. Rep. 844; Brickenkamp v. Rees, 69 Mo. 426; Bales v. Perry, 51 Mo. 449; Landrum v. Union Bank, 6 Mo. 48; Vail v. Jacobs, 62 Mo. 130; Smith v. Black, 115 U.S. 308; Connolly v. Belt, 5 Cranch C.C. 405.

Dunton v. Sharpe, 70 Miss. 850.

<br>

### --- Page 0632 ---

1456 TRUSTS UNDER POWER OF SALE, ETC. [§ 636.

Questions of postponement of the sale are matters regarding which, when they are not prescribed by the instrument under which he acts, special trust and confidence are reposed in the trustee, and they cannot be delegated to an agent. The trustee may employ such assistance as he may require in the discharge of his duties, but he must be present at the sale, and it must be conducted in such a manner, under his direct supervision, that the acts of his assistants shall be properly and truly his own acts. In Illinois it has been held that a trustee in a deed of trust has the undoubted right to employ an auctioneer to sell the lands conveyed, and if he is present at the sale, directing and controlling it, this will be a compliance with the terms of the power of sale, and will satisfy the demands of the law. Where a deed of trust is made to two trustees, and each or either is authorized by the instrument to sell in case of default, and the two unite in the notice of sale and in executing the conveyance to the purchaser, the transaction will be held valid though one only of the trustees was present at the sale. But a sale by one will be void unless the instrument

1 Bales v. Perry, 51 Mo. 449.  
2 McPherson v. Sanborn, 88 Ill. 150. See also Taylor v. Hopkins, 40 Ill. 442; Flower v. Elwood, 66 Ill. 488; Munn v. Burges, 70 Ill. 604; Warnecke v. Lembeck, 71 Ill. 91; Pardee v. Lindley, 31 Ill. 174; Strother v. Law, 54 Ill. 413; Bush v. Sherman, 80 Ill. 160; Hamilton v. Lembcke, 51 Ill. 415.  

3 Weld v. Rees, 48 Ill. 428. "We are of opinion that the giving of notice and entry upon the land and the conduct of the auction were all matters which the mortgagee might properly employ others to do under his discretion; that it did not require authority under seal for these purposes." Cranston v. Crane, 97 Mass. 459. See also Garland v. Watson, 74 Ala. 323; Welsh v. Coley, 82 Ala. 363; Hubbard v. Jarrell, 23 Md. 82.
