In Arkansas, it has been held that in appraising property, under the statute in force in Arkansas, no deduction can be made for prior liens. A sale of mortgaged property for less than two-thirds of its appraised value is void, though the amount of the bid exceeds two-thirds of the appraised value, after deducting the amount of a prior lien. The trustee may require that bidders at an auction sale shall give security at the time of the sale for the payment of their purchases. In Mississippi, it has been held that under a power to sell for cash the trustee may properly, by notice, require that ten per cent of any bid shall be deposited when the sale is adjudged.

§ 647. Whether the Estate Shall be Sold in Parcels or En Masse.—Though it is subject to modification both by statutory enactments and by the provisions of the instrument, it is the rule that where an estate consists of several separate tracts or parcels, it is the duty of the trustee or mortgagee to make an effort to sell in parcels before offering the tract as a whole. But where it is left to the discretion of the donee of the power he is bound to make the sale in such manner as best to subserve the interests of all the parties concerned.

Even where the instrument confers the power, while the latter is not entitled as matter of right, either to the protection of his own grant or of the statute. He may be burdened with the expense of one proceeding and deprived of the benefit of the other, at the option of the other party to the contract. It is impossible to suppose that the legislature contemplated this result. Ibid. 211.

1 Griffin v. Marine Co., 52 Ill. 130.

2 Ellenberger v. Griffey, 55 Ark. 268.

3 Smith v. Deeson (1893) (Miss.), 14 So. Rep. 40.

<br>

### --- Page 0662 ---

authorizes one method, he may employ the other, at the request of the grantor, where it is plain that this will secure a better price. In Illinois, it has been held that a clause in a deed of trust authorizing the trustee to sell the premises “entire, without division or in parcels,” as he may “think best,” will not prevent the owner from insisting that it was the duty of the trustee to offer the property in parcels, and where it is shown that a sale in parcels would have been more advantageous, and that the trustee was requested to offer the property in parcels, a sale en masse will be set aside, on bill by the grantor in the trust deed. Such a clause does not give an arbitrary discretion to the trustee.²

² Cassidy v. Cook, 99 Ill. 385. See also Olcott v. Bynum, 17 Wall. 44; Holmes v. Turners Falls Co., 150 Mass. 535; Ellsworth v. Lockwood, 42 N. Y. 89; Wells v. Wells, 47 Barb. 416; Lalor v. McCarthy, 24 Minn. 417; Curry v. Hill, 18 W. Va. 370; Durm v. Fish, 46 Mich. 312; Morse v. Byam, 55 Mich. 594; Johnson v. Williams, 4 Minn. 260; Sumrall v. Chaffin, 48 Mo. 402; Chesley v. Chesley, 49 Mo. 540; Carter v. Abshire, 48 Mo. 300. “The next question is: Did the trustee properly execute his duty in setting up and selling the property as a whole? The deed of trust contained a provision that in default of payment of the note the trustee should sell the property or a part thereof, to satisfy the same. There are cases holding under similar deeds that a trustee is bound to divide the property when he offers it, else the sale will not be good. But whether the deed contains such a clause or not, the rule is firmly established that a trustee, in exercising his duties and powers, is a trustee for both parties, and is bound to act in good faith and adopt all reasonable modes of proceeding, in order to render the sale most beneficial to the debtor. Where property is susceptible of division and it will bring more by being divided and sold in separate parcels or lots, than by being sold in a body, or where, by a sale of a part of the premises, a sufficient amount can be realized to pay off the secured debt, then it is the duty of the trustee to make the division and sell a portion accordingly, and if he fails in this the sale will be held invalid on application of the party aggrieved. There are instances in which the whole of a piece of property will sell for more than it would by being separated, and in all such cases the trustee must exercise a sound discretion. Tatum v. Holliday, 59 Mo. 422, 428. The fact that the whole tract sold in solido would not, generally, bring as much on competitive bidding as though sold in solido, is not good ground of complaint by a mortgagee who was present or represented at the sale, made no objection to the manner of selling, bid all he desired to bid, and himself became the purchaser of all. Middlesex Banking Co. v. Lester (1895) (S. Dak.), 64 N. W.-

<br>

### --- Page 0663 ---

§ 647.] TRUSTS UNDER POWER OF SALE, ETC. 1487  
In Missouri, the rule has been stated, as follows: Where property for sale under a deed of trust will bring more by being sold in separate subdivisions, it is the duty of the trustee to pursue that course, whether the deed contains a direction to that effect or not. But he must sell it in the lump or in parcels, according as will be most beneficial for the debtor; and he will be held to a strict accountability for the exercise of the discretion devolved on him. Where land is sold in parcels and enough of the estate has been sold to satisfy the mortgage or the claim secured by a deed of trust, the sale must be arrested. At that point the power of the trustee or of the mortgagee is exhausted, and any further sale will be treated as a nullity. In Illinois, it has been held that where a deed of trust is given ‘upon several  

Rep. 168. Fraud or prejudice to some one must be shown as the result of a failure of a trustee to sell the trust lands in separate parcels, before equity will set the sale aside on that ground. Kerfoot v. Billings (1896), 160 Ill. 563. A foreclosure sale, under a power of sale in a mortgage, of separate tracts of land, as one tract is not void but only voidable. Ryder v. Hulett, 44 Minn. 353. It is not enough to set aside a sale under a power, that the land was sold in parcels, and not in solido, and that the beneficiary’s agent had made a map from which he read the descriptions of the parcels to the trustee, and used it in making his bids without showing it to the other bidders, there being no evidence that anyone wished or asked to see it. Smith v. Deeson (1893) (Miss.), 14 So. Rep. 40.  

1 Carter v. Abshire, 48 Mo. 300.  

2 Baker v. Halligan, 75 Mo. 435; Charter v. Stevens, 3 Denio, 33; Pryor v. Baker, 133 Mass. 460.  
“The conclusion is unavoidable that grave error was committed on this side of the proceedings, and that the power of sale was unlawfully executed in respect to the number of parcels sold. When enough had been sold to satisfy the mortgage debt and all costs and expenses, the power was exhausted, and the holders of the mortgage were without authority to make sale of more parcels. The statute recognizes this natural and plain limit, and, in order to guard against abuse, expressly forbids the sale of more parcels, tracts or lots than shall be necessary to satisfy the amount due at the date of the notice of sale, with interest and the costs and expenses allowed by law.” Grover v. Fox, 36 Mich. 461, 471. It is the duty of a trustee not to sell more of a trust subject, than is necessary to satisfy the trust, unless the interest of the owner demands it, or he requests it. And whether the interest of the owner requires it, the trustee in the exercise of his discretion must determine. Curry v. Hill, 18 W. Va. 370.

<br>

### --- Page 0664 ---

tracts of land to secure a specified indebtedness, and the trustee is authorized to sell the land and pay the particular debt, and also all costs, commissions and liens on the land, and the holder of the particular indebtedness purchases a judgment rendered against the maker after the execution of the deed of trust, which is a lien upon the land, the authority of the trustee is not exhausted when he has sold enough of the land to pay the particular debt, but he may sell enough to pay the particular judgment also, provided the judgment is a bona fide debt. It has been held that where an estate, subject to a mortgage, is sold by the mortgagor in parcels at different times, the mortgage debt shall be satisfied, first out of that portion of the estate retained by the mortgagor, and then out of the parcels aliened, in the inverse order of alienation. This rule may, however, be controlled by recitals in the deed showing a different intent.

§ 648. Adjournment of Sale.—It is well established that a trustee or mortgagee conducting a sale of property under a power of sale mortgage or deed of trust, has power to adjourn the sale whenever it is required by the interests of the parties concerned. Where this is necessary, in order to secure a fair price for the property, there may be more than one adjournment. It is a general rule that a public officer, upon whom a power of sale is conferred by law, may adjourn an advertised public sale to a different time and place for the purpose of obtaining a better price for the property. In Richards v. Holmes, Mr. Justice Curtis,

1 Hall v. Gould, 79 Ill. 16.

2 Stephens v. Clay (1892), 17 Colo. 489; s. c., 30 Pac. Rep. 43.

3 Tinkcomy, Purdy, 5 Johns. 345; Russell v. Richards, 11 Me. 371; Fairfax v. Hopkins, 2 Cranch C. C. 134; Miller v. Hull, 4 Denio, 104; Westgate v. Handlin, 7 How. Pr. 372; Warren v. Leland, 9 Mass. 265; Hosmer v. Sargent, 8 Allen, 97; Dexter v. Shepard, 117 Mass. 480; Thompson v. Heywood, 129 Mass. 401; Briggs v. Briggs, 135 Mass. 306; Russell v. Richards, 11 Me. 371; Johnston v. Easton, 3 Ired. Eq. 330; Meyer v. Jefferson Ins. Co., 5 Mo. App. 245; Vail v. Jacobs, 62 Mo. 130; Thornton v. Boyden, 31 Ill. 200; Griffin v. Marine Co., 52 Ill. 130. That a sale, authorized under a deed of trust, is postponed for three years, is no such presumption of fraud as will avoid it, when no possession of the

<br>

### --- Page 0665 ---

§ 648.] TRUSTS UNDER POWER OF SALE, ETC. 1489  
referring to this rule said: “If such a power is implied where the law, acting in invitum, selects the officer, a sortior, it may be presumed to be granted to a trustee selected by the parties.” And as he has the right, so, also, it is his duty to adjourn the sale where it is required by a regard for the interests of the parties concerned. He is bound to make an honest and earnest effort to secure a fair price for the property, and wherever it appears essential to the accomplishment of this end he may adjourn the sale. In Massachusetts it has been held that in the execution of a power of sale in a mortgage of personal property, the
    
    
land conveyed, nor other benefit,  
is reserved to the grantor. Starke  
v. Etheridge, 71 N. Car. 240. If the  
sale is properly commenced on the  
day advertised and is not completed at the close of that day, it may be continued to the next day without a new publication. Lallance v. Fisher, 29 W. Va. 512. The notice of the adjourned sale need not be published for the same length of time as the original notice unless required by the statute. Jackson v. Clark, 7 Johns. 217; Sayles v. Smith, 12 Wend. 57; Westgate v. Handlin, 7 How. Pr. 372; Dana v. Farrington, 4 Minn. 433. If the notice of adjournment clearly describes the premises and gives the time and place of sale it will suffice. Richards v. Holmes, 18 How. 143; Dexter v. Shepard, 117 Mass. 480. A sale on Washington’s birthday, a legal holiday in most of the States, will not be invalid for that reason. Stewart v. Brown, 112 Mo. 171. “The postponement was in these words: ‘Note, the sale of the above property is postponed to Wednesday, the 8th day of September next. James Rogers.’ Of this postponement no notice was given on the door of the court house, and it was so far disregarded, that the sale  

94  

took place according to the original notice. Whether there could be a postponement before the day of sale, unless upon a six months’ notice, as the act directs, is one question; but it is a different question, whether, after a public notice of postponement by the mortgagee, he could proceed to sell at the time first appointed, disregarding wholly the postponement. If this was a sale by a sheriff, the law would protect the purchaser, and hold the sale valid; but in case of an insufficient notice, it would punish the officers. In this case the regularity of the sale is to be made out as a part of the purchaser’s title, and if irregular he takes nothing by his deed. In my opinion the sale is irregular and void; the mortgagee, after publicly postponing the sale, which was a thing wholly under his control, was bound by it, and could not so far disregard it, as to proceed on the original notice. If the contrary position should be upheld, it would enable mortgagees to commit frauds by selling, after they had, by their own acts, lulled the mortgagor into security.” Jackson v. Clark, 7 Johns. 214, 225.

Richards v. Holmes, 18 How. 147, 148.

<br>

### --- Page 0666 ---

1490 TRUSTS UNDER POWER OF SALE, ETC. [§ 649.

mortgagee has a right, in the exercise of a reasonable discretion, to adjourn the sale from time to time, without doing so, through the agency of a licensed auctioneer or giving any new notice to the mortgagor.1 In a case in Missouri the court said: "The trustee, we think, abused his discretion in not adjourning the sale. He had the power to do so, and, that being so, there can hardly be a question as to his duty."2

§ 649. Who May Purchase at a Sale under a Power.—It is well settled that a mortgagee under a mortgage with power of sale cannot purchase the property, either directly or through the agency of a third person, at his own sale. In his relation to the estate mortgaged, such mortgagee is a trustee. He represents not only his own interests but also the interests of the mortgagor. As a trustee he is subject to the rules by which trustees are governed in such cases. But while this is the rule, it may be set aside, or rendered inoperative by an express provision of the instrument, or by a statutory provision.* But in any event he is

Car. 450; Whitehead v. Hellen, 76 N. Car. 99. Where, in an action by a pur-

§ 649.] TRUSTS UNDER POWER OF SALE, ETC. 1491

bound by the requirements of his position to absolute fairness and good faith, and any lack in this regard will invalidate the sale. In the absence of a statutory regulation, or of a provision of the instrument, he cannot even sell to a firm of which he is a member. He cannot become a purchaser either in severalty, in joint tenancy or in any other form or manner. But it has been held that where the mortgagee has purchased through a third person, and the property is subsequently conveyed to a bona fide purchaser without notice, the mortgagor will not have the right to redeem as against such purchaser after the expiration of the statutory period.

chaser at a mortgage sale to recover the land from the mortgagor (the mortgagee being joined as party plaintiff), the judge presiding at the trial charged the jury that the burden was on the plaintiff to prove everything fair and honest, and no advantage taken of the defendants, it was not error to refuse to charge the jury that the burden of proof was on the plaintiff to show that he was not the partner or agent of the mortgagee when he bought the land. McMillan v. Baxley, 112 N. Car. 578. “It is perfectly well settled that a mortgagee, with a power of sale, cannot sell to himself, either alone or with others, nor to a trustee for himself; nor to any one employed by him to conduct the sale. A sale by a person to himself is no sale at all, and a power of sale does not authorize the donee of the power to take the property subject to it at a price fixed by himself, even although such price be the full value of the property. Such a transaction is not an exercise of the power, and the interposition of a trustee, although it gets over the difficulty so far as form is concerned, does not affect

In an English case, at a sale

the substance of the transaction.” Farrar v. Farrar, L. R. 40 Ch. D. 395, 409. See also Downes v. Grazebrook, 3 Meriv. 200; Robertson v. Norris, 1 Giff. 421; Whitcomb v. Minchin, 5 Madd. 91; Martinson v. Clowes, L. R. 21 Ch. D. 857.

Rowe, 2 Cal. 387; Rutherford v. Williams, 42 Mo. 18; Whitehead v. Hellen, 76 N. Car. 95; Robinson v. Amateur Assn., 14 S. Car. 148.

Ransford, 1 Mich. 338; Thurston v. Prentiss, 1 Mich. 193; Rutherford v. Williams, 42 Mo. 18; Robinson v. Cullom, 41 Ala. 693; Blockley v. Fowler, 21 Cal. 326; Benham v. Rowe, 2 Cal. 387. The mere fact that nearly two years after the sale the purchaser conveyed the property to the mortgagee, does not create a sufficiently strong pre-

<br>

### --- Page 0668 ---

1492 TRUSTS UNDER POWER OF SALE, ETC. [§ 650.

by auction under the direction of a building society as mortgagees, the secretary of the society openly bid for and became the purchaser of two lots on his own account. There was no proof of undervalue. It was held that, under the circumstances, the sale to the secretary could not be maintained as against the mortgagor. In a recent case in Illinois it was held that where a mortgage confers a power of sale upon the mortgagee, and a third party becomes a purchaser at a sale under such power, for the benefit of the mortgagee, the sale will be invalid, and may be set aside at the instance of the holder of the equity of redemption, as against the purchaser. In another recent case it was held that if the mortgagee becomes the purchaser at his own sale under a power in the mortgage, without the consent of the mortgagor, the latter may disaffirm the sale within a seasonable time, and claim a redemption and account; but not having exercised this right himself, he cannot assign or convey it to another person, so as to authorize the assignee to disaffirm and redeem. In New Hampshire, it has been held that an express stipulation inserted in a mortgage of real estate, that the mortgagee may sell the premises on failure of performance of the conditions by the mortgagor, is a valid grant of a power of sale; and a sale fairly made in pursuance of such power will be upheld. In such a case the mortgagee cannot be a purchaser at the sale.

§ 650, The Subject Continued.—Where a mortgage is pledged as security for a debt, and the property is sold under the power of sale in satisfaction of the pledgor’s debt, the pledgee cannot be a purchaser at the sale.

Assumption that the former was acting for the latter at the sale, to overcome the positive testimony of both to the contrary. Burr v. Borden, 61 Ill. 389.

§ 650.]  TRUSTS UNDER POWER OF SALE, ETC. 1493

purchase by a mortgagee of the mortgagor, will be upheld, unless it appears that the mortgagee took advantage of his position to obtain the equity of redemption at an inadequate price.¹ In England, it is held that the relation of trustee, as between the mortgagee and mortgagor, is not established until the sale has been made and the debt satisfied.² Where a third person has purchased at a mortgage sale, in good faith, the mortgagee may purchase of him. With the sale and the proper disposal of the proceeds, the fiduciary relation of the mortgagee ends. But where there is a previous agreement or understanding between the parties that the purchase shall be made for the purpose of a reconveyance, the trust will still attach to the mortgagee and the conveyance will be void, or at least voidable.³

¹ Roberts v. Fleming, 53 Ill. 196; Harrison v. Roberts, 6 Fla. 711; Walthall v. Rives, 34 Ala. 9.

² Kirkwood v. Thompson, 2 Hen. & M. 392. "Now that a mortgagee is in some sense a trustee for the mortgagor may be admitted, for every person in whom the legal estate is vested with a beneficial interest for another person, in a sense is a trustee for that person. In some sense, a mortgagee is in a worse position than a trustee, for a trustee in an ordinary case is not liable to a decree for willful default unless a special case be proved against him; whereas, such a decree is merely of course as against a mortgagee in possession. On the other hand a trustee can never make a benefit to himself by any dealing with the trust property; but if a second mortgagee should buy in the first mortgage for half its amount, or even obtain an assignment without consideration from the first mortgagee, I can have no doubt he would be entitled to charge the mortgagor with the full amount of the first mortgage in addition to his own." Wigram, V.C., in Dobson v. Land, 8 Hare, 216.

³ Munn v. Burges, 70 Ill. 604; Bush v. Sherman, 80 Ill. 160; Whitehead v. Hellen, 76 N. Car. 95; Hoit v. Russell, 56 N. H. 559. In the absence of fraud it was no objection that one of the beneficiaries bid off the land and afterwards directed that the deed be made to plaintiff. Jones v. Hagler (1891), 95 Ala. 529; s.c., 10 So. Rep. 345. The assignee of a mortgage has the right to purchase at the foreclosure sale, where the mortgage contains a power of sale. Ward v. Ward (1896) (Ala.), 19 So. Rep. 354. The fact that a purchaser at a sale under a trust deed had acted as intermediary in making the loan which the deed was given to secure did not render his purchase fraudulent as against the grantor; he being the agent of the borrower, and not of the loan company. Seip v. Grinnan (1896) (Texas), 36 S. W. Rep. 349. Where

<br>

### --- Page 0670 ---

1494 TRUSTS UNDER POWER OF SALE, ETC. [§ 650.

A recent case the rule was stated as follows: Where, at a sale under a power in a mortgage, third persons purchase the land, and afterwards inform the mortgagee that they cannot pay the purchase money, whereupon the mortgagee agrees to take the land at their bid, but there was no arrangement whatever between him and them at the time of the sale, the mortgagee is not a purchaser at his own sale, and hence the sale is effectual to cut off the equity of redemption. In Massachusetts it has recently been held that where, at the foreclosure of a mortgage, the trustees use reasonable efforts to obtain the value of the land, a sale to the mortgagee for one-fifth less than he had bid before, the prior sale at which such bid was made having been defeated by failure of the mortgagor to comply with his bid thereat, which was the highest, will not be set aside. In Alabama it has recently been held that, where, on default in a mortgage, the mortgagee, under a power, sells the land and becomes the purchaser, though no conveyance is made under the sale, he has both the legal and equitable title, and can recover possession, the mortgagor not having taken steps to redeem.

The mortgagee bid a certain sum at the trustee’s sale, but withdrew the bid at the suggestion of the trustee, and afterwards bid in the property for a much smaller sum, the sale was void. Fishburne v. Smith (1891), 34 S. Car. 336; s. c., 13 S. E. Rep. 525. “If it be true that a trustee who makes a sale of real estate under a trust deed is forever thereafter prohibited, owing to the relation he has occupied, from dealing in the trust property, then this transaction may be regarded as fraudulent in law; otherwise not. While public policy and fair dealing will not allow a trustee to, either directly or indirectly, become the purchaser of property at a sale which he has made, yet we do not understand that the doctrine has been so far extended in any court as to prohibit the trustee, for all time to come, from, in good faith, dealing in the property.” Watson v. Sherman, 84 Ill. 263, 266.

2 Stevenson v. Dana (1896), 166 Mass. 163; s. c., 98 N. E. Rep. 128.

3 Hambrick v. N. E. Mortgage Sec. Co. (1893), 100 Ala. 551; s. c., 13 So. Rep. 778. “There is considerable conflict of authority upon this question, some of the courts holding that a mortgagee under such a power can legally purchase the mortgaged property, and one of the best considered cases, viz., Howard v. Davis, 6 Texas, 174, assigns as a reason why a mortgagee should have the power to

<br>

### --- Page 0671 ---

§ 651.] TRUSTS UNDER POWER OF SALE, ETC. 1495

§ 651. The Same Subject.—The rule governing the purchase by a mortgagee at his own sale, as stated in the foregoing sections, applies in the main to the purchase by a trustee under a power of sale in a deed of trust. It has been held that where a trustee sells to a third person, and, subsequently, purchases the property of him, such purchase will not vitiate the original sale. In Missouri, it has been held that a trustee cannot buy for his own benefit at the trust sale, and his purchase at such sale will inure in equity to the use of the beneficiaries.

Purchase at such a sale, the following: "A mortgagee is a trustee, but in a qualified sense. He does not hold for the benefit of others, but for himself. He is a cestui que trust as well as trustee. He has an interest in the property. It is pledged expressly to secure his claim, and were he deprived of the power to purchase he might suffer a great loss by its sale at a low price. He has an interest that the bid shall amount to his incumbrance, and that the property be not sacrificed, to the injury as well of the mortgagor as the defeat of his own claim, as this may be the only fund for the discharge of his debt. Sales at foreclosures, whether under a power or by decree, are open and public, and are made after long notice, and it is to the interest of the mortgagor that the mortgagee shall enter into the competition at the sale." While in other of the States the doctrine prevails that a purchase by a mortgagee of the mortgaged property under such a power, is ipso facto voidable. In some States such a sale may be avoided by a disaffirmance thereof by the mortgagor within a reasonable time. The rule may be stated to be, in such cases, that an unauthorized purchase by the mortgagee arms a mortgagor, or his successors in title, with an option to have the sale declared invalid and the right of redemption established. It would seem that such a purchase is good for all purposes, except that it does not bar the mortgagor’s equity of redemption. The mortgagor may elect to abide the sale, or at his election he may redeem; and while in the execution of the power there might be such fraud as would of itself avoid the sale, and which would authorize a court of equity to set it aside without reference to the equity of redemption still remaining in the mortgagor, yet if the sale be fairly made and free from fraud, it cannot be attacked and set aside as a void sale simply because the mortgagee was himself the purchaser thereat. Besides, according to the authorities, there is less strictness in applying the rule to cases of a mortgagee purchasing at his own sale under the power, than there is in the case of a trustee purchasing. A mortgagee in such a case is not merely a trustee, but he is also a cestui que trust, and as such has an interest to protect." Palmer v. Young, 96 Ga. 246, 248.

Stephens v. Beall, 22 Wall. 329.

<br>

### --- Page 0672 ---

1496 TRUSTS UNDER POWER OF SALE, ETC. [§ 651.

to be reimbursed to the full amount of what he has expended for the property. Where the beneficiaries do this they are entitled to the benefit of the purchases. In a case before the Supreme Court of the United States, Mr. Justice Hunt stated the rule, as follows: "There is in a purchase by a trustee nothing that of itself and necessarily vitiates the original sale. Whether culpable or commendable depends upon the circumstances of each case. It may be wrong and it may be right. It may be approved by the parties interested and affirmed. It may be condemned by them and avoided. Where it is found that the transaction is itself perfectly fair and honest, that the purchase was not contemplated at the original sale, but was first thought of years afterward, and was then made for a full and fair consideration actually paid by the trustee, and after the fiduciary duty was at an end, we find no authority to justify us in pronouncing the original sale to have been fraudulent."

The rule in Missouri is stated by the court, in Johnson v. Atchison, as follows: "Our statute gives the grantor in a deed of trust the right to redeem within one year, if the property is bought by the cestui que trust, or his assignee, or by any other person for him. But the statute provides that: 'No party shall have the benefit of the preceding section until he shall have given security to the satisfaction of the circuit court, for the payment of the interest to accrue after the sale, and for all damage and waste that may be occasioned or permitted by the party whose property is sold. In case the circuit court is not in session, such security may be taken by the clerk of said court.'"

In general, where a sale under a trust deed is made by a trustee who has no interest in the sale, the beneficiary may purchase. The

1 Lass v. Sternberg, 50 Mo. 124. See also Thornton v. Irwin, 43 Mo. 153; Rea v. Copeland, 47 Mo. 83; Torry v. Bank of New Orleans, 9 Paige Ch. 663. Where the trustee is one of the secured creditors, who are empowered by the trust deed to bid at the sale, it is not a good objection that the sale was made to the trustee’s attorney for the benefit of all the creditors. Kennedy v. Dunn, 58 Cal. 339.

2 Stephen v. Beall, 22 Wall. 329, 341.

3 Johnson v. Atchison, 90 Mo.

<br>

### --- Page 0673 ---

§ 652. TRUSTS UNDER POWER OF SALE, ETC. 1497  
holder of a note secured by a trust deed may be a purchaser, and where he cannot be present at the sale he may authorize the auctioneer to make a bid for him, and if the sum which he names is the highest that is bid, and he becomes the purchaser the sale will be valid. But if there is any misrepresentation or unfairness, in consequence of which the property was sold for less than it would otherwise have brought, the sale may be avoided.

§ 652. Statutory Regulations.—In some of the States the right of the mortgagee to purchase at his own sale under a power is determined by statute. In New York the mortgagee is empowered to purchase, but without regard to the statute, the courts have held that he has the right to purchase at his own sale. In Elliott v. Wood, the Court of Appeals of New York held that the right of parties to agree upon the terms of a power of sale of mortgaged premises given to the mortgagee is clear, and courts have never assumed to control this right in the absence of fraud or some statutory regulation upon the subject. Parties may, in such cases, contract for private sale, and even without notice. But in general these statutory regulations are so framed as to protect, at least to some extent, the rights and interests of the debtor. Where there is evidence of fraud or unfairness by which the debtor is wronged the sale will be voidable.

Richards v. Holmes, 18 How. 143; Smith v. Black, 115 U. S. 308.

Bloom v. Renselaer, 15 Ill. 503.

3 Rev. St. (6th ed.) p. 847, § 7.

4 Elliott v. Wood, 45 N. Y. 71. See also Slee v. Manhattan Co., 1 Paige, 48; Bergen v. Bennett, 1 Caines’ Cas. 1; s. c., 2 Am. Dec. 281; Hubbell v. Sibley, 5 Lans. 51; Lansing v. Goelet, 9 Cow. 345; Nat'l Fire Ins. Co. v. Loomis, 11 Paige, 431.

5 The premises were bid in by Mr. H, it is said, for the plaintiff. Mr. H testified in substance that he bid them in for himself and would have kept them if he had not been afraid of a lawsuit. But we assume that he really purchased them for the plaintiff. The statute allows the mortgagee, or his assigns, to purchase the premises at the sale providing he acts fairly and in good faith in making the sale. § 3531, R. S.; Maxwell v. Newton, 65 Wis. 261, 267. Gen.-Stat. R. I., ch. 165, § 15, allows the holder of a mortgage to purchase the mortgaged estate at a public auction under the powers given in the instrument, provided that a

<br>

### --- Page 0674 ---

§ 653.  
TRUSTS UNDER POWER OF SALE, ETC.

[§ 653.  
Sale at an Inadequate Price.—A trustee or mortgagee in selling property under a power of sale is bound to secure a fair price for it as far as that is practicable.

He is not at liberty to accept the sum required to pay the claim secured by the mortgage or deed of trust, where that would involve a sacrifice of a large part of the value of the property. In a case in Missouri, it was held that where a trustee permits property to be sacrificed under his deed of trust, by sale for a little over a tithe of its value,—as where property worth from $5,000 to $8,000 is sold for $1,000,—the sale will, on timely application, be set aside.

Certain prescribed notice thereof be given to the mortgagor. Held, that this notice must be given to anyone holding the equity of redemption under or through the mortgagor to make such purchase valid. McLaughlin v. Hanley, 12 R. I. 61. A sale of mortgaged property by the executors of the assignee of the mortgage can be made without the previous order of the orphans’ court, it being simply a proceeding for the collection of a debt. And the purchase of the property by the executors at such sale is unobjectionable, as section 14 of article 66 of the Code gives to the mortgagee the right to buy at his own sale, and authorizes his legal representatives to do so. Chilton v. Brooks, 71 Md. 445.

1 Vaill v. Jacobs, 62 Mo. 294; Judge v. Booge, 47 Mo. 544; Maloney v. Webb, 112 Mo. 575; s. C., 208 W. Rep. 683; Burns v. Middleton, 104 Ill. 411; Bowman v. Ash, 36 Ill. App. 115; Parmley v. Walker, 102 Ill. 617; Laclede Bank v. Keeler, 109 Ill. 385; Hoodless v. Reid, 112 Ill. 105; Cleaver v. Green, 107 Ill. 67; Chilton v. Brooks, 71 Md. 445; Condon v. Maynard, 71 Md. 601; Horsey v. Hough, 38 Md. 130; Bradford v. McConibay, 15 W. Va. 732; Lallance v. Fisher, 29 W. Va. 512; Robinson v. Amateur Assn., 14 S. Car. 148; Alexander v. Meservey, 35 S. Car. 417; MeNair v. Pope, 100 N. Car. 404; Kennedy v. Dunn, 58 Cal. 339; King v. Bronson, 122 Mass. 122; Wing v. Hayford, 124 Mass. 249; Landrum v. Union Bank, 63 Mo. 48; Keiser v. Gammon, 95 Mo. 217; Kline v. Vogel, 11 Mo. App. 211; Million v. McRae, 9 Mo. App. 344; Hudgins v. Morrow, 47 Ark. 515; Maxwell v. Newton, 65 Wis. 261; Cross v. Allen, 141 U. S. 528; Smith v. Black, 115 U. S. 308; Hitz v. Nat’l Life Ins. Co., 3 McArthur, 170; Loveland v. Clark, 11 Colo. 265. Inadequacy of price paid for land at a mortgage foreclosure sale thereof will not of itself avoid or affect the sale. Ward v. Ward (1896) (Ala.), 19 So. Rep. 354. Mere inadequacy of price, unaccompanied by fraud or unfair dealing, is not a sufficient ground for setting aside a sale under a mortgage or deed of trust. Hardwicke v. Hamilton, 121 Mo. 465. The fact that land sold for less than its worth, at foreclosure sale under a trust deed, is

<br>

### --- Page 0675 ---

§ 653.

TRUSTS UNDER POWER OF SALE, ETC.

In his opinion in this case Mr. Justice Sherwood said:  
"Neither the law nor the parties intend that the trustee shall be a nose of wax, a mere figurehead, in the hands of the creditor and of the auctioneer.

Not conclusive evidence of fraud on the part of, or collusion between, the purchaser and the trustee, requiring the sale to be set aside.  
Seip v. Grinnan (1896) (Tex. Civ. App.), 36 S.W. Rep. 349. "The sale made by H of the premises in the deed of trust described, under the decree appointing him successor in trust, is sought to be set aside by denial of the indebtedness, claiming the same to have been paid and by reason of the value of the property being much greater than the amount bid thereon. There is no evidence to show the payment of the indebtedness, but, on the contrary, the existence of the indebtedness and the good faith of the parties to the deed and the decree is shown; knowledge of the time of the sale existed, and the fact that the value of the property was much greater than the amount bid at the sale would not authorize the sale to be set aside where the transaction was shown to be in good faith."  
Bowman v. Ash, 36 Ill. App. 115, 119. See also Parmly v. Walker, 102 Ill. 617; Cleaver v. Green, 107 Ill. 68; Burns v. Middleton, 104 Ill. 411.

The price of $100 for property worth $500, sold under a trust deed, is not so grossly inadequate as to raise the presumption of fraud.  
Harlin v. Nation (1894), 126 Mo. 97; S.C., 278 W. Rep. 330. "The resolution of this question is sometimes very difficult, for the reason that the authorities on the subject are exceedingly scarce, and the cases are very rare in which courts have set aside sales of this sort because the consideration paid was inadequate. The basis of the rule is well understood, and all the decisions on the subject are gathered in works on equity under the general head of constructive fraud. The general doctrine is that where there are no other inequitable incidents which furnish a basis for the decree, it will not be entered because the price is inadequate, unless the case very clearly demonstrates that the difference between the value of the property and the price that it brought is such as to shock the judgment and conscience of the chancellor."  
Martin v. Barth, 4 Colo. App. 346, 349.

Where the opinions of witnesses as to the value of the property vary widely, and no other facts appear to discredit the sale, or to raise doubts as to its fairness, inadequacy of price is not a reason for setting it aside.  
Chilton v. Brooks, 71 Md. 445. "The second ground for exception, inadequacy of price, has been passed upon frequently by this court in other cases. Mere inadequacy of price is not sufficient to set aside a sale, unless it be so gross as to indicate want of reasonable judgment and discretion, or misconduct and fraud in the mortgagee, or some mistake or unfairness for which the purchaser is responsible. But, where there is any other just cause to doubt the propriety of the sale, it is always a consideration very proper to be viewed by the court in connection

<br>

### --- Page 0676 ---

1500 TRUSTS UNDER POWER OF SALE, ETC. [§ 654]

tion to act fairly by all interested, and when he fails in his duty in this regard, the sales he makes will be set aside on timely application. In a recent case it was held that the question whether or not a sale made under a decree of foreclosure should be confirmed, rejected or set aside, and resale ordered, is within the sound judicial discretion of the court, and an order setting aside such sale and ordering a resale may be made by the court when, in its opinion, the price bid for the property is so inadequate as to work injustice to the parties, though the proceedings on the sale may have been regular, and the sale fairly made. But the mere fact that property is sold for much less than its value, and for much less than it was possible to realize at private sale, will not invalidate the transaction. The mortgagee or trustee may act in good faith and with entire fairness, and still be unable to sell the property for its full value. In a recent case in Illinois, it was held that the fact that property sold under a power of sale in a deed of trust does not bring as much as it could be made to bring at private sale by judicious management and unusual advertising, is no sufficient reason for setting aside such a sale, where there have been no unfair or fraudulent practices.

§ 654. The Rights of the Purchaser.—The purchaser at a sale by a trustee under a power of sale in a mortgage or deed of trust, subject to certain limitations, assumes the responsibility of his purchase.

The legal rule of caveat emptor applies, that the sale has been made at a reduced price. Condon v. Maynard, 71 Md. 601, 606. See also Chilton v. Brooks, 69 Md. 587; Mahoney v. Mackubin, 52 Md. 306.

1 Vail v. Jacobs, 62 Mo. 130, 134.

2 State v. Campbell (1894), 58 Dak. 636; s. c., 60 N. W. Rep. 32. Where, on a foreclosure sale under a deed of trust, it appeared that the property was sold in bulk for an inadequate price, and at an unusual hour, and that the debtor’s interest was thereby sacrificed, it was held that the sale should be set aside and the debtor be permitted to redeem. Montgomery v. Miller, 131 Mo. 595. To set aside a sale under a deed of trust for inadequacy of price, irregularity in, or want of sufficient notice of sale, or because of incumbrances over the land at the time of sale, a party must proceed without unreasonable delay. Fowler v. Lewis (1892), 36 W. Va. 112; s. c., 148 E. Rep. 447.

3 Hoodless v. Reid, 112 Ill. 105.

<br>

### --- Page 0677 ---

§ 654.] TRUSTS UNDER POWER OF SALE, ETC. 1501

emptor applies to purchases of this character. It will be presumed that he had notice of any irregularities of which the trustee or mortgagee may have been guilty in conducting the sale. In a recent case it was held that the rule of caveat emptor applies to trustee’s sales, and the purchaser is, in proceedings by a party injured, conclusively charged with notice of irregularities by the trustee in executing the power of sale.

Stephens v. Clay (1892), 17 Colo. 489; s.c., 30 Pac. Rep. 43.  
“A sale by a trustee, like a sale by a commissioner, is without warranty; but there is this obvious difference between the two, the contract of a purchaser at a sale by the commissioner is incomplete till his bid is accepted by the court, who is the real seller of the property; the commissioner of sale being the mere agent of the court. The bid is accepted by the court by the confirmation of the sale; after that, though the purchaser before the deed is made to him, finds out that the title to the land is defective; he is, nevertheless, bound to receive it and pay the purchase money. In a sale by a trustee, the court does not accept the bid of the purchaser, but it is accepted by the auctioneer, when he knocks the land down, and on the making by him of a memorandum of sale and its terms signed by the auctioneer, the contract for the sale is as complete as the contract for the sale made by a commissioner is when the court accepts the bid by confirming the sale. After such knocking down of the land by the auctioneer and the making of such memorandum, the purchaser must accept the deed and pay the purchase money, though he does find the title defective. He must, if he

But this is not operative against a mere

wishes to do so, investigate the title in this case, as in the other, while the contract is incomplete, that is, in the last case, before the land is knocked down to him.” Fleming v. Holt, 12 W. Va. 143, 156. A purchaser of land, under a power of sale contained in a mortgage, who, after he has taken possession of the land, pays a tax, assessed upon the land, cannot maintain an action against the subsequent mortgagee to recover the amount of the tax so paid. Swan v. Emerson, 129 Mass. 289. If a deed conveys real and personal property to a trustee upon trust to sell the property for cash or on credit at any time he thinks proper and to pay over the proceeds to an infant, when he attains the age of twenty-one, whether the property be sold before or after the infant attains his majority, the purchaser is not bound to see to the application of the purchase money. The question, is a purchaser bound to see to the application of the purchase money? has reference to the state of things at the time the deed is made, and depends entirely upon the wording of the deed and not on the state of facts which may exist when the sale is actually made. Woodwine v. Woodrum, 19 W. Va. 67. The property to be sold was described in a trustee’s notice of sale, and also announced at the

<br>

### --- Page 0678 ---

1502 TRUSTS UNDER POWER OF SALE, ETC. [§ 654.

remote purchaser. In Illinois it has been held that the original purchaser at a sale by a mortgagee, under a power of sale contained in the mortgage, is chargeable with notice of defects and irregularities attending the sale, and cannot evade their effect. But with remote purchasers the rule is different; all matters in pais, affecting the sale, it not being void, but only voidable, must be brought home to their knowledge, on a proper case made, sustained by proof. Where the purchaser unites in a fraud with the mortgagee, and, in consequence of the fraud, the sale is invalid, the purchaser will not be able to plead his title against a subsequent mortgagee. In a recent case in Massachusetts it was held, that if the owner of the equity of redemption of land purchases at a sale made in pursuance of a power contained in a mortgage, and the sale is invalid on account of the fraud of the mortgagee participated in by the purchaser, he cannot, as against a subsequent mortgagee, set up title

sale, as consisting of eighty acres, and was sold en masse at a certain price per acre. It was subsequently ascertained that the tract contained but twenty-three acres. It was held that the purchaser could not recover back from the trustee the amount per acre paid for the number of acres short, but that he would be entitled to have the sale set aside on the ground of mistake. Coons v. North, 27 Mo. 73. Where a sale of land was had under a power of sale in a mortgage, at a time when the mortgagor was residing in one of the rebellious States, during the late war, and the purchaser at such sale was a stranger to the mortgage, without notice of any reason why the power could not properly be exercised, he would be protected against any claim on the part of the debtor to a right of redemption based upon the fact of the inability of the latter to communicate with his creditor; and the same protection

would be accorded to a bona fide vendee of such purchaser, although he held the mortgage by assignment at the time of the sale. Willard v. Boggs, 56 Ill. 163. Only the parties to the sale can take advantage of any defects or irregularities in the memorandum made by the auctioneer; and if they complete the sale without objection, by payment of the purchase money, and execution and acceptance of a deed, it does not lie in the mouth of the mortgagor, whose lands were thus sold under a power contained in the mortgage, when sued by the purchaser, to object to the sufficiency of the memorandum made by the auctioneer, or to the fact that the mortgagee’s agent acted as auctioneer at the sale. Lewis v. Wells, 50 Ala. 198.

'Hamilton v. Lubukee, 51 Ill. 415; Pierce v. Grimley, 77 Mich. 273; Equitable Trust Co. v. Fisher, 106 Ill. 189; Wood v. Augustine, 61 Mo. 46.

<br>

§ 654.] TRUSTS UNDER POWER OF SALE, ETC. 1503

through the prior mortgage, but this will be deemed to have merged. Where there is a mistake made at a sale by which the purchaser is a sufferer, equity will recognize his claim to relief. In a recent case in Minnesota it was held that where, by reason of a mistake in the computation of interest on a note secured by a mortgage, there is claimed to be due in the notice of foreclosure sale a larger sum than is legally due, and the premises are bid in by the mortgagee for the sum so claimed, but in good faith, believing that he is only bidding for the sum actually due, and the mortgagor is attempting to recover by action against the mortgagee, as surplus, the excessive interest so computed and included in the sum bid, and the premises are of less value than the sum actually and legally due, the mortgagee may be afforded equitable relief, and a resale ordered. Where from any cause the sale is invalid, and fails to convey the legal title, the purchaser will take the place of the mortgagee. In Missouri it has been held that a purchaser at a mortgagee’s sale, who has paid the money, is substituted to the rights of the mortgagee; and, in case the foreclosure is not legally conclusive, he cannot be dispossessed by the mortgagor by action of ejectment, but the mortgagor can redeem the property. In a more recent case the same court held that

Taylor v. Agricultural Association, 68 Ala. 229. Where at a void foreclosure sale the mortgagee becomes the purchaser of the mortgaged premises, and enters into possession thereof, and then sells and attempts to convey such premises by deed, such deed operates as an assignment of the mortgage debt, as well as the mortgage securing the same, to the grantee in such deed, and each successive deed to said premises by persons holding under such mortgage must have the same effect. If for any cause in the foreclosure suit, the proceeding is ineffectual to foreclose the mortgage and the mortgagee purchases at a sale under such

a foreclosure sale and payment of the purchase money which do not pass the legal title to the land, owing to a misdescription in the advertisement and deed, do not operate as a discharge of the debt and mortgage, but confer on the purchaser an equitable right to the security of the mortgage for the amount of the mortgage debt.

§ 655. Subsequent Purchaser without Notice.—Where the deed from the trustee to the purchaser is in due form, and contains no stipulations that indicate any irregularity in the sale, and the purchaser has no notice of any irregularity, he will be protected in equity as an innocent purchaser. In a case in Illinois it was held that a purchaser under a trust deed containing a power of sale is chargeable with notice of defects and irregularities attending the sale, and their effect cannot be evaded by him. But the rule is different as to remote and subsequent purchasers. If there is nothing on the face of the deed from the trustee in a deed of trust to the purchaser, showing that the sale was made in violation of, and contrary to, the power contained in the deed of trust, a subsequent purchaser who has no notice, in fact, of any irregularity in the sale by the trustee, will be protected as an innocent purchaser. In a later case, before

void proceedings and enters into the possession under such sale, his relation to the mortgaged premises is that of mortgagee in possession. Cooke v. Cooper, 18 Ore. 142. See also Niles v. Lansford, 1 Mich. 388.

Lanier v. McIntosh, 117 Mo. 508.

Gunnell v. Cockerill, 79 Ill. 79. One of the trustees became the owner by assignment of the note, the payment of which the deed of trust was given to secure, but the purchaser at the sale had no notice of this, and believed that the sale was regularly made for the benefit of the payee of the note. Held, the purchaser's title to the property was not affected by the fact that one of the trustees had purchased the note. Carey v. Brown, 62 Cal. 373. Where a paid-up mortgage, containing a power of sale duly recorded therewith, is allowed to remain undischarged of record, and to be regularly foreclosed by advertisement under the statute without objection, a purchaser at the sale, without notice and for value, upon duly recording his certificate of purchase, duly executed and acknowledged, together with the affidavits of publication and sale provided by statute, will acquire a valid title to the property upon the expiration of the year without redemption as

<br>

### --- Page 0681 ---

§ 656.] TRUSTS UNDER POWER OF SALE, ETC, 1505  
the same court, this rule was sustained. It was held that  
an innocent purchaser of land without notice of any irregularity in the sale to his grantor under a power in a mortgage, will be protected in his title against an equitable right to avoid the sale as to his grantor, and notice will not be presumed.?

§ 656. The Setting Aside of a Sale.—Where a sale has not been conducted in accordance with the provisions of the power, and it appears that the trustee or mortgagee has been guilty of a fraudulent, unfair or oppressive act in the execution of the power of sale, equity will afford relief by setting aside the sale, and by granting an opportunity of redemption.?- Where it is shown that the mortgage was given against the mortgagor and his assigns. Merchant v. Woods, 27 Minn. 396. A foreclosure of a mortgage by virtue of a power under the statute, is not founded on any judgment or decree of any court; but is the mere act of the mortgagee. The statute makes such sales a conclusive bar only in favor of a bona fide purchaser, without notice; and the mortgagee, being a party to the usurious contract, is in no better situation than if no foreclosure had taken place. Jackson v. Dominick, 14 Johns. 435. A purchaser of land who has no notice of any irregularities in the proceedings by which his vendor acquired title, will be protected against the same. Where real estate was sold under a deed of trust, and the proceedings were such that the former owner might have had the sale set aside in equity, and he, with full knowledge of the irregularities of the sale, stands by and sees the purchaser sell to a third party without notice, and interposes no claim or objection, and allows such third person to pay out his money for the property and make large expenditures in improvements, such second purchaser will be protected against the former owner. Jenkins v. Pierce, 98 Ill. 646. Where a trustee’s deed, made upon a sale under a valid deed of trust, shows the sale to have been made in strict conformity with the power contained in the trust deed, and the purchaser had had no notice of any irregularities in the sale, his title will be protected as respects any such irregularities, if any there were, as that of an innocent purchaser. Hosmer v. Campbell, 98 Ill. 572.

1 McHany v. Schenk, 88 Ill. 357. See also Hamilton v. Lubukee, 51 Ill. 415. +

2 Matthie v. Edwards, 2 Coll. 465; Orme v. Wright, 3 Jur. 19; Jones v. Matthie, 11 Jur. 504; Bigler v. Waller, 14 Wall. 297; Chappell v. Gould, 42 Md. 466; Wicks v. Westcott, 59 Md. 270; Grover v. Fox, 36 Mich. 461; Equitable Trust Co. v. Fisher, 106 Ill. 189; Ventres v. Cobb, 105 Ill. 33; Webber v. Curtiss, 104 Ill. 309; Waller v. Arnold, 71 Ill. 350; Weld v. Rees, 48 Ill.
