1506

428; Longwith v. Butler, 8 Ill. 32; Benkendorf v. Vincenz, 52 Mo. 441; Soule v. Ludlow, 3 Hun, 603; s.c., 6 Tomp. & C. (N.Y.) 24; Hubbell v. Sibley, 5 Lans. (N.Y.) 51; Jencks v. Alexander, 11 Paige, 619; Leet v. McMaster, 51 Barb. 236; Ellsworth v. Lockwood, 42 N.Y. 89; Montague v. Dawes, 14 Allen, 369; Hood v. Adams, 124 Mass. 481; Thompson v. Heywood, 129 Mass. 401; Briggs v. Briggs, 135 Mass. 306; Littell v. Grady, 68 Ark. 584. "One who undertakes to execute a power of sale is bound to the observance of good faith and a suitable regard for the interests of his principal. He cannot shelter himself under a bare literal compliance with the conditions imposed by the terms of the power. He must use a reasonable degree of effort and diligence to secure and protect the interests of the party who intrusts him with the power. A stranger to his proceedings, finding them all correct in form, and purchasing in good faith, may not be affected by his unfaithfulness. But whenever his proceedings can be set aside without injustice to innocent third parties, it will be done upon proof that they have been conducted in disregard of the rights of the donor of the power. When a party who is intrusted with a power to sell attempts also to become the purchaser, he will be held to the strictest good faith, and the utmost diligence for the protection of the rights of his principal." Wells, Justice, in Montague v. Dawes, 14 Allen, 399. A judgment creditor, whose lien is subordinate to a trust deed, cannot attack the title of a purchaser at a sale by the trustee on the ground that a former owner of the trust note had promised the judgment creditor to give him

TRUSTS UNDER POWER OF SALE, ETC.

[§ 656.]

notice of the sale, but which he had refrained from doing, at the instance of the purchaser, and by reason of which the creditor did not learn of the sale and was thus prevented from bidding. The public notice was all the notice to which the creditor was entitled. Tishomingo Sav. Inst. v. Duke (1887) (Miss.), 1 So. Rep. 165. "The court will require entire good faith in a mortgagee acting under a power; but if he acts in such good faith, and fully conforms to the terms of his power, we cannot set aside a sale because it is a hardship upon the mortgagor. The hardship, if any, results from the contract of the mortgagor, and we cannot relieve him from it without violating the rights of the mortgagee." Learned v. Geer, 139 Mass. 31, 32. Where the owner of the equity of redemption knows of defects attending the sale under a power in a mortgage, he must, as a general rule, proceed with diligence in his application to set the sale aside, or a court of equity will refuse him relief. No particular time is prescribed in which such relief must be sought, but each case must depend on its own facts and circumstances. Nichols v. Otto, 132 Ill. 91. "It thus appears that the property was sold in bulk for an inadequate price, at an unusual hour, and the plaintiff's entire interest in the property sacrificed thereby. While no one of these facts would of itself afford ground for setting aside the sale, yet when all of them are found concurring as they do in this sale, otherwise not void of suspicion, the sale should be set aside, and the plaintiff be permitted to redeem, on the authority of many cases decided in this court, of which it is only nec-

<br>

### --- Page 0683 ---

§ 656. TRUSTS UNDER POWER OF SALE, ETC. 1507

Without any consideration, the sale may be set aside, though the execution of the power was in all respects regular. Irregularities or defects in the execution of the power that are unimportant, and that have not wrought any injustice or injury to any party in interest, will not affect the title. Any irregularity in the sale may be waived by the debtor, if he so elect, and in the absence of an express agreement he may pursue such a course that his act will be construed as a waiver. In a recent case it was held that acquiescence for an unreasonable length of time in a sale under a power in a mortgage, which is merely voidable at the election of the person who has the equity of redemption, will, if unexplained, operate as a confirmation of the sale, and a waiver of all objections to it; but when the sale is absolutely void, such effect will not be attributed to acquiescence, or a failure to seek redemption, until after the expiration of the period prescribed as a bar by the statute of limitations. In a case where one conveyed to a trustee, for the purpose of paying his debts, all his interest in the goods in a certain store, his books, notes, accounts, etc., and the trustee sold the whole at public sale for a price upon which he fixed, it was held that the person who made the conveyance, being present at the sale and not objecting, was bound by it, at least at law, however irregular the sale may have been. In order to avail himself of his rights under an irregular or fraudulent sale the debtor must use all due diligence in the prosecution of his claim. An unnecessary and unreasonable delay will be construed as a waiver of his right. In a recent case in Minnesota it was held that under the statute  

essary to cite the few following as examples: Holdsworth v. Shannon, 113 Mo. 508; Stoffel v. Schroeder, 62 Mo. 147; Kelly v. Hart, 61 Mo. 463; Montgomery v. Miller, 131 Mo. 595, 599.

1 Walker v. Carleton, 97 Ill. 582.

2 Corrothers v. Harris, 23 W. Va. 177; Woerther v. Backhoff, 12 Mo. App. 586.

3 Sloan v. Frothingham, 63 Ala. 46; How. Pr. 441; Cornell v. Newkirk, 144 Ill. 241; Hoyt v. Pawtucket Sav. Inst., 110 Ill. 399; McDonald v. Stow, 109 Ill. 40; Breit v. Yeaton, 101 Ill. 242; Nichols v. Otto, 132 Ill. 91; Maher v. Farwell, 97 Ill. 571; Bush v. Sherman, 80 Ill. 160.

4 Lamb v. Goodwin, 320.

5 Ired.

<br>

### --- Page 0684 ---

1508 [§ 657.  
of that State an action to set aside a foreclosure sale made under a power must be brought in all cases with reasonable diligence, and not later than five years. What may be determined to be reasonable diligence must depend largely upon the circumstances of each case. But notice which is fairly sufficient to put a person upon inquiry is sufficient to create the obligation of subsequent diligence.

TRUSTS UNDER POWER OF SALE, ETC.

§ 657. The Subject Continued.—In an action to set aside a sale under a trust deed or mortgage with power of sale the burden of proof is on the complainant. In a recent case in a bill to set aside a sale made under a trust deed complainants alleged that they were present all day at the time and place appointed for the sale, and neither heard nor saw any sale; and they averred, on information and belief, that there was no public sale as required by the terms of the deed. The grantee of the purchaser at such sale in her answer denied all knowledge, information and belief of fraud or concealment in the sale.

1 Marcotte v. Hartman, 46 Minn. 202; s. c., 48 N. W. Rep. 767. See also Bacon v. Northwestern Mut. Life Ins. Co., 131 U.S. 258; Demarest v. Wynkoop, 3 Johns. Ch. 145; Learned v. Foster, 117 Mass. 89; Dempster v. West, 69 Ill. 613; Irish v. Antioch College, 126 Ill. 474; Butterfield v. Farnham, 19 Minn. 85; Fowler v. Lewis, 36 W. Va. 112; Helen v. Yerger, 61 Miss. 44; Fraker v. Houck, 36 Fed. Rep. 403; Askew v. Sanders, 84 Ala. 356; Bausman v. Eads, 46 Minn. 148. Where a trustee, under a trust deed given to secure a note without any consideration, had advertised the premises for sale, the fact that the grantor may have indirectly recognized the power to sell, by asking and obtaining a postponement of the sale, will not estop him from afterwards insisting there was no power to sell. A delay of only eight months in bringing suit to set aside a sale under a deed of trust is not unreasonable, and will not bar the suit. Walker v. Carleton, 97 Ill. 582. An unexplained delay of seventeen years in filing a bill to set aside a sale of land by a trustee in a deed of trust upon the ground of mere irregularities in the sale, is such laches as will defeat relief. Kerfoot v. Billings, 160 Ill. 563. In the absence of ratification, the right of a mortgagor to avoid a sale under a power where the mortgagee has indirectly become the purchaser, is not barred by his laches for a shorter period than the statutory limitation of ten years. Jones v. Pullen, 115 N. Car. 465. The mortgagor is not required to bring an action to set aside such unauthorized sale before the expiration of the year of redemption. Hull v. King, 38 Minn. 349.

<br>

### --- Page 0685 ---

§ 658. | TRUSTS UNDER POWER OF SALE, ETC. 1509

a sufficient denial as to her of the charge of fraud, and puts the complainants upon proof of the charge. In a recent case it was held that a sale of lands under a mortgage, in strict conformity to the terms of the power, cuts off the equity of redemption as effectually as a decree of foreclosure; and it cannot be impeached by the mortgagor or his heirs, because credit was extended to the purchaser, nor because the mortgagee afterwards agreed to take the land in payment of his bid, there being no prearrangement between them that he would do so, nor because no conveyance was executed between them. Where an estate is incumbered with two mortgages, and the holder of the first mortgage proposes to sell under a power of sale, it is his duty to give notice to the holder of the junior mortgage of such intention. And where the trustee or mortgagee promises the holder of the junior mortgage to give such notice, a failure to fulfill such promise will invalidate the sale. In a recent case it was held that where the trustee in a first deed of trust promises to inform the holder of a second deed of trust of the former’s intention to sell, and the circumstances attending the sale show it was an unfair one and that it was made for speculative purposes, on the part of both the trustee and the purchaser, such sale will be set aside. It has been held that parties in interest, who are infants at the time of sale, cannot assent to irregularities therein, nor be estopped by reason of failure to take steps to set the same aside, and upon reaching their majority they may pursue their remedy.

§ 658. The Right of Redemption.—Where lands are sold under a power of sale mortgage, or deed of trust, and

1 Hamilton v. Halpin, 68 Miss. 99; 8 So. Rep. 739. See also Tartt v. Clayton, 109 Ill. 579; Bush v. Sherman, 80 Ill. 160; LaFrance v. Fisher, 29 W. Va. 512; Burke v. Adair, 23 W. Va. 95; Graham v. Fitts, 53 Miss. 307; McNew v. Booth, 42 Mo. 192; Forrester v. Scoville, 51 Mo. 268; Kennedy v. Kennedy, 57 Mo. 76; Forrester v. Moore, 77 Mo. 651; Jackson v. Wood, 88 Mo. 77; Keiser v. Gammon, 95 Mo. 217; Hayes v. Frey, 54 Wis. 503.

2 Durden v. Whetstone, 92 Ala. 480.

3 Cassady v. Wallace, 102 Mo. 575.

4 Tatum v. Holliday, 52 Mo. 422.

<br>

### --- Page 0686 ---

1510 TRUSTS UNDER POWER OF SALE, ETC. [§ 658.
the power is executed in strict compliance with the provisions of the instrument, the sale cuts off the equity of redemption, and leaves the debtor to such rights as may be conferred by statute. He must seek redress, if at all, in the manner which the statute prescribes. It is the object of a power of sale in a mortgage or deed of trust to cut off the equity of redemption, and where this is not prohibited by statute the debtor is left to such protection as the statute may provide. In a recent case it was held that a sale of lands under a mortgage, in strict compliance with the terms of the power, cuts off the equity of redemption, and reduces it to a mere statutory right which must be asserted on the conditions prescribed, even though no conveyance is executed to the purchaser; if no sufficient memorandum of the sale was made to meet the requirements of the statute of frauds, neither the mortgagor nor his heirs can take any advantage of its insufficiency, so long as the mortgagee and the purchaser recognize the sale as valid and binding, nor can they complain that credit was extended to the purchaser, while the mortgage required a sale for cash. The power of sale will be upheld and the equity of redemption will be cut off only as the power is exercised for the purpose for which it was conferred. In an English case, Vice-Chancellor Sir J. Stuart, in delivering the opinion, said: "Wherever a power is given, whether it be a power of raising portions for

1 Newburn v. Bass, 82 Ala. 622.
A power of sale in a mortgage, conferred on the mortgagee himself, is valid, and may be executed in parts without resort to a court of equity. Such a power is irrevocable, and, when fairly executed, cuts off the equity of redemption. Hyde v. Warren, 46 Minn. 13. A sale of lands under a power contained in a mortgage or deed of trust for the benefit of a creditor, cuts off the mortgagor's equity of redemption as effectually as a decree of strict foreclosure, and leaves nothing in him but the statutory right or privilege of redemption, which is not subject to levy and sale under execution at law. Bailey v. Timberlake, 74 Ala. 221. A trustee’s sale of land under a trust deed operates as a complete foreclosure and cuts off a junior deed of trust. Plum v. Studebaker Bros. Co., 89 Miss. 162. Where a mortgagor has an equity of redemption, subject to a power of sale, and the land mortgaged is actually sold after forfeiture, the right of the mortgagor is entirely extinguished. Paschal v. Harris, 74 N. Car. 335.

<br>

### --- Page 0687 ---

§ 658. ] TRUSTS UNDER POWER OF SALE, ETC. 1511  
children, a power of sale in a mortgage deed or a power giving dominion over property for a particular and specified purpose, the court requires that the power shall be exercised with a view only to that which is the legitimate purpose for affecting which the power was conferred. The legitimate purpose for which the power to sell in this defendant’s mortgage deed was given was to secure to him repayment of his mortgage money. If he uses the power to sell which he gets for that purpose for another purpose, from any ill motive to effect means and purposes of his own, or to serve the purposes of other individuals, the court considers that to be what it calls a fraud in the exercise of the power, because it is using the power for purposes foreign to the legitimate purpose for which it was intended.’ Where the right of redemption exists it must be

¹ Robertson v. Norris, 4 Jur. (N. S.) 155, 157. A bond for $32,000, given for a loan of $16,000, was secured by a deed of trust, by which the trustee was empowered to sell upon the non-payment of any semi-annual installment of interest, or the sale of the land for taxes. Pending its sale in consequence of such defaults, the debtor and creditor entered into negotiations for a settlement; the latter stating that he wanted his money merely and not the land. A written stipulation was prepared by their attorneys, and, although it was not signed by the creditor, it was understood that he would do so, and that the title would be purchased by him at the sale, and held only as security for the debt, and that the debtor would be allowed to redeem. The latter, relying upon this understanding, did not attend the sale, or procure the attendance of bidders, and the land was sold en masse to the creditor for $8,500, although it was divided into ten lots and consisted of two tracts, fronting on different streets, and was worth at least $30,000. It was held that the debtor should be allowed to redeem. Stinson v. Pepper, 47 Fed. Rep. 676. Where, on a foreclosure sale under a deed of trust, it appeared that the property was sold in bulk for an inadequate price, and at an unusual hour, and that the debtor’s interest was thereby sacrificed, it was held that the sale should be set aside and the debtor be permitted to redeem. Montgomery v. Miller, 131 Mo. 595. The beneficiary in a trust deed agreed with the grantor that the property should be sold by the trustee and bought in by the beneficiary and then conveyed in fee to the grantor, who was to give a mortgage back upon an extension of the loan. The sale was accordingly made, and the property bought in by the beneficiary, who refused to carry out the arrangement, and quitclaimed the property to a third person having notice. It was held that the trans-

<br>

### --- Page 0688 ---

1512 TRUSTS UNDER POWER OF SALE, ETC. — [§ 699.
exercised, directly, by the mortgagor. The right cannot be assigned or otherwise conveyed. It has been held that if the mortgagee becomes the purchaser at his own sale under a power in the mortgage, without the consent of the mortgagor, the latter may disaffirm the sale within a seasonable time, and claim a redemption and account; but, not having exercised this right himself, he cannot assign or convey it to another person, so as to authorize the assignee to disaffirm and redeem.

§ 659. The Right of Redemption Continued.—Where the debtor secures a decree of the court enabling him to redeem, on account of irregularities in the sale, there is no fixed and definite period for the redemption. The court will give him a reasonable time to pay the debt secured, and the time will be made longer or shorter according to the circumstances. In a recent case in Illinois it was held that a sale of land under a power in a deed of trust is not void for irregularities in the notice and sale, but only voidable; and when the debtor files his bill to set aside such a sale and for leave to redeem, if he is allowed to redeem, he should be required to pay the sum due, within a reasonable time, and on his failure to pay as required his bill should be dismissed, and the sale under the trust deed should be allowed to stand confirmed and unimpeached. In Alabama the period to extend the time for redemption is held to supersede the action was in effect a mortgage, and the grantor was entitled to redeem, as against such purchaser with notice. Union Mut. Life Ins. Co. v. Slee, 123 Ill. 57.

MeCall v. Mash, 89 Ala. 487. A purchaser from a mortgagor, with notice of the mortgage and the power of sale therein, and the provision for extinguishing the equity of redemption by exercise of such power, cannot redeem from the sale under such power. Maulding v. Coffin (1894), 6 Tex. Civ. App. 416; s.c., 258 W. Rep. 480. An agreement for a valuable consideration made during the statutory foreclosure sale and deed. Dodge v. Brewer, 31 Mich. 227. Taxes voluntarily paid by the purchaser after the foreclosure sale cannot be added to the amount required to be paid for redemption. Nopson v. Horton, 20 Minn. 268.

Burgess v. Ruggles, 146 Ill. 506. Under a valid statutory foreclosure of a mortgage, the time of redemption cannot be extended to await the determination of a suit by a second mortgagee for an accounting, for use or rent of the premises had or received by the...

<br>

### --- Page 0689 ---

§ 659.] TRUSTS UNDER POWER OF SALE, ETC. 1513  
statute allows the mortgagor or debtor two years after the sale for redemption, but all persons interested in the equity of redemption must claim their rights during that period. In Aiken v. Bridgeford it was held that a junior mortgagee or assignee of the equity of redemption is not included in the terms of the statute, which gives a right to redeem lands sold under a judgment, decree, or power of sale in a mortgage; and the right to redeem, by any of the persons upon whom it is conferred, must be asserted within two years after a sale under a power in the mortgage. In Powers v. Andrews it was held that the statutory right of redemption is limited to the persons on whom it is expressly conferred; and it is not conferred upon a junior mortgagee or assignee of the equity of redemption. In Michigan it is limited to the first mortgagee pending the time of redemption. The amount due must be paid or tendered within the time fixed by the statute, or the time stipulated, if an extension has been agreed on by the parties. Hoover v. Johnson, 47 Minn. 434. See also Bovey Lumber Co. v. Tucker, 48 Minn. 223; Nopson v. Horton, 20 Minn. 268; Todd v. Johnson, 50 Minn. 508; Lowry v. Akers, 50 Minn. 341; Cable v. Minneapolis Stock Yards Co., 47 Minn. 417.

1 Aiken v. Bridgeford, 81 Ala. 295.

2 Powers v. Andrews, 84 Ala. 289. "It has often been said by this court that this right of redemption under the statute is purely the creature of legislation, and has no existence without it. It is essentially different from the equity of redemption, recognized by the common law. That right is property, capable of sale by transfer, or under execution, or decree of a chancery court. It can only be exercised before a foreclosure of the mortgage under a decree of a court of equity, or before a sale, under a power in the mortgage. It cannot be exercised after a valid foreclosure either under a power of sale or under a decree, unless in the case of voidable sales, where the mortgagee has acted as both seller and purchaser without the consent of the mortgagor, so as to justify the court in setting aside the sale for constructive fraud. The statutory right of redemption, on the contrary, comes into existence only after the equity of redemption proper has been cut off by sale or foreclosure. Until then, it would seem, it can only spring into life. And we have uniformly decided that this privilege is neither property, nor the right of property that is not subject to levy or sale as such under execution, and that it is a right or privilege personal to the debtor. It necessarily follows from these principles, which are now too well settled to be disturbed, that the statutory right of redemption can only be exercised by the persons named in the statute, in the mode, within the time and upon the conditions there prescribed."

<br>

### --- Page 0690 ---

151d TRUSTS UNDER POWER OF SALE, ETC.  [§ 660.

has been held that inadequacy of price cannot vitiate a statutory foreclosure sale which is otherwise fair and regular; for the owner of the equity of redemption cannot be prejudiced thereby, since he may always redeem within the year, by refunding the amount paid, with interest at the rate fixed by the statute. In Missouri the rule has been stated as follows: Land sold under a deed of trust and bought by the cestui que trust or his assigns is subject to redemption by the debtor within one year from the date of sale, if he shall give security to the satisfaction of the Circuit Court or to the clerk in vacation for the payment of interest after the sale, and for all damage and waste that may be occasioned or permitted by the party whose property is sold.

§ 660. The Power of Sale a Cumulative Remedy.— The equitable remedy of foreclosure is not barred or in any way abridged, although in construing the statute it must be interpreted liberally in favor of the debtor to prevent the oppressive sacrifice of his estate. Powers v. Andrews, 84 Ala. 289, 291. See also Downs v. Hopkins, 65 Ala. 588; Cramer v. Watson, 73 Ala. 127; Parmer v. Parmer, 74 Ala. 285; Otis v. McMillan, 70 Ala. 46, 62; Childress v. Monette, 54 Ala. 317; Mewburn v. Bass, 82 Ala. 626; Cooper v. Hornsby, 71 Ala. 62; Seale v. Pheiffer, 77 Ala. 278. A sale in execution of the power given in the mortgage, after the mortgagor's equity of redemption has been sold under execution, cuts off the equity of redemption acquired by the purchaser, as effectually as a decree of strict foreclosure would, and leaves nothing but the statutory right of redemption, which is the personal privilege of the debtor, not the subject of levy and sale under execution at law, and cannot be asserted by a purchase at execution sale before the statutory right has arisen. Childress v. Monette, 54 Ala. 317.

1 Cameron v. Adams, 31 Mich. 426, 428. "Courts of equity have larger powers for relief against the consequences of inevitable accident in private dealings, and may doubtless control their own process and decrees to that end. But we think there is no such power to relieve against statutory forfeitures. Where a valid legislative act has determined the conditions on which rights shall vest or be forfeited, and there has been no fraud in conducting the legal measures, no court can interpose conditions or qualifications in violation of the statute. The parties have a right to stand upon the terms of the law. This principle has not been open to controversy, and is familiar and elementary." Cameron v. Adams, 31 Mich. 426, 428.

2 Godfrey v. Stock, 116 Mo. 403.

<br>

### --- Page 0691 ---

§ 660. | TRUSTS UNDER POWER OF SALE, ETC. 1515

manner disturbed by a power of sale. The power of sale in mortgages and trust deeds is not regarded by courts of equity with favor. Though this power is upheld, where it is not prohibited by statute, foreclosure in chancery is encouraged. In Mississippi it has been held that when a mortgage contains a power to the mortgagee to sell the mortgaged premises, that power does not preclude the equitable remedy of foreclosure. The utmost that can be said is that the power of sale is cumulative to the remedy by foreclosure. But, on the contrary, so peremptory is the remedy by sale, so great the temptation to act oppressively or unfairly, and so completely is the debtor in the power of his creditor, that courts of equity will watch the proceedings very closely, and, on very slight cause, will construe the sale strictly against the mortgagee. And it is upon such considerations that this court, while upholding the power of sale as valid, has commended foreclosure in chancery as the better practice. In California it has been held that a power of sale contained in a mortgage is merely a cumulative remedy and does not affect the right to foreclosure in chancery. Where a mortgage contains a power of sale, the mortgagee has his election either to foreclose in chancery and obtain a judicial sale or to sell under the power. In the opinion in this case,

1 Thompson v. Houze, 48 Miss. 444.

v. Sawyer, 17 Cal. 589; Walton v. Cody, 1 Wis. 420; Atwater v. Kinman, Harr. (Mich.) 243; Bank v. Bell Silver, etc. Min. Co., 8 Mont. 32; Denver Brick, etc. Co. v. McAllister, 6 Colo. 261; Charleston v. Caulfield, 19 S. Car. 201; Frierson v. Blanton, 1 Baxt. (Tenn.) 272; Knox v. McCain, 13 Lea, 197; Pheenix Mut. L. Ins. Co. v. Grant, 3 McArthur (D.C.), 42; Vaughan v. Marable, 64 Ala. 60; American Mortgage Co. v. McCall, 96 Ala. 200; Marriott v. Givens, 8 Ala. 694; Green v. Gaston, 56 Miss. 748; Marx v. Davis, 56 Miss. 745; McDonald v. Vinson, 56 Miss. 497; McAllister v. Plant, 54 Miss. 106;

2 Cormerais v. Genella, 22 Cal. 116; Ryan v. Newcomb, 125 Ill. 91; Warwick v. Hull, 102 Ill. 280; Funk v. McReynolds, 33 Ill. 496; Hurd v. Case, 32 Ill. 45; Brisbane v. Stoughton, 17 Ohio, 482; Cockell v. Bacon, 16 Beay. 158; In re Wilkinson, 41 L. J. Ch. 302; s.c., L. R. 13 Eq. 634; Wayne v. Hanham, 9 Hare, 62; Huston v. Seeley, 27 Iowa, 183; White v. Savery, 50 Iowa, 515; Fanning v. Kerr, 7 Iowa, 450; Crocker v. Robinson, 8 Iowa, 404; Alexander v. Central R. Co., 3 Dill. Cl. 8.) 487; Brickell v. Batchelder, 62 Cal. 623; Fogarty

<br>

### --- Page 0692 ---

1516

TRUSTS UNDER POWER OF SALE, ETC.

[§ 660.

The court said: "In this State we have no such special statute, and it may be doubtful, perhaps, whether, under this provision, any sale of property could be made, according to law.

Thompson v. Houze, 48 Miss. 444;  
Dibrell v. Carlisle, 48 Miss. 691;  
Wafford v. Holmes County, 44 Miss. 579;  
Blackwell v. Barnett, 52 Tex. 326;  
Morrison v. Bean, 15 Tex. 267.

A power of sale given in a mortgage does not oust the jurisdiction of a court of chancery, nor preclude a party from resorting to that tribunal. It is cumulative only; and, in many cases, various conflicting interests render it highly proper that mortgagees should resort to judicial proceeding, rather than to the exercise of the summary rights conferred by the instruments under which they claim. McAllister v. Plant, 54 Miss. 106.

A foreclosure by advertisement of one of two mortgages of even date and intended to be simultaneous, is not effectual to determine the rights of a purchaser under such foreclosure as against the holder of the other mortgage; hence a bill in equity is proper to determine the respective rights and to obtain a sale of the property. Van Aken v. Gleason, 34 Mich. 478.

Equity will foreclose a deed of trust, where the bill shows that the land has, since the execution of the deed, been subdivided among numerous parties in interest, and serious complications involved. Phoenix Mut. Life Ins. Co. v. Grant, 3 McArthur, 42.

When the power does not authorize the mortgagee to purchase at a sale under it, and the mortgagor is insolvent, and a dispute exists as to the validity of the mortgage, by reason of which no sale under the power could be made for a fairly adequate price, a proper case is presented for foreclosure in equity, and for the application of the rents and profits to the payment of the mortgage debt. American Mortgage Co. v. McCall, 96 Ala. 200.

The creditor for whose benefit a number of trust deeds to several trustees have been given, may maintain one suit to foreclose them all, particularly where some are defective as to the power of sale. Nor does the fact that other persons claim liens on the land affect the creditor’s right to have the interests of all parties settled in one suit. Grant v. Phoenix Mut. Life Ins. Co., 121 U.S. 105.

If a mortgagee create a trust and provide that a power of sale is to be executed by the trustee on certain contingencies, he may be controlled, etc., by a court of equity at the suit of a cestui que trust, and when that court decides that the contingency has arisen to give it jurisdiction its decision cannot be impeached collaterally. Youngman v. Elmira, etc. Railroad Co., 65 Pa. St. 278.

Courts of equity, regarding a deed of trust as a security made by the debtor for the payment of his creditors, will give to the creditor the substantial benefits of such deed; and where the deed gives to the trustee no power to enter upon and take possession of the premises, will and should appoint a receiver, if the trustee, after accepting and acting under the appointment, by combination and collusion with the grantor in the

<br>

### --- Page 0693 ---

§ 661. TRUSTS UNDER POWER OF SALE, ETC. 1517

foreclosure rendered by some competent court. It is clear that there is nothing in the instrument forbidding a sale under a judicial decree; and the mortgagee has his election to foreclose in that way or under the power of sale vested in him by the mortgage. It is decidedly for the benefit of all parties that the mortgage should be foreclosed in the former mode rather than the latter. In that way there is no doubt of the right of the mortgagor or parties holding under him to redeem the property within six months after the sale, as provided by the statute. If the latter mode should be adopted, a doubt might arise whether such right of redemption existed. We have never heard it doubted before that courts of equity have the power to foreclose mortgages with power of sale, nor has the able counsel for the appellant referred us to a case in which the right to foreclose such an instrument in a court of competent jurisdiction has been denied. The power of sale contained in the mortgage is a mere cumulative remedy, and does not in the least affect the right to foreclose in chancery. It does not in the least change the character of the instrument as a mortgage, because the mortgagee is a trustee for himself and other parties.

§ 661. Cumulative Remedy Continued.—A power of sale is not an essential part of a mortgage, and a defect in the creation of the power in no way affects the validity of the instrument. It is simply a shorter method of disposing of the property than by foreclosure in equity. But where the power is executed on a shorter notice than that authorized by the statute, the consequence may be to prolong the right to redeem until foreclosure by lapse of time or in equity. In Butler v. Ladue, the court said: "The power of sale appended to the mortgage is undoubtedly limited to the

deed, declines to execute the trust, and after instituting an action of ejectment to recover the possession of the premises, causes the same to be dismissed against the wishes of cestui que trust, the property being inadequate to the payment of the debt secured by the conveyance. Meyers v. Estell, 48 Miss. 372.

1 Cormerais v. Genella, 22 Cal. 116, 124.

<br>

### --- Page 0694 ---

1518 TRUSTS UNDER POWER OF SALE, ETC. (§ 661.

case of the mortgagees being damnified, by paying the debts which the mortgagor failed to pay. But the power of sale, although usual, is no necessary part of the mortgage, and need not be co-extensive with its conditions. It cannot do away with any condition actually expressed and could only be resorted to for explanation where there should be some ambiguity requiring such aid. In a case where, during the pendency of a bill by the maker of a trust deed, against the creditor thereby secured, charging usury and payment, and asking for an accounting, and for redemption on payment of the sum found due, a sale was made, under the trust deed, to third persons, it was held that the power of sale was improperly exercised pending the suit, and that the sale should for that reason be set aside, and a redemption allowed on payment of principal and interest.

Butler v. Ladue, 12 Mich. 173, 179. A power of sale is no necessary part of a mortgage, and a defect in it does not affect the validity of the mortgage. State Bank v. Chappelle, 40 Mich. 447. The omission of the power of sale from a mortgage merely limits the mode of foreclosure to that by bill in equity. Cowles v. Marble, 37 Mich. 168. When a mortgage has been foreclosed by a sale under the power contained in it, the mortgagee cannot afterwards file a bill to foreclose, although he became himself the purchaser at the sale, or subsequently bought from the purchaser at the sale. McLean v. Pressley, 56 Ala. 211. "That the two modes of foreclosure are cumulative, and that a void attempt to foreclose by advertisement does not destroy the lien of the mortgage or cut off the right to resort to foreclosure by action, is too well settled to admit of discussion." Rogers v. Barton, 39 Minn. 39, 41. "It is well settled that a power of sale in a mortgage or deed of trust does not in any way affect the

jurisdiction of chancery to enforce the rights of parties thereto, nor abridge in the slightest degree the right of a person to resort to a court of chancery, as he might do if no such provision had been made for enforcing the security without the aid of a court. All the powers conferred by such an instrument are additional to what the law grants, and neither affect the jurisdiction of a court of chancery nor the option the holder has to invoke its jurisdiction, as if the instrument contained no such provision. A power to sell, or to appoint a trustee, or the like, enlarges the right of the person to whom it is given, but does not diminish it. McDonald v. Vinson, 56 Miss. 497, 499. In this case the court held that where the trustee in a deed of trust cannot execute the trust for want of time to advertise the sale before the bar of the statute of limitations, it may be enforced by a bill in chancery, filed any time before such bar.

Ryan v. Newcomb, 125 Ill. 91.

<br>

### --- Page 0695 ---

§ 661.

TRUSTS UNDER POWER OF SALE, ETC.

been held that where, on bill to enjoin the executor of a mortgagee from making sale of mortgaged premises under a power of sale in the mortgage, the executor files a cross-bill to foreclose the mortgage, this may be treated as a waiver of any purpose to proceed under the power.

1 Warrick v. Hull, 102 Ill. 280.

Where the trustee sells under the power, upon default in but one of three notes secured, and the holder of that purchases all the property at the sale for one-third of its value, with the knowledge that the other notes are outstanding and the debtor redeems from the sale, equity will again foreclose to protect the other notes. Shields v. Dyer, 86 Tenn. 41.

<br>

### --- Page 0696 ---

CHAPTER XXXII.

PERPETUITIES AND ACCUMULATIONS.

§ 662. Introductory.  
663. Accumulations for Charities.  
664. Accumulations for the Payment of Portions.  

§ 665. The Subject Continued.  
666. Interest on Portions.  
667. Advancement by Portion.  

§ 662. Introductory.—The rule for the limitation of perpetuities and accumulations prohibits the settlement of property beyond a life or lives in being, and twenty-one years thereafter. By the Thelluson Act it is provided that no property can be settled so that the rents, or produce thereof, shall be wholly or partially accumulated longer than the life of the settlor, or the term of twenty-one years from his death, or during the minority of any person who shall be living, or in ventre sa mere, at the time of the settlor’s death, or during the minority of any person, who under the trusts would, for the time being, if of full age, be entitled to the rents, dividends or produce; and in every case where any accumulations are directed otherwise, such direction shall be null and void, and the accumulations shall go to the person who would have been entitled to the rents and produce if such accumulations had not been directed. But this rule does not extend to a provision for the payment of the debts of the settlor, or of other persons, or for the produce of timber. In this country accumulations are restricted to a much greater extent than by the English statutes. In New York no accumulation of the rents and

139 & 40 Geo. III, ch. XCVII., § 1. See Thelluson v. Woodford, Ves. 227.  
Ibid. § 2.

<br>

### --- Page 0697 ---

§ 662. PERPETUITIES AND ACCUMULATIONS, 2k

Profits of land, or of the income of personal property, is permitted, for the benefit of adults, for any period, however short, and in the case of minors, accumulations are permitted only during the minority of such persons as would, under the instrument directing the accumulation, for the time being, if of full age, be entitled to the rents and profits, directed to be accumulated. In Michigan, Minnesota, Wisconsin, and California, and perhaps in other States, there are statutory provisions of a similar character. In Pray v. Hegeman, it was held that the provisions of the Revised Statutes authorizing an accumulation of the income of real and personal property for the benefit of minors require that the accumulation shall be for the benefit of a minor, solely and during his minority, and that, when the period of accumulation ceases, the accumulated funds shall be released from further restraint and paid over to the person for whose benefit the accumulation is directed. A direction for accumulation during a minority, accompanied with a gift of the income of the accumulated fund after the expiration of the minority, to the minor for life, and of the principal, upon his death, to other persons, is void. In

1. Rey. St. (9th ed.) 1793, § 28.  
2. Laws ch. 85, §§ 15-26.  
3. Stat. ch. 31.  
4. Rev. Stat., ch. 31. See also Civil Code California, § 716; Idaho Rev. Stats. 1887, tit. 5, ch. 1, §§ 2839 et seq. A will creating trusts, suspending the power of alienation longer than two lives which would, in Michigan, be in violation of the statute, is not void where the immediate and absolute sale of all the testator’s estate in Michigan is directed, and it is to be converted into lands in another State where the trusts are declared. Ford v. Ford, 80 Mich. 42. A devise of the residue of the personal estate of a testator to certain trustees in trust to be invested, and to divide the accumulations of income thereof in certain proportions to certain persons named during life, and to the surviving husband or widow of each until remarriage, and to their children during minority or until marriage, and to pay to such children the residuum of the estate in certain proportions, when attaining age or marrying, creates a valid trust if accepted by the trustees, under the provisions of the Civil Code. Goldtree v. Thompson, 79 Cal. 613.

Pray v. Hegeman, 92 N. Y. 508. In determining the validity of limitations of estates under the Revised Statutes, it is not sufficient that the estates attempted to be created may, by the happening of subsequent events, be terminated within the prescribed period, if

<br>

### --- Page 0698 ---

PERPETUITIES AND ACCUMULATIONS.

some of the other States the rule is less rigid than in New York, but the time fixed is shorter than that allowed at common law, as in Alabama, where the period for accumulation is common law.

Such events might so happen that such estates might extend beyond such period; they must be so limited that in every possible contingency they will absolutely terminate within such period. Haynes v. Sherman, 117 N. Y. 433. The prohibition against the illegal suspension of the power of alienation cannot be evaded by a devise to executors absolutely, relying upon their honor to comply with a letter of secret instructions as to the disposition of the property devised in a manner involving an illegal suspension. O'Hara v. Dudley, 14 Abb. N. C. 713; s.c., 95 N. Y. 403. A limitation during the life of the wife of a son, unmarried at the time of testator’s death, being possibly for a life not in esse, is void. Tiers v. Tiers, 98 N. Y. 568.

Where testator left his residuary estate in trust for his children and grandchildren, the interest to be invested and kept together for ten years after the testator’s death and then the estate to be divided, the portions given to his children "to be held for and during their natural lives respectively," remainder to their children, the trust was held to be void because illegally suspending the power of alienation; the title to the residuary real estate on testator’s death vested in his heirs-at-law, as in case of intestacy. Rice v. Barrett, 102 N. Y. 161. The creation of a trust term, by will, to continue until the youngest of the testator’s children and grandchildren, attaining the age of twenty-one years, shall have attained that age, when the number exceeds two, is void, as suspending the power of alienation for more than two lives in being; the power of alienation can be suspended in no other case than that recognized in the act. Hawley v. James, 16 Wend. 61; s. c., 5 Paige, 318; Hone v. Van Schaick, 20 Wend. 564; s. c., 7 Paige, 221; Wood v. Wood, 5 Paige, 596; De Peyster v. Clendining, 8 Paige. 295; Van Vechten v. Van Vechten, 8 Paige, 104; Thompson v. Clendining, 1 Sand. Ch. 387; Savage v. Burnham, 17 N. Y. 561; Bean v. Hockman, 31 Barb. 78; Persons v. Snook, 40 Barb. 144; Lang v. Ropke, 5 Sand. 363.

The absolute power of alienation of real estate may be suspended during the minority of a minor, indicated in the instrument creating the suspension, and, in such case, the suspension will cease with the death of the minor before coming to majority. Simpson v. Cook, 24 Minn. 180.

A testator devised and bequeathed in trust for each of his two living daughters for life, one-third of his estate, and the remaining third in trust for the three children of a deceased daughter for life; the trust to determine “after the deaths of my two daughters, * * * during whose lives I wish them to continue.” Upon the event of my grandchildren, respectively, arriving at the age of 30, so far as regards such grandchildren and their respective shares—that is to say they shall cease and determine in the case of each grandchild, upon and as she or he reaches the age of 30, * * * and that such grandchildren or grandchild, from that

<br>

§ 663.

PERPETUITIES AND ACCUMULATIONS.

lation is limited to ten years, except in the case of a minor. In some of the States the common law rule is sustained.1

§ 663. Accumulations for Charities.—At law accumulations for charities were made an exception to the rule against perpetuities. No definite limit has been fixed beyond which accumulations for charities are prohibited. Where accumulations are not regulated by statute a direction in an instrument to accumulate a fund for a charitable object is not necessarily void, but any statutory enactments prohibiting

period and age, shall hold their respective shares * * * free and discharged of the said trust to themselves for the term of their natural lives respectively, and after their deaths to the lawful issue of their bodies, their heirs and assigns, forever.” The testator throughout the will designated only the children of his deceased daughter as his “grandchildren” or “granddaughters,” and in another part gave the shares of the two living daughters, after their deaths, “to their children and heirs,” etc. Held, that such provision applied only to the children of the deceased daughter, and did not violate the rule against perpetuities. Dulany v. Middleton (1890), 72 Md. 67; s. c., 19 Atl. Rep. 146. Where a power to appoint is general in the donee to appoint to whomsoever he may choose, either by deed or will, the donee has absolute control, and the degree of remoteness, so far as the creation of perpetuities is concerned, must be measured from the exercise of the power and not from the time of its creation. Lawrence’s Estate, 136 Pa. St. 354; s. c., 26 W. N. C. 537.

1 Brown v. Brown (1888), 86 Tenn. 277; s. c., 65 S. W. Rep. 869; Stout v. Stout, 44 N.J. Eq. 479; Dorr v. Loring, 147 Mass. 530; Turley v. Turley, 11 Ohio St. 173; Woodruff v. Pleasants, 81 Va. 37; “Rule Against Perpetuities,” 14 Am. Law Rev. 237. To constitute a valid executory devise, the contingency upon which it is to take effect must occur within a life or lives in being, and twenty-one years, and the period of gestation thereafter. Booker v. Booker, 5 Humph. 505; Armstrong v. Park, 9 Humph. 195. Testator bequeathed his property in trust to his executors to pay to his daughter an annuity of $600, and on his death to her children an annuity of $300 each until they arrived at the age of 25 years, at which time there should be paid to each child as he arrived at that age $10,000. If, at the death of the daughter, any of her children were of the age of 25 years, the said sum should be paid in lieu of the annuity. At the termination of the trust as to all the beneficiaries and remainder-men, the property should be divided among the grandchildren then living. Held, that, as the trust might, in case the children were born to the daughter after testator’s death, be extended beyond a life in being and 21 years thereafter, it was void, as contrary to the rule against perpetuities. Lawrence v. Smith (1896), 163 Ill. 149; s. c., 45 N. E. Rep. 259.

<br>

### --- Page 0700 ---

1524 PERPETUITIES AND ACCUMULATIONS.

[§ 664.]

Accumulations will apply to trusts for charities, unless they are specifically excepted. Where the term fixed by the instrument for accumulation is unusual and is deemed extravagant the trust may not be upheld as created, but in such a case a court of equity will so reform the trust as to make it answer the intention of the grantor. In an English case, where the testator directed funds to be provided for certain charity schools, by accumulating his property, but fixed no time for the continuance of the accumulation, which must necessarily have exceeded the legal period, the court held the direction to accumulate to be void, and, consequently, the ulterior dispositions of the will to fail; but, as the testator had shown an intention to devote his property to charitable purposes, it directed his intention to be carried into effect cy-près, by means of a scheme to be settled by the Master.¹ But in this country a larger liberty in this direction is sustained. In Massachusetts it has been held that bequests to trustees for charitable purposes are not within the common rule against perpetuities, and may leave the mode of application and the selection of the particular objects to the discretion of the trustees.²

§ 664. Accumulations for the Payment of Portions.—In legal parlance a portion is a sum of money provided for

¹ Martin v. Maugham, 14 Sim. 230, 232. See also Perin v. Carey, 24 How. 465; Richmond v. Davis, 103 Ind. 449; Webster v. Morris, 66 Wis. 366; Yard’s Appeal, 64 Pa. St. 95; Magdalen v. Attorney-General, 6 H. L. Cas. 202; Jones v. Habersham, 107 U. S. 174; Grissom v. Hill, 17 Ark. 483; Griffin v. Graham, 1 Hawk. 130; Franklin v. Armfield, 2 Sneed, 305; Russell v. Allen, 107 U. S. 163; Miller v. Chittenden, 2 Iowa, 362.

² Suckson v. Phillips, 14 Allen, 539, 540. A gift to three persons named and their successors, in trust for the establishment and maintenance of a public library, was held to be invalid as attempting to create a perpetuity; the fact that the persons named as trustees were described as officers of three existing corporations did not render it effectual as a gift to those bodies. Cottman v. Grace, 112 N. Y. 299. A direction to trustees to "set apart" the income of a sum to the ecclesiastical education of poor young men for the Roman Catholic priesthood, such provision to be "a permanent one," and the trustees to make such arrangements that after their death the income should continue so to be appropriated, was held to be void as against the statute of perpetuities. O'Hara v. Dudley, 95 N.Y. 403. See CHARITIES, ante.

<br>

### --- Page 0701 ---

§ 664. PERPETUITIES AND ACCUMULATIONS. 1525

the benefit of children, or of other persons, and to be paid to them on the occurrence of a prescribed contingency, as their marriage or their attaining a fixed age. In England it is held that a portion must consist of a part only of an estate. In Edwards v. Tuck, Lord Cranworth said: "I think that a direction to accumulate all of a person's property to be handed over to some child or children when they attain twenty-one, can never be said to be a direction for raising portions for the child or children; it is not raising a portion at all; it is everything. 'Portion' ordinarily means merely a part or a share, and though I do not know that the gift of a whole might not, in some circumstances, come under the term of a gift of a portion, yet I do not think it comes within the meaning of a portion in this clause of the act, which points to the raising of something out of something, else, for the benefit of some children or class of children, * * * If every direction for accumulation for a child was a portion, the intention of the legislature, which was to prevent accumulations, such accumulations being most frequently directed for the benefit of children, would be entirely defeated." In England money for portions is ordinarily raised from the rents and profits of the estate, or by mortgage or by the sale of the term. In this country portions are usually directed from the rents and profits of the estate, or from the mortgage or sale of a part of it. The directions to raise such portions are held to be, primarily, a charge on the realty of the estate, and where there is a personal covenant to raise the portions, and a definite sum is fixed, still the charge is to be treated as a charge on the land, and the personalty of the covenantor or grantor as simply security for the fulfillment of the agreement. Where the instrument by which the trust is created giving

Browne v. Buckton, 2 Sim. (N. S.) 91; Matthews v. Keble, L. R. 3 Ch. 691; Trafford v. Ashton, 1 P. Wms. 415; Allen v. Backhouse, 2 Ves. & B. 65; Mills v. Banks, 3 P. Wms. 7; Booth v. Blundell, 19 Ves. 528; Gerrard v. Gerrard, 2 Vern. 458; Stanhope v. Thacker, Pr. Ch. 436; Garmstone v. Gaunt, 1 Coll. 577; Hall v. Hurt, 2 J. & H. 76; Ivy v. Gilbert, 2 P. Wms. 13; Small v. Wing, 2 Bro. P. C. 66; Bennett v. Wyndham, 23 Beav. 521; Smith v. Evans, Amb. 633.

Where real estates were devised in strict settlement, subject to a trust for raising portions for younger children during the minority of the tenant for life, out of the rents and profits, or by sale or mortgage: Held, that certain funds which had arisen from the rents during the minority of the tenant for life, were applicable to the payment of the portion, and that the deficiency only could be raised by sale or mortgage. Warter v. Hutchinson, 18 Sim. & St. 276.

A testator directed that his trustees should apply as much as might be necessary of the income of his residuary personal estate for the maintenance of his son (a lunatic) for life, and upon further trust to invest any surplus, and to treat it as part of the testator’s personal estate; and after his son’s death he gave his personal estate to his son’s children, and in default of issue to two other persons. By a codicil he directed that two bond debts, and all other debts which should be due from B S, one of the residuary legatees, should not be payable unless and until his share of the residue should become vested, and that the amount of the debts and interest should then be deducted from his share. Shortly after the date of this codicil, B S gave the testator a bond for £8,000 conditioned to be void if B S should pay £4,000 with lawful interest, within three months after his taking an absolute interest in a moiety of the residue, his interest being contingent during the life of the testator’s son. B S lived to acquire an absolute vested interest. Held, first, that there was an express direction to accumulate, and that the accumulations beyond the period of twenty-one years from the testator’s death were void and belonged to the testator’s next of kin. Secondly, that the accumulations were not within the exceptions in the Thelluson Act respecting portions for children or provision for debts. Held, also, that B S must be charged with the full amount of interest on the first two bonds. Held, also, that on the third bond he was chargeable with interest only from the time when he acquired a vested interest in the residue. Matthews v. Keble, L. R. 4 Eq. 467; s.c., L. R. 3 Ch. 691.

A testator devised estates to trustees for ninety-nine years, upon trust, during twenty-one years, and so much longer during the life of his only son as there should be in existence any younger children or child of his son, to raise £2,000 per annum, and to invest and accumulate this annual sum, and stand possessed of it and the accumulations, upon certain trusts thereby declared, being trusts for

<br>

### --- Page 0703 ---

§ 665.

PERPETUITIES AND ACCUMULATIONS,

The Subject Continued.—Under ordinary circumstances the entire amount provided for in the instrument is to be raised if practicable.

The son's younger children; and, subject to the term, the estates were devised to the use of the son for life, with remainder to the use of his first and other sons successively in tail, with remainders over. Held, that the trusts for accumulation were valid, being a provision for raising portions within the exception in the Thelluson Act. Beech v. Lord St. Vincent, 3 De G. & S. 678. A testator devised real estate in trust for his son G during the joint lives of himself and T, and after the decease of either of them upon trust to invest and accumulate the rents, etc., until £3,000 sterling should have been invested, and subject thereto upon trust for G for life, with remainder to his first and other sons in tail, with successive remainders over; and he directed that the stock to be purchased and the accumulations thereof should be in trust for the children of G, and for default of such issue upon such trusts as G should by will appoint, and in default of appointment, for his next of kin. G and T survived the testator seventeen years, when G died without issue, having by will appointed the £3,000 to F D, a stranger, and the trustee invested and accumulated the rents for a period of ten years from that time, when £3,000 had been invested. Held, first, that the direction to accumulate was void, except for the period of twenty-one years from the death of the testator, the £3,000 not being in the event which happened, viz., the death of G without issue, a portion for his children, nor under the power of appointment a portion for G himself. Secondly, that F D was entitled to so much of the stock as was purchased with the accumulations that took place within the twenty-one years from the death of the testator, and to the interest thereon; and, thirdly, that, subject thereto, the income directed to be accumulated, with the accumulations thereon, belonged to the persons from time to time entitled to the rents and profits under the limitations in the testator’s will. In re Clulow’s Trusts, 28 L. J. Ch. 696. It has been contended that this case falls within the exception respecting provisions for raising portions for children of persons taking an interest under the devise; but an answer to this argument was given at the bar, that as two of the grandchildren are not lawful children of their parents they cannot be brought within the exception. The gift is entire to the grandchildren born and named or to be afterwards born, to be divided amongst them. If the testator intended accumulation for the benefit of any, he intended it for the benefit of all, legitimate and illegitimate. Such a disposition, therefore, if illegal as to any, would be illegal to all; and, consequently, the excess of accumulation, if void to any, is void as to all; for it is impossible to say what other disposition the testator would have made, if he had been aware that the disposition which he had made would partially fail in respect of some of the objects of his bounty. Shaw v. Rhodes, 1 Myl. & Cr. 135, 159.

<br>

### --- Page 0704 ---

1528 PERPETUITIES AND ACCUMULATIONS. [§ 665.

1on in regard to the matter the whole amount is to be raised, though a part of those named may have died. This rule applies where a part of the lands have been forfeited or otherwise lost, provided the remainder is sufficient for the purpose. Where the estate proves insufficient and the trustees are unable to raise the full sum named in the instrument, the portionists are to be paid pro rata. In England it is held that the costs of raising portions are payable out of the estate, and are not to be made a charge on the portions. Where the instrument makes either the personal or real estate exclusively liable, the court will determine from the apparent intention of the grantor which is to be regarded as the primary and which the collateral source of payment. Where the portionist dies before the vesting of the fund his portion sinks, unless a contrary intention appears from the instrument, and this will not be presumed by an interim provision in regard to interest. Portions will sink also where there is a failure to comply with the condition on which the portion is directed, as e.g., that of marriage with consent of a trustee. It has been held that a portion is satisfied by a legacy left by a parent, or one in loco parentis, provided the legacy exceeds or is equal to the portion.

§ 666.] PERPETUITIES AND ACCUMULATIONS. 1529

§ 666. Interest on Portions.—In England it is held that interest is payable on portions at four per cent. from the time when they are raisable, though not from the period when they vest. But if the portions are payable out of rents and profits until a sufficient sum is obtained, no interest will be due in the meantime. Where portions are to be raised under a power, interest is to be computed from the time specified by the instrument conferring the power. Where portions are payable at full age or on the marriage of the portionist, and the death of the father occurs before the contingent event, interest will be allowed by way of maintenance, until the time for payment, though it is not provided for in the settlement, and the rule is the same though the terms be in reversion. Past maintenance may be recovered on the portions of any children that have died after the death of the father and before the time of payment, but not in the case of any who died before the father.

An accumulation of interest is not to be permitted.

It must be appropriated for maintenance.

Interest for the maintenance of children is due from the death.

51; Bengough v. Walker, 15 Ves. 507; Goodfellow v. Burchett, 2 Vern. 298; Cooper v. McDonald, L. R. 16 Eq. 258; Russell v. St. Aubyn, L. R. 2 Ch. D. 398; Bennet v. Houldsworth, L. R. 6 Ch. D. 671; Davis v. Chambers, 7 De G., M. & G. 386; Palmer v. Newell, 8 De G., M. & G. 74; Ford v. Tynte, 2 H. & M. 324, 331.

(1878), 150; Stainforth v. Stainforth, 2 Vern. 460. Though laches may not prevent the raising of the portion, it will prevent interest being allowed until action brought. Merry v. ### --- Page 0706 ---

§ 667. Advancement by Portion.—As the term is used in the English statute of distributions, "advancement by portion" is a sum given by a parent to establish a child in life, or to make a provision for a child. Sums given for the following purposes are held to be advancement by portion: Payment of the admission fee to one of the Inns of Court in the case of a child intended for the bar; the price of a commission and outfit of a child entering the army; the price of plant and machinery and other payments for the purpose of starting a child in business. The following payments were held not to be advancement by portion: The payment of a fee to a special pleader in the case of a child intended for the bar; price of outfit and passage money of an officer in the army and his wife on going out to India with his regiment; payment of debts incurred by an officer in the army; assisting a clergyman in paying his housekeeping and other expenses. In Trimmer v. Bayne the young man had represented to his father that it was extremely important they should be paid in order that he might keep his position in the army, and the father had paid those sums in order to assist him, it would have been clearly an advance. I cannot conceive stronger language than that. In my opinion, if a sum of money is paid by a father for the benefit of his son, it is an "advancement by portion."

No money is applicable for advancement, unless, as is usual, such an application is expressly provided for. Warr v. Warr, Pr. Ch. 213; Robinson v. Cleator, 15 Ves. 526; Lewis v. Lewis, 1 Cox, 162. On the subject of maintenance in respect of expectant portions, consult Knapp v. Knapp, L. R. 12 Eq. 238.

With all deference to the master of the rolls, I cannot agree in his view. I think that a sum of money given by a father to his son to pay his debts is, or may be, an advancement as much as a sum of money given him for any other purpose. In Boyd v. Boyd, L. R. 4 Eq. 305, 308, Wood, V. C., said: "Wherever a sum is paid for a particular purpose, which is thought good and right by the father, and which the son himself desires, if it be money which is drawn out in considerable amount, and not a small sum, it must be treated as an advance. The payment of the money is the important thing—the court does not look to the application." As to the debts, suppose the...

<br>

### --- Page 0707 ---

§ 667.] | PERPETUITIES AND ACCUMULATIONS, 1531  
Rule was stated, by Lord Chancellor Eldon, as follows:  
“The rule is settled that where a parent, or a person loco parentis, gives a legacy as a portion, and afterwards, upon marriage or any other occasion calling for it advances in the nature of a portion to that child, that will amount to an ademption of the gift by the will; and this court will presume he meant to satisfy the one by the other. It differs from the performance or satisfaction of a covenant in this: that the court overlooks small differences in the circumstances of that, which is proposed to be given, and that, in satisfaction of which it is contended to be given. The court does not inquire whether the portion by the will is entirely and absolutely to the child; or what is afterwards making the provisions of the children equal goes throughout the whole Act; first, it gives the two-thirds of the personal estate (the mother being allowed her third) equally among all the children. But then the Act takes it into consideration, that there may be some of the children who have received a portion or advancement before, but not so much as to make up their full share; in that case such child, so advanced but in part, shall have so much more out of the intestate’s personal estate as will suffice to make his share equal to that of the other children. ‘The statute takes nothing away that has been given to any of the children, however unequal that may have been; how much soever that may exceed the remainder of the personal estate left by the intestate at his death, the child may, if he pleases, keep it all; if he be not contented, but would have more, then he must bring into hotchpot what he has before received; this manifestly seems to be the intention of the Act, grounded upon the most just rule of equity, equality.’ I think the intention of the statute was not to disturb anything which had been done by the intestate in his lifetime, but that which had been done should be taken into account in estimating his children's shares of his estate.’ Blockley v. Blockley, L. R. 29 Ch. D. 250, 252. Any sum of considerable amount paid out of the common fund of a family, to or for the benefit of a child, is an “advance” within the meaning of the statute of distributions; the intention of the statute being to make the provision for all the children equal. A premium paid upon the occasion of a son being articled to an attorney and solicitor held an advance to the son, though the profession was afterwards relinquished. A sum paid for the purchase of a commission in the army for a son held an advance; but whether the sum paid for the outfit of a son on entering the army is an advance, query. Sums paid by a father for a son to enable him to pay debts of honor, the non-payment of which would have compelled him to leave the army, held advances. Boyd v. Boyd, L. R. 4 Eq. 305.

<br>

advanced in this form; a settlement upon marriage; which not being a performance of a covenant or satisfaction of a debt, yet is presumed satisfaction of the intended portion.!

### --- Page 0709 ---

CHAPTER XXVIII.

EQUITABLE AND STATUTORY LIMITATIONS.

§ 668. The Rule in General.  
§ 669. Where the Rule is Operative.  
§ 670. Breach of Trust.  
§ 671. Concerning Stale Claims.  
§ 672. Bar from Acquiescence.

§ 668. The Rule in General.—It is the rule of equity that express trusts are not subject to the operation of the statute of limitations, but like many others, this rule is not absolutely inflexible. It applies only where the trust is clearly and definitely declared, and is recognized by the parties. But where the trustee denies the right of the cestui que trust, and the possession of the property becomes adverse, lapse of time from that period may constitute a

1 Lewis v. Hawkins, 23 Wall. 126; Speidel v. Henrici, 120 U. S. 377; Biddle v. Whitehill, 135 U. S. 621; Prevost v. Gratz, 6 Wheat. 497; Gordon v. Small, 53 Md. 550; Howell v. Howell, 15 Wis. 55; Spear v. Evans, 51 Wis. 42; Bostwick v. Dickson, 65 Wis. 593; Armstrong v. Campbell, 3 Yerg. 201; De Couche v. Savetier, 3 Johns. Ch. 190; Payne v. Hathaway, 3 Vt. 212; Kutz’s Appeal, 40 Pa. St. 90; Clay v. Clay, 7 Bush, 95; Bonner v. Young, 68 Ala. 35; McGaughey v. Brown, 46 Ark. 25; Woodward v. Jaggers, 48 Ark. 248; Cunningham v. McKindley, 22 Ind. 149; Gutch v. Fosdick, 48 N. J. Eq. 353; Leach v. Wilson County, 68 Tex. 353; Wilson v. Green, 49 Iowa, 251.

No lapse of time is a bar to a direct trust, as between trustee and cestui que trust. At least it will not begin to run before the trust is openly disavowed by the trustee, and this is fully made known to the cestui que trust. Chicago & E. I. R. R. Co. v. Hay, 119 Ill. 493.

No lapse of time is a bar to a direct or express trust, as between the trustee and cestui que trust. The relation between a corporation and its directors being that of a direct trust, as contradistinguished from an implied one, the statute of limitations presents no bar to a bill by a receiver of the corporation to hold the directors liable for misappropriation of the corporate assets or funds. Ellis v. Ward, 137 Ill. 509.

<br>

### --- Page 0710 ---

1534 [§ 668.

EQUITABLE AND STATUTORY LIMITATIONS.

bar in equity. It is held also that where there is both a legal and an equitable remedy in regard to the same claim, the equitable is subject to the same rule as the legal remedy. In the case of Coster v. Murray, Chief Justice Spencer stated the rule, as follows: "I have, therefore, no hesitation in saying that in a case where there is a concurrent jurisdiction in the courts of common law and equity, the rule must be the same, and the statute of limitations may be pleaded with the same effect in the one court as in the other. In cases of trusts and fraud, peculiarly, appropriately and exclusively the objects of equity jurisdiction, according to the established doctrine, the statute cannot be pleaded." The trusts that are not affected by the statute of limitations are only such technical and continuing trusts as are not cognizable at law. Trusts which arise by operation of law, or by the decree of a court, are not included in the rule. In implied trusts, unless the cause of action has been fraudulently concealed, the lapse of time is as complete a bar in equity as in law. And where the trust is constructive, and the decree of the court is founded on fraud or mistake, the statute of limitations will run from

volved, if time and long acquiescence have obscured the rights of the parties, or other circumstances have given rise to presumptions unfavorable to its continuance. Sanchez v. Dow, 23 Fla. 445. "We

Anderson v. Northrop, 30 Fla. 612; Chadwick v. Chadwick, 59 Mich. 87; Ward v. Harvey, 11 Ind. 741; Wood v. Wood, 3 Ala. 75; Lucas v. Daniels, 34 Ala.; Hastie v. Aiken, 67 Ala.; Smith v. Ricords, 52 Mo.; Morgan v. Morgan, 10 Ga.; Scott v. Haddock, 11 Ga.; Inman v. Brooks, 9 Pick.; Inman v. Rodgers, 10 Pick.; Speidel v. Henrici, 120 U. S. 386; North Carolina Univ. v. Bank, 96 N. Car. 280; Roberts v. Bedell, 61 Barb. 37; Cooper v. Lee, 75 Tex. 114; Sheldon v. Sheldon, 3 Wis. 699; Cunningham v. McKindley, 22 Ind. 149; Wrenn v. Followell, 52 Ark. 76. Equity will bar demands become stale by lapse of time, even though a trust be in-

take the distinction to be, that if it is an express trust or agency, a demand is necessary to terminate the trust and set the statute in operation; but if it is only an implied or constructive trust or agency, then no demand is necessary, but the statute is put in motion as soon as the property is taken into possession or the money received." Robertson v. Dunn, 87 N. Car. 191, 195.

Coster v. Murray, 20 Johns.

§ 669,] EQUITABLE AND STATUTORY LIMITATIONS. 1535  
the time that the circumstances on which the decree was based came to light. In the opinion in this case Mr. Justice Gray said: “As a general rule, doubtless, length of time is no bar to a trust clearly established, and express trusts are not within the statute of limitations, because the possession of the trustee is presumed to be the possession of his cestui que trust. But this rule is, in accordance with the reason on which it is founded, and as has been clearly pointed out by Chancellor Kent and Mr. Justice Story, subject to this qualification, that time begins to run against a trust as soon as it is openly disavowed by the trustee, insisting upon an adverse right and interest which is clearly and unequivocally made known to the cestui que trust; as when, for instance, such transactions take place between the trustee and the cestui que trust as would in case of tenants in common amount to an ouster of one of them by the other.”

§ 669. Where the Rule is Operative.—The rule applies to the relations of the trustee to the cestui que trust,

### --- Page 0714 ---

1538 EQUITABLE AND STATUTORY LIMITATIONS. [§ 670.

the form. In a case in Georgia it was held that where the trustee of a feme covert, thereto authorized, loans money to a stranger (the latter knowing that it is a trust fund), and takes a note payable to himself as trustee, in the absence of all fraud and conspiracy, the statute of limitations is a good defense to the stranger, as well in equity against the cestui que trust, as at law against the trustee. It has been held that where a debtor borrowed the money of a trustee, knowing it to be trust money, the statute of limitations was not operative as a bar to the claim.

§ 670. Breach of Trust.—Where a trustee conveys an estate to a third person for a valuable consideration, without notice, and in breach of trust, the ordinary rule in such cases is operative. The purchaser will hold the estate discharged of the trust. But, if the conveyance was with notice or without a valuable consideration, the purchaser will hold the property as a constructive trustee and the cestui que trust may enforce his claim in equity. In such case the rule relating to an express trust will apply and the statute will not run.

§ 670.] EQUITABLE AND STATUTORY LIMITATIONS. 1539

only as he has been made such by a decree of the court, and in this direction the cestui que trust must not sleep upon his rights. Where the trustee has held the property adversely for a long period denying the trust, the equitable claim of the cestui que trust may be barred. In Massachusetts it has been held that if a trustee sells the trust estate to a purchaser for value, with warranty, and without any intimation, in the deed of conveyance, of a subsisting trust, and the vendee enters and occupies the estate, doing no act which recognizes in any manner the existence of the trust, and there is no fraud or concealment, and the cestui que trust is under no disability, the possession must be regarded as adverse, both as to the trustee and the cestui que trust; and the time which would bar the legal right is equally effectual to bar the equitable right. In Pennsylvania the rule has been stated as follows: "Equity, in proceedings to rescind contracts, refuses relief to stale demands, and requires conscience, good faith and reasonable diligence in the complainant. After a great delay the party is held to have waived his right to relief. A party entitled to an estate expectant on the death of another, being sui juris, assigned his expectancy. He could have sued for the rescission of the deed at any time after he made it, notwithstanding his right of enjoyment was suspended. The grantee died twenty-one years after the assignment; the existing life estate did not excuse the delay in impeaching the deed; good faith required him to do it whilst his assignee was alive to defend it. The evidence being such as would have justified a jury in finding that the assignment was in trust, and an auditor and the court below having so found, the Supreme Court would not reverse the finding. All prima facie presumptions are in favor of such a finding, and he who alleges error must prove it. The assignment being a trust there could be no laches, for the time for the execution of it could not arrive until the death of the life tenant.

¹ Merriam v. Hassam, 14 Allen, 516, 521. See also Williams v. First Presby. Soc., 1 Ohio St. 478; Attorney-General v. Federal St. Meeting House, 3 Gray, 1; Melling v. Leak, 32 Eng. L. & Eq. 442.

<br>

### --- Page 0716 ---

1540 EQUITABLE AND STATUTORY LIMITATIONS. [§ 670.

The decree of the court below charging the assignee with costs, approved under the circumstances. But in cases of this character the rights of the beneficiary cannot be barred until he is in possession. Where he holds in remainder or in reversion the statute will not begin to run until his right to the possession is established by the determination of the prescribed estate, and where he is under a disability the statute will not begin to run until the disability is terminated. Where a fraud has been perpetrated the rule is that the statute will not begin to run until the fraud is discovered.

“To hold that by concealing a fraud or by committing a fraud in a manner that it concealed itself until such time as the party committing the fraud could plead the statute of limitations to protect it, is to make the law which was designed to prevent fraud the means by which it is made successful and secure. And we see no reason why this principle should not be as applicable to suits tried on the common law side of the courts calendar as to those on the equity side. While we might follow the construction of the State courts in this matter, where those statutes governed the case, in construing this statute of limitation passed by the Congress of the United States as part of the law of bankruptcy, we hold that when there has been no negligence or laches on the part of a plaintiff in coming to the knowledge of the fraud, which is the foundation of the suit, and when the fraud has been concealed, or is of such character as to conceal itself, the statute does not begin to run until the fraud is discovered by, or becomes known to, the party suing, or those in privity with him.” Bailey v. Glover, 21 Wall. 342, 349. Mere ignorance of the

<br>

### --- Page 0717 ---

§ 671.] EQUITABLE AND STATUTORY LIMITATIONS. 1541

§ 671. Concerning Stale Claims.—The courts are strongly disinclined to enter upon the investigation of claims that have become stale from long neglect. Where the statute of limitations cannot be pleaded in bar, and, even in cases of fraud which, if recent, would constitute an unquestionable claim to relief, courts have been induced by lapse of time to refuse their interference. They act upon the maxim of the law: Expedite reipublicae ut sit finis litium. In an English case, Sir Thomas Plumer, M. R., said: "Where there has been a long period, during which a party has, under an innocent mistake, misapplied a fund, from the laches and neglect of others, that is, from no one of the public setting him right, and where the accounts have, in consequence, become entangled, the court, under its general discretion, considering the enormous expense of the inquiries, the great hardship of calling upon representatives to refund what families have spent, acting on the notion of it being their property, has been in the habit, while giving the relief, of fixing a period to the account. Unless it be upon these grounds, I do not know how the court can take upon itself to set a limit. The accident of where the information was filed, or where the demand was made, can only be material as putting the parties on their guard, and, therefore, leaving them without excuse for any errors they may commit. It must be admitted that the result of the authorities is, that in such case the court is bound to be guided by the particular circumstances."¹ Questions of existence of a cause of action by an assignee in bankruptcy does not remove the bar against such action prescribed by a statute of limitation; but in order to set aside such bar, within the rule as announced in Bailey v. Glover, 21 Wall. 342, there must be no laches on the part of the assignee in coming to the knowledge of the fraud which is the foundation of the suit. Avery v. Cleary, 132 U.S. 604.

Barwell, 34 Beay. 371; McKnight v. Taylor, 1 How. 161; Thompson v. McGaw, 2 Watts, 161; Piatt v. Vattier, 9 Pet. 446; Price’s Appeal, 54 Pa. St. 472; Irish v. Antioch College, 126 Ill. 474; s.c., 9 Am. St. Rep. 638; Power’s Appeal, 125 Pa. St. 175; s.c., 11 Am. St. Rep. 882; Penna. R. R. Co.’s Appeal, 125 Pa.

EQUITABLE AND STATUTORY LIMITATIONS,
