this character involve no little difficulty, and have received very careful and thorough consideration by the courts. In an English case, Lord Chancellor Erskine said: “As to the effect of length of time, where there is no bar by the

St. 189; Fidelity Ins. Co.’s Appeal, 115 Pa. St. 157; Wright v. Fisher, 65 Mich. 279; s. c., 8 Am. St. Rep. 886; Bangs v. Stephenson, 63 Mich. 661; Walet v. Haskins, 68 Tex. 418; s. c., 2 Am. Saunders Co., 16 Neb. 123; s. c., 19 N. W. Rep. 698; North v. Platte County (1890), 29 Neb. 447; s. c., 45 N. W. Rep. 692; Hanner v. Moulton, 138 U.S. 486; McCoy v. McCoy, 29 W. Va. 794.

Where creditors obtained judgment in 1870 on debts contracted prior to 1868, and in 1873 their debtor became a bankrupt, reserving his homestead, and in 1875 obtained his discharge, such creditors could not invoke the aid of equity to subject this homestead to the payment of their judgments. These creditors having had for several years a plain, adequate and complete remedy at law, they cannot invoke the aid of equity to restore to them those rights which they lost through their own laches. Solomons v. Shaw, 25 S. Car. 112.

Forty-three years after the ancestor of the plaintiffs acquired title, more than twenty years after that ancestor had positive information of the wrong upon which the claims set up in this bill in equity are grounded, twenty-five years after the purchase by defendant in good faith and with no knowledge of the wrong, this suit was commenced, without any assertion of the right now set up having been made during all that time. Held, that these facts disclosed laches which forbade the interference of a court of equity. Underwood v. Dugan, 139 U. S. 380.

The B. W. & N. R. R. Co. entered into an agreement with defendant and another as trustees for purchasers of the franchise and property of a railroad by which said company agreed to lease its franchise, rights and privileges to a new company to be organized by the trustees, and to receive therefor a specified amount of the stock of the new company. It was provided that in case the new company at any time during the period of the lease should issue mortgage bonds, the holders of said stock so transferred should have the right to exchange their stock for an equal amount of the bonds. The agreement was carried out, the stock delivered in

<br>

### --- Page 0719 ---

§ 672.] EQUITABLE AND STATUTORY LIMITATIONS. 1543  
statute of limitations, a court of equity will never lay down, as a general proposition, that though the fact, that imposition has been practiced, is established, the party is too late; and by the accident of the death of the person, who might have contradicted him, shall be deprived of his right to relief. The true operation of length of time is by way of evidence; and, acknowledging the rule as to dealings between a trustee and the cestui que trust, and that the transaction should be more narrowly watched than in the case of strangers, yet it is to be examined according to the rules of evidence; standing upon the eternal principles of justice; and recognized by the whole theory and practice of the law of England.’’

§ 672. Bar from Acquiescence.—Where a person sleeps upon his rights by acquiescence in a transaction for a considerable period, such acquiescence will be a bar to relief, and it may be held a bar, where the period is comparatively short. Where a person suffers an act, in violation of his rights, to be performed with his apparent approbation, or with his knowledge and without protest, he cannot at a later period claim relief. If his treatment of the matter amounts to acquiescence he is bound by it, and he cannot thereafter undo what he has done. In like manner payment for the lease was soon thereafter sold and transferred by the lessor, but the transfer was not entered on the books of the new company. Subsequently, the new company issued mortgage bonds. Nine years thereafter and after the owners of his stock were chargeable with laches in the exercise of the option, and so waived all right to elect to exchange the stock for bonds. Catlin v. Green, 120 N. Y. 441.

Morse v. Royal, 12 Ves. 355, 374.

Kent v. Jackson, 14 Beav. 384; Styles v. Guy, 1 H. & Tw. 328; Graham v. Birkenhead R’y Co., 2 Macn. & G. 146; Leeds v. Amherst, 2 Phil. 123; Phillipson v. Gatty, 7 Hare, 523; Stafford v. Stafford, 1 De G. & J. 202; Jorden v. Money, 5 H. L. Cas. 185; Rennie v. Young, 2 De G. & J. 142; Leonard v. Gascoyne, 11 Ves. 333; Brice v. Stokes,

EQUITABLE AND STATUTORY LIMITATIONS.

[§ 672.]

Where a person stands by and permits another to deal with his property in violation of his rights and enters no protest or objection, he will not, subsequently, be heard in a petition for relief. According to Lord Cottenham, his acquies-

11 Ves. 319; In re Niles, 113 N. Y. 547, 4 Poole v. Mundy, 103 Mass. 174; Walker v. Symonds, 3 Swanst. 64; Farrant v. Blanchford, 1 De G., J. & S. 107; Booth v. Booth, 1 Beay. 125; Roby v. Ridehalgh, 7 De G., M. & G. 104; Munch v. Cockerell, 5 Myl. & Cr. 178. Parties to a cause knowing of a combination to chill the bidding at a judicial sale made therein cannot, after a long acquiescence, have the sale set aside; but parties to the conspiracy cannot obtain the aid of the court in specifically enforcing their illegal agreement with their co-conspirators. Baggott v. Sawyer, 25 S. Car. 405. ‘Irrespective of any evidence of accounts being rendered to which he assented, he has a right to rely on the maxim that where there is an express trust time never runs to the cestui que trust, as it was used to be said, “nullum tempus occurrit regi,” or “nullum occurrit ecclesiam.” Might such a bill as this be filed at the end of a century by the plaintiff's personal representative against the personal representative of the three trustees if they could be found out? I apprehend that in such a case it is unnecessary to presume the execution of a release or to resort to any statute of limitations. A court of equity will not allow a dormant claim to be set up when the means of resisting it, if unfounded, have perished, much less cast a burden of proving such an affirmative as that forty years ago cottage rents were properly collected when the witnesses who might have proved

the fact have long ago been called into another state of existence. It has been beautifully remarked, with respect to the emblem of Time, who is depicted as carrying a scythe and an hourglass, that while with the one he cuts down the evidence which might protect innocence, with the other he metes out the period when innocence can no longer be assailed.” Bright v. Ligerton, 2 De G., F. & J. 606, 616. A B, a trustee, misapplied a trust fund, of which he was tenant for life, and he died in 1834. C D, who then became entitled to it, died in 1858, having taken no proceeding to recover it. A bill filed in 1863 by the representatives, C D, against the representatives of A B, to recover the fund, was dismissed with costs, on the ground of the lapse of time. Hodgson v. Bibby, 32 Beav. 221. By a marriage settlement, it was declared that a sum of money, then in the hands of the lady’s brother, should be held by three trustees (one of them being the brother) upon trust, at the request in writing of the lady, to pay to her the whole or any part absolutely; and until such request, upon trust, when and as the same should come into their hands, to invest the same, and pay the interest to the wife for life, for her separate use, and after her decease as she should by will appoint, and in default of appointment to the husband. The money was allowed to remain for thirteen years in the hands of the brother, who paid the interest to the husband, and also

<br>

§ 672.] EQUITABLE AND STATUTORY LIMITATIONS,

1545

Cence raises a counter equity, and makes it inequitable in him to complain of a matter, to which,—though he may not have positively sanctioned it—he has given his acquiescence by his silence, as the act proceeded.

Paid him part of the principal, with the knowledge of the wife. Upon the death of the husband, the wife filed a bill against the three trustees to compel them to replace the balance of the fund. The brother had become insolvent. Held, that the trustees were guilty of a breach of trust; but that the wife was barred by acquiescence, from claiming as against the two trustees, who had neglected to call in the money. Jones v. Higgins, L. R. 2 Eq. 538.

### --- Page 0722 ---

CHAPTER XXXIV.

TRUST COMPANIES AS TRUSTEES.

§ 673. Introductory.  
§ 674. Powers and Liabilities in General.  
§ 675. The Subject Continued.  
§ 676. Claim of Beneficiary for Interest.  
§ 677. Claim for Compensation.  
§ 678. Accumulations by Trust Companies.

§ 673. Introductory.—The object of this chapter is not to expound the law relating to trust companies, in general, but simply to notice some points in which, in their capacity as trustees, they are subject to the equitable rules by which trustees of other classes are governed. Trust companies have their being, not under the operation of the common law, nor by the decisions of the courts, but by statutory enactment. In consequence their powers, duties, liabilities, etc., are determined, to a great extent, by their charters, or by the general statutes under which they are organized. The Banking Law of New York, under which trust companies are incorporated in that State, provides that, “any court having jurisdiction to appoint a trustee, guardian, receiver or committee of the estate of a lunatic, idiot, or habitual drunkard, or to make any fiduciary appointment, may appoint any such corporation to be such trustee, guardian, receiver or committee, or to act in any other fiduciary capacity.”¹

¹ Birdseye’s Rev. Stat. N. Y. (1896 ed.), p. 229, § 157. The important characteristic possessed by these corporations is that they are not required to give security. Section 158 of the New York statute cited above contains this provision: “No bond or other security, except as hereinafter provided, shall be required from any such corporation for or in respect to any trust, nor when appointed executor, ad-

<br>

### --- Page 0723 ---

§ 673.1

TRUST COMPANIES AS TRUSTEES.

But it is now held that it is not absolutely necessary that the authority to accept a trust should be conferred upon a corporation by its charter, or by the general law under which it is organized. It will be presumed where the nature of the trust is such as to fall within the general objects of the corporation. It has been held that the power administrator, guardian, trustee, receiver, committee or depositary. All investments of money received by any such corporation in either of such characters shall be at its sole risk, and for all losses of such money the capital stock, property and effects of the corporation shall be absolutely liable, unless the investments are such as the courts recognize as proper when made by an individual acting as trustee, executor, administrator, guardian, receiver, committee or depositary, or such as are permitted in and by the instrument or words creating or defining the trust. If dissolved by the legislature, or the court, or otherwise, the debts due from the corporation as such executor, administrator, guardian, trustee, committee or depositary, shall have the preference. The court or officer making such appointment may, upon proper application, require any corporation which shall have been so appointed, to give such security as to the court or officer shall seem proper, or, upon failure of such corporation to give security as required, to remove such corporation from and to revoke such appointment. Such court or officer may make orders respecting such trusts, and require the corporation to render all accounts which such court or officer might lawfully require, if such executor, administrator, guardian, trustee, receiver, committee or depositary were a natural person.

13 Gen. Stat. New Jersey, p. 3251, § 11, is as follows: That, in addition to the provisions of the act to which this is a supplement and the powers and authority therein and thereby given, it shall and may be lawful for any company or association incorporated by or under any law of this State, and possessing any of the powers conferred by the third section of the act to which this is a supplement, or similar powers, and with a capital stock of not less than one hundred thousand dollars, to act as agents to transfer, register and countersign, and for the purchase and sale of stocks, bonds or other obligations of any corporation, association, municipality, State or public authority, and to receive and manage any sinking fund thereof, upon such terms as may be agreed upon, and to act as assignees, receivers, agents, executors, administrators or guardian, and execute trusts of every description not inconsistent with the laws of the State of New Jersey or of the United States.

2 Chapin v. School Dist., 35 N. H. 445; Robertson v. Bullions, 11 N. Y. 243; Vidal v. Girard, 2 How. 126, 189. "The first objection is, that 'the devise is void because a corporation is not capable of taking and holding property as a trustee.' It being generally admitted that corporations are the mere creatures of the legislature, which can invest them with more or fewer

<br>

### --- Page 0724 ---

1548 TRUST COMPANIES AS TRUSTEES. [§ 674.

of a corporation to take and hold property for its own purposes, involves the right to execute a trust which relates to other parties.¹ The equitable rules relating to trusts of this class are of special and of rapidly increasing importance. During the past few years the business of trust companies, of this character, has been rapidly developed, and the tendency is to a continued advance in this direction. The amount of material for the illustration of the law on this subject is very limited. In consequence of the recent origin of this method of executing trusts there have been very few cases decided that have a direct bearing on the subject. But the paucity of the cases which may be cited is by no means an indication of the importance of the law which they involve.

§ 674. Powers and Liabilities in General.—As a rule the powers and liabilities of trust companies, and other corporations, acting in the capacity of trustees, are powers and capacities, and these more or less enlarged according to its own good pleasure; it has appeared to my mind a little singular, how the principle of this objection could have become a question of general discussion. What are the powers and capacities of a particular corporation may very naturally be a subject of inquiry. In some cases the inquiry might be a question of law and sometimes a matter of fact, to be decided respectively by the court or the jury. I can only account for the general inquiry, by supposing that the oldest corporations were of prescriptive origin, and that immemorial usage did not permit them to take property in trust for their persons; and that, instead of reasoning from the abstract of corporations, or the power of the crown or of parliament to create new ones, lawyers drew too strict a conclusion, in the nature of a maxim, from those in existence, and applied it as a principle of construction to all of a more modern date, as they were beginning to exercise powers in trust.² Phillips’ Academy v. King, 12 Mass. 546, 553.

¹ In re Howe, 1 Paige, 214. “It is a general rule that corporations cannot exercise any powers not given to them by their charters or acts of incorporation, and for that reason they cannot act as trustees in relation to any matters in which the corporation has no interest. But wherever property is devised or granted to a corporation, partly for its own use and partly for the use of others, the power of the corporation to take and hold the property for its own use carries with it as a necessary incident the power to execute that part of the trust which relates to others.” Ibid. See also Jackson v. Hartwell, 8 Johns. 422.

<br>

### --- Page 0725 ---

§ 674.] TRUST COMPANIES AS TRUSTEES. 1549

such as are conferred by statute! In the decisions of the courts relating to their common law and equitable powers, at different periods, there has been some diversity. The early English text writers assert the right of corporations to be executors. At a later period it was denied or at least regarded as doubtful whether a corporation aggregate could act in this capacity. This was on the ground that a corporation cannot take the oath required. There are some early American decisions in the same direction. At a comparatively recent date it was held, in Maryland, that letters testamentary or of administration will not be granted to a corporation; nor will a corporation, when named executor, be allowed, according to the English practice, to designate a person to receive administration with the will annexed. But the decisions on this point by the courts of this country have not been uniform. No general rule has been established. In New Jersey the point appears

1 See Ante, § 673.

2 Swinburne on Wills, Part V., § 9.

3 Blackstone’s Commentaries, 477; Wentworth, Executors, ch. 1, p. 39 (14th ed.).

4 In re Thompson’s Estate, 33 Barh. 334.

5 Georgetown College v. Browne, 34 Md. 450. “But even if the will of Miss B had devised and bequeathed all her property directly to this corporation, it could not have claimed administration, because we take it to be well settled, that a corporation cannot become an executor or administrator; nor has the English practice of allowing a corporation, when named executor, to designate a person styled a syndic, to receive administration with the will annexed, ever prevailed in this State, but, on the contrary, all power of delegation over the right has been expressly repudiated and denied by our courts.” Ibid. 455. See also Association v. Lowdermilk, 50 Md. 175; Search v. Ellicott (1889) (Md.), 18 Atl. Rep. 863. But a special act of the legislature incorporating a trust company, and investing it with certain powers, is not void under § 48, art. 3, Const. Md., providing that corporations shall not be created by special act except where there is no general law existing for the creation of corporations of the same general character as the one proposed, where it appears that a corporation organized under the general law providing for the formation of trust companies, would be powerless to exercise such corporate powers and rights as were granted by the special act. Reed v. Baltimore Trust, etc. Co., 72 Md. 531.

6 Deringer v. Deringer, 5 Houst. 416; Porter v. Trall, 30 N. J. Eq. 106. “There is then no inherent disability or disqualification be-

<br>

### --- Page 0726 ---

1550 TRUST COMPANIES AS TRUSTEES. [§ 674.

to have been left unsettled.’ But the law relating to the powers and liabilities of trust companies as trustees is of recent origin, and, to a great extent, as already said, it is statutory. The decisions of the courts have reference chiefly to the construction and interpretation of statutory enactments. The right of a legislature to confer trustee powers upon a trust company has been repeatedly questioned. It has been maintained that the conferring of such powers is in conflict, not only with the common law, but, as well, with the well established principles of equity, and that it is in contravention of public policy. But the constitutionality of these enactments has been uniformly sustained by the courts.

The recent decisions on this point, though not numerous, are all to the same effect.

longing to a corporation as such which excludes it from acting as an administrator, and it may accept the office if not prohibited by its charter or forbidden by statute, whenever from the objects of its incorporation and the nature of its business it may become necessary and proper, and it is able to comply with the conditions prescribed by law as to giving bond, etc. Practically, the position of the plaintiff is meritorious and unobjectionable. With the express power contained in its charter to receive the appointment of administrator, and with its capital stock pledged as the security required for the faithful performance of its duties, it brings an action in its representative capacity for the recovery of a debt due to its intestate, and is met at the outset by technical rules, which, whatever may have been the reason of their origin and adoption, have either become obsolete, or have been so modified and relaxed as to be no longer of general application. The execution of the bond would, at best, amount to little more than a form, and be without substantial benefit or necessity, but still the defendant is entitled to it, if it is insisted upon, and the plaintiff has a full and lawful power to execute it as it would have to make or endorse a promissory note or accept a bill of exchange, or to execute any other description of bond which may be fairly and legitimately considered as necessary and proper in the usual course of its business. It has not been made to appear in what manner the interests of this State or of its citizens would be impaired, or in what way its policy would be invaded or subverted, by sustaining the plaintiff's action. Admitting that a corporation may be unable to act as an original administrator under the provisions of the general statute, it does not follow that it may not be recognized as a foreign administrator on the production of letters duly authenticated and giving bond.’ Deringer v. Deringer, 5 Houst. 416, 430.

1 Porter v. Trall, 30 N. J. Eq. 106.

<br>

### --- Page 0727 ---

§ 675.] TRUST COMPANIES AS TRUSTEES. 1551

§ 675. The Subject Continued.—In the leading case of the Minnesota Loan & Trust Company v. Beebe, it was held that the statute authorizing the organization of annuity, safe deposit and trust companies, and granting to such corporations power to act as guardians of the estates of insane persons was valid. In the opinion in this case, the court said: "To the appointment of corporations, organized for that special purpose, under well-guarded statutes, to the position of trustee of a trust, executor of a will, administrator or guardian of an estate, or other place of trust, involving the custody and management of property only, there can be no possible objection on either constitutional grounds or considerations of policy. The common law grounds upon which it was held that corporations could not act in any of these fiduciary capacities were purely artificial. The reason given by Blackstone why a corporation aggregate could not act as an executor or administrator is that it could not take the necessary oath; but even at common law, in England, this technical difficulty was evaded by the corporation naming an agent, called a ‘syndic,’ to whom letters were issued. Moreover, it is, of course, entirely competent for the legislature to dispense altogether with an oath in such cases. Another reason often assigned why a corporation could not act as a trustee was that, as a court of equity often enforced a trust by laying hold of the conscience of the trustee, therefore, inasmuch as a corporation has no conscience, it is not qualified to act as trustee. The reason most commonly given why a corporation could not act as trustee, executor, guardian, or in other such fiduciary capacity, was that such an appointment involved a personal trust, and, therefore, a corporation lacked one of the essential requisites of a good trustee,—personal confidence. But at least as to trusts, technically so-called, this doctrine has long since been exploded, even at common law, as too artificial. And there are now numerous instances in which corporations have been expressly empowered by statute to administer estates, and

1 Minnesota Loan & Trust Co. v. Beebe, 40 Minn. 7.

<br>

### --- Page 0728 ---

1552 TRUST COMPANIES AS TRUSTEES. [§ 675.

neither the validity nor policy of such legislation has ever before, to our knowledge, been questioned. In fact, in many of the States, particularly the older ones, this is fast becoming the favorite method of administering estates and executing trusts. The fact that such corporations have perpetuity of existence, that they are less liable than natural persons to sudden fluctuations of fortune; that, being organized for that special purpose, they can administer estates more efficiently and economically; and that, in case of large estates, it is often difficult to find a natural person who is both able and willing to accept the trust and give the necessary bonds, have suggested the necessity and created the demand for such organization.

1 Minnesota Loan & Trust Co. v. Beehe, 40 Minn. 7, 9. "The statute is criticised because it does not require the corporation to take an oath or give bond as in the case of natural persons. But this is purely a question for the legislature. If they deem the securities deposited with the State auditor, and the other safeguards placed by the statute around the organization and management of such corporations to insure the faithful execution of all trusts imposed upon them, as an equivalent for the bond and oath required of natural persons, they have the undoubted power to so provide. * * * For example, it is urged that they amount to legislative interference with the constitutional powers of the probate court, to which is given jurisdiction over the estate of deceased persons and persons under guardianship. It need only be suggested in answer to this, that the jurisdiction of the courts pertains to the execution of the laws and not to their enactment. This statute neither appoints any guardian or administrator, nor compels the courts to appoint any particular person. It merely provides that a certain class of persons shall be qualified to act in those capacities." Ibid. A statute authorizing a safe deposit and trust company to qualify and act as statutory guardian of infants, upon the execution of its own bond with the pledge of its capital stock as security for the faithful discharge of its duty, is constitutional. The legislature has the power to prescribe the terms upon which the company may act as such guardian. Johnson v. Johnson, 88 Ky. 275; s.c., 118 W. Rep. 5. Under the statute providing that the capital of the Louisville Safety Vault & Trust Co. shall be taken and considered as the surety required by law for the faithful performance of its duties, and other security shall not be required upon its appointment to any of the offices or duties to be assumed by it, in a proceeding by it as guardian of infants for sale of their land and reinvestment, if the bond required by statute in such cases is necessary, one given by the company in the name of its president is sufficient. Phalan v. Louisville Safety Vault & Trust Co.,

<br>

### --- Page 0729 ---

§ 676.] TRUST COMPANIES AS TRUSTEES, 1553

§ 676. Claim of Beneficiary for Interest.—Where a trust company makes use of a trust fund in its own business it will be held liable in a settlement for interest on such funds at the legal rate. In an important and instructive case before the Supreme Court of Minnesota, where a trust company made use of a part of the trust fund coming to its possession as executor, in its own private business of a loan and trust company, but kept no separate account of the investments made from the trust fund, or of the interest or profits received or made as a consequence of such use, it was held that the trust company was properly charged upon a settlement of its accounts as executor, with interest on so much of the trust fund as was so used by it, at the rate of seven per cent per annum. In the same case it was farther held that where a trust company has charge of several trust funds belonging to separate estates or parties, and has used in its own business a part of such funds, and its cash

10 S. W. Rep. 10. See also Coleman v. Parrott (1890) (Ky.), 138 S. W. Rep. 525; s. c., 32 Am. & Eng. Corp. Cas. 1; Bank v. Payne, 86 Ky. 446. A Pennsylvania trust company was appointed the guardian of an infant legatee by the orphans’ court of Philadelphia, and upon a transcript of the record of the orphans’ court, was appointed guardian by the New York surrogate’s court, without being required to give the usual bond. By the laws of Pennsylvania, the trust company was relieved from the necessity of giving bonds as guardian, its capital and property being declared liable in case of default. Held, that the guardianship letters were properly granted; and further, that upon an application by the trust company for a payment of part of the legacy, the executors would be protected in such payment, although the transcript from the orphans’ court did not show that any bond or security had been given, as required by chapter 59 of the Laws of 1870; that the provisions of the Pennsylvania statute being similar to those of this State in like cases supplied the place of such security, and complied with the spirit of the Act of 1875, chapter 42. In re Cordova, 4 Redf. (Surr.) 66. “As far as the present application asks for the appointment of a guardian of property, it is granted. The respondent’s objection to the corporation nominated does not seem to me to be well founded. Letters may issue therefore to the Union Trust Company.” Ledwith v. Ledwith, 1 Dem. 154, 157. Where the residuary legatee is a corporation aggregate, administration with the will annexed will be granted to one of their own number, named by them for that purpose. In re Kirkpatrick, 22 N. J. Eq. 463.

1 St. Paul Trust Co. v. Kittson (1895), 62 Minn. 408; s. c., 65 N. W. Rep. 74.

<br>

### --- Page 0730 ---

1554 TRUST COMPANIES AS TRUSTEES. [§ 677.

balance kept on hand is made up of money belonging to all of the trust funds commingled, for the purpose of ascertaining how much money belonging to any particular estate it has so used, only the proportionate part of its cash on hand which belongs to such estate can be deducted from the cash balance due to such estate.

§ 677. Claim for Compensation.—Where trust companies act as trustees the service which they render is in accordance with the object of their organization. It is a part of their proper and ordinary business. For such services they are entitled to a suitable compensation. This may be a subject of agreement between the company and the settlor; it may be determined by a fixed rule of the company, or it may be settled by an order of the court where the court appoints the trustee. In a recent case it was held that a trust company, acting as guardian for infants, must employ agents in the collection of rents, and can lawfully charge the sums paid them. And when acting as trustees for the real owners it must keep the premises insured, and is entitled to be allowed for premiums paid. In the case of Rogers v. The New York Life Insurance & Trust Co., the trust company held funds belonging to a person of unsound mind deposited by his so-called trustee under an agreement as to the compensation. Upon the death of the beneficiary, the trustee became his administrator, and, on his application, the surrogate ordered that the fund remain in the company’s custody, subject to the court’s order. It was held that the contract fixed the compensation after, as well as before the order of the surrogate, since the order merely perpetuated the agreement of the parties.

1 St. Paul Trust Co. v. Kittson (1895), 62 Minn. 408; s. c., 65 N. W. Rep. 74, Rogers v. N. Y. Life Ins. & Trust Co., 1 N. Y. Supl. 271. “It was clear that the trust company

2 Garvey v. Owens, 35 N. Y. St. Rep. 133. See also St. Paul Trust Co. v. Kittson, 62 Minn. 408; s. c., 65 N. W. Rep. 74; Camden Safe Deposit and Trust Co. v. Ingsam, 40 N. J. Eq. 3. the trust company was entitled to employ agents for the collection of these rents; and having that legal right they could lawfully pay for the services of such agents. The point made by the appellant seems to be that de-

<br>

### --- Page 0731 ---

§ 678. Accumulations by Trust Companies.—In New York the rule in regard to perpetuities and accumulations is modified in certain cases where trust companies are trustees. It is provided by statute that, “on all sums of money not less than one hundred dollars which shall be collected and received by such corporation acting as executor, administrator, guardian, trustee, receiver or committee under the appointment of any court or officer, or in any fiduciary capacity under such appointment, or as a depositary of moneys paid into court, interest shall be allowed by such corporation not less than the rate of two per cent. per annum until the moneys so received shall be duly expended or distributed. If such interest moneys, or any part thereof, shall not annually be expended or distributed pursuant to the terms or provisions of the trust under which such moneys are held, the amount thereof not so expended or distributed shall be accumulated by such corporation for the benefit of the parties interested in such trust fund, and shall be added to the principal to constitute a new principal, upon which interest shall thereafter be computed.”

Defendants were collecting rents upon their own account. But it appears that the trust company, as guardian of the infants, were in reality managing the estate, and as such had a right to employ all suitable agents. * * * All the payments which they made were made from moneys they had received from these premises, except a very small sum; and it is not just that they should receive interest for payments made out of moneys belonging to the plaintiff, which they had received unless interest is also charged upon the receipts.” Garvey v. Owens, 35 N. Y. St. Rep. 133.

¹ Birdseye’s Rev. Stat. (1896 ed.), p. 230, § 160.

### --- Page 0732 ---

CHAPTER XXXV.

THE CESTUI QUE TRUST.

RIGHTS AND REMEDIES—DIVISION FIRST.

RIGHTS AND REMEDIES IN RELATION TO THE TRUSTEE.

§ 679. Introductory.

680. Right to Judicial Construction of the Trust.

681. Right to Maintenance.

682. Right to an Accounting.

683. The Subject Continued.

684. Right to the Enforcement of the Trust.

685. Execution of the Trust by the Court.

686. Right to an Injunction.

687. Right to the Removal of the Trustee.

688. When a Receiver will be Appointed.

689. Trust Property Appropriated by Trustees.

690. Personal Liability of Trustee.

691. Liability of Trustee for Unreasonable Acts.

692. Liability of Trustee for Mismanagement.

693. Liability of Co-trustees.

§ 679.

694. Loss by Act of Cestui Que Trust.

695. Acquiescence of Cestui Que Trust.

696. Acquiescence by Lapse of Time.

697. Waiver of Claim by Cestui Que Trust.

698. The Right to Protect the Trust Estate.

699. Claim of Beneficiary to an Advancement.

700. Priority of Beneficiaries Claim in Case of Insolvency.

701. The Subject Continued.

702. Partition of Trust Estate.

703. Right of Beneficiary not in esse.

704. Right to Bring an Action.

705. Right to Terminate the Trust.

706. The Subject Continued.

Introductory.—In general, the rights of the cestui que trust are such as attach, in equity, to the ownership of the trust estate.

The equitable interest in the trust estate

<br>

### --- Page 0733 ---

§ 679.] CESTUI QUE TRUST. 1557

and the equitable title to it are in the cestui que trust, and his rights and remedies, both in relation to the trustee and to third parties, are such as belong to this relation. They are such as have been conferred by statutes or have been established by the decisions of courts of equity. The nature and extent of his interest in the trust estate may be limited or modified by the terms of the instrument creating the trust. But in a simple and direct express trust, his interest is that of proprietor, and his rights are such as proprietorship confers. Where the provisions and requirements of the instrument by which the trust is created place the control and management of the trust estate in the hands of the trustee, he is entitled, for the time, to possession, but without reference to his right in this direction the legal title is in the trustee.1

1 Croxall v. Sherard, 5 Wall. 268; Badgett v. Keating, 31 Ark. 400.

A husband by deed of trust gave a life estate in lands to his wife, with the full right to the rents and profits, and provided at her death, if he were then dead, one-half of the lands “shall inure to and belong to my devisees, and, in default of devisees, to the heirs-at-law” of his wife, “and the remainder half shall inure and belong to the devisees, and, in default of devisees, to the heirs-at-law” of the grantor. The grantor died intestate, and afterwards his wife died, having willed all her property equally to the three defendants. Held, that plaintiff, the niece and heir-at-law of the husband, and defendants, were tenants in common, the former owning a one-half interest and the latter a one-sixth interest each. Obmery v. Boyer (1890), 89 Ala. 273; s. C., 7 So. Rep. 663.

Under a declaration of a trust the trustee, when the cestui que trust was 22 years old, was to invest the fund in a farm, and the necessary implements and stock, the latter to be given the cestui que trust absolutely, and the farm to be taken in the trustee’s name. The trustee was also empowered to sell the farm at any time and invest in real estate securities. Held, that on the trustee’s failure to purchase a farm, the cestui que trust was not entitled to the value of the implements and stock he would have been entitled to had it been purchased. Milligan v. Pleasants (1891), 74 Md. 8; s. C., 21 Atl. Rep. 695.

A deed of trust conveyed real estate to a trustee, “in trust, nevertheless, for the sole and separate use of A, wife of B, and her heirs,” etc., with power in such trustee to convey a fee-simple. Held, that this vested in A an equitable estate in fee-simple, and that, upon the death of B, she was entitled to a conveyance of the real estate. Chadwick’s Appeal (1886) (Pa.), 7 Atl. Rep. 178.

Where land acquired by a husband is partly held by him in trust for his wife, her money being

<br>

### --- Page 0734 ---

1558

CESTUI QUE TRUST.

[§ 679.

tions of the instrument, the cestui que trust is entitled to the possession of the trust property, and to the net income arising from it.

As early as 1766 the English court of King’s Bench had this question under consideration and

used in the purchase, improvements afterwards placed on the land do not lessen the wife’s proportional interest, but follow the ownership of the land. Blum v. Rogers (1888), 71 Tex. 668; s. C., 95 S. W. Rep. 595. Where land was conveyed to a trustee for the use, during her life, of a cestui que trust who had paid the purchase money, and to her children and grandchildren at her death, with a provision that the trustee should sell the property, on the request of the cestui que trust, at any time during her life; on such sale, by request, the purchase money became the sole property of the cestui que trust, and her children had no interest therein. Weber v. Ueberroth (1888) (Pa.), 13 Atl. Rep. 194. Bondholders secured by a mortgage agreed that certain persons should purchase the property for them on foreclosure sale, and hold the legal title, with all the rights of absolute owners. They were also authorized to sell and convey, lease, occupy and control the property, being accountable for the management of the trust. After the sale the trustees issued to each bondholder a certificate, stating the amount of his interest. On each certificate was a note stating that the purchaser would be entitled to a new certificate on the return of this one properly indorsed. One of the owners sold her certificate and the trustees refused to give the purchaser a new one. Held, that accordingly as the trust was void or valid, either the trustees had no right to give, or plaintiff had no need for, the certificate. Cassagne v. Marvin, 1 N. Y. Supl. 590. Where a deed of trust directs the trustee to pay the income and dividends on certain bonds to M, and provides that “should the said M die, the said trust herein declared shall inure to the benefit of her heirs, but if she have no children the same shall revert to my estate,” the estate of M is merely an equitable estate for life, and on the birth of issue she is not entitled to take the corpus of the trust estate freed from the trust. Fowler’s Appeal (1889), 125 Pa. St. 388; s. C., 17 Atl. Rep. 431. An assignee of a bond who holds the legal title for another, on the death of the latter holds in trust for his legal representative; and if assignment is made by the assignee to the widow with notice by her of the character of his title, and without any consideration, she acquires no title on which she can recover, Grant v. Heverin (1888), 77 Cal. 263; s. C., 18 Pac. Rep. 647. Where declarant of a trust for the life of the cestui que trust fails to apply rents and profits to the use of the cestui que trust as provided in the declaration, the liability incurred thereby is personal, and is not a lien on the remainder held by the declarant in the property, to take effect on the death of the cestui que trust, though the declaration provide that in case of a sale there shall be an equitable lien on the proceeds. MacArthur v. Gordon, 5 N. Y. Supl. 513.

<br>

### --- Page 0735 ---

§ 680. ] CESTUI QUE TRUST. 1559  
beld,—Lord Mansfield delivering the opinion,—that at that  
time it had "been long looked upon as a settled point, that  
the formal title of a trustee should not, in an ejectment, be  
set up against the cestui que trust, because from the nature  
of the two rights the cestui que trust is to have the possession." Where the possession is in the trustee, he is in  
such a sense the representative of the cestui que trust, that  
to all intents and purposes his possession is deemed the  
possession of the cestui que trust. The right of the cestui  
que trust to possession of the property, as of all other rights  
relating to the trust, are enforceable in equity.

§ 680. Right to Judicial Construction of the Trust.—  
Where there is a doubt in regard to the existence or legality  
of a trust, or in regard to its limitations or provisions, the  
cestui que trust may file a bill in equity for a decree to  
establish the existence of the trust and for a judicial con-  
struction of the instrument by which the trust is created.  
In a case in Illinois it was held that on a bill filed by a  
guardian of a minor to have a construction of a deed of  
trust given by the minor’s mother in her lifetime and a  
declaration of the ward’s rights under it, the court may pass  
upon the validity of the trust deed, and if it is found void,  
and inoperative, it would be the duty of the court so to de-  
clare in its decree. To construe a deed or other instrument  
is nothing more than declaring its legal effect. In case of

1 Armstrong v. Peirse, 3 Burr. 1898, 1901; Attorney-General v. Gower, Barn. 150; Brown v. How, Barn. 354; Glover v. Stamps, 73 Ga. 209; s. c., 54 Am. Rep. 870; Ingham v. Burrell, 31 Kan. 333; Matthews v. McPherson, 65 N. Car. 109; Hadden v. Chorn, 8 B. Mon. 70; Russell v. Clark, 7 Cranch, 69; Fausler v. Jones, 7 Ind. 277.

2 Miller v. Bingham, 1 Ired. Eq. 423; s.c., 36 Am. Dec. 58; Murphy v. Grice, 2 Dev. & B. Eq. 199; Lewis v. Hawkins, 23 Wall. 119; Green v. Otter, 3 B. Mon. 102.

3 Patterson v. Pullman, 104 Ill. 80. For some years prior to September, 1859, one Simpson Moyer owned and occupied, with his wife and family, a farm of about one hundred acres, which was then worth about $3,500, but was subject to a mortgage of $1,500. Moyer had then living thirteen children, of whom only one, a son, was of full age and resided in the vicinity. Moyer, being far gone in his last sickness, and desiring to provide for his infant children, executed and delivered a deed of the farm (which was substantially all his property) to his said son, the de-

<br>

### --- Page 0736 ---

1560 CESTUI QUE TRUST. [§ 680.]

A mistake the cestui que trust has a right to have the trust reformed by the court. In a case before the Supreme Court of the United States, it was held that where the evidence exhibited in the record shows that the purchase of land was made upon certain trusts which, through a mistake, the trustee failed to have properly declared in the deed, the cestui que trust is entitled to a decree directing the deed to be reformed. It has been held that cestuis que trust who have vested interests, though not the right of immediate enjoyment, may maintain a bill to have the trusts of the deed declared, and the trustee removed for misconduct. In Massachusetts this rule has not been fully upheld. In Baylies v. Payson, it was held that a bill in equity does not lie merely for the purpose of declaring a trust, even though the defendant denies the existence of the trust; but if he is about to leave the country, the trust may be declared, and the bill retained for further direction.

Defendant, upon the verbal understanding and agreement, that the farm should be kept and held by the latter as a home for the family during the minority of the children, and that when the youngest child should come of age, it or its proceeds should be divided among all of them. No consideration was paid by the defendant, nor did he agree to pay any. The father furnished the money to pay for recording the deed, and continued to occupy the farm until the time of his death, which occurred about ten days after the deed was made. After the father’s decease the family continued to occupy the farm, paying the taxes, and the interest upon the mortgage, keeping the farm in repair, and making some improvements of a permanent nature. The defendant was never in possession of any part of the farm, nor did he ever receive the rents or profits thereof. The defendant having repudiated the trust upon which the farm was conveyed to him, and claimed to be the sole owner thereof, this action was brought by one of the children to have it adjudged that he held the farm as a trustee for the surviving children, and to compel him to convey to them their respective shares therein. Held, that the action was properly brought and could be maintained. Moyer v. Moyer, 21 Hun, 67.

1 Walden v. Skinner, 101 U. S. 577.

2 Cooper v. Day, 1 Rich. Eq. 26.

3 Baylies v. Payson, 5 Allen, 473.

See also Wager v. Wager, 89 N. Y. 161; Bowers v. Smith, 10 Paige, 200; Hayes v. Jackson, 6 Mass. 153; Post v. Hover, 33 N. Y. 602; Read v. Steadman, 26 Beay. 495; Travers v. Travers, L. R. 14 Eq. 285.

### --- Page 0737 ---

§ 681.] CESTUI QUE TRUST. 1561

§ 681. Right to Maintenance.—The rule in regard to maintenance is subject to numerous modifications. Where the instrument contains no directions in regard to maintenance, the claim of the cestui que trust will be recognized, even where there is a direction to accumulate. Under ordinary circumstances where the instrument contains no directions in regard to it, the trustee should apply to the court for permission to use the income for this purpose. But he may apply the income to the maintenance of the beneficiary on his own responsibility, and presume that the act will be sustained by the court. But this rule is subject

1 Greenwell v. Greenwell, 5 Ves. 194, 195 n.; Evans v. Massey, 1 Y. & J. 196; Stretch v. Watkins, 1 Madd. 253; Buckworth v. Buckworth, 1 Cox, 80; Jervoise v. Silk, Coop. 52; In re Burke, 4 Sandf. Ch. 617; Rice v. Tonnele, 4 Sandf. Ch. 568; Heyward v. Cuthbert, 4 Desauss. 445; Wilkes v. Rogers, 6 Johns. 566; McKnight v. Walsh, 24 N. J. Eq. 498.

2 Nice v. Tonnele, 4 Sandf. Ch. 568; Bethea v. McColl, 5 Ala. 312; Corbin v. Wilson, 2 Ash. 178; Newport v. Cook, 2 Ash. 337. Under a conveyance of lands in trust “for the only proper use and benefit of the beneficiary," "for and during the term of his natural life,” the trustee "to hold said lands for the benefit of the beneficiary, only, and to account to him or his guardian for the rents or yearly issues,” the beneficiary’s interest is assignable. Henson v. Wright (1890), 88 Tenn. 501; s.c., 12 S. W. Rep. 1035. By order of court, on the request of the donors, that the funds be invested and applied “in such a way as will best promote the interest of the family,” money was invested for the use and benefit of a widow and her children during her life, and until

the youngest child should reach the age of 21, and all the female children should marry and be provided with homes. The widow died. All the children reached the age of 21 and but one daughter was unmarried, and she had a home with her aunt. Held, that the trust was absolute, without regard to the pecuniary circumstances of the cestui que trust, and the unmarried daughter was entitled to the whole income. Ward v. Funsten (1889), 86 Va. 359; s.c., 108 E. Rep. 415. Certain property was conveyed to J in trust for the grantor during life, and after her death to hold, keep properly invested, collect rents and profits, and the net income to apply at the trustee's direction to the decent and comfortable support of her son C, with power, if the income was insufficient, to use the principal, so far as necessary, first obtaining the consent of this court. After the death of C, provision was made for the distribution of any remaining part of the corpus of the trust. It was held, that it was not competent for the trustee to purchase with the corpus of the trust an annuity for C, though the income of the trust was insufficient

<br>

### --- Page 0738 ---

1562

CESTUI QUE TRUST,

[§ 681.]

to modification from the circumstances of the cestui que trust. If he is an infant and has a father living who is able to support him, his first claim is on the father.

The father is bound to support his children without regard to their

for the support of C, and the annuity would probably suffice, and the purchase was assented to by some of the donees over. Eldredge v. Green, 17 R.I. 17. A will devising property to a trustee for twenty-one years, with the discretion to convey or pay over to either of the beneficiaries before that time, “for his or her advancement in life, the whole or any portion of his or her share of the trust estate,” authorizes such advancement only on some occasion when the beneficiary contemplates some new undertaking requiring pecuniary outlay, and holding out a prospect of permanent benefit. Bailey v. Bailey (1888), 16 R.I. 251; s. c., 14 Atl. Rep. 917. Where testatrix devised and bequeathed property to her executors as trustees, to apply so much thereof as they should deem judicious for the care, support and maintenance of her husband, the executors, as trustees, took title only to so much of the estate as was expended for the care and support of the beneficiary, and are entitled to commissions in both capacities on so much only. In re Bennett's Estate (Surr.), 38 N.Y. Supl. 945; s. c., 16 Mise. Rep. 199. Where land is devised by testatrix to her sons, in trust for the support and education of their families, the property and the rents and profits thereof to be held by them, and applied during their lives to such purpose, and the will provides that the land shall in no event be liable for their debts, the crops grown thereon are all exempt. Maryland Grange Agency v. Lee (1890), 72 Md. 161; s. c., 19 Atl. Rep. 534. A declaration of trust gave the trustee power to invest the same, and accumulate the income, until the cestui que trust was 22 years old, or, at the trustee’s option, to apply the income during such period to his maintenance and education. When he was 22 years old the fund was to be invested in a farm and the necessary implements and stock, the latter to be given to the cestui que trust absolutely, and the farm to be taken in the trustee’s name. The trustee was also empowered to sell the farm at any time and invest in real estate securities. Held, that, on the trustee’s election to accumulate the income the cestui que trust was not entitled to the income accumulated before he was 22 years old. Milligan v. Pleasants (1891), 74 Md. 8; s. c., 21 Atl. Rep. 695. Part of the proceeds of trust funds appropriated by a father were made subject to the marriage settlement of his son, a beneficiary in remainder, who was ignorant of the source of the settled property. It was held that the son’s representatives were only entitled to have his share of the trust funds replaced after deducting the value of the proceeds settled. A debt due from a father to a son is not satisfied, in whole or in part, by a gift of less amount, or contingent, or uncertain in nature. Crichton v. Crichton, L.R. (1895), 2 Ch. D. 853. Under deed of land in 1874 to A, in trust for the sole and separate use,

<br>

### --- Page 0739 ---

§ 682.] CESTUI QUE TRUST. 1563  
property, present or prospective. But where he has no father, or has a father who is unable to support him, the court will recognize his right to maintenance from the income of the estate. In a recent case in New York, where the testator directed that a specified sum be invested in certain securities, and that the income be paid to his daughter for her support during life, it was held that, though the fund was not invested as directed, the daughter is entitled to receive from the trustee the amount which the fund, if invested as directed, would have produced. It has been held that where a trustee under a will allowing the cestui que trust free use of a dwelling-house during her life fails to keep the premises in tenantable condition, the cestui que trust has not the right to make such repairs, and look to the trustee for indemnity, as her remedy is to apply for his removal.

§ 682. Right to an Accounting.—Where there is no question in regard to the trust, the beneficiary is, at all times, entitled to a full statement of account by the trustee.

Benefit and behoof of B, a married woman, and her children (she then having children), with the provision that, upon the written request of B, the trustee may sell, dispose of, convey any part, or the whole, of the said land and make good titles thereto and receive the purchase money and pay the same over to B or her order, the married woman took an equal share with each of her children; and she having parted with her interest by a mortgage executed by the trustee on her written request, and a foreclosure and sale thereunder, her power under the deed was exhausted, and the purchaser became entitled to her share, and to a partition of the land between himself and the children. McNair v. Craig, 36 S. Car. 100.

1 Buckworth v. Buckworth, 1 Cox, 80; Jervoise v. Silk, Coop. 52; Rice v. Tonnele, 4 Sand. Ch. 568; Heyward v. Cuthbert, 4 Desaus. 445; Wilkes v. Rogers, 6 Johns. 566; McKnight v. Walsh, 24 N. J. Eq. 498; Ex parte Mountfort, 15 Ves. 445; Ex parte Whitfield, 2 Atk. 315; Ex parte Kent, 3 Bro. Ch. 88; Ex parte Salter, 3 Bro. Ch. 500; Ex parte Starkie, 3 Sim. 399; Ex parte Chambers, 1 Russ. & Myl. 577; Ex parte Green, 1 J. & W. 253; Ex parte Myercough, 1 J. & W. 151; Ex parte Hayes, 13 Jur. 765; s.c., 3 De G. & Sm. 485; In re Bostwick, 4 Johns. Ch. 100; Cross v. Bevan, 2 Sim. (N. S.) 53; Fairman v. Green, 10 Ves. 45; 2 Story, Equity Jurisprudence, 1341. See also ch. XVII., ante.

2 In re Lasak’s Estate, 20 N. Y. Supl. 74.

3 Fickett v. Cohu, 1 N. Y. Supl. 436.

<br>

### --- Page 0740 ---

1564

CESTUI QUE TRUST.

[§ 682.

On the demand of the cestui que trust, the trustee is bound to produce his books. Even where he has kept his accounts as trustee with those relating to his private business, he will be compelled to comply with the demand. It has been held also that where a trustee keeps his trust accounts in the books of a firm of which he is a member, such books must be produced.

1 Freeman v. Fairlie, 3 Meriv. 43; Hopkinson v. Burghley, L. R. 2 Ch. 447; Springett v. Dashwood, 2 Giff. 521; Walker v. Symonds, 3 Swanst. 58; Newton v. Askew, 11 Beay. 152; Gray v. Haig, 20 Beav. 152 n.; Burrows v. Wells, 5 De G., M. & G. 253; Clare v. Ormond, Jac. 120; Pearse v. Green, 1 J. & W. 140; Hardwick v. Vernon, 14 Ves. 510; White v. Lincoln, 8 Ves. 363; Turner v. Corney, 5 Beav. 515; Anon., 4 Madd. 473; Kemp v. Burr, 4 Giff. 348; Wroe v. Seed, 4 Giff. 425; Horton v. Brockleborst, 29 Beay. 504; Ottley v. Gilby, 8 Beay. 602; Talbot v. Marshfield, 2 Dr. & Sm. 285; Airey v. Hall, 38m. & Giff. 315; Bugden v. Tylee, 21 Beav. 545; Brown v. Oakshott, 12 Beay. 252; Wynne v. Humbertson, 27 Beav. 421. In an action for an accounting against a voluntary trustee, it is proper to require him to show what disposition he made of the assets. Lehman v. Rothbarth, 159 Ill. 270; s. c., 42 N. E. Rep. 777. Where a beneficiary has received an equivalent for his interest in trust property, he cannot maintain an action against the trustee for conversion thereof, without tendering a return of such equivalent. Graves v. Pinchback, 47 Ark. 470; s. c., 15 S. W. Rep. 682. The probate court has no jurisdiction over the accounts of trustees by construction or officials de son tort. Bailey v. Bailey, 67 Vt. 494; s. c., 82 Atl. Rep. 470.

An agreement between several to purchase real estate to sell for their joint benefit establishes fiduciary relation, and where one of them induces his associates to sell the property for a price which is in fact all it is worth, he having a secret agreement with the purchaser, pursuant to which he realizes a still larger sum from the property than his associates, they are entitled to an accounting, and to recover from him their share of that excess. Newell v. Cochran (1889), 41 Minn. 374; s. c., 43 N. W. Rep. 84. To a bill by stockholders against the trustees of a company to compel an accounting and distribution of the dividends and profits on the completion of the trust, the real owner of shares obtained by fraud from the trustees by a nominal owner is a proper party complainant, though such real owner has not complied with the trust agreement; the compliance by the nominal owner inuring to the benefit of such real owner. Hazard v. Dillon, 34 Fed. Rep. 485. In the case of a trust, as between a principal and agent, it is well settled that a court of equity has jurisdiction to enforce an accounting and a surrender of the trust property, the time when the trustee should do so having arrived, and he having refused. Weaver v. Fisher, 110 Ill. 146.

<br>

### --- Page 0741 ---

§ 682.] CESTUI QUE TRUST. 1565

keep a full and accurate account of all his business transactions relating to the trust, and any failure to do so, in consequence of which there is doubt or uncertainty in regard to the business, will raise a presumption against him. The burden of proof will be upon the trustee to show that the business has been conducted in a fair and conscientious manner. But where an agent kept a trust account in the same book with those of other trust estates, which he had managed for other persons, it was held that in the absence of such other persons he could not be compelled to produce the books. It is not necessary for the cestui que trust to allege any lack of good faith on the part of the trustee, in order to be entitled to an accounting. In a recent case it was held that the right of the beneficiary of a trust to enforce an accounting from the trustee does not rest upon fraud, and no such allegation is necessary in the complaint; nor is it necessary to allege that such an amount has been realized by the trustee as to entitle the beneficiary to a share of the profits, as he has a right to know how much has been realized and expended, so as to determine whether he is entitled to any payment. The fact that no amount is due cannot affect the right to an accounting when the trustee has denied the trust. Where it appears that there has been an error of any sort in the accounting the cestui que trust is entitled to a correction, and his right on this point is not lost by delay or even by the determination of the trust. In a recent case it was held that where a trust has been determined by the accomplishment of the purposes for which it was created, and the trustee’s bond has been surrendered and he has been practically discharged by a performance of all the trusts, he is not thereby necessarily re-

1 Blauvelt v. Ackerman, 23 N. J. Eq. 495. A trustee must keep and render accurate accounts of matters connected with the trust estate, and omissions or inaccuracies in his accounts inimical to the interest of his cestui que trust give rise to presumptions against him, which are decisive, unless overcome by collateral proofs establishing his perfect fairness and equity. Hottel v. Mason, 16 Colo. 43.

2 Airy v. Hall, 3 Sm. & Giff. 315.

3 Green v. Brooks, 81 Cal. 328.

<br>

### --- Page 0742 ---

1566 CESTUI QUE TRUST, [§ 683.

leased from responsibility. When the trustee has performed all the trusts, reconveyed the balance of the trust property, and rendered his accounts to the cestui que trust, which are by the latter received in final settlement, subject to rectifications in relation to interests and compensation, assumpsit for money had and received may be maintained by the cestui que trust against the trustee to correct the accounts and receive any balance in his favor upon a proper restating of the accounts. Where a beneficiary demands an accounting, and the trustee incurs expense in rendering it, he is entitled to reimbursement. In a recent case it was held that the general rule is, that a cestui que trust has a right to demand a full investigation and explanation of the accounts, but the trustee is entitled to be reimbursed for all his costs and expenses in accounting, when it appears that he acted honestly. If both parties are materially in fault, the expenses should be borne equally.

§ 683. The Subject Continued.—Where there is no question in regard to the equitable title to the trust estate, the beneficiaries have the right to examine all the documents and papers relating to the trust; and, if the trustee has employed professional counsel in his management of the business of the trust, the beneficiaries have a right to know what advice he has received. This holding is on the ground that the compensation for such counsel must be deducted from the income of the beneficiaries. Where there is more than one beneficiary they must all of them be represented, either directly or indirectly, in the case before the final disposition of the papers will be made. But it has been held that parties to bonds, mortgages or bills due to the trust estate need not be brought before the court at least where they are not beneficiaries. Where there is more

* Howard v. Patterson, 72 Me. 57.  
4 Bugden v. Tylee, 21 Beav. 545;  
2 Kingsbury v. Powers, 131 Ill.  
Gough v. Offley, 5 De G. & Sm. 182.  
653. Where a beneficiary demands  

3 Wynne v. Humberstone, 27 Beav. 421;  
Devaynes v. Robinson, 21 Beav. 42;  
Talbot v. Marshfield, 2 Dr. & Sm. 285.  

information as to his rights under a settlement, which cannot be furnished by the trustee without the assistance of a solicitor, the trustee

<br>

### --- Page 0743 ---

§ 684. ] CESTUI QUE TRUST. 1567

than one trustee, each has a right to consult the books and papers, relating to the trust estate, that, for the time, are in the hands of their co-trustees. But while the trustee denies the relation, and it has not been established by a decree of the court, the beneficiaries are held and treated as strangers, and, as such, they have no right to examine the papers relating to the trust; and where a suit is pending, or is in contemplation between the trustee and the beneficiaries, and the trustee has taken the opinion of counsel in regard to his own rights and interests, as opposed to those of the beneficiaries, they have no right to access to such opinion.

§ 684. Right to the Enforcement of the Trust.—Any person who may be appointed a trustee of an express trust is at liberty to decline the position. No one can be compelled to become the trustee of an express trust. But having accepted the position and assumed the responsibilities of the trust, the trustee is no longer at liberty to decline to discharge the duties of the office. So long as he continues in the position he can be compelled to execute the trust. In Georgia it has been held that “if the trustee omits to act when duty requires him to be active, or if he is wanting

is not bound to incur that expense (or if he be himself a solicitor with power to charge, he is not bound to incur the loss of time) unless the beneficiary is willing to pay the costs of complying with his requisition. In re Bosworth, 58 L. J. Ch. 432. A trustee who manages an insolvent estate informally without appointment, but at the request of the heirs, will not be held to show formal vouchers for his accounts where his acts have been in good faith and on consultation with the heirs. Groom v. Thompson (1891) (Ky.), 16 S. W. Rep. 369.

Sloo v. Law, 3 Blatch. 459.

Tulbot v. Marshfield, 2 Dr. & Sm. 285; Wynne v. Humberston, 27 Beav. 421; Devaynes v. Robinson, 20 Beav. 42; Brown v. Oakshott, 12 Beav. 252. The fact that in such transaction such creditor made and verified a claim against the estate for the full amount of the outstanding notes, and assigned the same to the trustee individually, would not constitute complicity in an attempt to defraud the trust estate, it being done with the understanding that such estate was insolvent, and that the trustee would pay a large part of the indebtedness from his own funds, and he having the right as trustee to charge his estate only with the amount actually paid out. Burling v. Newlands (1896), 112 Cal. 476; S. c., 44 Pac. Rep. 810.

1568

CESTUI QUE TRUST.

§ 684,

in necessary care and diligence in the due execution of the trust which he has undertaken, a court of equity will interpose.’’!

1 Jones v. Dougherty, 10 Ga. 273, 289. A deed of trust being under seal, and reciting a consideration, is presumed to be given for a valuable consideration; and the obligation of the trustee therein, being based on the transfer to him of the property therein described, rests upon a valuable consideration; and, where a husband and wife execute a deed of trust of lands involved in litigation, to secure certain liabilities, and conditioned for the return of a portion of the land to the wife if the litigation results in a recovery of the land by them, or, if a money judgment is recovered, then a certain portion of the money to be turned over to her, and a money judgment is recovered, but before the money reaches the trustee's hands the wife dies, her administrator is entitled to receive it, and the husband cannot recover it on the ground that the trust was a voluntary one. It was immaterial that the amount to be recovered by the litigation was not defined. The trust did not fail because the wife died before the trustee received the money. Jones v. Shipley (1886), 90 Mo. 307; s. c., 25 W. Rep. 400. A bill will lie by cestuis que trust to cancel a lease made by their trustee in payment of her private debt to the lessees. Complainants, if infants, could maintain such a bill in order to charge the recipient of the rents and profits of their land as their bailiff or trustee, though their title was legal instead of equitable. Johns v. Williams (1889), 66 Miss. 350; s. c., 6 So. Rep. 207.

The owner of government bonds deposited them with his banker, together with a written declaration of trust, that his wife should enjoy the interest during her life, and at her death they were to revert to his heirs. After his death the widow and one of the heirs agreed in writing that the bonds should be released from the trust for the purpose of better investment. The widow took possession of the fund, and at her death willed all of her estate to one of the heirs, excluding the other. Held, that the latter could recover his interest in the trust fund. Comer v. Comer, 24 Ill. App. 526; affirmed, 120 Ill. 420; s. c., 11 N. E. Rep. 848. Where property has been withheld from the true owner, first, by an individual, and then by a corporation organized and controlled by him, the true owner can take judgment for the fruits of such property against both the individual and the corporation for the latter period, but against the individual only for the former. Wheeler v. Billings, 72 Fed. Rep. 301; s. c., 18 C. C. A. 573. “While the court may compel trustees to execute the trust assumed by a corporation according to the scheme prescribed, it has no power, unless specially conferred by statute, to sequester the funds of a corporation, and deprive it of them, and dispose of them, as it may judge to be equitable and just, among those beneficially interested.” Savings Institution v. Mankin, 23 Me. 360, 365.

Testatrix devised all her land to H in trust for his nieces C and P. P conveyed her interest to C. Before the trust was closed, the trustees

§ 684. ] CESTUI QUE TRUST. 1569  
trust refuses to make the affidavit, and take the steps necessary to a trial of the right of property, the cestui que trust may resort to a court of chancery to have the trust enforced. It appears that this right will be enforced even where there is a provision in the deed that the beneficiary shall have no legal or equitable title until the death of the settlor. In a recent case in New York, where a deed creating a trust reserved a full power of revocation to the settlor, and it was provided as a condition of the grant that the beneficiaries should have no legal or equitable right to the principal or income, "but receive the same as proceeding solely from the bounty" of the settlor, "and subject to  

represented to C and P that it was necessary to pay off the claims of certain heirs, to raise money by mortgage, whereupon C executed a mortgage of the trust estate; P and the trustee signing as witnesses. The mortgage foreclosed by advertisement and the land was purchased by complainants, who brought this suit against C, P and H, to have the mortgage declared an equitable one, his purchase an assignment, and for a decree against all the defendants for any deficiency. Held, that under How. St. Mich. § 5578, providing that the title, both legal and equitable, shall be in the trustee, the only interest of the cestui que trust being the right to enforce the trust, C and P could not assign or encumber such interest, and the mortgage by C was entirely void as to her, that the mortgage not being made to carry out any trust created by the will, it was also void as to the trustee, under section 5583, providing that when the trust is expressed in the instrument creating it, all sales, etc., by trustees in contravention of it are void; and that P’s signing as witness, and her assumption of the mortgage in  

a subsequent deed from C, could not ratify as to her, a mortgage which was void. Weaver v. Van Akin (1888), 71 Mich. 69; s.c., 38 N. W. Rep. 677.

Brown, 26 Ill. 369; Kerr v. Hill, 27 W. Va. 576; Clarke v. Deveaux, 1 S. Car. 172; Kintner v. Jones, 122 Ind. 148; Robertson v. Snblett, 6 Humph. 313; Constant v. Matteson, 22 D. 546; Irvine v. 1570 CESTUI QUE TRUST. [§ 685.]

his power of revocation; that the trustee should hold subject to the settlor’s direction and control until his death; and that if any attempt should be made to interfere with the execution of the trust, or to claim the securities, contrary to the conditions imposed, then the trust should at once cease and determine, it was held that the right of the beneficiaries to enforce the trust against the trustee while it remained unrevoked by the settlor was not intended to be denied, and that the provision that the trustee should hold subject to the settlor’s direction and control was only a statement to the effect of the reservation of the power of revocation, and that a valid trust was created. In a recent case in New Jersey it was held that the cestui que trust cannot be said to have slept upon his right to relief in a court of equity against the trustee until the latter has assumed some position of antagonism to, or defiance of, the former’s rights, of which he has notice, and a mere retention by the trustee of a portion of the income of the trust fund, with the consent of the cestui que trust and without any pretense that it is so retained upon a claim of rights, does not produce such hostile attitude.

§ 685. Execution of the Trust by the Court.—Where other remedies have failed the cestui que trust may file a bill in equity for the payment of the trust fund into court. But in order to secure a decree to this end the plaintiff's bill must clearly set forth his equity in the trust property, and it must be essentially admitted in the answer of the defendant, as no aliunde evidence in regard to the merits of the case can be introduced. It should appear also from the answer of the defendant that he has the trust fund in possession or that he has received the fund in trust. If the

1 Van Cott v. Prentice, 104 N.Y. 45; s. c., 10 N. E. Rep. 257.
2 Dyer v. Waters (1890), 46 N. J. Eq. 484; s. c., 19 Atl. Rep. 129.
3 Richardson v. England, 4 Myl. & Cr. 171; Beaumont v. Meredith, 3 Ves. & B. 181; Dubless v. Flint, 4 Myl. & Cr. 502; McHardy v. Hitchcock, 11 Beav. 73; Bank v. Ottoman Co., L. 2 Eq. 366.
