An attorney for a guardian ad litem of a lunatic sued out execution on judgment for the guardian, and, at the sale of the land on which it was levied, became the purchaser, taking title in his own name, paying the price bid by receipting the execution, and paying the costs out of his own funds. He subsequently allowed the land to be sold for taxes and again became the purchaser, paying his own money and taking the tax certificate to himself. It was held, in a suit by the lunatic after restoration, to have title vested in him, on the theory that the attorney held as trustee, that a demurrer to the bill on the ground that "there is no offer in the bill to pay back the money paid out by respondent for taxes and costs, with interest," should have been overruled, as it was the trustee’s fault that there were costs by reason of the non-payment of the taxes, and therefore complainant was not bound to refund such costs. Godwin v. Whitehead (1892), 95 Ala. 409; S. C., 11 So. Rep. 65.

In determining whether a case of fraud is made out against a trustee, such as under Gen. Stat. N. H. ch. 230, § 43, will render him liable for costs, although not chargeable as trustee, the court may act either upon the finding of the jury, or upon its own view of the evidence. Kent v. Hutchins, 50 N. H. 92.

Where an action has been occasioned by the misconduct of a trustee, the costs incurred in the action subsequent to the judgment are in the discretion of the court. In this case the trustee was held not entitled to costs. Easton v. Landor (1892), W. N. 176.

A trustee holding property in trust for a married woman, who refuses to convey the legal title to a purchaser from the husband and wife, and answers a bill seeking a conveyance, resisting the relief sought, so far identifies himself with the cause of his codefendants as to make himself liable to a decree for costs. Knowles v. Knowles, 86

<br>

### --- Page 0856 ---

1680

sion for the action, his costs will be disallowed.

COSTS AND ATTORNEY’S FEES.

[§ 732.

This rule will apply also where the conduct of the trustee is factious or vexatious, or where he manifests unnecessary and unreasonable caution. 

But where there has been a breach of trust and a suit becomes necessary in the execution of the trust, that is altogether apart from the breach of trust, it is the rule that the trustee will be allowed his costs as between solicitor and client, but any costs occasioned by his breach.

II. 1. Where the trustee of a settlement became bankrupt shortly after the commencement of an action for the execution of the trust, and an order was made for payment by him into court of a sum of money certified to be due from him to the estate, he was held entitled to his costs of the action, though he was not to receive them until he had made good his default. Lewis v. Trask, L. R. 21 Ch. D. 862. The court will go so far as it can to give trustees their costs, charges and expenses properly incurred. But where a trustee’s own misconduct has occasioned the proceedings through which costs subsequent to judgment have been incurred, the costs are in the discretion of the court, and will not ordinarily be allowed him. Easton v. Landor, 67 L. T. 833. The answer did not deny the validity or legality of the petitioner’s claims, but set up the pendency of an action in which the trustee was plaintiff and the petitioner and others were defendants, for the purpose of settling conflicting claims, alleged by the trustee to have been made upon the fund and its income. No proof was given in support of these allegations. Held, the facts stated did not in any way tend to show that the petitioner’s claim was of doubtful validity, or that the action was necessary; but if this were otherwise, in the absence of the denial of validity or legality required by the Code (§ 2805), the pendency of the action was immaterial and was no bar to an accounting. Also held, that it was in the discretion of the supreme court to impose the costs of an unsuccessful appeal from the surrogate’s decision upon the trustee personally. Also, that upon affirmance here of the judgment of the general term, the trustee should be charged with the costs. In re McCarter, 94 N. Y. 558. A trustee having acted honestly, coming into court to render his account, is entitled to his costs, and sometimes his attorney fees; but the orator sought to avoid his own debt, and omitted to give credits which he could have stated, and thus put the defendants to expense to prove them, the supreme court refused to reverse the decision below, allowing neither party costs. Chamberlin v. Estey, 55 Vt. 378.

1 Easton v. Landor, 67 L. T. 833.

2 Marshall v. Sladden, 4 D. & S. 468; Attorney-General v. Murdoch, 2 Kay & J. 571; Griffin v. Brady, 39 L. J. Ch. 136; Palairet v. Carew, 32 Beav. 564; In re Cull, L. R. 20 Eq. 561; Firmin v. Pulham, 2 De G. & S. 99; Patterson v. Woollen, L. R. 2 Ch. D. 586; Cockroft v. Sutcliffe, 25 L. J. Ch. 313.

<br>

### --- Page 0857 ---

§ 733. ] COSTS AND ATTORNEY’S FEES, 16381

of trust will be disallowed. Where there has been a loss to the estate the trustee will not be allowed his costs until he has made reparation for the injury sustained in consequence of the breach of trust. The rule appears to be now well established in its application to costs incurred by a trustee defendant, even though he may have become bankrupt.

§ 733. In Case of Unreasonable Expenses.—It is the rule that trustees will be allowed such expenses as are necessarily and properly incurred in connection with suits relating to the trust estate. In an English case it was held that a receiver is not entitled to be reimbursed the expenses of journeys to, and residence in, a foreign country, for the purpose of prosecuting proceedings for the recovery of property belonging to the estate, before the tribunals of that country, unless he has the express sanction and authority of the court for such journeys and residence. In a recent case in New York, it was held that a charge for the expenses of a referee in traveling from one State to another to attend the reference will not be allowed.

1 Campbell v. Bainbridge, L. R. 6 Eq. 269; Pride v. Fooks, 2 Beav. 430; Bell v. Turner, 47 L. J. Ch. 75.

2 Bax v. Palmer, 56 L. J. Ch. 318.

3 Lewis v. Trask, L. R. 21 Ch. D. 862; Hannay v. Basham, L. R. 23 Ch. D. 195; McEwen v. Crombie, L. R. 25 Ch. D. 175.

4 Malcolm v. O'Callaghan, 3 Myl. & Cr. 52.

5 Willett’s Estate, 2 N. Y. Supl. 665. See also Berryhill’s Appeal, 35 Pa. St. 245; Floyd v. Davis, 98 Cal. 591; Ss. c., 33 Pac. Rep. 746.

### --- Page 0858 ---

CHAPTER XXXIX.

THE COMPENSATION OF TRUSTEES.

§ 734. The Common Law Rule.  
§ 735. The Rule in the United States.  
§ 736. The Compensation Determined.  
§ 737. Fidelity Required.  
§ 738. Determined by the Instrument.  
§ 739. Where Determined by the Court.  
§ 740. Compensation of New Trustee.  
§ 741. Reimbursement for Necessary Expenditures.  
§ 742. For Employment of Assistants.  
§ 743. Compensation as Executor and Trustee.  
§ 744. Compensation as Solicitor.  
§ 745. Compensation as Solicitor Continued.  
§ 746. In Trading with Trust Funds.  
§ 747. Lien of Trustee for Compensation.  
§ 748. Waiver of Compensation.  

§ 734. The Common Law Rule.—In England the common law doctrine is still upheld. It provides that no compensation is to be made to a trustee for his time or labor. The rule is subject to exceptions, but unless the instrument by which the trust is created provides for compensation, or there is something out of the usual course in the nature or circumstances of the trust, no compensation for services will be allowed. The rule, as upheld in England, is stated by Mr. Underhill, in his work on Trusts and Trustees, as follows: "A trustee has no right to charge for his time and trouble except in the following cases: (1) Where the settlement provides for it. (2) Where he has, at the time of accepting the trust, expressly stipulated for a remuneration, and the beneficiaries have freely and without unfair pressure assented to such stipulation. (3) Where the trust is before the court, and the trustee has, before accepting the

<br>

### --- Page 0859 ---

COMPENSATION OF TRUSTEES.

§ 734. ] 1683
trust, expressly stipulated for such remuneration. (4) Where one who is not an express trustee has properly traded with another’s money under circumstances which make him a constructive trustee of the profits. (5) Where the trust property is abroad, and it is the custom of the local courts to allow remuneration. The common law rule is in force in this country except as it has been modified by the decisions of the courts or by statutory regulations, but in most of the States it is no longer accepted.

1 Underhill’s Law of Trusts & Trustees (4th ed. 1894), 391. See also Bonithon v. Hockmore, 1 Vern. 316; Moore v. Frowd, 3 Myl. & Cr. 50; Charitable Corp. v. Sutton, 2 Atk. 406; Ayliffe v. Murray, 2 Atk. 58; Robinson v. Pelt, 3 P. Wms. 251; s. C., 2 Lead. Cas. Eq. 238; In re Ormsby, 1B. & B. 189; Burton v. Wookey, 6 Madd. 368; Hamilton v. Wright, 9 Cl. & F. 111; Webb v. Shaftesbury, 7 Ves. 480; Willis v. Kibble, 1 Beay. 559; In re Sherwood, 3 Beav. 338; Douglas v. Archbutt, 2 DeG. & J. 148; Barrett v. Hartley, 12 Jur. (N.S.) 426; Brown v. Litton, 1 P. Wms. 140; Chambers v. Goldwin, 9 Ves. 267. At common law a trustee was not entitled to compensation, and could not recover on a quantum meruit; and in Illinois the president and directors of a railway company are trustees for the stockholders, and for that reason the law does not imply a promise to pay them for discharging the duties imposed upon persons occupying that position. But a person not a director, and having no control over the funds and property of the corporation, rendering services, does not occupy the position of trustee to the company, and may recover a reasonable compensation for services rendered. Cheney v. L. B. & M. Ry. Co., 68 Ill. 570.

“The English books lay it down that, regularly, a trustee is not to have anything for his own labor and pains, though they admit, that even there, some have thought this a great hardship. It is true we have adopted the principles of the English law in many respects, so far as they are not incompatible with the principles of our own government, but we have not, by that, adopted indiscriminately all their decisions; and whatsoever may be my respect for them, as having been delivered by able judges, yet I must do here as they sometimes do there, that is to say, reject those decisions which do not conform to the standard of that justice, which commands us to live honestly, hurt nobody, and render everyone his due. Those who laid down the rule there, that a trustee should not have anything for his own labor and pains, seem to have done it without regard to that simple principle, but of unerring truth, that the laborer is worthy of his own hire; and hence it might well have been thought a hardship, even in that country where so monstrous a rule was suffered to obtain; it certainly has never taken foothold in our country; but if it has, so far as I can go, I shall blot it out forever.” Miller v. Beverley’s, 4 Hen. & M. 415, 419.

<br>

### --- Page 0860 ---

1684 COMPENSATION OF TRUSTEES, [§ 735.

§ 735. The Rule in the United States.—In most of the States it is well settled that a trustee who discharges the duties of his office with fidelity shall receive a reasonable compensation for his services. In general, the compensation is received in the form of a commission, and is taken from the income of the trust estate. In a leading case before the Supreme Court of the United States, the rule is stated by Mr. Justice Grier, as follows: "In England, courts of equity adhere to the principle which has its origin in the Roman law, ‘that a trustee shall not profit by his trust,’ and, therefore, that a trustee shall have no allowance for his care and trouble. A different rule prevails generally, if not universally, in this country. Here it is considered just and reasonable that a trustee should receive a fair compensation for his services; and in most cases it is gauged by a certain percentage on the amount of the estate."

1 Barney v. Saunders, 16 How. 535, 542. "The laws of this State allow to guardians, executors and administrators a compensation for their services, and in analogy and upon equitable principles we know no reason why a trustee ought not to be allowed a compensation for the care and responsibility incident to his position." Muscogee Lumber Co. v. Hyer, 18 Fla. 698; s.c., 43 Am. Rep. 332. A trustee is entitled to a fair compensation for his services out of the funds in his hands. Clark v. Platt, 30 Conn. 282. A trustee is entitled to a reasonable compensation for his services, under all the circumstances of the case. Urann v. Coates, 117 Mass. 41. Upon the termination of a trust where the real estate remained unsold and the commissions on the rents were not an adequate compensation for the care and management of the estate, an amount proportionate to the trouble and responsibility incurred was taken from the corpus of the estate and paid to the trustees. Biddle’s Appeal, 83 Pa. St. 340. According to the provisions of the 12th section of the Act of 1764, a trustee in this State is entitled to compensation for the time, and extraordinary trouble, and pains, necessarily bestowed in the management of the trust estate. Lowe v. Morris, 13 Ga. 165. Where a testator gave all his property to his executors in trust, with the power of sale, and directed them to pay and transfer to his son all the residuary “property, real and personal,” on his attaining majority, the trustees are not entitled to commissions on the value of real estate transferred to the son, since the trust and the legal title in the trustees terminate with the majority of the son, and the term "transfer" indicates testator’s purpose to have the real estate not previously disposed of transferred in specie. In re Thompson’s Estate, 1 N. Y. Supl. 213. Where one-third of the residue of an estate is bequeathed

<br>

### --- Page 0861 ---

§ 735.] COMPENSATION OF TRUSTEES. 1635

With regard to the manner in which compensation shall be made to a trustee, there is some difference among the States, but with regard to the principle upon which the rules are based, there is essential unanimity. In a case before the Supreme Court of Alabama, this principle is stated by Mr. Justice Goldthwaite, as follows: "The practice which has obtained in this State as to compensation, executors and administrators, in cases which do not fall within the statute, has been by a percentage on the receipts and disbursements; and the principle to be extracted from the decisions which this court has made upon that subject, is, that there is no fixed or uniform rate or limit to this compensation, but that the amount varies according to the circumstances of each particular case. The services actually rendered, considered with reference to the amount of trouble and the loss of time, and whether they were required or proper to be performed in the management of the trust,—the respon-

to be invested by the executors who are to pay the interest to the widow during life, the executors are entitled to compensation out of the income for their services in administering the fund. All kinds of trustees are entitled to a reasonable compensation for their services as they are rendered, and, unless a contrary intention appear, the compensation must come out of the income of the fund with which they are intrusted. Spangler’s Estate, 21 Pa. St. 335. Executors who are given power to sell real estate are charged with no duty with respect to it which entitles them to a commission on its value, unless a sale is made; but where as trustee they are given the legal title, and charged with the care and responsibility of its management, they are entitled to commissions on its face value as well as on the rents. In re Martin’s Estate, 39 N.Y. Supl. 189; s.c., 16 Misc. Rep. 245. By an equitable construction of, and by analogy to, the statutes of this State, allowing commissions to executors, guardians and trustees, under judicial sales, commissions may be allowed conventional trustees, though there was no agreement between the parties to that effect. Ringgold v. Ringgold, 1 H. & G. 11. A voluntary trustee is not liable to the trust estate for commissions received from the estate, after assumption of trust, for services rendered prior thereto under contract. Lehman v. Rothbarth, 159 Ill. 270; s. c., 42 N. E. Rep. 777. Where plaintiff cared for securities at the request of defendant, his right to compensation therefor is not affected by a contract of which he has no knowledge, made between defendant and a third person, whereby the latter received the benefit of plaintiff’s services, Louisville, N. A. & C. Ry. Co. v. Hubbard (1888), 116 Ind. 193; s. c., 18 N. E. Rep. 611.

<br>

### --- Page 0862 ---

1686 COMPENSATION OF TRUSTEES. {§ 735.

sibility involved, measured by the condition, character and value of the estate,—may very properly enter into the question of compensation; but, as courts, in making allowances to trustees, do not proceed on the principle of specific compensation for services rendered, care should be taken in fixing the amount, that trusts of this character should not become money-making occupations; and such would be the inevitable result, if services rendered by trustees were to be estimated according to the rules obtaining between man and man when dealing with each other at arms’ length. Compensation, when applied to trustees, means but little, if anything, more than a liberal indemnity. We should much prefer a fixed and definite standard, applicable to all cases, for the reason, that it would be less subject to abuse; but no such standard has been fixed by law, and the only principles we can lay down for the regulation of the compensation are those which we consider deducible from the authorities to which we have referred. The court, in making the allowance, is to look to the loss of time, trouble, risk and responsibility, which are demanded by the nature of the trust, and which is actually incurred, and to allow the trustee such a remuneration as a prudent and just man would, in view of these circumstances, consider a fair compensation, without, however, being governed by the business charges usually made for the like services; and the rule, as a general one, equally applies to all allowances made to executors, whether under the statute or without reference to it."+

1 Gould v. Hays, 25 Ala. 426, 431. See also Harris v. Martin, 9 Ala. 895; Magee v. Cowperthwaite, 10 Ala. 966; Ashurst v. Ashurst, 13 Ala. 781; Spence v. Whittaker, 3 Port. 327; Phillips v. Thompson, 9 Port. 667; Benford v. Daniels, 13 Ala. 667; Bethea v. McColl, 5 Ala. 314; Grimball v. Cruse, 70 Ala. 534; Clark v. Platt, 30 Conn. 282; Lowe v. Morris, 13 Ga. 165; Phillips v. Bustard, 1 B. Mon. 348; Winder v. Diffenderfer, 2 Bland, 166; Widener v. Fay, 51 Md. 273; Longley v. Hall, 110 Pick. 12; Schwarz v. Wendell, Walk. (Mich.) 267; Barnebee v. Beckley, 43 Mich. 613; Clark v. Hoyt, 8 Ired. Eq. 222; Boyd v. Hawkins, 2 Dev. Eq. 329; Royster v. Johnson, 73 N. Car. 474; Morris v. Harris, 19 Ohio St. 15; Kinney v. Heatley, 13 Ore. 35; Hubbard v. Fisher, 25 Vt. 539; Lane’s Appeal, 24 Pa. St. 487; Heckert’s Appeal, 24 Pa. St. 482.

<br>

### --- Page 0863 ---

§ 736.

COMPENSATION OF TRUSTEES.

The Compensation Determined.—In accordance with the principle generally accepted in this country, that a trustee who executes his trust in good faith is entitled to compensation, and, as already said, it is ordinarily allowed in the form of commissions. These commissions usually consist of a fixed percentage of the amount of property sold, or of the income collected and disbursed.

In many of the States the amount is determined by statute; in others it is fixed by a decree of the court, with reference to the degree of responsibility involved and of the amount of care exercised, or of the labor performed.  

In some of the States there are statutes regulating the commissions to be paid to trustees. In other of the States the decisions of the courts based upon the analogy of the office of trustee to that of the executor, administrator and guardian, have allowed the trustee similar compensation for services. Some of the States in earlier times followed the English rule and refused compensation altogether, but have since changed. A few of the States still cling to the English view that the services of the trustee shall be gratuitous except in cases set forth in § 734 ante. In New York, Chancellor Kent in Green v. Winter, 1 Johns. Ch. 37, and Manning v. Manning, 1 Johns. Ch. 534, adopted the English doctrine. Statutes have since been enacted covering the matter and the rate established is five per cent upon the first one thousand dollars, two and one-half per cent upon the next nine thousand dollars, and one per cent upon all above these amounts. Code Civ. Proc. §§ 2730, 2802.

See the following cases on the subject of commissions: In re Robinson, 3 Johns. Ch. 43; Dakin v. Deming, 6 Paige, 95; Meacham v. Sternes, 9 Paige, 403; Livingstone’s Case, 9 Paige, 442; Jewett v. Woodward, 1 Edw. Ch. 199; De Peyster’s Case, 4 Sandf. Ch. 514; Wagstaffe v. Lowerre, 23 Barb. 224; McWhorter v. Benson, Hopk. Ch. 28; Vanderheyden v. Vanderheyden, 2 Paige, 288; Hosack v. Rogers, 9 Paige, 461; Burtis v. Dodge, 1 Barb. Ch. 77; Halsey v. Van Amringe, 6 Paige, 12; Stevenson v. Maxwell, 2 Sandf. Ch. 284; Valentine v. Valentine, 3 Barb. Ch. 438; Savage v. Sherman, 87 N. Y. 277; Clute v. Gould, 28 Hun, 348; Foote v. Bruggerhoff, 66 Hun, 406.

On annual income: See In re Mason, 98 N. Y. 527; In re Selleck, 111 N. Y. 284; In re Kellogg, 7 Paige, 265; Hancox v. Meeker, 95 N. Y. 528; In re Mese role, 36 Hun, 298. On land: Phoenix v. Livingstone, 101 N. Y. 451; In re Tilden, 44 Hun, 441; Bruce v. Lorillard, 62 Hun, 416; In re Martens, 16 Mise. Rep. 245. On incumbrances: In re Security L. I. & A. Co., 31 Hun, 36; s. c., 95 N. Y. 654. On securities sold: Schenck v. Dart, 22 N. Y. 420; Hawley v. Singer, 5 Dem. 82; Farquharson v. Nugent, 6 Dem. 296; Cairns v. Chaubert, 9 Paige, 169; Ogden v. Murray, 39 N. Y. 202; In re Hawley, 104 N. Y. 250; McAlpine v. Potter, 126 N. Y.

<br>

### --- Page 0864 ---

1688

COMPENSATION

[§ 736, OF TRUSTEES.

centage or gross sum that the trustee shall receive may also be fixed by the parties in interest. It may be a matter of agreement between the trustee and the cestui que trust, and while the court will investigate a transaction of that

285. On legacies and annuities:
Westerfield v. Westerfield, 1 Bradf. 198; Cammann v. Cammann, 2 Dem. 211; Whitson v. Whitson, 53 N. Y. 479; Livingstone v. Murray, 68 N. Y. 485. A trustee under a will bought a farm to protect the trust, other than trust funds being used therefor, and others than the cestuis que trust being interested. To determine his rights and duties in regard thereto, he brought an action against all parties interested. The judgment directed sale and distribution of the proceeds. Held, that the trustee could not be allowed, out of the trust estate, commissions on the whole amount of the proceeds of the sale, but only on such part thereof as belonged to that estate. McKee v. Weeden, 37 N. Y. Supl. 465; s. c., 1 App. Div. 583. A decree on an accounting by trustees, adjusting and allowing commissions on an income, is not conclusive, though all the beneficiaries were parties to the proceedings, unless the amount of the commissions on the income is separately stated from the gross amount of the commissions allowed, In re Curtiss’ Estate, 37 N. Y. Supl. 586; s. c., 15 Misc. Rep. 545. In Pennsylvania a reasonable compensation has been allowed from an early date. The amount usually allowed is five per cent., but a discretion is lodged in the court allowing. See Wilson v. Wilson, 3 Binn. 560; Prevost v. Gratz, 3 Wash. C. C. 434; McElhenny’s Appeal, 46 Pa. St. 347; Pusey v. Clemson, 9 Serg. & R. 204; Kennedy’s Appeal, 4 Pa. St. 149; Biddle’s Appeal, 83 Pa. St. 340; Walker's Estate, 9 Serg. & R. 223; Armstrong’s Estate, 6 Watts, 236; Nathans v. Morris, 4 Whart. 388; Stevenson’s Estate, 4 Whart. 98; Pennell’s Appeal, 2 Pa. St. 216; Heckert’s Appeal, 24 Pa. St. 482; Duval’s Appeal, 38 Pa. St. 112; Robb’s Appeal, 41 Pa. St. 45; Snyder's Appeal, 54 Pa. St. 67; Whelen’s Appeal, 70 Pa. St. 410; Eshelman’s Appeal, 74 Pa. St. 42; Cassel’s Case, 3 Watts, 443; Aston’s Estate, 5 Whart. 228; McCausland’s Appeal, 38 Pa. St. 466; Luken’s Appeal, 47 Pa. St. 356; Perkin’s Appeal, 108 Pa. St. 314; s. c., 56 Am. Rep. 208; Carriers’ Appeal, 79 Pa. St. 239. An allowance to executors and trustees of six per cent. commission for collecting rents of thirty-six houses, is sufficient where there appears no evidence of peculiar difficulty in the collection. Wood’s Appeal, 86 Pa. St. 846. The usual compensation of fiduciaries is a commission of two and one-half per cent. on real estate sold, and five per cent. on personalty, and a less rate may be allowed where it appears that it would be a just compensation; but if a greater rate be charged, it devolves on those claiming it to prove that the amount and nature of the services entitled them to it. Dunlap’s Appeal (1888) (Pa.), 14 Atl. Rep. 262. Where the gain is over appraised value, resulting from the sale of securities in the hands of trustees is large, a commission of three and one-half per

<br>

### --- Page 0865 ---

§ 736. 

COMPENSATION OF TRUSTEES,

character, yet, if it appears to be altogether fair and equitable, it will be sustained.

In a case in Massachusetts it was held that an agreement made by a trustee with his cestui que trust, in regard to the amount of compensation he shall receive, is sufficient. In re Lafferty’s Estate, 17 Pa. C. Ct. Rep. 401; s. c., 5 Pa. Dist. Rep. 75. In Massachusetts reasonable compensation is allowed. See Barrett v. Joy, 16 Mass. 221; Gibson v. Crehore, 5 Pick. 146; Urann v. v. Coates, 117 Mass. 41; Blake v. Pegram, 109 Mass. 541; Scudder v. Crocker, 1 Cush. 323; Ellis v. Ellis, 12 Pick. 178; Longley v. Hall, 11 Pick. 120; Denny v. Allen, 1 Pick. 147. “When it is once admitted that a trustee may be paid for ordinary services, it is hard not to admit also that there may be circumstances under which he may be allowed an additional sum for extraordinary services which it was not his duty to render, the allowance not standing on contract any more than in the common case, but being subject to the discretion and control of the court. Whatever the general principle, there is no doubt that a testator has a right to provide for more liberal compensation to trustees than the law would give them apart from the will.” Turnbull v. Pomeroy, 140 Mass. 117, 118. In Maryland the court may vary the commission from five to ten per cent. on the amount of the inventory. See Wilson v. Wilson, 3 Gill & J. 20; Ringgold v. Ringgold, 1 Har. & G. 27; Brady v. Dilley, 27 Md. 583; Nichols v. Hodges, 1 Pet. 562; McPherson v. Israel, 5 Gill & J. 60; Parker v. Gwynn, 4 Md. 423; Scott v. Dorsey, 1 Harr. & J. 227; McKim v. Duncan, 4 Gill 72; Lemmon v. Hall, 20 Md. 168; Owings v. State, 22 Md. 116; Northern, etc. R. R. Co. v. Keighler, 29 Md. 572; Abbott v. Baltimore, etc. Packet Co., 4 Md. Ch. 310; Ex parte Young, 8 Gill, 287; Green v. Putney, 1 Md. Ch. 267; Diffenderfer v. Winter, 3 Gill & J. 347; Chase v. Lockerman, 11 Gill & J. 185. In North Carolina by analogy the fees of executors and administrators is allowed trustees. Boyd v. Hawkins, 2 Dev. Eq. 329; Sherrill v. Shuford, 6 Ired. Eq. 228; Raiford v. Raiford, 6 Ired. Eq. 490; Ingram v. Kirkpatrick, 8 Ired. Eq. 62; Grant v. Pride, 1 Dev. Eq. 269; Perry v. Maxwell, 2 Dev. Eq. 488; Hodge v. Hawkins, 1 Dev. & B. Eq. 564. The assignee of a surviving partner who was appointed to settle the estate, had ten days’ public sale and four months’ private sale of the stock of goods, from which he realized $13,200, and collected without suit, notes and accounts amounting to $6,400; he unnecessarily and negligently delayed the payment of debts and the settlement of the estate. Held, that two and one-half per cent commissions on receipts and disbursements is enough to be allowed the assignee for his services, under the circumstances. Weisel v. Cobh (1896), 118 N. Car. 11; s.C., 24 S. E. Rep. 782. In New Hampshire the matter of compensation is within the discretion of the court and varies from two to five per cent. See Wendell v. French, 19 N. H. 210; Tuttle v. Robinson, 33 N. H. 118; Gordon v. West, 8 N. H. 444; Lund v. Lind,

[§ 736.]

receive for his care of the trust property, is not invalid, if the cestui que trust is sui juris and competent to act, and no fraud is practiced or undue advantage taken; and such agreement should be taken into consideration by the Probate Court in determining the amount the trustee is entitled

41 N. H. 355. In New Jersey commissions are allowed. See Moore v. Zabriskie, 3 Green Ch. 51; Blauvelt v. Ackerman, 23 N. J. Eq. 495; McKnight v. Walsh, 23 N. J. Eq. 136; Lathrop v. Smalley, 23 N. J. Eq. 192; Lloyd v. Rowe, 20 N. J. L. 680. In Mississippi the compensation within statutory limits is within discretion of court. Satterwhite v. Littlefield, 13 Sm. & M. 307; Cherry v. Jarratt, 25 Miss. 221; Spratt v. Baldwin, 33 Miss. 581; Powell v. Burrus, 55 Miss. 105. In Kentucky the rule in the later decisions is to allow reasonable compensation. Phillips v. Bustard, 1 B. Mon. 348; Green v. Fox, 12 B. Mon. 190; Fleming v. Wilson, 6 Bush, 610. In Alabama, in the absence of a statute upon the subject, the courts have always held that the trustee is entitled to reasonable compensation for his services. Spence v. Whittaker, 3 Port. 327; Phillips v. Thompson, 9 Port. 667; Bethea v. McColl, 5 Ala. 314; Benford v. Daniels, 13 Ala. 673; Gould v. Hays, 25 Ala. 432; Bendall v. Bendall, 24 Ala. 306; Pearson v. Darrington, 32 Ala. 270; Harris v. Martin, 9 Ala. 899; McGee v. Cowperthwaite, 10 Ala. 968; Pinckard v. Pinckard, 24 Ala. 250; Newberry v. Newberry, 28 Ala. 691; Ivey v. Coleman, 42 Ala. 410; Waller v. Ray, 48 Ala. 468; Woodruff v. Snedecor, 68 Ala. 437. In Virginia the usual rate is five percent on the amount of the receipts. Lyons v. Byrd, 2 Hen. & M. 22; Waddy v. Hawkins, 4 Leigh, 458; Boyd v. Oglesby, 23 Gratt. 674; Fitzgerald v. Jones, 1 Munf. 150; McCall v. Peachy, 3 Munf. 288; Cavendish v. Fleming, 3 Munf. 198; Carter v. Cutting, 5 Munf. 223; Whitehead v. Whitehead, 85 Va. 870. See generally decisions favoring compensation: Moore v. Felkel, 7 Fla. 44; Lowey v. Morris, 13 Ga. 169; Gilman v. Des Moines Valley R. R. Co., 41 Iowa, 22; Stretch v. Gowdy, 3 Tenn. Ch. 565; Laner v. Brunson, 21 S. Car. 41; Muckenfuss v. Heath, 1 Hill Ch. 182; Wallace v. Ellerbe, Rich. Eq. 49; Ramsay v. Ellis, 3 Desaus. 78; Teveau v. Ball, 1 McCord Ch. 458; Massey v. Massey, 2 Hill Ch. 492; Briggs v. Holcombe, 3 Rich. Eq. 15; Daniel v. Fain, 56 Lea, 258; Davenport v. Lawrence, 19 Tex. 317; Canfield v. Bostwick, 21 Conn. 551; Clark v. Pratt, 30 Conn. 282; Kendall v. New England Carpet Co., 13 Conn. 383; New Orleans v. Baltimore, 15 La. Ann. 625; Succession of Sprowl, 21 La. Ann. 544; Hubbard v. Fisher, 25 Vt. 539; Hapgood v. Jennison, 2 Vt. 294; Evarts v. Nason, 11 Vt. 122. Where a trustee has properly administered the trust, he will be allowed a rehearing as to a decree for compensation, which was entered without permitting him to prove the value of his services. Hodges v. McDuff, 76 Mich. 303. In Illinois the English rule prevails. Constant v. Matthews, 22 Ill. 547; Hough v. Harvey, 71 Ill. 72. The former practice in Delaware was in harmony with that of

<br>

### --- Page 0867 ---

§ 737. COMPENSATION OF TRUSTEES. 1691

to charge. Where provision is made by the instrument, by which the trust is created, for the compensation of the trustee, the court will be guided by the intention of the settlor without reference either to equitable rules or to statutory provisions. In New York it has been held that where an instrument creating a trust provides that the trustee shall have a reasonable compensation for his services, he is not confined to the statutory allowances to executors, etc.; but his compensation is to be adjusted at what shall be determined, upon judicial investigation, to be reasonable under the circumstances, without regard to the statute. But in whatever manner the amount of the compensation may be determined it must be for service actually rendered. Commissions to co-trustees will be apportioned according to the value of the actual service rendered by either to the trust. It appears that where a trustee accepts a trust coupled with an interest, and there is no provision for compensation in the instrument, it will not be allowed. In Arkansas it has been held that “though the English rule has been modified in this country, and commissions are allowed in some cases, still the trustee is entitled to none, where he accepts the trust coupled with an interest, and the deed expressly provides for the payment of the expenses of the trust, but is silent as to whether he shall have compensation for his trouble and attention.”

§ 737. Fidelity Required.—It is well established that a trustee is entitled to compensation only as he has faithfully discharged the duties of his trust. Where there has been a partial performance of duty that has been of value to the estate and a partial failure to execute the trust in a bona

State of Delaware in his discretion.” Laws of Delaware (1893), England. Egbert v. Brooks, 3 Harr. 112; State v. Platt, 4 Harr. 154. In 1887 a statute was passed providing: “That all trustees now or hereafter to be appointed by will may be allowed commissions as compensation for their services, the amount of the same to be determined by the chancellor of the

p. 712.

1 Bowker v. Pierce, 130 Mass. 262.

2 In re Schell, 53 N. Y. 263.

3 Willetts’ Estate, 2 N. Y. Supl. 665.

4 Imboden v. Hunter, 23 Ark. 622; s. c., 79 Am. Dec. 116, 131.

<br>

### --- Page 0868 ---

1692 COMPENSATION OF TRUSTEES. [§ 787.

side manner he may be allowed a partial compensation. The court will decree such compensation as, in view of all the circumstances, it shall deem just and proper. But where there has been a palpable breach of trust or a gross and deliberate neglect of duty, whether the consequence may have been prejudicial to the interest of the estate or otherwise, he will be adjudged to have forfeited his claim to a compensation. In a leading case before the Supreme Court of the United States, Mr. Justice Grier, in his opinion, said: "On principles of policy as well as morality and in order to secure a faithful and honest execution of a trust, as far as practicable, it would be inexpedient to allow a trustee who has acted dishonestly or fraudulently the same compensation with him who has acted uprightly in all respects, and there may be cases where negligence and want of care may amount to a want of good faith in the execution of the trust as little deserving of compensation as absolute fraud. If trustees, having a large estate to invest and accumulate for the benefit of an infant, for a number of years, will keep no books of account, make out no annual or other account of their trust estate, if they risk the trust funds in their own speculations, lend them to their relations without security, and in other ways show a reckless disregard of the duties which they have assumed, they can have but small claim on a court of equity for compensation in any shape or to any amount." In a recent case in New Jersey, it was

Matthews, 30 Md. 235; Marcy's Account, 24 N. J. Eq. 451; Dufford v. Smith, 46 N. J. Eq. 216; Tice, 28 N. J. Eq. 432; McKnight v. Walsh, 23 N. J. Eq. 136; Lathrop v. Smalley, 23 N. J. Eq. 192; Fall v. Simmons, 6 Ga. 274; Kenan v. Hall, 8 Ga. 417; Blauvelt v. Ackerman, 23 N. J. Eq. 495; Cook Baker, 35 Hun, 272; Sherrill v. Shuford, 6 Ired. Eq. 228; vy. Sutliff, Estate, 2 Watts & S. 557; Nagle's Estate, 12 Phila. 25; Berryhill's Appeal, 35 Pa. St. 245; Robinett's Appeal, 36 Pa. St. 174; Stehman’s Appeal, 5 Pa. St. 413; Hermstead’s Appeal, 60 Pa. St. 423; Swartzswalter’s Account, 4 Watts, 77; Norris’ Appeal, 71 Pa. St. 106; Singleton v. Lowndes, 9 S. Car. 465; Frazier v. Vaux, 1 Hill Eq. 203; McCloskey v. Gleason, 56 Vt.

### --- Page 0869 ---

§ 737.

COMPENSATION OF TRUSTEES,

held that where a trustee has neglected to keep an account of his investments of the trust funds, and of his receipts and disbursements of the same, and thereby expensive litigation has been occasioned and the courts have been de-

264; s. c., 48 Am. Rep. 770;  
Spaulding v. Wakefield, 53 Vt. 660; s. c., 38 Am. Rep. 709; Ward v. Funsten, 86 Va. 359. A trustee should not be allowed commissions on sums which he has invested in his own business, securing them on his own property without the knowledge of his cestui que trust, especially when there is evidence that he agreed to make no charge therefor. Harris v. Sheldon (Pa.), 16 Atl. Rep. 828. Commissions are a compensation for the faithful discharge of duty. Where a testator gave discretion to trustees to sell lands, and directed that, if sold, the proceeds should be invested in good landed security, held that if the funds arising from the sale had been in the hands of the trustees, and had remained so for a long period without any security, that they had violated their duty as trustees and were not entitled to commissions. Trustees who did not invest according to the terms of the trust compelled to pay the costs of the suit, the plaintiff having been driven into litigation to establish the amount of the trust fund. Warbass v. Armstrong, 10 N. J. Eq. 263. “A fiduciary, who acts in good faith and with reasonable prudence in the discharge of his duties, ought, undoubtedly, to be dealt with liberally, and ought not to be held personally responsible for losses that occur under such circumstances. But where a fiduciary utterly fails to obey the law requiring settlements of his accounts, and then turns around and asks that he be allowed out of the trust estate compensation to which he is not entitled except under peculiar circumstances, the case is different, and unless, in such a case, the lower court has plainly abused its discretion, in allowing or refusing compensation, its action ought not to be disturbed by the appellate court.” Ward v. Funsten, 86 Va. 359, 370. A trustee who neglects and willfully mismanages a trust estate cannot recover compensation for his services. Lehman v. Rothbarth, 159 Ill. 270; s. c., 42 N. E. Rep. 777. Neither a trustee who has rendered no services in connection with the fund or estate, and incurred no liability therefor, nor a defaulting trustee who rendered services, is entitled to commissions. In re Lafferty’s Estate (Orph. Ct.), 17 Pa. Co. Ct. Rep. 401; s.c., 5 Pa. Dist. Rep. 75. “The trust in this case was never executed. The trustee in substance converted the securities to his own use. He kept no proper account and rendered an account in many respects untrue, and subjected the cestui que trust to great difficulty and expense in attempting to unravel it. Commissions are allowed to trustees as a compensation for services in the execution of the trust, and in a case of gross neglect or of unfaithfulness, we think the court may properly disallow them.” Cook v. Lowry, 95 N. Y. 103, 114. Where a trustee, in violation of the trust, fails to invest the fund, and uses it in his own business, he is not entitled to commissions. Where

<br>

### --- Page 0870 ---

1694

COMPENSATION OF TRUSTEES.

[§ 737.]

Prived of means of being assured of the correctness of the accounting had, his neglect may be punished by resolving doubts against him, and also by withholding compensation.

The executor was a debtor of the testator, and a trust fund established by the testator consists of the debt, which the executor has never paid into the estate, but upon which he paid the interest as it accrued, he is not entitled to commissions. An executor or his representative is not entitled to commissions on any part of the assets not collected. The principal of a specific sum bequeathed as a trust fund is not liable to commissions; they must come out of the residue of the estate. McKnight v. Walsh, 23 N. J. Eq. 136. The sum in the hands of the trustees at the death of the grantor, arising from the income of the estate, was directed to be applied to the voluntary claims, pro rata, except one, to one of the trustees, who, in addition to being the legal adviser of the grantor, and also trustee, had stated in answer to a bill in equity that he had never claimed it. A trustee who acted in opposition to a trust was therefore denied compensation; but his claim as donee of a note under seal, on which judgment had been obtained against the executor of the will of the grantor, was admitted to a pro rata distribution with the other voluntary claims out of the income of the estate at the death of the grantor. Greenfield’s Estate, 24 Pa. St. 232. A trustee who has abused his trust is entitled to no commissions as trustee, but he will be allowed reasonable compensation for special and extraordinary services to the cestui que trust. Moore v. Zabriskie, 18 N. J. Eq. 51. When the damage resulting to beneficiaries from a trustee’s breach of trust is greater than the benefit derived from his services, he is entitled to no compensation. Judge of Probate v. Jackson, 58 N. H. 458. “Considering the nature of the office of a trustee, it follows that as on the one hand his compensation may, because of the discharge of his duties being attended with a very unusual degree of labor and risk, be increased; so, on the other hand, his compensation, because of his duties having been improperly, or but partially performed, may be altogether withheld, or proportionably diminished.” Gibson’s Case, 1 Bland, Ch. 138; s. c., 17 Am. Dec. 257, 264. Where a creditor having accepted a trust to provide for his own security, as well as the security of other debtors, repudiates the trust by insisting that the assignment to him was absolute, and refuses to pay other creditors out of the surplus, he will be allowed no commissions as a trustee, upon its being established by a creditor that he is a trustee. Ireland v. Potter, 16 Abb. Pr. 218; s. c., 25 How. Pr. 175. One in the possession of property, who is sought to be charged as trustee, and who denies the trust, claiming title as the absolute owner, cannot, upon a rendition of judgment against him, claim compensation for services in the management of the property. Pollard v. Lathrop, 12 Colo. 171. See also Ellicott v. Ellicott, 6 Gill & J. 35; Fahnestock’s Appeal, 104 Pa. St. 46.

<br>

### --- Page 0871 ---

§ 737. COMPENSATION OF TRUSTEES. 1695

from him. Where a trustee endeavors to escape responsibility and labor by denying the trust, he will not be heard in presenting a claim for compensation. In a case in South Carolina, the court said: “As it regards the claim for commissions by the trustee, we need not consider the abstract question raised. The defendant came before the court, in his answer, denying the existence of the trust, and contending that, contrary to the intent of the trust, he had turned over all the assets of the trust estate, many years ago, into the hands and exclusive control of the life tenant, without regarding the claims of those entitled to take by way of remainder after termination of the life estate. He claims that the trust itself was terminated by that act. This was a clear breach of trust. The defendant claims to have acted since that time as the agent of the life tenant merely,—in other words,—that he has not acted from the responsibility imposed by the duty of a trustee for that time. It is not a case for the allowance of commissions, assuming that they could be allowed. Such a claim must rest upon the due performance of the duties imposed by the trust, and when these duties are unperformed it is not demandable either at law or in equity. The answer only demands commissions in case the defendant ‘is held liable for the mischances of the fund.’ The defendant has not been held liable for ‘mischances,’ but for a departure from a clear line of legal duty. No equity exists to extend the demand of the answer beyond its direct sense.” In Pennsylvania it has been held that a trustee who vexes the heirs and delays distribution by an exorbitant claim for commissions is not entitled to the same compensation as one who makes a reasonable claim. A failure to execute the trust in the best manner, where the trustee acts in good faith and according to the best of his ability, will not deprive him of his compensation. In a recent case in Pennsylvania, it was held that where the trustee does not manage the estate of the cestui que trust

1 Welsh v. Brown (1893), 50 N. 568, 8 Carriers’ Appeal, 79 Pa. St. 230. Singleton v. Lowndes, 8 S. C. J. Eq. 387; 8s. C., 26 Atl. Rep. 465, 491.

<br>

### --- Page 0872 ---

1696 COMPENSATION OF TRUSTEES. [§ 738.

well, but does manage it conscientiously and to the best of his ability, and, while he may lack business qualifications, yet his integrity and intention to act for the best interests of his cestui que trust is unimpeached, it is not inequitable to allow him a small sum for commissions, and to charge the estate with one-half the costs of the audit of his accounts.

§ 738. Determined by the Instrument.—The creator of a trust may determine the compensation of the trustee by a provision of the instrument, and where the instrument fixes the amount of the compensation or provides that there shall be none, the court will not interpose unless the amount fixed is unreasonable and inequitable. In South Carolina it has been held that where the instrument creating a trust fixes the compensation which the trustee shall be entitled to for his trouble in executing the trust, the general law in reference to the commissions of trustees has no application to the case, and the trustee must be content with the compensation allowed by the instrument. Where a claim is set

rate, the value of the services should be ascertained by judicial investigation. In re Schell, 53 N. Y. 263, 265. The testator, by his

Beall, 3 Wis. 395. "Where the instrument creating the trust provides that the trustee shall have a compensation for his services in executing the trust, such provision will be enforced. If the instrument declares the rate of compensation, it must be followed. If it establishes no

will, gave to his executors certain powers as trustees of his niece; authorized his "executors and trustees" to act collectively or individually in all matters appertaining to the estate; and, further provided as follows: "I give and bequeath to each of my executors and trustees, the sum of three thousand dollars, in lieu of any and all commissions, and in full compensation for their services in closing up my estate and making distribution thereof in conformity with and on the conditions hereinbefore stated." The executors each received $3,000, and, upon their accounting in their capacity as testamentary trustees, asked for commissions as such

### --- Page 0873 ---

§ 738.] COMPENSATION OF TRUSTEES. 1697

Up that the compensation provided by the instrument is excessive, the court will investigate, and if the point is established it will afford relief. In a case in Alabama, the court said: “With respect to the compensation allowed the trustee, P, for his services in the execution of the trust, the proof is insufficient to show that the commission provided by the contract to be paid to him was unreasonable. That the compensation is more than is usually allowed for collecting, disbursing, etc., is certainly true, but we must take into account the character of the duties the trustee had to perform. He was to collect many, and, perhaps, small amounts; his duties embraced a settlement of the affairs of a dissipated and reckless man, whose business was doubtless confused and difficult to arrange. Under these circumstances, and in the absence of proof that the allowance of 124 per cent. was unconscientious, we do not feel authorized to hold that such a provision in the trust deed would render it void upon its face.”

Trustees, contending that the provision for compensation contained in the will applied to them only as executors. Held, that such a construction would be contrary to the letter and spirit of the will, and that the application should be denied. Brownson v. Roberts, 5 Redf. 576. See also Bowker v. Pierce, 130 Mass. 262. “The fourth exception respecting the claim and allowance of an annual commission of ten dollars for managing the trust fund is disallowed, and solely on the ground that the parties themselves have made their own arrangement and settlement on the subject. Were the matter considered properly open, the court would feel strongly inclined to reject the claim altogether; not for the want of any power or right to make an equitable and just compensation to trustees, but because the trustees, in this instance, have not done their duty in properly investing the trust fund committed to their care.” Jackson v. Jackson, 3 N. J. Eq. 96, 113. “It is true that the amount may be excessive compensation, that the services now rendered are not of much value, and that the trustee may not live to fully execute the trust. But, as the legacy is not conditioned upon him living to execute it, and payment is not postponed until the termination of the trust, the presumption is that the contingency of the death of the trustee before its termination, and consequent inability to execute it, were considered by the testator when he fixed the compensation. The amount of this legacy, then, is the compensation fixed by the testator for the trustee’s services, notwithstanding these contingencies.” Harper's Appeal, 111 Pa. St. 243, 245.

1 Donelson v. Posey, 13 Ala. 752, 768.

<br>

### --- Page 0874 ---

1698 COMPENSATION OF TRUSTEES. [§ 739.]

testator, having said that the trustee, whom he also appointed his executor, shall have "ten per cent. on the whole amount of property which may come into his hands as trustee," it was held that he was entitled to this percentage on the whole amount of property, and not on the income only, irrespective of the sum which may have been allowed him by the Orphans’ Court as executor; and, in this respect, the two offices are to be regarded as distinct, as if filled by two different persons.

§ 739. Where Determined by the Court.—In the absence of any statutory provisions, of any contract between the parties in interest, or of any direction of the instrument, the compensation of a trustee will be fixed by the court. In such cases the amount of compensation will be determined with reference to the character of the trust and of all the circumstances of the case. The court will be guided by its sense of what is right and proper, not by any established rule. In a case in Pennsylvania, the court said: "It has been long settled in Pennsylvania that trustees of all sorts are entitled to reasonable compensation for their services, though there be no stipulation on the subject in the instrument creating the trust. But the law has fixed no rule for measuring the rate of compensation, and it is obvious, from the infinitely diversified circumstances attending trusts, that no inflexible rule ever can be prescribed. The amount of compensation must depend on the discretion, which is nothing else than the reason and conscience of the tribunals having jurisdiction of the trust. In the admeasurement of it, regard is to be had to the amount and character of the estate, and to the labor, skill and success attending the administration of it." In a recent case in

Walsh, 23 N. J.

§ 739.] COMPENSATION OF TRUSTEES. 1699  
New York a testator directed his three executors to carry on his business for the lifetime of one of his daughters. One executor, at his death, was employed in the business at a salary of $25,000 per year, and another at a salary of $10,000, and they were allowed by the court the same salary for managing the business, to which they devoted their entire attention, in addition to the fees allowed them by law as executors. The third executor was engaged in other business, and received no extra compensation. The business had a capital of $7,500,000, which the executors increased in ten years to $9,200,000. The executor drawing a salary of $25,000 died, and the entire management of the business devolved on the survivors. It was held that a judgment allowing each survivor an additional compensa-

Eq. 136; Moore v. Zabriskie, 18 N.-J. Eq. 51; Lathrop v. Smalley, 23 N. J. Eq. 192; Harland’s Accounts, 5 Rawle, 323; Biddle’s Appeal, 83 Pa. St. 340; s. c., 24 Am. Rep. 183; McElhenny’s Appeal, 46 Pa. St. 347; Kennedy’s Appeal, 4 Pa. St. 149. “With us it is the usual and common practice to allow executors, administrators and guardians a percentage upon the amount of the receipts and disbursements as a compensation for the performance of the trust. This percentage has never been fixed by statute, and until some specific rule is declared upon the subject, it is evident each case must be governed by its peculiar circumstances. It is apparent, however, the quantum of trouble, and loss of time, is not the only matter to be considered, as the settlement of an estate of $500 may involve as much difficulty as one of $50,000. The compensation must also, to a great extent, be controlled by the amount of the estate. It is evident enough, from a consideration of all the cases, that the courts do not proceed, when making the allowance to a trustee, upon the principle of specific compensation for services rendered, or to be rendered. The usual course of the court of chancery is to refer it to a master to settle the allowance. These are the general principles applicable to allowances, and we can perceive no reason why one who is not a mere voluntary trustee, appointed by a will or deed, and is also an attorney, shall not be allowed a reasonable compensation for his services in the latter capacity, when such have been necessarily and bona fide rendered for the benefit, or called into use by the necessities of the estate. But we do not think the amount of compensation is to be ascertained by inquiring what such services would usually be rated at. In the inquiry it would be more proper for the court to ascertain what a prudent individual, invested with the functions of administrator, would feel authorized to pay an attorney, taking into consideration all the circumstances of the estate.” Harris v. Martin, 9 Ala. 895, 899.

<br>

### --- Page 0876 ---

1700 COMPENSATION OF TRUSTEES. [§ 740.

tion of $5,000 yearly, to date from the death of their co-executor, should be affirmed.

§ 740. Compensation of New Trustee.—It has been held that where the compensation of a trustee has been fixed by the instrument and allowed by the court to the trustee named in the instrument, a successor of such trustee will be entitled to a reasonable compensation for the services that he may render. In a case in Kentucky, it was held that compensation to executors and trustees being fixed in the will at one thousand dollars, that amount having been allowed to the first executor and trustee who qualified, a subsequent executor and trustee who qualified is held to be entitled to an allowance of at least five per cent. upon the profits realized upon a large and well managed trust fund. In Maryland it has been held that the practice of allowing a reasonable compensation to the estate of a deceased trustee, who dies before the completion of the trust, is well settled, and sanctioned by authority. The substituted trustees must be content to have their commissions regulated by the chancery rule. They derive their appointment from the court, and the order appointing them makes no provision for their compensation. As the appointees and officers of the court their compensation must be regulated by the rules of the court.

1 Lawrence v. Garner, 1 N. Y. Supl. 534.

2 Young v. Smith, 9 Bush, 421.

3 Widener v. Fay, 51 Md. 273.

In some cases the fund may, to a certain extent, be burdened with double commissions, as in this instance, where the trustee dies after having actually received an amount of the proceeds of sale equal to the sum allowed to him as commissions upon the whole by a previous order of the court. In such case the court cannot revoke its order merely because of the death of the trustee; and, therefore, the only mode in which this double charge could be prevented or corrected would be to alter the practice, so as to postpone the payment of the trustee’s commission until the whole of his duties had been performed, or to authorize summary proceedings to be instituted to make his representatives refund in part, with which the succeeding trustee may be compensated for his trouble in collecting the balance. Under such circumstances, it seems to be fair, by way of analogy to the rule laid down by the legislature in regard to sheriffs and others, to apportion the commission or poundage, where it can

<br>

### --- Page 0877 ---

§ 741. Compensation of Trustees.

§ 741. Reimbursement for Necessary Expenditures.—It is well established that a trustee has a valid claim upon the trust estate for necessary expenses and for other expenditures made in the proper discharge of his official duty. The principle on which this doctrine is based is stated by Lord Chancellor Eldon, as follows: "It is in the nature of the office of a trustee, whether expressed in the instrument or not, that the trust property shall reimburse him all the charges and expenses incurred in the execution of the trust. That is implied in every such deed." In a leading case: "The accounts must be taken, between the preceding and succeeding trustee, according to the sum which each may have collected, or on a consideration of the trouble and merits of each. But in this case the fund has been already charged with full commissions, and, therefore, should not be again charged with more than a necessary recompense to the present trustee for his trouble; which in this as in all similar cases must be regulated according to the services actually rendered." Gibson’s Case, 1 Bland Ch. 138, 149; see also, 17 Am. Dec. 257, 264.

Where by the terms of a deed of trust the trustee was to be allowed for "all just and reasonable expenses, costs, charges and commissions attending the due execution of" the trust, held that the trustee is entitled to be allowed attorney's fees paid his counsel, where it appears that the...
