### --- Page 0878 ---

1702

English case

COMPENSATION OF TRUSTEES.

[§ 741.]

It was held that where persons intrusted with the administration of a fund have incurred legitimate and proper expenses in performing duties thrown upon them by their fiduciary situation, they have a right, both at law and

fees were fair and reasonable, and that the services rendered were indispensable to the trustee in the proper execution of his trust.

Brady v. Dilley, 27 Md. 570. The general rule is, that if trustees bring suits against strangers, or strangers bring suits against the trustees, respecting the trust funds, costs will be awarded against the losing party as in other suits. If such trustees are compelled to pay costs, the amount so paid, including proper attorney's fees, will be allowed to them in their accounts, if the litigation was just and proper.

Darby v. Gilligan, 837 W. Va. 59. Trustees acting honestly are entitled to pay and retain the amount of a bill of costs, although some of the items in it are statute-barred, and the cestuis que trust cannot intervene and prevent the payment.

Budgett v. Budgett (1895), 1 Ch. 202. Expenses which a trustee must necessarily encounter, and which can be clearly and satisfactorily seen by the court, ought to be allowed, as the court on application at the proper time would have allowed them.

Hatton v. Weems, 12 Gill & J. 838. In an action against a son for the rent of land held by him in trust for his father and mother during their life, he should be reimbursed for boarding and caring for his mother.

Leeper v. Taylor (1892), 111 Mo. 312; s.c., 195 W. Rep. 955. The beneficiary of a trust instituted two actions against the trustees, the first being for trust funds and the second for the removal of the trustees for improper conduct. Both actions were unfounded.

Held, that the trustees were entitled to credits in their accounts for reasonable counsel fees expended by them in defense of these actions.

Lycan v. Miller, 56 Mo. App. 79. Trustees have an inherent equitable right to be reimbursed all expenses reasonably incurred in the execution of the trust, and it is immaterial that there are no provisions for such expenses in the instrument of trust. All such expenses are a lien upon the trust property, and the trustee will not be compelled to part with the property until such expenses are paid.

Held, that in this case the trustees had done nothing whereby they had lost such lien. Rensselaer & S. R.R. Co. v. Miller, 47 Vt. 146. In an action by the trustees of the Lick trust for a settlement of their accounts, and for permission to pay over to the residuary donees a part of what would, in the end, be coming to them, where the various beneficiaries named in the trust deed appeared by answer and cross-complaint, and the action was virtually transformed into a contest between the various trusts as against the residuary donees, the attorney’s fees allowed by the court to the attorneys for the various beneficiaries under the trust deed should not be charged to the trust fund, but each particular trust should be charged with its own attorney's fee.

Floyd v. Davis, 98 Cal. 591. Where the agent

<br>

### --- Page 0879 ---

§ 741.] COMPENSATION OF TRUSTEES. 1703  
in equity, to reimburse themselves out of the funds in their hands, without any special provision to that effect. A municipal corporation is justified in discharging, out of the corporate funds, the expenses of opposing quo warranto informations against individual members of the corporation, if the object of such informations be to impeach the title, or destroy the legal existence of the corporation as a body.¹ In Maine it has been held that where a controversy existed among the claimants of a trust fund, both as to the persons entitled to receive it and the respective proportions thereof, and a bill in equity was brought by one claimant against the trustee and other claimants, such trustee is entitled to be paid from the fund, in addition to compensation for care of the property, the expenses by him necessarily incurred in defending the suit.² It appears that this rule may be applied to expenditures for improvements provided the ex-

has purchased for his principal, but with his own funds, tax certificates upon the principal’s property, taking them in his own name, and has afterwards had the deeds thereof issued to himself, subsequently conveying the property, that instrument of conveyance will operate as an assignment to his grantees of his claim to be reimbursed for the amount expended in payment of the taxes upon the enforcement of the implied trust, and such grantees will be entitled to be reimbursed for that amount, with interest accounting in return, to the principal for the rents and profits of the property while they or their grantor have had possession. Waller v. Jones (1895), 107 Ala. 331; S. C., 1880. Rep. 277. Where testator gave all his property to his executors in trust to pay the income from one-third of his estate to his wife during her life, the commissions for receiving and disbursing the income are payable out of the income and not

from the corpus of the fund or residue of the estate. In re Thompson’s Estate, N. Y. Supl. 213. Defendant's decedent received as trustee evidences of indebtedness against a corporation from plaintiff’s decedent, which he exchanged for stock in the corporation. The stock, which was issued in the name of defendant's decedent, became more valuable than the indebtedness. Held, that in determining the amount due plaintiff's decedent, as defendant’s decedent retained the stock, he cannot recover for services in obtaining it, and, if his estate is to be credited with the services, it must surrender the stock procured by the services which plaintiff pays for. Lane v. Royster (1896), 118 N. Car. 159; S. C., 245 E. Rep. 703. See also Page v. Boynton, 63 N. H. 190; Biddle’s Appeal, 83 Pa. St. 340.

¹ Attorney-General v. Norwich, 2 Myl. & Cr. 406.  
² Morton v. Barrett, 22 Me. 257.

<br>

### --- Page 0880 ---

1704 COMPENSATION OF TRUSTEES. [§ 742.

Expenditure is profitable to the estate. In a recent case in West Virginia, it was held that where a trustee in good faith expends his own funds in improving the property of the cestui que trust, and the property is enhanced in value by such improvements to the extent of the expense thereof, such trustee is entitled to be reimbursed such expense out of the increased rents occasioned by such improvements.¹

§ 742. For Employment of Assistants.—In general it is held that a trustee may employ such assistants as may be essential to the proper execution of the trust, and pay them from the funds of the estate, but the rule is subject to modification. The decision in each case will depend upon the circumstances. The rule in England is stated by Sir John Leach, M. R., as follows: "Generally speaking, executors are not allowed to employ an agent to perform those duties which, by accepting the office of executors, they have taken upon themselves, but there may be very special circumstances in which it may be thought fit to allow them such expenses as they may have incurred by the employment of agents. It is for the Master to determine whether an executor who makes a claim for the employment of an agent ought to be allowed to charge his testator's estate with such a burden. The Master has here thought that the executor ought not to be allowed to charge the testator's estate with the whole commission claimed, but that 24 per cent is a fit allowance."² In another English case it was held that a trustee and executor, though taking under the will a commission as a satisfaction for his trouble, was entitled to allowances under a general trust to act and manage, as he

¹ Dickel v. Smith (1896) (W. Va.), 248 E. Rep. 564.

² Weiss v. Dill, 3 Myl. & K. 26, 27. Testator gave annuities to his trustees for their trouble in the execution of his will, and died possessed of several houses, let at weekly rents. "The trustees are justified in paying a person to collect these rents, and do not, therefore, lose their annuities." Wilkinson v. Wilkinson, 2 S. & S. 236. An executor upon transferring stock to a legatee paid one-sixteenth per cent. to a stock broker for identifying him at the bank. He was allowed the payment in passing his accounts. Jones v. Powell, 6 Beav. 488.

<br>

### --- Page 0881 ---

§ 743. COMPENSATION OF TRUSTEES. 1705

should think proper, and out of the rents and profits to pay all rates and taxes, charges of repairs, stewards, bailiffs and gamekeepers’ salaries and expenses, and all other charges and expenses he should think proper. In New Jersey the rule has been stated, as follows: “A trustee may employ necessary assistants in executing his trust and pay them. He may employ agents, collectors, accountants and other persons properly employed in similar affairs.” This decision is not supported by any American authorities, and it may be doubted whether the rule in the absolute and comprehensive form in which it is here stated would be generally sustained.

§ 743. Compensation as Executor and as Trustee.— Where a person occupying the position of executor or administrator is entitled to compensation as such, but subsequently comes into possession of the estate as trustee, he will be allowed compensation as trustee in addition to that received in the capacity of executor or administrator. But he will not be allowed two commissions for service at one and the same period. Before he can be entitled to compensation as a trustee he must have ceased to act in the capacity of executor, and, of course, have ceased to receive compensation as such. In a recent case in New York the rule is stated by Mr. Justice Finch, as follows: “Taking the adjudged cases together, they appear to establish that, to entitle the same persons to commissions as executors and as trustees, the will must provide, either by express terms or by fair intendment, for the separation of the two functions and duties, one duty to precede the other, and to be performed before the latter is begun, or substantially so performed; and must not provide for the co-existence, continuously and from the beginning, of the two functions and duties; and that where the will does so provide for the separate and successive duties, that of trustee must be actually entered upon and its performance begun, either by a

1 Webb v. Earl of Shaftesbury, 7 Ves. 480.  
2 Parker v. Johnson, 37 N. J. Eq. 367.

<br>

### --- Page 0882 ---

1706 COMPENSATION OF TRUSTEES. [§ 743.

real severance of the trust fund from the general assets, or a judicial decree which wholly discharges the executor and leaves him acting and liable.

1 Johnson v. Lawrence, 95 N. Y. 154, 162. See also Phenix v. Livingstone, 101 N. Y. 451; In re Crawford, 113 N. Y. 560; Gibson’s Case, 1 Bland Ch. 138; s. c., 17 Am. Dec. 257; In re McAlpine, 15 N. Y. St. Rep. 532; Clute v. Gould, 28 Hun, 348; In re Willetts, 112 N. Y. 289; In re Carman, 3 Redf. 46; In re Campbell, 1 Dem. 415; In re Jackson, 32 Hun, 200; Laytin v. Davidson, 29 Hun, 622; Holley v. S. G., 4 Edw. Ch. 284. If a person be appointed in a will an executor and trustee, such person is entitled to commissions, calculated on the corpus of the estate in each capacity, at such rate as will yield a reasonable compensation for the service in each of such respective offices. Pitney v. Everson, 42 N. J. Eq. 361. The executors were constituted trustees of three distinct trusts; the will directed property for those trusts to be set aside and the income paid over annually to the beneficiaries. The accounts of the executors, as such, were fully and finally settled, and the trust funds were separated by the decree of the surrogate on such accounting. It was held, that, thereafter, the executors acted simply as trustees, and were entitled to commissions, as such, in addition to those received by them as executors. In re Mason, 98 N. Y. 527. Neither Code Civ. Proc. § 2736, nor any other statute, requires that two or more executors of an estate valued at $100,000, or upwards, though severally entitled to full commissions upon principal, are limited to the allowance of a single rate, as regard income. The only as trustee.”? But the double or triple rate, if awardable upon the principal fund, must be allowed also upon the income thereof. Waters v. Faber, 2 Dem. 290. Where a will gave the testator’s residuary estate to his executors in trust, with authority to sell real estate and divide the whole into specified parts, each to be kept invested and the income paid to a beneficiary named during life, it was held that, upon the division, the duties of the executors, as such, ceased, and they held the property as trustees, and so they were entitled to double commissions. In re Crawford, 113 N. Y. 560. Where the same persons were appointed and acted as executors and testamentary trustees, and their duties as executors and trustees were separate and distinct, they are entitled to full commissions on the principal of the estate as executors and to another full commission as trustees. Testator gave the residue of his estate to his executors, in trust for his children, and directed the “executors” to pay over the net income of each share during the life of the beneficiary, and authorized them, “for the survivor of them, or their successors in the trust,” to make certain advances to the beneficiaries. The will further provided, that “the net income of the respective sums so held in trust for my children shall be paid to them, or applied to their use, during the term of their natural lives, and that the legal title to the residue shall vest in ‘my executors and trustees.’” Held, that the duties of the persons named as executors were distinct from their duties

<br>

### --- Page 0883 ---

§ 743. COMPENSATION OF TRUSTEES, 1707  
Payment of double commissions is not regarded by courts of equity with favor, and where such commissions render the compensation obviously excessive, equity will afford relief. In an important and instructive case in Massachusetts, the court said: "The commission of five per cent upon income received as guardian, in addition to a like sum charged upon the same money as received in the capacity of trustee, is manifestly excessive. Such a commission upon income received and paid over is allowable only as a convenient measure of compensation for services supposed to have been actually rendered. It implies something more than mere nominal service and the responsibility of the trust. There is no rule of law and no principle of right by which such commissions are to be charged or allowed without regard to the rendition of actual services therefor. Where the same person is both guardian and trustee, it would be a reproach to the law, and to the courts charged with the protection of

as trustees. In re Curtiss’ Estate (1896), 37 N. Y. Suppl. 586. A trustee is entitled to double commissions on a certain amount of the trust fund, which he, as executor, turned over to himself as trustee, but not on the full amount. Willett’s Estate, 2 N. Y. Suppl. 666. The will of W., after directing the payment of debts and expenses, named six persons as "executors of and trustees under" it. A series of separate trust estates were constituted, running for the lives of the specified beneficiaries. Some of these required specific sums to be set apart; others provided for the severance of the trust estates from the general assets, and their management by five of the six persons so named, holding as trustees. A large portion of the trust estate consisted of real estate, and provision was made for partition. Authority was conferred upon the trustees to lease and to sell certain

portions, and general authority for the management of the land. The trustees were also empowered, in their discretion, to commit, by revocable power of attorney, the management of certain of the trust estates to the beneficiary. The accounts of the executors as such were settled, leaving in their hands only the trust estates, which were severed from the general assets, and thereafter separate accounts were kept with each beneficiary. Held, that by the will, the testator contemplated and provided for two separate duties to be performed by his representatives, first as executors, and thereafter as trustees, and that they were entitled to commissions in both capacities, but that they were not entitled to commissions on the value of the real estate unsold at the termination of the trusts. Phoenix v. Livingston, 101 N. Y. 451.

<br>

### --- Page 0884 ---

1708 COMPENSATION OF TRUSTEES. [§ 744.

such trust, to allow him to charge full compensation in both capacities for the same service.”1

§ 744. Compensation as Solicitor.—In England it is well settled that a trustee who is a solicitor will not be allowed to make a charge for his professional services in the execution of the trust. In a familiar English case the ground of this rule is stated by Lord Lyndhurst, as follows: “A trustee placed in the position of a solicitor might, if allowed to perform the duties of a solicitor, and to be paid for them, find it very often proper to institute and carry on legal proceedings which he would not do if he were to derive no emolument from them himself, and if he were to employ another person.”2 This rule applies not only to the solicitor-trustee as an individual, but as well to the legal firm of which he may be a member. Such firm, acting as the solicitor of the trustee, cannot charge for services, either in litigation or for preparing legal papers for the trust estate.3 In a leading and important English case the doctrine is set forth, as follows: E, a partner in a firm of country solicitors, was one of two trustees of a will which contained no power to charge for professional services. E and his co-trustee were respondents to an application for maintenance by a next friend on behalf of an infant under the summary procedure of the court, and E’s firm, through their London agents, acted as solicitors for E and his co-trustees and made profit costs. It was held that E’s firm were entitled to receive those

1 Blake v. Pegram, 101 Mass. 592, 600.

2 New v. Jones, 9 Jar. Prec. 338.

3 Lawton v. Elwes, L. R. 34 Ch. D. 675; In re Roberts, L. R. 43 Ch. D. 52; Ex parte Liquorish, L. R. 25 Q. B. Div. 176; Fisher v. Doody, 67 L. T. 650. A mortgagee cannot charge his mortgagor with more than his principal, interest and costs; he is not entitled to charge the mortgagor with any sum payable for his, the mortgagee’s own profit costs as coming within benefit, such as professional or profit costs for the preparation of the mortgage deed, if he is a solicitor, or for the valuation of the property for the purpose of the mortgage, if he is a surveyor; nor, on the principle that a mortgagee cannot clog the equity of redemption with any by agreement, can he contract with the mortgagor for any such payment. Field v. Hopkins, L. R. 44 Ch. D. 524.

<br>

### --- Page 0885 ---

COMPENSATION OF TRUSTEES.

§ 744.) 1709

the exception laid down in Cradock v. Piper. Although that case has been often disapproved, it has been so long acted on as a binding authority that it ought not now to be overruled. The exception applies not only to proceedings in a hostile suit, but to friendly proceedings in Chambers, such as an application for maintenance of an infant. After the death of E’s co-trustee, E was made defendant to an administration action in which a receiver was appointed, and E’s firm, through their London agents, acted for the receiver and made profit costs. It was held that these profit costs could not be retained by the firm, on the principle that a trustee must not place himself in a position in which his interest conflicts with his duty. E and his firm made profit costs by preparing leases and agreements for leases of portions of the trust estate, which costs were paid by the lessees. It was held that although the costs were paid by the lessees, the solicitors were employed on behalf of the trust estate, and that E and his firm must account to the estate for the costs. E and his co-trustees appointed E’s partner steward of a manor which formed part of the trust estate, and fees for manorial business were paid to the steward by the tenants and brought into the partnership accounts. It was held that the fees, not being received by the steward in his character of solicitor, were not liable to be accounted for to the trust estate. But where the trust provides that a solicitor-trustee may charge for professional services they will be allowed by the court, but his charges will be limited by the instrument. If it provides simply for professional services he will not be permitted to make a charge for time and labor expended in another capacity. But where the in-

1 Cradock v. Piper, 1 Macn. & G. 664.

2 Lawton v. Elwes, L. R. 34 Ch. D. 675.

3 Newton v. Chapman, L. R. 27 Ch. D. 584. A solicitor was appointed executor, and was to be "at liberty to charge for his professional services." It was held, that he was only entitled to charge for services strictly professional, and not for matters which an executor ought to have done without the intervention of a solicitor, such as for attendances to pay premiums on policies, attending at the bank to make transfers, attendances on proctors, auctioneers, legatees and creditors. Harbin v. Darby, 28 Beav. 325.

<br>

### --- Page 0886 ---

1710 COMPENSATION OF TRUSTEES. [§ 745.

strument authorizes a charge for professional services and for all business done and time expended in the execution of the trust, a reasonable charge for services, whether professional or otherwise, will be upheld.?

§ 745. Compensation as Solicitor Continued.—In the decisions of the American courts on this subject there is some divergence, but in the main the English rule is followed. In a case in New York, it was held that a guardian will not be allowed an extra compensation for services, although not strictly within the line of his duties, as where the guardian is an attorney and counselor at law he cannot charge for professional services rendered in the affairs of his ward, but is restricted to the statutory allowance. Neither an order of a surrogate, before the services are rendered,

1 Ames v. Taylor, L. R. 25 Ch. D. 72; Stocken v. Dawes, 6 Beav. 371; Burdon v. Burdon, 1 V. & B. 170; Barrett v. Hartley, L. R. 2 Eq. 7873; In re Freeman, W. N. (1887), p. 210; Brown v. De Tastet, Jac. 284; Wedderburn v. Wedderburn, 22 Beav. 84; Brown v. Litton, 1 P. Wms. 140. A testator, by his will, authorized any trustee thereof who might be a solicitor to make the usual professional, or other proper and reasonable charges, for all business done and time expended in relation to the trusts of the will, whether such business was usually within the business of a solicitor or not. On the further consideration of an action for the administration of the testator’s estate, an order was made for the taxation of the costs, charges and expenses of the trustees, and it was directed that the taxing master should have regard to the terms of the will as to the costs of the trustees: Held, that the taxing master had power to allow to a trustee, who was a solicitor, the proper charges for business not strictly of a professional nature transacted by him in relation to the trust estate. Ames v. Taylor, L. R. 25 Ch. D. 72. A testator appointed B & G his executors and trustees; bequeathed to G, if he should accept the offices of trustee and executor, £200, and declared that G and every future trustee of his will, who might be a solicitor, should be entitled to receive out of the estate his usual professional costs and charges for business transacted by him, including business not strictly professional, but which might or would have been performed in person by a trustee not being a solicitor. Considerable sums were charged by G against the estate for business done by him, including charges for his trouble in matters not strictly professional. Held, that G, although a legacy was given to him in his capacity of trustee, was entitled under the above clause to charge for his trouble as well as to make professional charges for business done by him as solicitor. Bennett v. Bennett, L. R. (1893), 2 Ch. £13.

<br>

### --- Page 0887 ---

§ 745.) COMPENSATION OF TRUSTEES, 1711

directing the performance thereof, and fixing the extra compensation, nor an order ratifying and allowing it will legalize the charge. In a case in Maryland, on an ex parte petition of the mortgagee, an order was passed allowing him, out of the proceeds of sale, a certain sum for professional services rendered by him in defending the order ratifying the sale, and for his personal expenses in attending to the taking of testimony on the subject. On exception to the auditor’s account allowing this sum, it was held that this was a charge that ought not to be allowed against the proceeds of sale, to the prejudice of the mortgagor.

1 Morgan v. Hannas, 49 N. Y. 667. A trustee cannot be allowed a counsel fee for his own services as a lawyer, in addition to his expenses and commissions. Binssey v. Paige, 1 Abb. App. Dec. 139. A trustee cannot demand a compensation for services beyond what is founded on the positive agreement of the parties, and where a trustee, who was a counselor at law, was to be allowed for “all his advances and responsibilities,” it was held that, though he was entitled to a liberal indemnity for his expenses and responsibilities incurred in the due and faithful execution of his trust, yet he was not entitled to a counsel fee, as a general retainer, nor for anything more than what is understood, in the language of a court of equity, to be “just allowances.” Green v. Winter, 1 Johns. Ch. 26. One of the executors of a will, therefore, who is an attorney and counselor at law, cannot be allowed any fee whatever from the estate, for professionally defending and conducting an action brought against the estate, although requested by his coexecutors to appear in such action and undertake such defense, with a promise of compensation; and although the legatees and next of kin united in such request. Accordingly, where C, a counselor at law, who was one of the executors of M, was requested by his coexecutors, who promised compensation from the estate, to defend an action of ejectment brought against a grantee of the testator, with warranty, who had given notice to the executors to defend, and the legatees and devisees had also united in a request to C to undertake such defense, and C thereupon undertook the case, tried it at circuit, argued it at general term and at the court of appeals, and, after a second trial, negotiated an advantageous settlement of the litigation. Held, that the surrogate, on the settlement of the executor’s accounts, properly refused to allow anything to C for such legal services. Collier v. Munn, 41 N. Y. 143.

2 Mahoney v. Mackubin, 51 Md. 268. An executor, being a trustee for the estate he represents, can receive no compensation for his time and trouble in organizing and working up a defense to a suit against the estate, or a claim for dower, nor can he receive compensation for professional services, as an attorney at law in defending such suit. Hough v. Harvey, 71 Md. 72.

<br>

### --- Page 0888 ---

1712 COMPENSATION OF TRUSTEES. [§ 745.

Connecticut the rule has been stated, as follows: "A trustee is not entitled to charge his cestui que trust for professional services of himself or his partner. Trustees can only be allowed costs out of pocket for professional services transacted by a firm, one of whom is a trustee, though the business be done by one of the partners who is not a trustee. A trustee must not be in a position where his interest is opposed to his duty, and cannot receive pay for services where his relation is such that his personal interest may conflict with the duties of his trust." In Pennsylvania the opposite view is upheld. In a recent case the rule in that State is set forth, as follows: "It cannot be doubted that for services of an extraordinary character, rendered by a trustee, he is entitled to extra compensation beyond the usual allowance for receiving and disbursing trust funds. If professional services, necessary to the proper administration of the trust, have been rendered by a trustee in person, he is clearly entitled to such reasonable compensation as he

would have paid had he been

1 Babcock v. Hubbard, 56 Conn. 284, 287.

2 Perkin’s Appeal, 108 Pa. St. 314; s.c., 56 Am. Rep. 208, 209. When a trustee renders professional services in compelling a guardian to perform his duty, he is entitled to such reasonable compensation as he would have paid had he been obliged to employ counsel. Lowrie’s Appeal, 1 Grant’s Cas. 373. Money advanced by trustees to the cestuis que trust, with the understanding that the same should be repaid out of the rent of the trust property, is a lien on net incoming rents. The same is true respecting the charges for legal services of one of the trustees in the management of the trust property. The rents must be applied to the payment of such allowances until they are liquidated. Ellig v. Naglee, 9 Cal. 683., "If the services of one administrator be actually

obliged to employ counsel."

worth more than those of another, if, for instance, in addition to the naked duties of an administrator, he brings in aid the services, the experience and the counsel of an attorney of the court, there is no reason why his compensation should not be regulated accordingly, and why he should not be paid for the discharge of services, which an administrator, not qualified to render them, would have found it his duty to procure at the expense of the estate. The suggestion of the appellant's counsel, that "Mr. French is entitled to no more fees than if he were not a counselor at law," may, with the qualifications suggested, be true. If, however, he has performed the duties of a counselor at law, or if he were selected for an arduous duty, requiring unusual legal knowledge, and selected by those who had an interest in his services.

<br>

### --- Page 0889 ---

§ 746.] COMPENSATION OF TRUSTEES. 1713  
§ 746. In Trading with Trust Funds.—The English rule is held not to apply where a trustee, whether express or constructive, having rightfully and properly become possessed of the trust property, employs it in a legitimate and successful business venture. In such a case, though he is by implication a trustee of the profits of the business, he will be entitled to a fair and reasonable remuneration for his labor. In the leading case of Brown v. Litton, the plaintiff’s testator was the captain of a ship, and on a voyage had in his possession £800, which he proposed to invest in trade. The captain died on the voyage, and the defendant,—the mate of the ship,—becoming captain, took the money and invested it in trade. He was successful in his investment, and returned to England. On the filing of a bill against him for an accounting, Lord Keeper Harcourt said: “He ought clearly to account for the profits made of the money; the primary intention in carrying abroad this money was to invest it in trade, and not to return home again with it, and, therefore, the defendant, having observed the intention of the testator in trading therewith, and having such prudent care in the management of it, as, it might be presumed, he would have taken of his own money, the defendant would not have been liable to answer for any loss that might have happened. To recompense the defendant for his care in trading with the money, the Master shall settle a proper salary for the pains and trouble he had incurred in the management of it.” In a more recent case, where, on the death of one partner, the survivor retaining his capital and employing it in the trade, he was decreed to account for the profits derived from it, the court making him proper allowances for the management of the business.

and for the express purpose of obviating, by the selection, the need of retaining counsel and the hazards of unadvised proceedings, it would ill-accord with the purposes of justice, and, as we conceive, would not be promotive of the best interest of parties, even to hold that the administrator, for such services rendered and duties performed, is not entitled to receive an adequate compensation commensurate with their real value and importance.” Wendell v. French, 19 N. H. 205, 210.

1 Brown v. Litton, 1 P. Wms. 440.  
2 Brown v. De Tastet, Jac. 284.

<br>

### --- Page 0890 ---

1714 COMPENSATION OF TRUSTEES, [§ 747.

§ 747. Lien of Trustee for Compensation.—In some of the States it has been held that a trustee has a lien on the trust fund in his possession for compensation for services rendered the estate. In Vermont it has been held that a trustee is entitled to retain compensation for his services as trustee, out of the trust fund in his hands, and it is unnecessary to plead the same in off-set, and it makes no difference whether the compensation of the trustee is given by express or implied contract. In another State it has been held that a provision in a mortgage that a trustee shall receive a just compensation for his services does not constitute a lien on the property mortgaged. In a recent case where, in an action to have a mortgage which had been paid formally discharged, a trustee under the mortgage refused to sign the proper acknowledgment, because of a clause in the mortgage reading “each of the trustees shall be entitled to just compensation for all services which he may render,” claiming thereby a lien on the mortgage property, it was held that the terms of the mortgage gave him no lien for his services.

By partnership articles, on the death of a partner, the survivors were entitled to the good will. A firm partner died in 1801, having appointed his copartners executors. They continued the trade, having made out an account, from which it appeared that the assets (consisting nearly wholly of outstanding debts) were £496,000, the liabilities £410,000, and the surplus £35,900, of which A B's share was £55. The survivors carried this to the credit of the deceased partner's account, and they paid the amount with interest to the cestuis que trust as they respectively came of age. In 1831 the children claimed to participate in the profits of the subsequent trading, which in the thirty years had amounted to £308,000, on the ground that their capital had been employed therein. At the hearing inquiries were directed with a view to charge them. But, on the master’s report, the court having come to the conclusion that at the testator’s death the surplus was merely nominal, that the business to wind up was insolvent, and that the subsequent profits were attributable to good will and the personal exertions and capital of the surviving partners: Held, that the plaintiffs were not entitled to participate in the profits, so far as those profits were attributable to the good will and connection in trade of the old firm, and that their share in that portion of the profits in which they would be entitled to participate could not be estimated higher than the interest already paid. Wedderburn v. Wedderburn, 22 Beav. 84.

1 Hubbard v. Fisher, 25 Vt. 539.

2 Mercantile Trust & Deposit Co. v. Pickerell, 99 N. Car. 139; s. c.,

<br>

### --- Page 0891 ---

§ 748. ] COMPENSATION OF TRUSTEES. 1715

§ 748. Waiver of Compensation.—It is well established that where a trustee has a legal claim to compensation, it may be waived by a definite and positive agreement. And where from any reason such an agreement is made and concluded a subsequent claim to compensation will not be sustained. In a case where trustees appointed by a last will for the sale of real estate refused to act, J, a new trustee, was appointed by the Chancellor in their place, and, having sold the estate, he claimed a commission for his trouble, but it appearing that he had waived all claim to commission anterior to his appointment, and by a family arrangement, in which he was concerned, he was procured to be appointed trustee, on the express agreement that no commissions were to be charged, it was held that he was not entitled to any commissions, but only to his actual expenses incurred in the execution of the trust. Where it appears that the assuming of the trust was voluntary, and that the motive assigned was that of friendship for the beneficiaries, a subsequent claim to compensation will not be upheld.?

5 S. E. Rep. 417. "He claims that his lien arises by virtue of the following provision of the mortgage: 'That each of the said trustees shall be entitled to just compensation for all services which he, in common with his associates or either of them, or otherwise, may hereinafter render under the trust created by these presents, which compensation shall be paid by the party of the first part,' who was the defendant corporation. This provides for compensation, but there is no provision or clause of the mortgage that such compensation shall constitute a part of the mortgage debt, or that it shall be a lien of any nature upon the property embraced by the mortgage. The most that could be claimed was that the court, when its aid should be asked, would not, in the

exercise of a sound and just discretion, compel the trustees to acknowledge the satisfaction and discharge of the mortgage with reasonable compensation to them should be paid by the mortgagor. The court would probably thus protect and help the trustee in a case like this; but this source of protection was not a lien on the property, it is simply a power of the court to compel fair dealing, not to be exercised as to do prejudice to any party interested." Ibid.

¹ Ridgely v. Gittings, 2 Har. & G. 58.

² Vestry, etc. v. Barksdale, 1 Stroh. Eq. 197; Barry v. Barry, 1 Md. Ch. 20; Haglar v. McCombs, 66 N. Car. 345. On the petition of East the executrix of her husband, to the orphans’ court for an allowance of commission on the

1716

COMPENSATION OF TRUSTEES,

[§ 748.]

Created fixes the compensation, the trustee cannot claim a larger commission. His acceptance of the trust will be held as an agreement to accept such compensation as the instrument directs.

Amount of the inventories returned, that court decreed that no commission be allowed: 1. Because in all the settlements made by the agent of the executrix all his expenses were charged and allowed under an express avowal by him that no commission would be charged by her. 2. In consequence of the abandonment of commission charges against the estate, where vouchers were not in form, or altogether wanting, were allowed. On appeal reversed and decreed that the executrix be allowed for her commission 5 per cent. on the amount of the inventories, excluding what may have been lost or may have perished. Eversfield v. Eversfield, 4 Har. & J. 12. In Denmead v. Denmead, 62 Md. 321, it is held that a trustee, by waiving certain commissions allowed him by the decree appointing him trustee is not thereby estopped, from claiming and receiving commissions subsequently earned. In the course of the opinion the court says: "But his (the trustee’s) bounty was entirely voluntary; and it by no means followed that, because he had given away commissions in the past, he was obliged to continue to do so in the future. He was still free to do as he chose with his own. His acceptance of the trusteeship could not be an equitable renunciation of commissions, which might thereafter be allowed, unless it could be construed to be a contract with the court to this effect. Trustees are appointed because of their fitness to perform their duties, and the commissions are the legitimate compensation for their services. It would be unprecedented and improper for the court to exact from a trustee, as the condition of his appointment, a promise to make a pecuniary sacrifice. We certainly do not believe that such a thing was intended by the court."

College v. Wellington, 13 Rich. Eq. 195; Jackson v. Jackson, 3 N. J. Eq. 113.

### --- Page 0893 ---

CHAPTER XL.

PLEADING AND PRACTICE IN TRUST CASES.

§ 749. Introductory.  
§ 750. Jurisdiction in Trust Cases.  
§ 751. Jurisdiction Continued.  
§ 752. Extent of Equity Jurisdiction.  
§ 753. The Reforming of the Instrument.  
§ 754. Parties to Trust Suits.  
§ 755. Parties to Trust Suits Continued.  
§ 756. The Averments of a Bill.  
§ 757. The Amendment of the Bill.  
§ 758. The Decree of the Court.

§ 749. Introductory.—It is not the object of this chapter to present a comprehensive, or even summary view of the law relating to pleading and practice in trust cases, but simply to touch upon a few points concerning the jurisdiction of courts of equity in such cases, the parties that may be heard or that may be brought before them, the reformation of bills in equity, the decrees of the court, etc. There are no rules of pleading and practice that are peculiar to trust cases. The general principles by which pleading and practice in courts of equity are governed apply to all cases arising out of the establishment and administration of trusts. But the discussion of these principles, even in the most general or summary manner, would be altogether aside from the purposes of this work. Besides, during the progress of the work, these rules, in a variety of forms and in their application to very numerous subjects, have incidentally come under consideration. Nevertheless, it is assumed that a very brief and general notice of such of these rules as will be of primary importance to the advocate in bringing and conducting a suit relating to a trust, will not be unwelcome.

<br>

### --- Page 0894 ---

1718 PLEADING AND PRACTICE. [§ 750.

§ 750. Jurisdiction in Trust Cases.—It is the rule, that all actions for the establishment of the fiduciary relation, for the execution and enforcement of trusts, or of the obligations arising out of the trust relation, and for the investigation and adjustment of differences between the parties to a trust, are within the exclusive jurisdiction of equity. This doctrine may be modified to a certain extent by statute, or by special rules of the court; but except as so modified, all cases relating to trusts are within the jurisdiction of courts of chancery. This rule includes, not only express trusts, but also all trusts arising by implication of law. In a case before the Supreme Judicial Court of Massachusetts, it was held that the power of this court, under the statute, to hear and determine in equity "all suits and proceedings for the enforcing and regulating the execution of trusts, whether the trusts relate to real or personal estate," extends to implied as well as express trusts.1

1 Wright v. Dame, 39 Mass. 55. See also Haverstick v. Trudel, 51 Cal. 431; United States v. Gillespie, 9 Fed. Rep. 74; Norton v. Hixon, 25 Ill. 371; s. c., 79 Am. Dec. 338; Maryland Fire Ins. Co. v. Dalrymple, 25 Md. 242; s. c., 89 Am. Dec. 779; Dorsey v. Garey, 30 Md. 489; Stokes v. Frazier, 72 Ill. 428; Sturgeon v. Burrell, 1 Ill. App. 537; Reese v. Wallace, 113 Ill. 589; Weaver v. Fisher, 110 Ill. 146; Davis v. Hamlin, 108 Ill. 39; s. c., 48 Am. Dec. 541; Whitman v. Fisher, 74 Ill. 147; Hopkins v. Granger, 52 Ml. 502; McCartney v. Bostwick, 32 N. Y. 53; Cooper v. McClun, 16 Ill. 485. The jurisdiction of a court of chancery over a trust, to enforce it and prevent further abuse of it by removal of the delinquent trustee, is not to be doubted. Lasley v. Lasley, 1 Duv. 117. "In the case of a trust, as between a principal and agent, it is well settled that a court of equity has jurisdiction to enforce an accounting and a surrender of the trust property, the time when the trustee should do so having arrived, and he having refused. Weaver v. Fisher, 110 Ill. 146. A cestui que trust cannot maintain an action at law against a trustee while the trust is still open, his only remedy is by bill in equity. Davis v. Coburn, 128 Mass. 377. A court of equity has the same jurisdiction over security trusts, that it ordinarily possesses and exercises over other trust estates. Van Houten v. McKelway, 17 N. J. Eq. 126. In Pennsylvania, courts of common pleas have equity jurisdiction of a bill seeking a conveyance of real estate from defendant to complainant, if the case involves a trust. Appeal of Chadwicke (Pa.), 7 Atl. Rep. 178. Where the proceeds of a partition sale, resting in

<br>

### --- Page 0895 ---

§ 750.

PLEADING AND PRACTICE.

are of original and special equity jurisdiction. Thus, where defendants were trustees under deeds creating express trusts in favor of several persons, and imposing special duties upon the trustees in relation to the trust property, court for distribution, are, by agreement which is not a part of the partition suit, turned over to a trustee appointed, who gives a bond approved by the court, made payable to the State, the court has jurisdiction over the trust and authority to give effect to the bond, and the trustee and his sureties are liable for a violation of the trust. State v. Roudebush (1888), 114 Ind. 347; s.c., 16 N. E. Rep. 636.

“It may be conceded that the district courts have jurisdiction of actions against executors and administrators to declare and enforce trusts, in respect to real estate, in many cases, and that actions may be brought in those courts, in respect to certain controversies having their origin in the administration of the estates of deceased persons; but the probate courts have the exclusive jurisdiction of the accounts of executors and administrators, and of the final distribution of the estates of decedents.” Auguisola v. Arnaz, 51 Cal. 435, 438.

Corporate assets being a trust fund, the forum for its enforcement is a court of equity, and this is especially true where they constitute a joint trust after paying all its creditors. Foster v. Mullanphy Planing Mill Co., 92 Mo. 79.

There is no adequate remedy against a board of trustees for violating a trust by quo warranto brought by a private individual. “That tries the right only, and gives no sufficient relief for such breach. The remedy lies only in a court of chancery.” Dart v. Houston, 22 Ga. 506.

A bill in equity will lie by the trustees of colleges, named as beneficiaries in a will, to enforce their rights under the will. Trustees of Emory & Henry College v. Shoemaker College (1895), 92 Va. 320; s.c., 23 S. E. Rep. 765.

The court of common pleas in Pennsylvania, sitting as a court of equity, has jurisdiction of a bill to declare a resulting trust in land. Jackson’s Appeal (1887) (Pa.), 8 Atl. Rep. 870.

This court has the power to control, in a suit between trustees for different creditors, the sale of property conveyed and mortgaged to them as security for debts due such creditors, in such manner as to protect the equitable rights of all parties in interest, so far as it may be done without prejudice to the rights of any. But its exercise rests in sound discretion; it should be only for cause. Dunean v. Graflin, 26 N. J. Eq. 228.

Where on application of plaintiff, holding a life estate in certain unimproved lots under a marriage settlement, for their sale and investment of the proceeds in improved property, the court commissioner reported that the sale was for the interest of all concerned, it was a compliance with Code Va., ch. 124, § 2, providing for the sale of such property on petition on proper showing by the trustee. Payne v. Morriss (1888) (Va.), 5 S. E. Rep. 568.

A court of equity has jurisdiction over a bill by a cestui que trust, to have the will construed; the directions of the court to the trustees

<br>

### --- Page 0896 ---

1720

PLEADING AND PRACTICE,

and providing that, in a certain event, the property should go in equal shares to each of the beneficiaries, it was held that one of the beneficiaries could not, upon the happening of said event, maintain assumpsit against the trustees for

and executors in regard to the proper method of executing the trust; and, as auxiliary to this, to have an account rendered to enable it to ascertain what is the residue of the estate available for the purpose of the trust. United States v. Gillespie, 9 Fed. Rep. 74. Although probate courts have, to some degree, observed the general jurisdiction of equity over the subject of administration, yet equity may assume direction of the trusts of a will in a case properly brought before it. So held upon a bill brought by one executor against his coexecutor, who differed with him in regard to the disposition of the estate under the will, for aid in the execution of the trust. Woodfin v. Phebus, 30 Fed. Rep. 289. The enforcement of a specific execution of a trust, declared by parol in relation to real property, if plain and unambiguous in its terms and established by satisfactory evidence, comes within the jurisdiction of the court of equity. Haywood v. Ensley, 8 Humph. 460. Trusts do not come within the jurisdiction of a court of law, and the judgment against a trustee, in a suit against the administrator, by the next of kin, does not act as a bar to a suit in the court of equity. Blue v. Patterson, 1 Dev. & Bat. Eq. 457. Equity has inherent jurisdiction over trusts for charitable purposes such as building a church. Hopkins v. Upshur, 20 Tex. 89; s. c., 70 Am. Dec. 375. Where, by his will, a testator leaves his property to his son, in trust for the son’s children, the income of the property to be applied to the support and education of such children until the youngest reaches the age of 21 years, when the whole property is to be divided equally among the several children, and by said will the trustee is to be held accountable to the children for the income of the property; the trustee takes no beneficial interest in the estate, and it is such a trust as a court of equity has jurisdiction of, and the court can assume entire control and management of the property whenever its interposition is invoked by proper proceedings on the part of either the trustee or cestuis que trust, and such control is properly assumed by an order made when all the parties are before the court on proceedings for the removal of the trustee and appointment of a receiver. Burroughs v. Gaither (1886), 66 Md. 171; s. c., 7 Atl. Rep. 243. The plea of being a purchaser for value and without notice is simply the assertion of a superior equity, and the entertainment by a court of equity of a bill to subject the legal title to the land claimed to have been so purchased to a trust ex maleficio is not in contravention of the guaranty of the Pennsylvania constitution that a legal title shall be tried in a court of law, and that two verdicts of a jury concurring shall be required to settle it. Church’s Appeal (Pa.), 7 Atl. Rep. 751. See further, Norton v. Ray, 189 Mass. 230; Jasper v. Hazen, 1 N. Dak. 75;

<br>

### --- Page 0897 ---

§ 751.] PLEADING AND PRACTICE, 1721

her share, but that chancery was the proper forum, where the rights of all could be ascertained and settled! In a case before the Supreme Court of Illinois it was held that where purely legal titles are involved, and no other relief is sought, a court of equity will not assume jurisdiction to construe a will, but will remit the parties to their remedies at law; but if any trust is reposed in the executors, they may seek the aid and direction of a court of equity in the management or execution of the trust. Where money, under a trust has been misapplied, the remedy must be sought in equity, not at law. In a case in Illinois it was held that where a purchaser of swamp lands from a county seeks to recover back the purchase money paid therefor, upon the ground that a trust was devolved upon the county, under the several laws on that subject, requiring the application of the proceeds of the sales of swamp lands to their drainage, and that the money has been diverted to other purposes, the remedy, if any exists, is in chancery and not at law.

§ 751. Jurisdiction Continued.—It is a general rule of equity pleading and practice, that the jurisdiction of a court of equity is limited to cases in which there is no adequate remedy at law. But this doctrine does not necessarily apply to cases relating to trusts where equity has a jurisdiction that is in addition to any legal rights that the complainant may enjoy and that is concurrent with such.
